summary: B2B commercial financing platform using machine-learning underwriting to offer point-of-sale credit lines to small businesses purchasing from vendors. Pivoted its business model multiple times and effectively wound down independent operations around 2021 after struggling to sustain growth against B2B BNPL competitors.
lesson: Large debt facilities can fund rapid loan-book growth, but if the underlying B2B BNPL unit economics don't work, repeated pivots eat runway faster than they find product-market fit.
source: Fintech industry coverage of Behalf's decline and pivots (2020-2021)

name: Behavox
summary: AI compliance surveillance startup that faced explosive investor lawsuits and fraud allegations against its founder in 2023, with backers accusing him of falsifying financials to raise capital.
lesson: Founder integrity and financial transparency are existential risks that due diligence must catch before large rounds close.
source: Forbes and Financial Times reporting on Behavox investor lawsuits (2023)

name: Belly
summary: Chicago-based mobile loyalty and rewards-card platform backed by Andreessen Horowitz and NEA that failed to scale profitably, rebranded as Hatch Loyalty in 2017, and sold its product assets to Mobivity for just $3 million in 2018.
lesson: A loyalty platform's per-merchant revenue must exceed the cost of hardware and support, or growth simply accelerates cash burn.

name: Belong
summary: AI-powered recruiting platform aiming to give candidates more control over the hiring process shut down in 2020 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Candidate-centric recruiting models struggle when the paying customer (the employer) is not the one being served first.

name: Bench Accounting
funding: ~$113M (Bain Capital Ventures, Contour Venture Partners, Altman Capital)
summary: AI/software-assisted bookkeeping service for small businesses, combining automated transaction categorization with human bookkeepers. Abruptly shut down without warning in December 2024, locking out thousands of small-business customers from their financial records, before being revived days later by Employer.com, which acquired its assets.
lesson: A sudden, no-notice shutdown of an AI-assisted financial back-office service can strand thousands of small-business customers' books overnight, illustrating the outsized downstream risk when financial infrastructure startups fail without an orderly wind-down.
source: TechCrunch, Reuters coverage of Bench Accounting shutdown and revival (Dec 2024)

name: Bend Health
summary: Bend Health built a collaborative-care model for pediatric and family mental health integrated with primary care practices, but underwent major layoffs and ceased most operations in 2024 amid the broader contraction in venture-backed pediatric telehealth.
lesson: Pediatric behavioral health startups that scaled during the 2021-2022 funding peak were especially exposed when the funding environment tightened in 2023-2024.

name: Benefitfocus
summary: Public benefits-administration software company struggled for years with unprofitability and was acquired by Voya Financial in 2023 for about $570M, well below its earlier market capitalization peak.
lesson: Chronic unprofitability as a public company eventually forces a distressed sale even for an established category leader.

name: BenevolentAI
summary: Once Europe's most-hyped AI drug discovery unicorn, BenevolentAI's platform failed to produce late-stage clinical wins, its lead dermatitis drug missed trial endpoints, and its public listing lost over 90% of its value before a rescue financing and effective take-private restructuring in 2023-2024.
lesson: AI-generated drug candidates still must clear the same expensive, high-failure-rate clinical trial gauntlet as any other drug — platform hype does not de-risk biology.
source: Financial Times and Reuters coverage of BenevolentAI's 2023-24 collapse

name: Benson Hill
summary: Plant-breeding and food ingredient company Benson Hill, which went public via SPAC in 2022, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in July 2024 after mounting losses.
lesson: SPAC mergers can provide capital without fixing an underlying business model that isn't yet generating sustainable margins.
source: Reuters, Benson Hill Chapter 11 filing July 2024

name: Berg Health
funding: ~$400M invested in its AI platform and pipeline
summary: Early AI-driven biopharma pioneer (Berg LLC) that built a proprietary 'Interrogative Biology' AI platform to mine patient biological data for disease drivers, applied to oncology and diagnostics. After investing around $400 million over more than a decade without a marketed product, the company sold its AI platform and drug/diagnostic pipelines to an investor consortium (later branded BPGbio) in February 2023, effectively ending Berg as an independent entity.
lesson: A decade-plus and hundreds of millions of dollars spent building a proprietary biological AI platform is not sufficient without a commercialized product; distressed asset sales are the typical endgame for capital-intensive science platforms that never reach market.

name: Berg Health (Berg Pharma)
summary: Berg pioneered 'Interrogative Biology,' using AI to mine patient biological data for drug and diagnostic targets in cancer and Parkinson's disease, but after over a decade and hundreds of millions invested it never brought a product to market and quietly faded from industry relevance.
lesson: A novel AI-driven scientific methodology needs interim, verifiable wins — not just a compelling narrative — to sustain investor confidence over a decade-plus R&D timeline.
source: Forbes and Xconomy coverage of Berg's AI drug discovery ambitions

name: Berkeley Lights / PhenomeX
summary: AI/automation-enabled single-cell biology instrument company (later merged with IsoPlexis and renamed PhenomeX) that IPO'd in 2020 at $22/share, peaking near $103 in late 2020. A class-action lawsuit over allegedly misleading statements about its technology's capabilities and performance, combined with slowing growth, drove the stock down to $0.40/share by mid-2023. Bruker acquired PhenomeX for just $1.00/share (~$108M total) in an all-cash deal that closed in October 2023 — a roughly 95% decline from IPO value.
lesson: Investor lawsuits over overstated technology performance claims are a recurring failure pattern among AI/automation-driven life-science instrument companies that go public before their platform's real-world capability matches the pitch.

name: Berkshire Grey
summary: Warehouse robotic-picking automation company that went public via SPAC in 2021, but its stock collapsed over 90%, forcing a distressed take-private sale back to existing investor SoftBank for just ~$375M in 2023.
lesson: SPAC-era valuations for capital-intensive robotics companies outran actual deployment economics, and gravity eventually wins.

name: Bertha.ai
summary: Bertha.ai built an AI copywriting co-pilot plugin for WordPress and Chrome, but after ChatGPT arrested its growth trajectory the team stopped investing in repository compliance work and exited the official WordPress plugin directory, with the founders describing the product as having 'reached a natural stopping point.'
lesson: A narrow plugin-based AI writing tool built for a single platform ecosystem is highly exposed once general-purpose chat interfaces make the same capability available for free everywhere.
source: wp-content.co and Bertha.ai company blog post 'Bertha, the End of an Era?'

name: Better Booch
summary: Better Booch was a California kombucha and functional-beverage producer that shut down its production facility in 2021 after failing to compete with larger national kombucha brands for retail shelf space.
lesson: Functional-beverage categories consolidate quickly around a few national brands, squeezing out regional producers.

name: Better Place
summary: Israeli EV battery-swap network builder burned through its funding building charging and swap infrastructure faster than EV adoption could support, filing for bankruptcy in 2013.
lesson: Building infrastructure ahead of demand in a nascent market can bankrupt a company before the market matures.
source: NYT and Fortune retrospectives on Better Place bankruptcy, 2013

name: Better Therapeutics
summary: Developed an FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutic (AspyreRx) using AI-personalized cognitive behavioral therapy to treat cardiometabolic disease, but after going public via SPAC could not achieve meaningful payer reimbursement or revenue before filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy and delisting from Nasdaq.
lesson: FDA clearance and a SPAC listing create the appearance of maturity, but without payer contracts actually paying for the product, cash burn continues unchecked.
source: MobiHealthNews and Fierce Healthcare coverage of Better Therapeutics' bankruptcy (2023)

name: Better.com
summary: Digital mortgage lender that grew rapidly during the pandemic refinancing boom saw its reputation and finances collapse after its CEO fired 900 employees over a Zoom call.
lesson: Scaling headcount to match a temporary rate-driven demand spike leaves a company badly overextended once rates rise.

name: Better.com (AI mortgage underwriting)
summary: Digital mortgage lender using an AI-driven underwriting engine ('Tinman') to speed up loan approvals; suffered severe reputational damage from a viral mass-layoff Zoom call in 2021, repeated rounds of layoffs, executive turnover, and a stock collapse of over 95% after its delayed SPAC listing in 2023.
lesson: A genuinely fast AI underwriting engine can be undermined by erratic leadership and public trust failures that drive away both customers and employees.

name: Beyond Meat
summary: Plant-based meat pioneer that went public at a high valuation but has suffered years of declining sales, heavy losses, and a collapsed stock price as demand plateaued.
lesson: Early hype and a successful IPO don't guarantee that consumer demand for a sustainability-driven product category will keep growing.

name: Beyond the Rack
summary: Canadian flash-sale retail e-commerce site that expanded rapidly across North America ceased operations in 2016 after failing to reach profitability in the increasingly crowded flash-sale retail category.
lesson: Entering a retail category already crowded with well-funded competitors (Gilt, One Kings Lane, Rue La La) requires a clear differentiation to survive a shakeout.

name: Beyond Verbal
summary: Israeli startup building 'emotion analytics' technology that claimed to detect emotional and health signals from voice tone, targeting call centers and health-tech applications; failed to achieve mainstream commercial traction and effectively ceased active operations.
lesson: Claims about detecting deep emotional or medical states purely from voice tone struggled to meet the scientific and commercial validation enterprise buyers demanded.

name: Big Switch Networks
summary: Early SDN pioneer building white-box switching and network automation software for data centers, once considered a leading SDN unicorn candidate. After years of missed growth expectations, sold its assets and technology to Arista Networks (with some IP going to Broadcom) in 2020 in a distressed transaction.
lesson: Being early to the SDN wave was not enough; incumbents like Cisco and Arista absorbed the market's appetite for automation faster than a standalone challenger could scale.
source: SDxCentral and Data Center Knowledge coverage of Big Switch Networks asset sale, 2020

name: Bigelow Aerospace
summary: Inflatable space habitat pioneer, which flew the BEAM module to the ISS, laid off its entire workforce in March 2020 citing the COVID-19 downturn and never resumed full operations.
lesson: A company reliant on a single deep-pocketed founder and a still-nascent commercial space-station market has no buffer against an external shock.

name: Bigtincan
summary: Bigtincan built an AI-powered sales enablement platform and went public on the Australian Securities Exchange. The company struggled with profitability and a depressed share price through 2022-2024, ultimately being taken private in a distressed 2024 buyout well below its earlier valuation.
lesson: Going public before reaching sustainable profitability in a crowded category left Bigtincan exposed to public-market pressure that eventually forced a going-private deal at a steep discount.

name: BIMA (Milvik)
summary: Mobile microinsurance provider serving emerging markets in Asia, Africa and Latin America underwent major restructuring and divested several country operations in the early 2020s after failing to reach profitability at scale.
lesson: Microinsurance's low premiums require enormous distribution scale before per-policy margins can cover acquisition and claims costs.

name: BIME Analytics
summary: French cloud BI platform acquired by Zendesk in 2015 to build Zendesk Explore; the standalone BIME product was discontinued in 2018.
lesson: An acquisition aimed at absorbing technology into a new product line often ends the acquired brand's independent life.
source: Zendesk BIME acquisition and Explore transition announcements

name: Biogen (Aduhelm)
summary: Alzheimer's drug Aduhelm received a controversial accelerated FDA approval over its own advisory committee's objections, then was rejected by insurers and withdrawn from market in 2024.
lesson: An approval secured against expert consensus does not guarantee physician adoption or payer reimbursement.

name: BionicHIVE
summary: Israeli warehouse and logistics robotics coordination-AI firm shut down as the Israel war disrupted the economy and cut off foreign investor access.
lesson: Macro and geopolitical shocks can kill a viable startup by severing capital access.

name: Birchbox
summary: Pioneer of the beauty subscription box model, Birchbox was sold for parts to investment firm Viking Global Investors in 2021 after years of down-rounds and failed sale attempts, well below its peak valuation.
lesson: A first-mover subscription concept can be commoditized quickly once retailers and rivals copy the model at lower cost.
source: TechCrunch, Business Insider coverage of Birchbox sale to Viking Global, 2021

name: Bird
summary: Electric scooter-sharing pioneer that scaled rapidly across hundreds of cities but never achieved sustainable unit economics; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in December 2023.
lesson: Rapid geographic expansion without solving vehicle durability and repair costs burns cash faster than growth can offset.

name: Birst
summary: Cloud BI platform acquired by Infor in 2017 for roughly $150M; the Birst brand was ultimately discontinued as Infor consolidated its analytics stack around 2022.
lesson: Raising very large sums for a BI platform doesn't guarantee an independent future if the eventual acquirer folds the brand into its own roadmap.
source: Infor Birst acquisition and later product consolidation

name: Bitconnect
summary: Crypto lending platform promising outsized guaranteed returns via a proprietary trading bot shut down after regulators issued cease-and-desist orders, revealing a Ponzi scheme worth over $2B in losses.
lesson: Guaranteed fixed returns with an opaque, unaudited strategy are the classic signature of a Ponzi scheme.

name: Bitfount
summary: London-based Bitfount builds a federated AI and data-science platform for pooling clinical research data across healthcare systems without moving patient records. It took roughly five years from founding to close a modest €6.8M Series A in 2025, a slow capital-raising cadence relative to peers like Owkin and Apheris that reflects the difficulty smaller entrants face convincing healthcare data custodians to adopt federated infrastructure at scale.
lesson: Federated learning in healthcare requires long enterprise sales cycles with hospitals and research consortia; without early scale capital, smaller vendors risk being outpaced by better-funded rivals before they can prove the model works at meaningful scale.

name: Bittrex
summary: Early major US crypto exchange shut down its US operations in 2023 after settling SEC charges for operating an unregistered exchange, and later wound down globally.
lesson: Operating in a regulatory gray zone for years can catch up with a company once regulators formalize enforcement priorities.
source: SEC press release on Bittrex settlement, 2023

name: Bivvy Pet Insurance
funding: Backed by parent company Dog Gone Problems / undisclosed amount
summary: No-frills, low-cost pet insurance startup that announced in 2023 it would stop selling new policies and not renew existing ones because its team could not grow the product to sustainable scale, redirecting customers to Spot Pet Insurance.
lesson: A stripped-down, low-price insurance product can win initial signups but still fail if the underlying loss ratios and growth rate cannot support the MGA business long term.
source: Coverager reporting on Bivvy pet insurance discontinuation, 2023

name: Bizness Apps
summary: DIY mobile app builder for small businesses that shut down operations in 2019 as the market for generic white-label app builders shrank.
lesson: White-label no-code app builders targeting SMBs face brutal price competition and shrinking demand as native mobile web improves.

name: Bizongo
summary: Indian B2B packaging materials marketplace that underwent a steep valuation down round and major restructuring around 2023-2024 after a strategic pivot away from its original marketplace model.
lesson: Pivoting a marketplace's core business model mid-scale-up can strand both supplier relationships and investor confidence.
source: Indian tech press (Entrackr/Inc42) coverage of Bizongo's restructuring and down round

name: Blackboard Insurance (AIG/Two Sigma)
summary: AIG and Two Sigma's data-science-driven small commercial insurer, parent of MGA Attune, built machine-learning risk models to underwrite small business risk directly; placed into run-off in March 2020 after AIG recognized a roughly $210M pre-tax impairment loss.
lesson: Sophisticated AI risk models do not substitute for disciplined loss-ratio management; a large strategic backer will still cut losses if algorithmic underwriting fails to turn a profit.

name: Blendle
summary: A Dutch 'iTunes for news' platform that let readers pay per article for premium journalism failed to reach scale and pivoted away from its original micropayment model before effectively shutting down.
lesson: Micropayments for individual news articles have repeatedly struggled against readers' preference for either free content or flat subscriptions.

name: Blendoor
summary: Diversity-focused hiring app founded by Stephanie Lampkin that blinded candidate profiles of race, gender and age, later pivoting to score companies on diversity-pledge follow-through; despite strong press (MIT News, TechCrunch) it never raised beyond seed stage and stalled around 13 employees.
lesson: Mission-driven bias-reduction products can win press coverage while still failing to attract the growth capital needed to compete with mainstream ATS incumbents.

name: Blickfeld
summary: Munich-based solid-state lidar and perception-software maker. Filed for insolvency in June 2024, just six months after raising fresh growth financing, before being acquired out of the proceedings by Senstar Technologies in early 2025.
lesson: A funding round closed months earlier is no guarantee of survival if burn rate and time-to-revenue are badly mismatched - insolvency can follow a 'successful' raise within a single fiscal year.

name: Blinkbox Music
summary: UK music streaming service backed by retailer Tesco that shut down after less than two years as its parent retrenched from digital media investments.
lesson: Corporate-backed media ventures are vulnerable when the parent company's strategic priorities shift.

name: Blip.fm
summary: Twitter-integrated music discovery and DJ-style sharing service that failed to convert its early buzz into a lasting business and shut down in 2013.
lesson: Dependence on a third-party platform's API and audience graph creates fragile distribution that can collapse if that platform's dynamics change.

name: Blippar
summary: Augmented-reality marketing platform Blippar fell into administration in 2018 after burning through over $130M without finding a sustainable revenue model, and was later sold to new owners in a restructuring.
lesson: Flashy AR marketing technology can attract huge funding rounds while masking a lack of paying-customer demand.
source: TechCrunch, Financial Times coverage of Blippar administration 2018

name: Block.one (EOS)
summary: Raised a record $4 billion in a year-long ICO for the EOS blockchain but failed to deliver the promised dApp ecosystem or enterprise adoption, and later pivoted away from EOS development entirely.
lesson: Raising enormous capital does not substitute for shipping a product that developers and users actually want to build on.
source: SEC settlement with Block.one, 2019; EOS Network Foundation spin-off coverage

name: BlockFi
summary: Crypto lender filed for Chapter 11 after its exposure to FTX/Alameda, which had bailed it out earlier that year, became fatal.
lesson: Accepting a rescue loan from a counterparty that is itself fragile just delays and compounds the eventual failure.

name: Blokable
summary: Modular multifamily housing manufacturer that wound down operations around 2023-2024 after struggling to secure enough project volume to sustain its factory.
lesson: Modular housing manufacturers need long-term pipeline commitments from developers before committing to factory-scale capital investment.

name: Blooma AI (duplicate check - HouseCanary competitor Valuation Partners)
summary: Small AVM startup aiming to compete with HouseCanary and Quantarium on mortgage-lender valuation contracts; shut down in 2021 after failing to win enough enterprise lending clients away from established, more accurate incumbent AVM providers.
lesson: AVM accuracy and lender trust take years to build; a late entrant without a data or accuracy edge struggles to displace incumbents with established track records.

name: Bloomlife
summary: Bloomlife, maker of a wearable pregnancy contraction-tracking sensor, wound down its consumer hardware business in the early 2020s after failing to reach mass-market scale beyond an early-adopter niche.
lesson: Clinical-adjacent pregnancy wearables face a narrow window of use (the third trimester) that limits recurring revenue and makes customer acquisition costs hard to recoup.

name: BloomTech (Lambda School)
summary: Coding bootcamp built on income-share agreements that faced state regulatory actions and an FTC investigation over misleading job-placement statistics, shrinking dramatically.
lesson: Income-share-agreement business models invite regulatory scrutiny if outcome claims aren't rigorously verified.

name: Blooom
summary: Robo-advisor focused on optimizing employer-sponsored 401(k) accounts shut down its consumer service after failing to build a large enough subscriber base.
lesson: A low-dollar subscription robo-advisor needs massive scale to overcome thin per-user margins.

name: Blu Homes
summary: Prefabricated/modular green home manufacturer that struggled to scale factory production profitably and filed for bankruptcy in 2020.
lesson: Prefab housing economics depend on factory utilization; underused manufacturing capacity destroys unit economics quickly.
source: Builder Magazine, GreenBiz coverage of Blu Homes bankruptcy

name: Blue Apron
summary: Meal-kit subscription pioneer that IPO'd in 2017, struggled with customer acquisition costs and churn for years, and was acquired by Wonder Group in 2023 after its stock collapsed.
lesson: High customer acquisition costs paired with poor retention doom subscription commerce even with first-mover advantage.

name: Blue Danube Systems
summary: Developed AI-optimized massive MIMO beamforming antenna technology aimed at boosting capacity in dense mobile networks ahead of 5G rollouts. Ceased operations in 2019 after struggling to convert trials with carriers into large-scale deployments.
lesson: Hardware-plus-AI network capacity startups face brutally long carrier qualification cycles; running out of capital before 5G densification demand fully materialized proved fatal.
source: Light Reading and FierceWireless coverage of Blue Danube Systems shutdown

name: Blue Prism
summary: One of the original robotic-process-automation pioneers, but lost share to faster-moving UiPath and Automation Anywhere and struggled to pivot from rules-based bots to AI-native agents; acquired by SS&C Technologies in 2022 for ~$1.6B after a prolonged share-price slide.
lesson: Category creators can still lose if faster, better-funded challengers out-execute on product velocity.

name: bluebird bio
summary: Gene therapy pioneer won multiple approvals but pricing them at $2.8-3.1M each proved commercially unsustainable, forcing withdrawal from Europe and an 80%+ stock decline.
lesson: Curative one-time gene therapies still need a reimbursement pathway that health systems will actually pay for.

name: Bluecrew
summary: W-2 flexible staffing marketplace that was sold to Employbridge in 2022 in a deal reported as a distress sale after struggling to turn its worker-classification-compliant model into a profitable business.
lesson: Choosing a more compliance-friendly W-2 employment model can raise costs enough to erase the margin advantage of a staffing marketplace.
source: Staffing industry trade press coverage of the Bluecrew-Employbridge acquisition (2022)

name: Bluefly
summary: Early online discount fashion retailer filed for bankruptcy in 2018 after two decades of struggling to turn a sustainable profit against larger competitors.
lesson: Longevity alone doesn't protect a discount ecommerce retailer without a durable cost or sourcing advantage.

name: BlueNalu
summary: BlueNalu was a well-funded cultivated-seafood pioneer developing cell-cultured mahi-mahi and bluefin tuna, but slashed most of its staff in 2024 as cultivated-meat investment dried up before commercial-scale approval.
lesson: Cultivated seafood faces the same multi-year regulatory runway as cultivated meat, and funding cycles rarely match that timeline.

name: Bluenose Analytics
summary: Customer success analytics startup Bluenose Analytics quietly shut down and sold assets in 2017 after failing to differentiate in an increasingly crowded customer success software market.
lesson: Analytics-only customer success tools without workflow ownership struggle to retain customers who eventually consolidate onto a full platform.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Bluenose Analytics closure, 2017

name: Blueprint Robotics
summary: Baltimore-based panelized home-manufacturing company using robotic assembly lines that ceased operations and closed its factory in 2023.
lesson: Automating construction manufacturing does not help if end-market housing demand and pricing can't cover the factory's fixed costs.
source: Baltimore Business Journal coverage of Blueprint Robotics closure

name: Bluesmart
summary: Made GPS-enabled smart luggage with built-in batteries and locks, then collapsed in 2018 after major airlines banned checked bags with non-removable batteries, wiping out its core product.
lesson: A hardware business built on airline cooperation is one policy change away from being wiped out.

name: Bnext
summary: Spanish neobank targeting underbanked consumers ceased operations in 2023 after failing to raise further funding to sustain its growth.
lesson: Regional European neobanks competing against well-funded pan-European players like Revolut and N26 face a narrowing runway to differentiate.

name: Bó
summary: Digital-only bank launched by RBS (NatWest) in late 2019 to compete with Monzo and Starling; shut down just six months later in mid-2020 after failing to gain traction and to justify its cost amid COVID-19.
lesson: An incumbent bank spinning up a challenger brand still needs genuine differentiation, not just a new app skin.

name: Bodega (Stockwell)
summary: Automated micro-market vending-pantry startup founded by ex-Google engineers, widely criticized for its name and pitch to replace corner stores, rebranded as Stockwell.ai and ultimately shut down its retail vending business by 2023.
lesson: A retail concept that generates public backlash over its premise can struggle to build the retailer and landlord trust needed to scale placements.

name: Bodega / Stockwell AI
summary: AI-managed unmanned vending pantry startup (rebranded Stockwell AI after initial 'Bodega' branding drew public backlash for evoking corner stores) that used computer vision and inventory algorithms to auto-restock snack cabinets in offices and apartment buildings. Shut down in 2020 after the pandemic eliminated the office/co-living foot traffic its unattended kiosks depended on.
lesson: A hardware-plus-AI retail format tied to a single physical-presence pattern (office pantries) has no fallback when that pattern disappears overnight.
source: TechCrunch and retail trade press on Bodega/Stockwell AI shutdown, 2020

name: Bollinger Motors
summary: Electric truck and commercial vehicle maker Bollinger Motors filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2025 after failing to secure additional financing to scale production of its B4 truck.
lesson: Niche commercial EV makers face the same capital intensity as consumer EV makers without comparable volume to spread fixed costs.
source: Automotive News, 2025, Bollinger Motors Chapter 11 filing

name: Bolt Financial
summary: One-click checkout SaaS reached an $11B valuation before layoffs and public controversy over founder claims about funding and metrics led to a collapse in its valuation.
lesson: Founder credibility problems can destroy enterprise sales momentum even when the core product works.

name: Bolt Threads
summary: Biotech materials company behind Mylo mushroom-leather that paused production after brand partners delayed commitments, and later wound down.
lesson: Relying on a handful of brand partners for a novel material's commercial validation is risky if they can quietly walk away.

name: Bombfell
summary: Menswear personal-styling subscription service shut down operations in 2019, unable to compete against better-funded rivals like Stitch Fix and Trunk Club in the crowded try-before-you-buy apparel space.
lesson: Under-capitalized entrants in a subscription-styling niche get squeezed out once larger players with logistics scale enter.
source: GeekWire, Racked coverage of Bombfell wind-down 2019

name: Bond Technologies
summary: Banking-as-a-service platform for embedded finance was sold to FIS in 2023 in a distressed sale after struggling to reach scale amid intensifying BaaS competition.
lesson: A crowded BaaS market with thin margins can force even well-funded infrastructure players into fire sales.

name: Bond.ai
summary: Startup building an AI-driven 'emotional banking' chatbot for retail banks, aiming to give banks a conversational, empathetic digital assistant powered by sentiment analysis. Quietly dissolved around 2021 after failing to convert bank pilots into large-scale deployments.
lesson: Ambitious 'emotionally intelligent AI' positioning for banking chatbots struggled against more pragmatic, narrowly-scoped conversational AI vendors that banks found easier to deploy and measure ROI on.
source: Fintech industry coverage of Bond.ai closure (2021)

name: Bonobos
summary: Menswear D2C pioneer was acquired by Walmart for $310M in 2017 but never fit Walmart's e-commerce strategy; Walmart sold it to WHP Global and Express in 2023 for just $75M, roughly a 75% loss.
lesson: A D2C brand's culture and customer base can be a poor strategic fit inside a mass-market acquirer, destroying most of the deal's value.
source: CNBC, CNN Business, Fortune coverage of Walmart-Bonobos sale 2023

name: Bonsai AI
summary: 'Machine teaching' platform for building AI/reinforcement-learning models, acquired by Microsoft in 2018 and rebranded Project Bonsai. Microsoft retired the product entirely in 2023.
lesson: An acquihire can still end in the underlying product's discontinuation if it never found a large enough customer base inside the parent company's portfolio.

name: Boo.com
summary: Early online luxury fashion retailer spent $135M in 18 months on a simultaneous multi-country launch and an over-engineered website that loaded slowly for most users; it collapsed into receivership in May 2000, a symbol of dot-com-era excess.
lesson: Launching simultaneously across many markets before validating product-market fit and basic site performance multiplies the cost of failure.
source: Wikipedia, Museum of Failure, Smart Insights case study on Boo.com

name: Bosch-Daimler Athena Robotaxi Project
funding: Joint R&D investment by Bosch and Daimler (undisclosed, reported in the hundreds of millions)
summary: Joint venture between Bosch and Daimler (Mercedes-Benz) to co-develop driverless robotaxi technology, piloted with automated S-Class rideshare vehicles in San Jose starting in 2019. The partners quietly ended the 'Athena' project in August 2021, conceding that full urban robotaxi autonomy was more complex than either had anticipated.
lesson: Even two of the deepest-pocketed automotive and supplier partners can misjudge the true multi-year difficulty of urban robotaxi autonomy and choose to cut losses rather than keep funding an open-ended R&D program.

name: Boss Key Productions
summary: Cliff Bleszinski's studio bet on the hero-shooter LawBreakers and battle royale Radical Heights, both of which failed to find an audience amid stiff competition.
lesson: Launching into an oversaturated genre without a clear differentiator invites getting crushed by bigger incumbents.

name: Bossa Nova Robotics
summary: Maker of AI shelf-scanning robots that roamed Walmart aisles detecting out-of-stocks and pricing errors, deployed in ~500-1,000 stores. Walmart abruptly canceled its five-year rollout in November 2020, saying simpler human-based solutions worked just as well; the company halved its workforce, shut its European unit, and wound down the robotics business, pivoting to pure computer-vision analytics before fading out.
lesson: Single-customer dependency on an enterprise retail giant is existential risk — when Walmart walked away, there was no diversified customer base to fall back on.
source: The Robot Report, "Walmart ends inventory robot deal with Bossa Nova Robotics", 2020-2023

name: Bounce
summary: Indian dockless scooter-rental startup (formerly Metro Bikes) that shut down its core rental business in 2022 to pivot fully into electric-vehicle manufacturing.
lesson: Two-wheeler rental economics in price-sensitive markets pushed the company to abandon its original business entirely.

name: Bowery Farming
summary: Once the most valuable vertical farming startup in the US at a $2.3B valuation, Bowery Farming shut down and ceased operations in November 2024 after failing to find a buyer.
lesson: High private valuations do not guarantee a sustainable business model when core unit economics never turn positive.
source: The Information / TechCrunch, Bowery Farming shutdown Nov 2024

name: Bowflex (Nautilus Inc.)
summary: Home fitness equipment maker Nautilus Inc. (Bowflex) filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2024 after pandemic-era overproduction led to a sharp post-pandemic demand collapse and inventory glut.
lesson: Ramping manufacturing to meet a temporary pandemic demand spike left the company overexposed when demand normalized.

name: Boxbee
summary: On-demand storage and pickup logistics startup that shut down in 2016 after failing to scale its physical storage-and-delivery network profitably.
lesson: Physical-asset-heavy on-demand services need dense, repeatable routes to make unit economics work.

name: Boxed
summary: Bulk-goods e-commerce retailer that also built and sold an AI-driven fulfillment and analytics software platform to other retailers. Filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in April 2024 after its SPAC-era valuation collapsed and it could not achieve profitability on either its retail or software business lines.
lesson: Running a capital-intensive direct retail business alongside a nascent B2B software spinoff split management focus and capital at the exact moment both needed it most.
source: Bankruptcy court filings and trade press on Boxed Chapter 11, April 2024

name: Boxed.com
funding: ~$500M raised, went public via SPAC in 2021
summary: Bulk wholesale e-commerce marketplace for businesses and households that went public via SPAC then filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2024 after years of unprofitable growth and heavy cash burn.
lesson: A SPAC listing does not fix a fundamentally unprofitable bulk-goods logistics model.
source: Reuters/press coverage of Boxed's 2024 Chapter 11 filing

name: Braintrust
summary: Blockchain-token-based freelance talent marketplace whose BTRST token value collapsed and whose growth stalled well short of its ambitious projections after the broader crypto downturn.
lesson: Tying a marketplace's core incentive mechanism to a volatile token adds a second, unrelated failure mode on top of normal marketplace risk.
source: Crypto/tech press coverage of Braintrust's token and growth struggles

name: Branch Insurance
summary: Home and auto insurance startup offering instant bundled quotes ceased writing new policies and wound down operations in 2024 after failing to achieve sustainable underwriting margins.
lesson: Bundling home and auto for speed doesn't fix thin margins if claims costs outrun premium pricing.

name: BranchOut
summary: Facebook-based professional networking and recruiting app grew virally to millions of users but engagement collapsed once Facebook throttled platform distribution, and it shut down.
lesson: Building growth entirely on another platform's distribution graph is fragile the moment that platform changes policy.

name: Brandless
summary: DTC consumer goods retailer built around a data-driven 'no brand tax' pricing model and algorithmic supply-chain/pricing optimization to sell everyday products at a flat $3. Shut down operations in 2020 after burning through nearly $300M without reaching profitability, undone by high customer-acquisition costs its low price points couldn't support.
lesson: An algorithmically optimized low-price model still needs customer-acquisition costs low enough to match thin per-unit margins — SoftBank-scale capital can mask but not fix that math.
source: Trade press coverage of Brandless shutdown, February 2020

name: Breaker
summary: Social podcast discovery and listening app whose team was acquired by Twitter in 2020, after which the standalone app was shut down.
lesson: Building a standalone consumer app in a category dominated by platform-bundled players is extremely difficult to sustain independently.

name: Bricata
summary: Network detection and response platform combining machine learning with deep packet inspection to identify network intrusions. Ceased independent operations around 2021 after failing to scale against larger, better-funded NDR competitors like Vectra AI and Darktrace.
lesson: Entering the network detection and response category without a substantial funding war chest left Bricata unable to compete on go-to-market spend against category leaders.
source: Security trade press coverage of Bricata's decline

name: Bridj
summary: On-demand shuttle bus startup piloted in Boston, Kansas City and Sydney that shut down in 2017 after failing to find a sustainable model and losing a major transit-agency partnership.
lesson: Public transit-agency partnerships can be a lifeline or a liability depending on how dependent the startup's revenue is on them.

name: Bright Automotive
summary: Plug-in hybrid delivery van maker Bright Automotive shut down in 2012 after the Department of Energy denied its loan application, cutting off its planned path to production.
lesson: Building a business plan around a specific government loan program creates single-point-of-failure risk.
source: New York Times, February 2012, Bright Automotive shutdown

name: Bright Health
summary: Tech-enabled health insurance startup expanded rapidly into ACA and Medicare Advantage markets nationwide, then exited nearly all insurance lines and sold its remaining businesses by 2024 after massive losses.
lesson: Underwriting risk in health insurance punishes growth-at-all-costs expansion into new geographies and plan types.

name: Bright Health Group
summary: Used AI-driven risk-adjustment and care-coordination models to underwrite Medicare Advantage and ACA plans; stock collapsed over 95%, faced NYSE delisting, exited most insurance markets, and was effectively liquidated and renamed by 2024.
lesson: AI-driven risk scoring for government-program health insurance is only as good as the actuarial discipline behind it; rapid multi-state expansion masked deteriorating medical loss ratios until it was too late.

name: Bright Machines
summary: AI-powered 'software-defined microfactory' company combining computer vision and machine learning for automated electronics assembly and inspection, backed by BlackRock in a $106M Series C. Closed its Israel R&D center and cut staff amid internal reports of shifting strategy and leadership churn.
lesson: Ambitious 'AI microfactory' automation platforms require enormous integration engineering per customer site; repeated strategic resets and R&D center closures signal the company could not standardize its offering fast enough to reach efficient scale.

name: Britishvolt
summary: UK battery gigafactory startup that collapsed into administration after failing to secure enough funding to build its planned plant.
lesson: Announcing a gigafactory before securing full construction financing leaves a company exposed to any funding gap.

name: Brodmann17
summary: Israeli computer-vision startup building lightweight, low-power neural networks for ADAS perception. Shut down in December 2019 after struggling to raise a follow-on round, one of several vision-AI suppliers squeezed as automakers consolidated around larger Tier-1 ADAS platforms.
lesson: Niche efficiency advantages like lighter neural nets are not enough to survive when OEMs prefer bundled, full-stack ADAS platforms from larger, better-capitalized suppliers.

name: Brolly
summary: UK app that aggregated and managed a user's personal insurance policies in one place shut down in 2021 after failing to convert its user base into a profitable underwriting or brokerage business.
lesson: An insurance management app needs a genuine monetization engine behind the convenient interface, not just aggregation.

name: Bromium
summary: Endpoint isolation/micro-virtualization security vendor was acquired by HP Inc. in 2019 in a deal widely seen as underwhelming relative to the roughly $130M it had raised from investors.
lesson: Deep, hardware-adjacent security technology can be expensive to build and sell, and may not return venture-scale outcomes even with strong engineering.

name: Bud Financial
summary: UK open-banking company using AI to categorize transactions and power personalized banking products for bank partners. Underwent major layoffs and reported severe cash difficulties in 2023, with press reporting the company nearly entered administration before restructuring.
lesson: B2B open-banking AI infrastructure vendors are exposed to long, slow bank sales and integration cycles; if revenue ramps too slowly relative to burn, even well-funded infrastructure players face near-death cash crunches.
source: Sifted, AltFi coverage of Bud Financial financial difficulties (2023)

name: BuildDirect
summary: Online marketplace for building materials (flooring, décor) that filed for creditor protection (CCAA) in Canada in 2019 after years of losses.
lesson: Commoditized building-materials e-commerce faces thin margins that heavy customer-acquisition spending can quickly turn unprofitable.
source: BetaKit, Vancouver Sun coverage of BuildDirect CCAA filing

name: Builder.ai
summary: Marketed its 'Natasha' AI as an autonomous app-building agent, but relied heavily on ~700 human engineers in India manually coding client apps; inflated revenue forecasts and mounting debt led to insolvency in May 2025 after creditor Viola Credit seized $37M from its accounts.
lesson: AI-washing claims eventually collide with reality; technology claims must be auditable, not just marketed.

name: Built.io
summary: API and backend integration platform acquired by Software AG in 2018; the standalone product line was discontinued shortly after as it was merged into Software AG's integration suite.
lesson: Integration-layer startups acquired for their technology are frequently merged and rebranded out of existence.
source: Software AG Built.io acquisition and integration coverage

name: Bulletin
summary: B2B wholesale marketplace connecting independent product brands with retail store space that shut down in 2020, citing the impossibility of continuing its retail-space model during the COVID-19 pandemic.
lesson: A marketplace tightly coupled to physical retail footprint is acutely exposed to sudden shocks that shut down in-person commerce.

name: Bunker
summary: US startup providing on-demand liability insurance and compliance tracking for freelancers and independent contractors ceased operations in 2023 after running out of funding.
lesson: Serving the gig economy's insurance needs is a large addressable market in theory but a thin-margin one in practice.

name: Burst Media
summary: Online ad network Burst Media wound down operations around 2014-2015 after failing to transition its display network model to the programmatic era, with remaining assets absorbed by other firms.
lesson: Ad networks built on manual sales models often fail to adapt fast enough to programmatic buying.
source: AdExchanger reporting on Burst Media wind-down

name: Butterfly Network
summary: Butterfly Network's handheld, AI-guided ultrasound-on-a-chip promised to democratize imaging diagnostics, but after going public via SPAC at a rich valuation, slower-than-expected adoption and heavy cash burn forced major layoffs and a collapse of over 90% in market value.
lesson: Hardware-plus-AI medical devices carry the combined capital intensity of both device manufacturing and software development, which SPAC-era valuations often failed to price in realistically.
source: MedTech Dive and CNBC coverage of Butterfly Network's post-SPAC struggles

name: Buzzer
summary: Fan app letting users stream short 99-cent clips of live sporting events, backed by Michael Jordan, shut down after a licensing pivot failed to gain traction.
lesson: Micro-transaction clip streaming couldn't out-compete free social highlights already available from leagues and broadcasters.

name: BuzzFeed News
summary: BuzzFeed's Pulitzer Prize-winning news division was shut down in 2023 as parent company BuzzFeed Inc, which had gone public via SPAC, cut costs after its stock collapsed.
lesson: Prestige journalism can win awards while still being the least profitable division a public company sacrifices first.

name: By Miles
summary: UK pay-per-mile car insurance startup ceased writing new business and sold its book after struggling to reach profitability in a thin-margin niche.
lesson: Usage-based insurance pricing innovation doesn't by itself solve the capital intensity of running an insurance carrier.

name: Byju's
summary: Once the world's most valuable edtech company, Byju's collapsed under aggressive debt-fueled acquisitions, accounting irregularities, and auditor/board resignations, leading to insolvency proceedings.
lesson: Aggressive debt-fueled M&A without financial controls can sink even category-leading startups.

name: Byliner
summary: A digital publisher of long-form nonfiction e-singles by notable authors failed to build a sustainable subscription business and effectively ceased operations by 2014.
lesson: Premium long-form digital content struggled to find enough paying subscribers before ad-free bundles like Kindle Singles matured.

name: Byte
summary: Vine co-creator Dom Hofmann's short-video app reboot launched to fanfare in 2020 but couldn't compete with TikTok's scale and was shut down in 2023.
lesson: Nostalgia for a beloved defunct product doesn't overcome a much larger incumbent's head start.

name: Byton
summary: Chinese-backed EV startup Byton filed for bankruptcy in 2023 after years of production delays and cash shortages prevented its M-Byte SUV from ever reaching meaningful sales.
lesson: Years of delayed production while burning investor capital on prototypes signals fundamental execution problems, not just bad luck.

name: bZx / Ooki DAO
summary: DeFi lending protocol suffered multiple exploits from 2020-2021 and its successor Ooki DAO was ordered shut down by the CFTC in 2023 for offering illegal leveraged trading, with the agency winning a first-of-its-kind default judgment against the DAO itself.
lesson: Rebranding a protocol as a DAO does not shield it from regulatory liability for products offered to US persons.
source: CFTC press release on Ooki DAO judgment, 2023

name: C3 Metrics
summary: Marketing attribution startup C3 Metrics became embroiled in a high-profile lawsuit with WPP alleging fabricated data and credentials, effectively destroying its credibility and business.
lesson: Attribution and measurement vendors live or die by data integrity; fabrication claims can be fatal even without a final verdict.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday coverage of C3 Metrics/WPP lawsuit 2018-2019

name: C4X Discovery
funding: Publicly listed on AIM; £30M+ market cap pre-delisting
summary: UK AI/bioinformatics-driven small-molecule drug discovery company targeting immuno-inflammatory disease, publicly listed on London's AIM market. Citing a persistently languishing share price and the high regulatory cost of a public listing relative to the capital it could raise, C4X Discovery delisted from AIM in 2024 to pursue private venture funding instead — a tacit admission that public markets were not valuing the AI-drug-discovery story.
lesson: A sub-£30M market cap after over a decade as a public AI drug-discovery company shows that small-cap public investors lost patience with the category well before any clinical payoff, forcing a retreat to private markets.

name: CaaStle (Gwynnie Bee)
summary: Clothing-rental-as-a-service platform (originally size-inclusive rental brand Gwynnie Bee) shut down all US operations in 2025 after founder Christine Hunsicker was accused of a $530M fraud misrepresenting the company's financials to investors.
lesson: Founder fraud can instantly vaporize a company regardless of its underlying product traction or years of investor goodwill.
source: Business of Fashion, Wikipedia coverage of CaaStle/Christine Hunsicker fraud case 2025-2026

name: Cadre
summary: Real estate investing platform for accredited investors laid off much of its staff and scaled back its marketplace ambitions after high interest rates froze commercial real estate deal flow.
lesson: Alternative real estate investing platforms are highly sensitive to macro credit cycles outside their control.

name: Calibrate
summary: Telehealth weight-loss startup that combined coaching with GLP-1 prescriptions shut down in 2024 after larger competitors and direct-to-consumer GLP-1 access eroded its differentiation.
lesson: A crowded, fast-moving category can commoditize a startup's advantage within a couple of years.

name: Call9
summary: A startup placing paramedics with telemedicine gear inside nursing homes to treat frail elderly residents on-site and avoid costly ER transfers; it shut down in June 2019 after failing to raise additional capital to scale.
lesson: Reducing ER transfers for nursing-home residents is genuinely valuable, but the staffing-heavy delivery model is expensive to scale faster than new funding rounds can support.

name: Cambridge Analytica
summary: Political marketing and data firm Cambridge Analytica shut down in 2018 after revelations it harvested Facebook user data without consent for political ad targeting, triggering global regulatory backlash.
lesson: Data-targeting businesses that skirt consent rules face existential regulatory and reputational risk when exposed.
source: The Guardian, New York Times Cambridge Analytica investigation 2018

name: Cano Health
summary: A Medicare Advantage-focused senior primary care chain that went public via SPAC at a multi-billion-dollar valuation, then collapsed under debt from aggressive clinic acquisitions and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2025.
lesson: Debt-fueled roll-ups of senior primary care clinics can outrun the slow, capital-intensive economics of value-based Medicare reimbursement.

name: Canoo
summary: EV startup Canoo, known for its pod-like designs, ceased operations and began liquidation proceedings in early 2025 after years of production delays, executive turnover, and mounting debt.
lesson: Chronic executive turnover and shifting strategy are often leading indicators of an EV startup's inability to reach production at scale.
source: Reuters, January 2025, Canoo operations ceasing coverage

name: Cape Privacy
summary: Cape Privacy (originally Dropout Labs) built a platform for collaborative machine learning using secure multiparty computation (SMPC), aimed at helping regulated industries like finance jointly train models on encrypted data without exposing raw inputs. The original SMPC/federated-learning product failed to find enough enterprise traction, and the company later pivoted entirely to LLM-focused data redaction and agentic AI workflows, abandoning its founding privacy-preserving ML thesis.
lesson: Advanced cryptographic ML techniques (SMPC, homomorphic encryption) frequently struggle to find enterprise buyers willing to adopt novel infrastructure; startups betting entirely on this thesis often must pivot to more immediately monetizable adjacent products to survive.

name: Capital New York
summary: A New York City politics and media trade publication was acquired by Politico in 2014 and folded into Politico New York in 2015, ending its standalone brand.
lesson: Regional trade publications with a niche but small audience are natural acquisition and consolidation targets rather than standalone survivors.

name: Car2Go / Share Now
summary: Daimler's free-floating car-sharing service merged with BMW's DriveNow to form Share Now in 2019, exited North America the same year, and the whole venture shut down in early 2024.
lesson: Free-floating car-sharing fleets require fleet-scale capital that even automakers found unsustainable long term.

name: CareerBuilder
funding: Majority owned by Apollo Global Management since 2017
summary: Once one of the largest US job boards, CareerBuilder saw traffic and revenue decline sharply as Indeed and LinkedIn captured job-search demand, leading to majority ownership passing to private equity at a steep discount to its former valuation.
lesson: Classified-style job boards lost to search-and-aggregation models that offered better candidate experience at lower employer cost.
source: Chicago Tribune/press coverage of Apollo acquiring CareerBuilder 2017

name: CareMax
summary: A Medicare-focused senior primary care and value-based care company operating dozens of Florida clinics; it filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy in November 2024 with $693M in debt after an ill-fated bet on Steward Health Care's Medicare business.
lesson: Taking on a struggling partner's Medicare risk contracts to fuel growth can import that partner's insolvency risk directly onto your own balance sheet.

name: CareSync
summary: A Tampa-based chronic care management platform that coordinated care for patients (mostly elderly Medicare beneficiaries) with multiple chronic conditions; it abruptly ceased operations in June 2018 after a rescue deal fell through, laying off nearly 300 employees.
lesson: Coordinating chronic/elderly care is valuable but reimbursement lags badly behind the cost of delivering the service, so burn rates must be matched to realistic payer timelines.

name: CareZone
summary: A consumer app that helped families track an aging relative's medications, notes and contacts; Walmart bought its core technology for roughly $200M in 2020 and quietly sunset the original consumer app by 2023.
lesson: An acquisition can look like a win on paper while the product that mattered to loyal caregiving families still disappears.

name: Cargo Chief
summary: US digital freight brokerage and trucking marketplace startup that wound down around 2019 after failing to differentiate its brokerage technology against larger competitors.
lesson: Freight brokerage technology alone is not a durable moat without proprietary carrier density or shipper relationships.

name: Cargomatic
summary: 'Uber for truckers' local drayage and trucking marketplace that burned through roughly $15M in investor money and bank loans while deliberately operating at a loss. Lost its CEO, CFO and COO in quick succession, laid off roughly half its staff across two rounds, and became dependent on a single customer to survive.
lesson: A 'tech platform' that actually depends on manual data entry by staff was never the capital-light marketplace investors were pricing in — customer concentration risk and leadership churn compounded the underlying cash burn.

name: CarLotz
summary: Consignment-based used-car marketplace CarLotz sold its operating assets to Shift Technologies in 2023 after failing to reach profitability, and was later delisted.
lesson: A novel consignment model doesn't overcome the same operational cost structure problems that sink capital-intensive marketplaces.
source: CarLotz 8-K filings, 2023 asset sale to Shift

name: Case Crunch
summary: UK litigation-prediction startup that gained attention in 2017 for beating lawyers in a head-to-head insurance-claims prediction contest (86.6% vs 62.3% accuracy). Despite the viral proof-of-concept, the company never scaled beyond niche insurance and litigation-funder pilots, became embroiled in a later trademark dispute with a law firm, and reported no subsequent funding or growth milestones.
lesson: A single viral demonstration of AI outperforming humans doesn't guarantee a repeatable enterprise sales motion; litigation-prediction buyers needed far more trust-building than a press-friendly contest provided.

name: CaseCrunch
summary: UK legal AI startup known for the 2017 "Case Cruncher Alpha" challenge that beat human lawyers at predicting PPI mis-selling claim outcomes; failed to commercialize the technology into a lasting business.
lesson: Winning a headline-grabbing AI-vs-human benchmark does not guarantee a repeatable, fundable commercial product.
source: Legal Cheek and Artificial Lawyer coverage of CaseCrunch

name: Casper
funding: Public (NYSE, IPO 2020); taken private by Durational Capital for ~$286M (2022)
summary: DTC mattress and sleep-products company that used data-driven personalization and algorithmic recommendation quizzes to match customers to products, once valued near $1.1B privately. Went public in 2020 at a valuation well below its last private round, burned cash on marketing and retail expansion, and was taken private in 2022 at roughly a quarter of its IPO valuation.
lesson: Data-driven personalization doesn't create defensible margins in a category (mattresses) that a dozen copycats can replicate with the same DTC ad playbook.
source: SEC filings and trade press on Casper IPO underperformance and 2022 take-private, 2020-2022

name: Cassava Sciences
summary: Alzheimer's drug developer faced sustained allegations of manipulated preclinical data behind lead candidate simufilam, triggering FDA and DOJ investigations and a stock collapse.
lesson: Data integrity questions raised by independent scientists can unravel years of investor confidence even before a pivotal trial reads out.

name: CastAR
summary: CastAR, founded by former Valve engineers to build retroreflective AR glasses, shut down in 2017 after running out of funding and failing to ship its long-delayed Kickstarter-backed product.
lesson: Kickstarter crowdfunding validates initial interest but does not guarantee sufficient capital to solve remaining hard engineering problems.

name: Castlight Health
funding: IPO 2014 (NYSE: CSLT); sold to Vera Whole Health 2022
summary: Corporate health navigation and wellness platform Castlight Health went public in 2014 at a high valuation but its stock collapsed over 95%, and it was sold for a fraction of its value to Vera Whole Health in 2022.
lesson: An enterprise health-tech IPO priced on hype can face years of value destruction if sales cycles and retention underperform projections.

name: Castor Technologies
summary: Israeli software analyzing CAD files to recommend additive- vs traditional-manufacturing decisions; filed for court-ordered liquidation after a 2023+ industry-wide pullback in 3D-printing R&D budgets dried up its pipeline.
lesson: A software layer riding on customers' own R&D budgets is exposed when that end market's capex contracts.

name: Catalia Health
summary: Maker of Mabu, an AI companion robot that coached chronically ill and elderly patients through medication adherence and care plans in partnership with Pfizer and Kaiser Permanente; the company shut down after over-ordering robot hardware it couldn't sell through.
lesson: Hardware companion robots carry brutal unit economics — manufacturing commitments made on optimistic sales forecasts can sink a company even with strong pharma partnerships.

name: Catalog
summary: Music NFT marketplace for full-length song releases that ceased active operations in 2023 as the music-NFT trend collapsed alongside the wider crypto downturn.
lesson: First-mover platforms in a hype-driven niche market can be wiped out when the underlying speculative demand evaporates.

name: Cavirin
summary: Cloud security posture management startup was quietly acquired by ColorTokens in 2021 for an undisclosed, reportedly modest sum in a crowded CSPM market dominated by larger platform vendors.
lesson: Entering a hot security category late, without a clear differentiator, often ends in a small tuck-in sale rather than independent growth.

name: Cazena
summary: Cazena built a managed 'data lake as a service' and AI-ready cloud data platform for enterprises, but shut down around 2021 after failing to scale against native cloud-provider offerings and better-capitalized data-platform rivals like Databricks and Snowflake.
lesson: Managed infrastructure layers positioned between raw cloud services and enterprise AI use cases risk being squeezed out as hyperscalers and larger platforms absorb the same functionality.

name: Cazoo
summary: UK online used-car retailer Cazoo shrank from full vehicle retailing to a marketplace-only model in 2023 and was delisted from NYSE in 2024 after massive losses.
lesson: Rapid multi-country expansion of a capital-heavy retail model without proven unit economics accelerates cash burn rather than growth.

name: Celsius Network
summary: Crypto lending platform promising high-yield interest on deposits froze withdrawals and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after risky DeFi bets left it insolvent.
lesson: Yields that outpace any plausible underlying return are usually funded by new depositors, not real profit.

name: Century Tech
summary: UK-based AI adaptive learning platform for schools built with neuroscience and cognitive-science backing; entered administration/restructuring in 2023 after years of cash burn without reaching profitability in the UK schools market.
lesson: Selling AI adaptive learning into cash-strapped state school budgets caps achievable price points, making it hard to outrun burn even with strong pedagogical credibility.
source: Companies House filings; UK EdTech press on Century Tech restructuring 2023

name: Cepton
summary: Automotive lidar and perception-software maker whose marquee GM ADAS program was cancelled before reaching production. Agreed to be taken private by Japanese lighting supplier Koito in a distressed 2024 deal at $3.17/share, with the acquisition completing in January 2025.
lesson: Losing a single anchor OEM program can be existential for small public lidar/perception suppliers with no diversified revenue base.

name: Cerebral
summary: Fast-growing telehealth startup for ADHD and mental health prescriptions faced a DOJ investigation into controlled-substance prescribing practices, was cut off by pharmacy partners, and laid off the large majority of its staff in 2022–2023, surviving only in drastically reduced form.
lesson: Growth built on liberal controlled-substance prescribing invites regulatory intervention that can gut a company almost overnight.

name: Cerebral Inc.
summary: Cerebral scaled rapidly by prescribing controlled substances like Adderall via telehealth, but faced DOJ and FTC investigations, an SEC probe, mass layoffs, and the departure of its founder amid allegations of prioritizing growth over safe prescribing.
lesson: Growth incentives that conflict with clinical safety in regulated healthcare will eventually trigger regulatory intervention.

name: Cervest
funding: $34M+ (incl. $30M Series A, backed by Salesforce Ventures/Benioff)
summary: London-based Cervest built EarthScan, an AI platform that modeled physical climate risk (flood, heat, drought) for corporate assets. Despite being touted by the UK government as a flagship AI company, it entered administration in June 2023 after failing to pay most of its ~100 staff since April, having gone on a hiring spree just months earlier.
lesson: A hot narrative and marquee investors cannot substitute for burn discipline; leadership denial about an unsustainable cash trajectory (evidenced by a hiring spree right before collapse) turned a fixable problem into a sudden failure.
source: Proactive Investors, Trellis.net (2023 coverage of Cervest administration)

name: Championship Gaming Series
summary: DirecTV's ambitious city-franchise esports league folded after just two seasons when high production costs vastly outpaced television viewership and sponsorship revenue.
lesson: Traditional sports broadcast economics don't transfer directly to esports before mainstream audiences and infrastructure are ready.

name: Character.AI
summary: Built wildly popular AI 'character' agents (20M+ users) but could not sustain the compute costs of serving free consumer chat at scale; Google paid ~$2.7B to license its tech and re-hire founders Noam Shazeer and Daniel De Freitas in August 2024, leaving a much smaller independent consumer app.
lesson: Consumer AI agent products with viral engagement can still be capital-constrained businesses if monetization lags far behind compute costs.

name: Chari
summary: Morocco/Africa-focused B2B retail distribution marketplace for small shops that carried out significant layoffs and market retrenchment in 2023 amid tightening funding conditions.
lesson: Fast multi-country rollout of low-margin retail distribution outpaces the funding runway once venture capital cools.
source: African/MENA tech press coverage of Chari's 2023 layoffs

name: Chariot
summary: Crowdsourced commuter shuttle-van startup acquired by Ford in 2016 that was shut down in 2019 as Ford Smart Mobility cut underperforming units amid a broader restructuring.
lesson: Shuttle-van services face brutal per-seat economics that scale poorly even under automaker ownership.

name: Charisma.ai
funding: Modest grants and small rounds (incl. £1M consortium grant, 2024)
summary: One of the earliest believable-conversational-avatar platforms for games, education and training, built in Oxford, UK. Nearly a decade after founding it has never raised a significant venture round or scaled past project-based consultancy work, in contrast to well-funded peers like Inworld and Convai.
lesson: Being early to a category is only an advantage if a company can convert that head start into capital and distribution before better-funded competitors arrive; longevity without scale is its own kind of failure.

name: Charlotte Russe
summary: Fast-fashion women's retailer filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2019 and liquidated all stores after failing to find a buyer, weighed down by debt from its 2009 LBO and declining mall traffic.
lesson: Heavy LBO debt loads leave little room for retailers to fund the digital transformation needed to survive.
source: Reuters coverage of Charlotte Russe Chapter 11 filing, 2019

name: Chartio
summary: Self-service BI and dashboarding tool acquired by Atlassian in late 2021; Atlassian shut it down almost immediately in 2022, giving customers only months to migrate.
lesson: An acquihire can result in near-immediate shutdown of the acquired product, leaving customers with very short migration windows.

name: Chef'd
funding: $35M+ (Campbell Soup was an investor and retail partner)
summary: Chef'd sold à la carte, retailer-branded meal kits including a partnership with Campbell's, but it abruptly ceased operations in 2018 after Campbell's exited its investment and no buyer emerged in time.
lesson: Dependence on a single large strategic partner for both funding and distribution creates existential risk if that partner exits.

name: Chegg
summary: Homework-help platform's stock collapsed over 99% from its 2021 peak after the CEO disclosed that ChatGPT was materially eroding subscriber growth, triggering mass layoffs.
lesson: Generative AI can rapidly commoditize a business built on giving students step-by-step answers.

name: Chisel AI
summary: Toronto-based NLP/AI platform that read unstructured commercial insurance policy documents for carriers like Zurich; ceased operations in May 2022 despite enterprise traction.
lesson: Even with credible enterprise pilots, thin-margin AI tooling vendors selling into slow insurance procurement cycles can run out of runway before reaching scale.

name: Chitika
summary: Contextual ad network Chitika shut down its publisher ad network in 2020 after nearly two decades, citing an inability to compete with the scale of Google's and Amazon's ad networks.
lesson: Independent contextual ad networks eventually get squeezed out as duopoly platforms capture ad-buyer budgets.
source: Company announcement, AdExchanger coverage of Chitika shutdown 2020

name: Chorus.ai standalone content tools
funding: Acquired by ZoomInfo (2021), folded from standalone content-intelligence roadmap
summary: Several early AI conversation-intelligence startups that had expanded into AI-generated marketing/sales content briefing tools, including Chorus.ai's standalone content-signal product line, were absorbed into larger platforms (ZoomInfo) post-acquisition, with the original standalone content roadmap discontinued in favor of the acquirer's integrated suite.
lesson: A promising AI content-signal product line can be shelved entirely once its parent company is acquired and priorities shift to the acquirer's own roadmap.
source: ZoomInfo acquisition coverage of Chorus.ai (2021), industry trade press

name: Chowbotics
summary: Made 'Sally,' a robotic kiosk assembling fresh salads/bowls from ~22 ingredients, deployed in hospitals, campuses and grocers; DoorDash shut the unit down 18 months after acquiring it in 2021 after it missed internal performance benchmarks.
lesson: Acquisition is not an exit for a startup's mission; a strategic acquirer can kill the product fast if it doesn't fit the parent's roadmap.

name: Cignifi
summary: Boston-based startup using mobile phone usage data and machine learning to generate credit and marketing scores for unbanked consumers in emerging markets (Brazil, Mexico, others). The business quietly wound down around 2019 after failing to reach scale against larger alternative-data competitors.
lesson: Alternative-data credit scoring for the unbanked requires deep, long-term telco and bank partnerships in each market; without scale, the per-market integration cost outpaced the revenue the scores generated.
source: Industry press coverage of alternative credit scoring consolidation (2019)

name: CipherCloud
summary: Early cloud access security broker (CASB) pioneer was acquired by Lookout in 2022 for an undisclosed, reportedly low valuation after losing ground to competitors like Netskope and Skyhigh.
lesson: Being first to a security category is not enough; slower iteration than well-funded fast-followers can erase an early leader's advantage.

name: Circ
summary: Berlin-based e-scooter startup (rebranded from Flash) acquired by Bird in 2020 as the European scooter market consolidated rapidly.
lesson: In a saturated market, being acquired by a larger rival is often the only viable exit.

name: Circa
summary: A mobile-first news app that broke stories into structured, updatable facts shut down in 2015 after parent company Sinclair Broadcast Group declined to continue funding it.
lesson: An innovative story format is not enough if the corporate parent funding it doesn't see a path to a sustaining business model.

name: CircleBack
summary: CircleBack built an AI-powered contact management tool that automatically cleaned and enriched CRM and address-book data used heavily by sales teams. The company was unable to build a sustainable business model around contact-data hygiene and shut down operations in 2018.
lesson: Automated contact-hygiene is a valuable feature but proved too thin a wedge to sustain an independent company once larger CRM and data-enrichment platforms absorbed the same functionality.

name: CircleUp
summary: Equity crowdfunding platform for consumer brands pivoted away from its investing marketplace to become a data analytics company after the crowdfunding model underdelivered.
lesson: Crowdfunding platforms can generate deal flow without generating a durable revenue model.

name: Citrus Lane
funding: Acquired by Care.com for up to $48.6M (2014)
summary: Citrus Lane, a baby and kids' products subscription box, was acquired by Care.com in 2014 but ceased operations in October 2015 after the parent concluded it would take far longer than expected to scale profitably.
lesson: An acquirer's strategic rationale for buying a subscription-box business can evaporate quickly if the underlying unit economics don't improve post-acquisition.

name: Civic
summary: Blockchain-based identity verification platform that raised $33M in a 2017 token sale; repeatedly pivoted its product but never achieved mainstream identity-verification adoption.
lesson: Token-funded startups can raise large sums without validating real product demand, delaying the reckoning rather than avoiding it.

name: Civil Comments
summary: Commenting-platform startup that used crowd-rated toxicity scoring across roughly 50 English-language news sites to flag abusive comments. Shut down in 2017; rather than disappear entirely, the founders open-sourced their ~2 million labeled comments, which Google's Jigsaw used to help build and train the Perspective API.
lesson: A promising dataset and moderation approach can outlive the company that built it — Civil Comments failed commercially but its labeled data became foundational for the industry's most widely used toxicity-detection API.
source: Perspective API research page; TensorFlow Datasets documentation

name: Civilised Bank
summary: UK challenger bank aimed at small businesses and consumers withdrew its banking license application and folded in 2020 without ever launching to the public.
lesson: Winning a full banking license is a multi-year, capital-intensive process that many aspiring challenger banks never complete.

name: Clara Labs
summary: Offered a human-assisted AI scheduling agent blending automation with human review for accuracy, but couldn't reach scale economics against better-funded rivals and calendar-native features from Google and Microsoft, and quietly ceased operating.
lesson: AI-plus-human-in-the-loop agents have structurally worse unit economics than fully automated alternatives unless the automation ratio keeps climbing.

name: Clara Lending
summary: AI-driven digital mortgage platform aiming to speed up home loan origination; shut down in 2018 after less than two years of operation, reportedly due to a costly and slower-than-expected technology buildout and inability to scale loan volume profitably.
lesson: Underestimating the complexity of building AI-driven mortgage infrastructure from scratch can burn through a large raise before any meaningful loan volume is achieved.

name: Clarifai
funding: $100M+ raised; 2023 Series D at a valuation reported below prior rounds
summary: Computer vision and AI data/model platform that was an early leader in visual recognition APIs. The company underwent a down round in 2023 and reported workforce reductions as it faced intensifying competition from foundation-model providers and struggled to maintain its earlier growth trajectory.
lesson: Being an early leader in a narrow AI capability (computer vision APIs) became less defensible once general-purpose foundation models began subsuming many specialized use cases.
source: Reports on Clarifai's 2023 down round and restructuring (The Information, Business Insider)

name: Clarity Money
summary: AI-powered personal finance app (from the founder of Billshark/BillGuard) that used machine learning to identify unwanted subscriptions and recommend financial products. Acquired by Goldman Sachs in 2018 to jump-start its Marcus consumer platform, then shut down by Goldman in 2019.
lesson: Being acquired by a major bank for your AI personalization engine can still mean the original consumer app is discarded once its technology is absorbed into the acquirer's own brand.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Goldman Sachs shutting down Clarity Money (2019)

name: ClearEdge Power
summary: ClearEdge Power made stationary fuel cells for distributed power generation and filed for bankruptcy in 2014, with assets acquired by Doosan.
lesson: Distributed fuel cell generation needs a clear cost-per-kWh advantage over grid power to justify continued capital infusions.

name: ClearFit
summary: Canadian AI-driven hiring-assessment platform ("Guided Interviewing") using predictive fit scoring to help small businesses screen candidates faster; gained early traction in Canadian tech media but discontinued active development and marketing as larger, better-funded assessment platforms took over the category.
lesson: Regional AI-hiring tools built for SMBs struggle to keep pace once well-funded platforms roll the same predictive-fit features into enterprise suites.
source: Canadian tech-press coverage of early SMB hiring-assessment tools

name: ClearSlide
summary: ClearSlide was an early sales enablement platform with content analytics and engagement tracking, raising over $100M from top investors. Growth stalled against Highspot and Seismic, and the company was sold to Corel (via GetGo) in 2019 in a deal widely viewed as a distressed sale far below the value of capital invested.
lesson: Being an early sales-enablement pioneer wasn't enough once better-capitalized, faster-iterating rivals (Highspot, Seismic) captured the enterprise segment, leaving early movers to sell at a steep discount.
source: TechCrunch, industry reporting on Corel acquisition

name: Clearspire
summary: Hybrid law firm and legal technology company that split legal services and technology into separate entities; shut down in 2014 amid a dispute between its co-founders and unsustainable economics.
lesson: Innovative alternative legal business structures still need aligned founder incentives and a clear regulatory path to survive.
