source: Above the Law and American Lawyer coverage of Clearspire's collapse (2014)

name: Clearview AI
summary: Clearview AI built a facial-recognition search engine for law enforcement by scraping billions of images without consent. It faces or has paid regulatory fines across multiple countries (UK, Italy, France, Greece, Australia), was banned outright in several jurisdictions, and settled a landmark class-action in Illinois under BIPA, leaving the company operating under severe legal and reputational constraints.
lesson: Building a government surveillance product on non-consensual data collection invites coordinated multi-jurisdiction regulatory action that can outlast and outcost any single contract win.
source: https://www.aclu.org/press-releases; general public reporting on Clearview AI GDPR fines and BIPA settlement

name: Clearwire
summary: Built a nationwide WiMAX-based 4G wireless network, burning over $10 billion, before being acquired by Sprint in 2013 having never reached profitability.
lesson: Betting an entire national network build on a wireless standard that loses the industry standards war is an existential risk.

name: ClimateTrade
summary: Spain-based ClimateTrade built a blockchain-and-data marketplace for tracking and trading verified carbon offsets for corporate buyers. Like most carbon marketplaces, it was squeezed hard by the 2023-2024 collapse in voluntary carbon demand and credibility scrutiny of offset quality, sharply narrowing its growth trajectory.
lesson: Carbon-offset marketplaces built during a funding boom faced a structural demand air-pocket once buyers grew skeptical of credit quality — distribution alone can't fix a trust problem in the underlying asset.
source: Sifted, MIT Technology Review coverage of the 2023-2024 voluntary carbon market downturn

name: Clinc
summary: Conversational AI startup providing voice-based virtual assistants for banks (including Wells Fargo, USAA); collapsed in 2022 amid a bitter legal dispute between the ousted founder and the board, laying off most staff.
lesson: A public, contentious founder-board fight destroyed enterprise customer confidence and operational continuity faster than the underlying technology or market could recover from.

name: Close5
summary: eBay's local classifieds marketplace app aimed at competing with letgo and OfferUp was shut down in 2017 after failing to gain sufficient market share.
lesson: Launching a me-too marketplace app into an already-crowded category rarely overtakes established leaders.

name: CloudBoost
summary: Mobile backend-as-a-service startup (an early Parse alternative) that shut down its service in 2018 after struggling to build a sustainable customer base.
lesson: Building a replacement for a shuttered platform (Parse) does not guarantee demand if the underlying market has already moved on.

name: Cloudera
summary: Big data platform pioneer that IPO'd in 2017 but never delivered the growth investors expected, ultimately taken private by KKR and CD&R in 2021 at a price reflecting years of stock decline.
lesson: A well-funded IPO cannot offset a slow-growing core product being bypassed by cloud-native data warehouses.

name: Cloudmark
summary: Email and messaging security pioneer was acquired by Proofpoint in 2017 for a price well below its earlier private valuations, reflecting years of slowing growth in a maturing anti-spam market.
lesson: First-generation anti-spam/messaging security technology can become commoditized, eroding pricing power and exit value over time.

name: CloudMedX
summary: Early clinical-NLP and AI risk-prediction startup aimed to help hospitals reduce readmissions and improve documentation, but struggled to raise beyond a modest seed round in a market being consolidated by larger, better-capitalized health-AI platforms, eventually shutting down quietly.
lesson: Undercapitalized clinical-AI startups competing against well-funded platform players in the same niche rarely survive a prolonged enterprise sales cycle.
source: Crunchbase and health-IT trade press coverage of CloudMedX's closure

name: CloudMinds
summary: Chinese-American 'cloud robot' and computer-vision AI company backed by SoftBank raised hundreds of millions and was reportedly preparing a large US IPO, but was placed on the US Commerce Department's Entity List in 2020 over national-security concerns and filed for bankruptcy in the US in 2021.
lesson: Cross-border AI companies straddling US and Chinese capital and technology can be existentially exposed to geopolitical export-control decisions outside their control.
source: Reuters coverage of CloudMinds' US Entity List designation and bankruptcy, 2021

name: CloudPassage
summary: Cloud workload security pioneer that raised over $90M was acquired by Fidelis Cybersecurity in 2021 for undisclosed terms, well after larger competitors had captured most of the cloud security posture management market.
lesson: Being an early cloud security category creator provides no lasting moat if larger, better-capitalized rivals out-execute on go-to-market.

name: CloudSight (CamFind)
summary: Maker of the CamFind visual-search app, which let users photograph any object to search the web for it, wound down its consumer app and pivoted its underlying CloudSight image-recognition API business before effectively fading out by around 2018 as larger platforms folded similar visual search into their own apps.
lesson: A clever visual-search demo can attract early app-store buzz, but it is defenseless once Google, Amazon, and Pinterest bundle the same capability natively.
source: Crunchbase and tech press coverage of CloudSight/CamFind's decline

name: Clover Health
summary: Used its proprietary Clover Assistant AI and algorithmic risk-adjustment tool to underwrite and manage Medicare Advantage risk; stock lost over 90% of its value amid a DOJ investigation into its AI-driven risk-coding practices and years of heavy losses.
lesson: AI tools that influence risk-adjustment coding in government-backed insurance invite intense regulatory scrutiny, and the resulting legal overhang can destroy shareholder value even if the underlying business survives.

name: Clovis Oncology
summary: Developer of PARP inhibitor Rubraca filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after the approved ovarian cancer drug underperformed commercially against competitors.
lesson: Being second-to-market in oncology against an entrenched competitor drug can doom even an approved therapy.

name: ClusterHQ
summary: Maker of Flocker, a container data volume manager for Docker, shut down abruptly after failing to find a sustainable revenue model in the fast-moving container ecosystem.
lesson: Infrastructure tooling built for a rapidly consolidating ecosystem can be obsoleted before it reaches revenue scale.

name: CNN+
summary: WarnerMedia's standalone CNN streaming news service launched with major star hires, then was shut down after just three weeks amid the Warner Bros. Discovery merger and low subscriber numbers.
lesson: Launching a major product days before a change of corporate ownership invites it being killed regardless of its own merits.

name: CoBank/AgriDigital blockchain grain platform
summary: Blockchain-based grain commodity management platform AgriDigital scaled back significantly around 2021 after failing to achieve broad industry adoption of its blockchain settlement layer.
lesson: Blockchain-based supply chain tools often struggle when the underlying industry lacks the digital-trust problem the technology is meant to solve.

name: Cocoon Cam
summary: Cocoon Cam, a contactless baby breathing and sleep monitor startup, discontinued its consumer product and support around 2020 after struggling to raise follow-on funding in the crowded baby-monitor hardware market.
lesson: Camera-based baby monitors compete against well-capitalized incumbents (Nanit, Owlet) and commodity baby monitors from established brands, squeezing smaller entrants out of shelf space and funding.

name: Coda Automotive
summary: EV maker Coda Automotive filed for bankruptcy in 2013 after its sedan received poor reviews and it could not compete with better-funded rivals like Tesla and Nissan.
lesson: Entering the capital-intensive EV market with an uncompetitive product against well-funded incumbents is a losing combination.
source: Reuters, May 2013, Coda Automotive bankruptcy filing

name: Coda Automotive / Coda Energy
summary: Coda pivoted from selling an EV sedan to focusing on its lithium-ion battery and energy storage division, but the automotive arm filed for bankruptcy in 2013 after weak sales.
lesson: A late pivot to energy storage couldn't rescue a company after its core EV product failed commercially.

name: Code Spaces
summary: Hosted source code and project management service that was forced to shut down within hours after an attacker gained access to its AWS control panel and deleted most of its infrastructure and backups.
lesson: Relying on a single cloud console without offline, immutable backups turns a security breach into an instant company-ending event.

name: Code42
summary: Insider-risk and data-loss-detection platform (Incydr) that uses behavioral analytics to flag risky file movement. After selling its legacy CrashPlan backup business -- more than half its revenue -- while retaining most of the associated costs, Code42 conducted layoffs of roughly 15% of staff in both November 2022 and July 2023 as new sales failed to offset the lost revenue base.
lesson: Divesting a legacy cash-generating business to focus on a newer AI-driven security product line can backfire if the cost structure doesn't shrink in proportion to the lost revenue.
source: Star Tribune, 'Minneapolis tech firm Code42 lays off 15% of workforce'; Blocks & Files coverage (2023)

name: Codealike
summary: Codealike offered developer productivity and coding-activity analytics with early machine-learning-driven insights, but development and updates largely stalled by the early 2020s as AI-native coding assistants absorbed developer attention and budget.
lesson: Adjacent developer-analytics products can be starved of both attention and funding once a hype cycle (generative AI coding assistants) redirects the entire market's investment focus toward a narrower category.

name: Codeium (Windsurf)
summary: Built proprietary code-completion and AI coding-agent foundation models. A planned ~$3B OpenAI acquisition collapsed in 2025 reportedly over Microsoft IP-access concerns; Google then paid ~$2.4B to license the technology and hire CEO Varun Mohan and key researchers, and the remaining Windsurf product/company was acquired by Cognition.
lesson: In a fast-moving M&A environment, deal structure and existing investor/partner entanglements (like Microsoft's OpenAI stake) can kill an acquisition even after terms are agreed, forcing a scramble for a fallback exit.
source: Bloomberg, The Verge reporting on OpenAI-Codeium collapse and Google/Cognition deals, 2025

name: CodeParrot
summary: Y Combinator W23-backed CodeParrot turned Figma designs and screenshots into production-ready frontend code using LLMs. Co-founders Vedant Agarwala and Royal Jain shut the startup down after struggling to reach sustainable growth, peaking at just $1,500 in monthly recurring revenue after its final pivot.
lesson: A YC-backed team with a plausible AI wedge (design-to-code) can still fail to convert technical novelty into paying customers; monthly recurring revenue in the low thousands after multiple pivots signals a company should wind down rather than keep burning limited seed capital.

name: CodeSee
summary: CodeSee built AI-assisted codebase visualization and code-health tooling to help engineers understand large codebases. It shut down commercial operations in February 2024 and its remaining assets were acquired by GitKraken in May 2024.
lesson: Developer-tooling visualization products can build passionate niche followings without ever finding a large enough paying market; an asset-sale exit is often the realistic outcome when growth stalls.

name: Cofense
funding: acquired out of PhishMe by a BlackRock-led consortium for ~$400M in 2018; rebranded as Cofense
summary: AI/ML-assisted phishing detection and response platform (crowdsourced threat intel plus automated triage) formerly known as PhishMe. Since its 2018 buyout, Cofense has cut staff across three rounds of layoffs within roughly 15 months, with employees and former staff describing recurring financial losses and management instability, though the company continues to operate.
lesson: A private-equity-style buyout at a high valuation can load a company with expectations it cannot meet organically, leading to a slow-motion decline of repeated layoffs rather than a single dramatic failure.
source: Glassdoor employee reviews; Channel Futures coverage of Cofense post-acquisition

name: Cogito Corp
summary: MIT Media Lab spin-out providing real-time voice-tone analytics and behavioral coaching for call-center agents; faced significant workforce reductions and business contraction in 2023-2024 amid tightening enterprise AI budgets and competition from newer contact-center AI entrants.
lesson: An early lead in voice-tone analytics eroded once well-funded, broader contact-center AI platforms (Cresta, Observe.AI) offered overlapping capabilities as part of larger suites.

name: Cognii
summary: AI virtual-tutor and open-response grading engine using NLP to score short-answer questions and provide formative feedback; remained a small, thinly-funded player that never achieved the scale or funding of later NLP-grading entrants like Gradescope.
lesson: Being technologically early in NLP-based grading isn't sufficient if better-funded competitors later out-execute on go-to-market and integration with LMS ecosystems.
source: Crunchbase funding records; company website archive via Wayback Machine

name: CognitiveScale
summary: CognitiveScale built an enterprise 'augmented intelligence' platform (Cortex) targeting healthcare and financial services, but ceased independent operations around 2020 after failing to scale revenue, with assets and IP later absorbed by other parties.
lesson: Positioning as a generic 'cognitive computing' platform without a sharply defined wedge made it hard to compete once IBM Watson-style enterprise AI hype cooled.

name: Cohere Technologies
summary: Wireless technology company (unrelated to the Cohere AI language-model company) developing OTFS modulation and AI-assisted spectrum multiplexing technology it claimed could dramatically boost cellular network capacity. Wound down most operations after failing to achieve commercial carrier adoption of its modulation technology at scale.
lesson: Physical-layer modulation innovations require standards-body buy-in and chipset ecosystem support; without that, even patented and well-funded 'better than 5G' claims struggle to reach commercial deployment.
source: Trade press coverage of Cohere Technologies wind-down, 2022-2023

name: Coin
summary: Made a single card that could store and emulate multiple credit/debit cards, was acquired by Fitbit in 2016 for its payments tech, then Fitbit discontinued it in 2017.
lesson: Payment-replacing hardware faces card-network and bank pushback that can undermine the product even after a successful launch.

name: Coincheck
summary: Japanese exchange suffered one of the largest crypto hacks in history in January 2018, losing $530M in NEM tokens, and was forced into acquisition by Monex Group to survive and repay customers.
lesson: Storing hundreds of millions in customer assets in a hot wallet without multisig protections is an unforced catastrophic risk.
source: Japan FSA administrative action against Coincheck, 2018

name: Coinfirm
summary: Blockchain analytics and crypto AML/compliance company that ceased core operations around 2023 amid the broader crypto downturn and funding drought affecting regtech vendors.
lesson: Crypto-compliance vendors are highly exposed to boom-bust cycles in the underlying crypto asset markets they monitor.
source: The Block and CoinDesk coverage of Coinfirm's decline (2023)

name: CoinFLEX
summary: Crypto derivatives exchange halted withdrawals in June 2022 after a large counterparty defaulted on a debt obligation, and later pursued a recovery token and restructuring.
lesson: Extending large uncollateralized credit to individual counterparties on an exchange can freeze the whole platform when it defaults.
source: CoinFLEX statement on withdrawal halt, June 2022

name: Color (Color Labs)
summary: Photo-sharing app that raised a massive seed round on founder pedigree alone, launched to widespread criticism, and shut down within about a year.
lesson: Large funding rounds based on founder reputation cannot substitute for validated product-market fit at launch.

name: Commerce One
summary: Pioneering B2B e-procurement marketplace platform whose valuation and revenue collapsed with the dot-com crash; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2004.
lesson: A hot enterprise-software category can still implode if the customer base cannot fund adoption fast enough to match investor expectations.
source: Wikipedia / contemporary business press coverage of Commerce One bankruptcy (2004)

name: Common Living AI leasing
funding: $105M+ (equity + debt, investors incl. Norwest Venture Partners)
summary: Co-living rental company using AI-driven pricing and matching to lease shared housing units; scaled back significantly and exited most markets by 2023 after failing to make the co-living model profitable amid softening urban demand.
lesson: AI-optimized pricing cannot fix a co-living unit economics problem if underlying demand for shared housing structurally weakens post-pandemic.

name: Como
summary: Israeli DIY app-builder platform for small businesses that shut down its service in 2018 despite substantial venture funding.
lesson: Consumer-facing SMB app builders need a low-cost, high-retention model since churn among small-business customers is inherently high.
source: Como shutdown coverage, Israeli tech press 2018

name: Compass Pathways
summary: Compass Pathways, a public company developing psilocybin therapy for treatment-resistant depression, has seen its stock price collapse over 90% from IPO highs as trials progressed slower than investors expected and commercialization remains years away.
lesson: Psychedelic drug development timelines routinely outlast public-market investor patience, punishing even scientifically credible programs.

name: CompleteFarmer
summary: Ghanaian agtech marketplace CompleteFarmer scaled back operations significantly around 2023 amid broader African agtech funding contraction and unit-economics pressure.
lesson: Early-stage agri-marketplaces in nascent funding ecosystems are highly exposed to broader venture capital pullbacks.
source: TechCabal, African agtech funding downturn coverage 2023

name: Compound Photonics
funding: $150M+ raised across venture and debt rounds
summary: Compound Photonics, developer of microdisplay technology for AR glasses, struggled for over a decade to commercialize its display chips at scale, resulting in significant investor write-downs.
lesson: Deep-tech display component companies serving the AR industry face extremely long, capital-intensive paths to commercialization that can exhaust investor patience.
source: Industry reporting on Compound Photonics funding history and struggles

name: Compute North
summary: Bitcoin mining data-center operator filed for bankruptcy in September 2022 amid the broader mining downturn triggered by falling BTC prices and rising energy costs.
lesson: Hosting providers carrying heavy fixed infrastructure costs are acutely exposed when miner customers can't pay during downturns.

name: comScore
summary: Digital measurement firm comScore restated years of financial results after an accounting scandal involving its non-cash trade transactions, wiping out most of its market value and triggering leadership turnover and lawsuits.
lesson: Aggressive non-cash revenue recognition practices can unravel a media measurement company's credibility overnight.
source: SEC filings, Wall Street Journal coverage of comScore accounting scandal 2016-2017

name: Concirrus
summary: UK marine and specialty insurtech providing IoT-driven risk analytics for underwriters went into administration after failing to secure further investment.
lesson: Selling analytics into slow-moving specialty insurance markets can take longer than venture timelines allow.

name: Conergy AG
summary: German solar company Conergy filed for insolvency in 2013 after years of losses from the same Chinese-driven module price collapse that felled Q-Cells and Solon.
lesson: An entire national industry cluster can be wiped out together when a structural oversupply shock hits a commoditized product.

name: Contactually
summary: Relationship-focused CRM for real estate agents was acquired by Compass in 2019 and fully shut down in 2020 as Compass built its own internal tools.
lesson: Vertical CRM tools acquired by a customer in their core market are often absorbed into internal build efforts rather than kept alive.
source: Compass internal announcement of Contactually shutdown, 2020

name: Content Technologies Inc.
summary: AI startup (Cati) that used machine learning to auto-generate personalized textbooks and adaptive courseware from source material; pivoted toward enterprise AI content generation as its original education-publishing model failed to gain sustained traction.
lesson: Automating textbook creation solved a supply-side problem publishers didn't urgently feel; without demand-side pull from institutions, the technology had to be repurposed for a different buyer entirely.
source: Crunchbase; TechCrunch coverage of Content Technologies' pivot to enterprise AI

name: Context Relevant
summary: Big-data machine learning platform (founded by a former DARPA researcher) that aimed to automate large-scale predictive analytics, but shut down abruptly in 2017 after burning through significant funding without reaching product-market fit.
lesson: Large early funding rounds based on founder pedigree and ambitious vision can mask the absence of a validated enterprise product, leading to an abrupt shutdown once capital runs out.
source: GeekWire coverage of Context Relevant's 2017 shutdown

name: Continual
summary: Declarative feature-store and operational ML platform built on top of the modern data stack (Snowflake/dbt-centric), which shut down in 2023 as the company concluded the category could not support a large independent venture-scale business.
lesson: Building 'on top of' the modern data stack can leave a startup exposed if the underlying platforms (warehouses, orchestration tools) absorb the same functionality natively.

name: Convergys
summary: Large outsourced customer contact-center operator Convergys was acquired by rival Synnex/Concentrix in 2018 after years of margin pressure in the traditional BPO customer service business.
lesson: Traditional labor-intensive customer service outsourcers face structural margin compression as automation and offshore competition both squeeze pricing.

name: Conversica
summary: Conversica built AI-powered conversational assistants that generate personalized marketing and sales follow-up messages at scale, but underwent at least two major rounds of layoffs (18-33% of staff each) in 2023-2024 amid slowing growth and mounting competition from cheaper AI messaging tools.
lesson: Even category-pioneer AI marketing-content companies can be worn down by repeated restructuring once well-funded, cheaper competitors commoditize the core conversational-AI capability.
source: Glassdoor employee reviews, GetLatka revenue estimates for Conversica (2024)

name: Convey
summary: Post-purchase delivery experience and last-mile exception-management software company that wound down in 2023, with some technology assets acquired by FedEx.
lesson: Middleware layers on top of carrier data can be squeezed out once carriers build the same visibility features natively.
source: FreightWaves, Convey wind-down and FedEx asset deal 2023

name: Convoy
summary: Seattle-based digital freight network that used machine-learning pricing and automated matching to connect shippers with truckers, backed by Bezos Expeditions, Baillie Gifford and T. Rowe Price at a $3.8B peak valuation. Ceased operations in October 2023 after failing to find a buyer; Flexport later acquired its technology assets for about $16M.
lesson: Algorithmic freight-matching still runs on thin trucking margins; a capital-markets pullback plus a freight recession can sink even a $1B+-funded network almost overnight.

name: Coord
summary: Curb-space and parking-data startup spun out of Sidewalk Labs that wound down in 2022 after failing to build a large enough commercial base for city curb-management data.
lesson: City infrastructure data products can have long sales cycles that outlast a startup's runway.

name: Copia Global
summary: Kenyan B2B/B2C last-mile goods marketplace serving rural agents that entered administration in 2024 after prolonged cash burn and failure to reach profitability.
lesson: Last-mile distribution to low-density rural markets requires extremely disciplined logistics costs to ever break even.
source: African tech press coverage of Copia Global's 2024 administration filing

name: Copper
summary: Teen banking and debit card startup shut down its app in 2023 after failing to raise further funding in a shrinking niche crowded with better-capitalized rivals like Greenlight.
lesson: Entering a niche already dominated by a well-funded leader leaves little room for a smaller challenger to raise follow-on capital.

name: Copymatic
summary: Copymatic, an early GPT-3-based AI copywriting tool founded by Ryan Faber, was later acquired by Next Net Media and effectively deprioritized as an independent product as the founder-led company changed hands without new investment or active product development.
lesson: Unfunded AI copywriting tools launched during the GPT-3 wave had little room to survive once free ChatGPT access collapsed willingness to pay for basic text generation.
source: Crunchbase company profile for Copymatic AI, ownership records showing Next Net Media acquisition

name: Copysmith (Describely)
summary: Copysmith raised a $10M Series A in 2021 for its general-purpose AI copywriting platform, but as that category commoditized it was forced into a defensive roll-up (acquiring Frase) and a full rebrand around Describely, an e-commerce-specific product-content tool, abandoning its original broad-market copywriting positioning entirely.
lesson: When a generic AI-writing category commoditizes, a defensive rebrand into a narrower vertical is often a sign the original business model has already failed, even if the company keeps operating under a new name.
source: Copysmith/Describely company site, Crunchbase funding records

name: Coqui
summary: Open-source text-to-speech and voice AI startup founded by former Mozilla speech researchers, building on the popular open-source Coqui-TTS toolkit; shut down in January 2024, citing difficulty building a sustainable business around open models.
lesson: Even technically respected open-source voice AI could not out-run better-funded proprietary competitors (like ElevenLabs) once voice cloning quality became table stakes.

name: Core Scientific
summary: One of the largest US Bitcoin miners filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in December 2022 after equipment-financing debt and falling BTC prices made its balance sheet unsustainable; it emerged from bankruptcy in 2024.
lesson: Debt-financed hardware expansion in a commodity-mining business is extremely exposed to price and hash-rate cycles.
source: Core Scientific Chapter 11 filings, 2022; emergence plan 2024

name: CorePower Yoga
summary: National yoga chain CorePower Yoga filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2020 and permanently closed numerous studios after pandemic shutdowns eliminated in-person revenue.
lesson: Studio-based fitness formats with long real estate leases carry structural risk during prolonged closures.

name: Corinthian Colleges
summary: For-profit college chain with large online programs that collapsed in 2015 amid federal investigations into deceptive enrollment and job-placement claims.
lesson: Aggressive recruiting built on inflated outcome claims eventually triggers regulatory collapse.

name: Cortex (Cortex Intelligence)
summary: Cortex, a content-intelligence platform that used AI to predict optimal timing and format for social marketing content, struggled to sustain independent growth as larger social-management and analytics suites added similar predictive features natively, diminishing its standalone value proposition.
lesson: Predictive content-intelligence features are easily absorbed as a checkbox feature inside larger social-management platforms, eroding the case for a standalone point solution.
source: Crunchbase company profile, social-media-management market comparisons

name: Costello
summary: Costello built an AI-assisted sales playbook and coaching tool that surfaced deal-stage-specific guidance inside the CRM. The lightly-funded startup was unable to scale beyond a small customer base and was quietly acquired for its team/technology around 2021, ending independent operations.
lesson: Undercapitalized entrants in the crowded sales-coaching/playbook niche struggled to reach the scale needed to compete with well-funded conversation-intelligence platforms bundling similar features.

name: CoTweet
summary: Social-media customer service tool CoTweet was acquired by ExactTarget in 2010 and discontinued as a standalone product by 2012 as social features were folded into ExactTarget's marketing suite.
lesson: Early social-customer-service tools were often acquired for their user base and then dissolved into a broader marketing platform roadmap.

name: Counter Logic Gaming
funding: Privately funded, later owned by NRG
summary: One of the oldest North American LoL organizations, CLG folded its LCS franchise operations in 2023 as the economics of the franchised league became untenable for smaller-market teams.
lesson: First-mover longevity in esports doesn't protect a brand once league-wide cost structures outrun its revenue base.

name: Covariant
summary: Built foundation models for warehouse-picking robots but couldn't scale revenue against capital-intensive robotics economics; Amazon hired its three founders and ~25% of staff in August 2024 and took a non-exclusive tech license, leaving Covariant described in litigation as a 'zombie company.'
lesson: Hardware-plus-AI robotics ventures need enormous capital runway; a slick model doesn't fix the unit economics of physical deployment.

name: Cover
summary: Accessory dwelling unit (ADU) prefab manufacturer in Los Angeles that shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Small-scale prefab housing startups often can't achieve manufacturing economies of scale fast enough before capital runs out.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Cover ADU shutdown

name: Coverfox
summary: Indian online insurance marketplace for auto, health and life policies wound down its core consumer brokerage after failing to raise further funding, with remaining assets absorbed by smaller players.
lesson: Early leadership in an insurance marketplace category erodes quickly once better-capitalized rivals enter with subsidized acquisition.

name: Covetrus
funding: Formed via Henry Schein Animal Health/Vets First Choice merger; taken private by CD&R and TPG for ~$4B (2022)
summary: Animal-health technology and distribution company spun out via merger in 2019 that struggled with integration and heavy debt, leading to a 2022 take-private deal after a weak public run.
lesson: Merging two large, complex businesses under public-market scrutiny can create years of integration drag that erodes shareholder value until a buyout resets the balance sheet.

name: Covisint
funding: Founded by GM, Ford, DaimlerChrysler as a joint venture
summary: Automotive-industry B2B procurement exchange founded by the Big Three automakers; failed to become the dominant transaction hub they envisioned and was eventually sold for a small fraction of its original valuation before being absorbed into OpenText.
lesson: Consortium-owned marketplaces struggle when founding members compete with each other and cannot agree on shared governance.

name: Covrzy
summary: Bengaluru-based Antler-backed insurtech offering AI-assisted commercial insurance broking for small businesses; shut down after a persistent cash crunch and two failed acquisition attempts, despite holding an IRDAI broking license.
lesson: Thin seed funding in a capital-intensive, licensed insurance business leaves almost no room for error; even a functioning product and regulatory approval could not outrun a prolonged cash crunch.

name: Coya
summary: German digital insurer using algorithmic underwriting and a giveback pricing model; unable to build an efficient online acquisition engine and was absorbed into French rival Luko in a 2022 distressed merger.
lesson: Novel pricing and rebate mechanics do not fix a fundamentally expensive customer-acquisition problem in commoditized home insurance.

name: Craftsy
summary: Online marketplace and classes platform for crafting supplies and instruction was sold off in pieces starting in 2018, with the original platform ceasing operations by 2019.
lesson: Bundling ecommerce and content/education products can dilute focus on either business.

name: Craiyon (DALL-E mini)
funding: No significant venture funding; ad-revenue supported
summary: The viral open-source 'DALL-E mini' meme-image generator (rebranded Craiyon after a trademark dispute with OpenAI) attracted tens of millions of users during its 2022 viral moment but was monetized only through ad revenue and never converted its viral traffic into a durable funded business as image quality of well-funded competitors surged ahead.
lesson: Viral usage numbers are not a business model; without a monetization or funding plan in place before the spike, a breakout generative-AI moment can fade as fast as it arrived once better-funded rivals catch up on quality.

name: Cream Finance
summary: DeFi lending protocol suffered at least five major exploits between 2021-2022 totaling over $130M in losses, and its team effectively discontinued active development and shut down its front end by 2023.
lesson: Repeated smart-contract exploits without decisive remediation erode user trust until a protocol becomes economically unviable.
source: Rekt.news coverage of Cream Finance exploits, 2021-2023

name: Cred Inc.
summary: Crypto lending platform filed for bankruptcy in November 2020 after risky investments and an alleged fraud by a business partner drained its reserves.
lesson: Outsourcing treasury management to unvetted partners can expose a lender to fraud it cannot detect until it's too late.

name: CropOne (Crop One Holdings)
summary: Crop One's joint venture with Emirates Flight Catering, Bustanica-precursor Crop One Dubai facility, wound down its original operations in 2022 amid financial strain in the vertical farming sector.
lesson: Vertical farming joint ventures still must clear the same energy and labor cost hurdles as standalone operators.

name: Cruise
summary: GM's robotaxi subsidiary suspended all driverless operations nationwide in late 2023 after a pedestrian dragging incident in San Francisco, leading to major layoffs, leadership departures, and GM later halting robotaxi development entirely in 2024.
lesson: A single serious safety incident can unravel years of regulatory goodwill and force a strategic retreat from autonomy.

name: Cruise (GM)
summary: GM's robotaxi subsidiary Cruise suspended all driverless operations nationwide in late 2023 after a pedestrian-dragging incident and safety-reporting scandal, and GM ended its robotaxi funding in 2024.
lesson: A single serious safety incident combined with a slow or misleading regulatory response can end years of technology investment almost overnight.
source: GM press release, December 2024, ending Cruise robotaxi development

name: CryptoNumerics
funding: Seed funding backed by 11.2 Capital and others
summary: Toronto-based CryptoNumerics built tools to create privacy-protected datasets with quantifiable re-identification risk. It was acquired by Snowflake in 2020 for a reported $7.1M — a modest asset-sale outcome relative to the capital invested, reflecting the difficulty small privacy-tooling vendors face achieving independent scale in a market increasingly dominated by cloud platform incumbents.
lesson: Point-solution privacy tooling is vulnerable to being commoditized and cheaply absorbed by cloud data-platform incumbents (Snowflake, Databricks) once those platforms decide to build or buy the capability natively.

name: CubicFarm Systems
summary: Publicly traded vertical farming and cattle-feed technology company CubicFarms saw its stock delisted from major exchanges and underwent severe financial distress by 2024.
lesson: Diversifying across multiple unproven agtech product lines can spread limited capital too thin to prove out any single one.

name: Cuddly
summary: Online donation platform connecting donors directly with animal shelters and rescues that shut down operations in 2020.
lesson: Donation-platform startups competing against established giving platforms and direct shelter fundraising can struggle to build a defensible, monetizable niche.
source: Industry and Crunchbase reporting on Cuddly shutdown

name: Cue Health
summary: Cue Health's AI-connected molecular diagnostics platform, including a COVID test with a companion app, scaled massively during the pandemic on federal contracts, but demand collapsed post-pandemic and the company could not build a sustainable non-COVID diagnostics business, ceasing most operations and delisting from Nasdaq in 2024.
lesson: Building a diagnostics company primarily around a single pandemic-driven demand spike leaves almost no margin for error when that demand evaporates.
source: CNBC and MedTech Dive coverage of Cue Health's 2024 wind-down

name: Cupid plc
summary: UK-listed online dating operator whose stock collapsed after revelations that some of its sites used fake profiles and chatbots to entice paying subscribers into conversations.
lesson: Using fake engagement to juice subscription revenue is a fraud risk that can destroy a public company's valuation almost instantly once exposed.

name: Curai Health
summary: Founded by ex-Google AI researchers to build an AI-driven virtual primary care service that could scale physician capacity, Curai struggled to prove the unit economics of AI-augmented, human-in-the-loop primary care at scale, leading to substantial layoffs and restructuring in 2023-2024.
lesson: Even with elite AI research talent, 'AI-augmented' clinical labor models must still prove that per-visit costs fall enough to beat traditional telehealth economics.
source: Fierce Healthcare and Business Insider coverage of Curai Health layoffs

name: Curbside
summary: Mobile curbside pickup and local commerce platform partnering with retailers shut down in 2019 after failing to build a sustainable standalone business.
lesson: Middleman commerce infrastructure is vulnerable once retailers build the same capability in-house.

name: CureVac
summary: Early mRNA vaccine pioneer's COVID-19 candidate showed only ~47% efficacy, losing the vaccine race to Moderna and Pfizer/BioNTech despite two decades of platform development.
lesson: Being first to a technology platform does not guarantee being first with a winning clinical product.

name: Current Health
summary: A remote patient monitoring platform for hospital-at-home care, heavily used for elderly post-discharge patients; Best Buy took a $475M goodwill impairment on its home-health bet and sold Current Health back to its founder in 2025 after major losses.
lesson: Even a well-funded corporate parent will divest a health-tech unit once losses outpace strategic patience, regardless of the underlying clinical promise.

name: Curves International
summary: Women's fitness franchise Curves shrank from a peak of roughly 10,000 locations worldwide to a small fraction of that as low-cost gyms and boutique studios outcompeted its format.
lesson: A once-dominant franchise format can be structurally outcompeted as the market shifts toward newer fitness models.

name: Cyan Inc
summary: Packet-optical networking and SDN software company that went public in 2013 with a valuation over $1B, offering software-driven network management (Blue Planet) alongside optical hardware. Share price collapsed after IPO amid slowing sales, and the company was acquired by Ciena in 2015 for a fraction of its earlier peak valuation.
lesson: An enthusiastic IPO valuation built on anticipated SDN transformation demand can unwind quickly once actual carrier capex spending disappoints, leaving public shareholders and the company exposed.
source: Ciena acquisition announcement 2015; financial press coverage of Cyan's IPO and decline

name: Cyberathlete Professional League (CPL)
summary: One of the earliest professional esports leagues, the CPL ceased operations in 2008 amid mounting financial losses and failed attempts to license its format internationally.
lesson: Being first to professionalize a competitive gaming scene doesn't matter if the sponsorship market isn't mature enough to fund it.

name: Cyberbit
summary: Israeli OT/ICS security and cyber-range training company underwent significant layoffs and strategic retrenchment in the early 2020s as the specialized OT security market proved smaller and slower-growing than projected.
lesson: Specialized security niches (like OT/ICS) can have a much smaller addressable market than the broader enterprise security narrative suggests to investors.

name: Cybereason
funding: over $1B raised, including a $325M round in 2019 and $275M in 2021 from SoftBank
summary: AI-driven EDR/XDR platform (MalOp detection engine) that reached a $3.3B valuation in 2021 on SoftBank backing. After SoftBank's WeWork-driven cutbacks forced Cybereason to prioritize profitability, the company underwent three rounds of layoffs (2022-2024), replaced its founding CEO, and saw its valuation collapse roughly 90% to about $300M by 2023.
lesson: Hyper-growth funded by a single strategic investor with its own liquidity problems (SoftBank/WeWork) can force a security vendor into abrupt, value-destroying downsizing regardless of product quality.
source: BankInfoSecurity, 'Cybereason Doing Its 3rd Round of Layoffs Since 2022'; GovInfoSecurity valuation reporting

name: CyberSponse
summary: Security orchestration and automated response (SOAR) vendor was hit by co-founder litigation and governance disputes before being acquired by FireEye in 2019 for a modest sum.
lesson: Internal founder disputes and governance breakdowns can force a distress sale even for a company in a growing product category.

name: Cyclica
summary: Toronto-based AI drug discovery company built a polypharmacology-prediction platform, but after years without a major independent pipeline win, was acquired by larger rival Recursion Pharmaceuticals for a modest, largely stock-based sum in a distressed deal.
lesson: In AI drug discovery, scale and proprietary data volume increasingly determine winners — smaller platforms without differentiated data often end up as acquisition targets rather than independent successes.
source: Endpoints News and Recursion Pharmaceuticals acquisition announcement (2023)

name: Cygilant
summary: Managed detection and response / SOC-as-a-service provider (formerly EIQnetworks) wound down operations around 2020 after years of subscale growth in the managed security services market.
lesson: Managed security services businesses need real scale to cover 24/7 SOC costs; subscale providers can quietly run out of runway.

name: Cylance
funding: $177M raised before $1.4B acquisition by BlackBerry (2019); resold for $160M in 2024
summary: Pioneer of AI/machine-learning-based antivirus that replaced signature detection with predictive math models. BlackBerry bought Cylance for $1.4B in 2019 hoping to pivot into cybersecurity, but the product failed to keep pace with EDR/XDR competitors and BlackBerry sold the Cylance endpoint business to Arctic Wolf in December 2024 for just $160M cash and stock -- roughly 11% of the original price.
lesson: A breakthrough AI detection model is not a durable moat once every competitor adds machine learning; an acquirer's failure to invest in the roadmap can destroy nearly 90% of a deal's value in five years.
source: TechCrunch, 'BlackBerry sells Cylance for $160M, a fraction of the $1.4B it paid in 2018' (Dec 2024); Arctic Wolf press release

name: Cymmetria
funding: approximately $14M raised before being acquired by Stage Fund in 2019
summary: Israeli deception-technology startup (MazeRunner) whose decoy/honeypot network used behavioral analytics to catch nation-state intrusions. Acquired by private equity firm Stage Fund in September 2019, but within months Stage Fund's chairman ordered the Israeli R&D team dismissed; employees filed for company liquidation in December 2019 over roughly $285,600 in unpaid wages.
lesson: An acquisition is not always a successful exit -- a financial buyer with no operating plan for the acquired team can turn what looked like a soft landing into full liquidation within months.
source: Calcalist (CTech), 'Employees of Cyber Deception Company Cymmetria File for Liquidation' (2019)

name: Cyngn
summary: Public developer of autonomous-driving software (DriveMod) for industrial vehicles like forklifts and tuggers; despite $132M raised, market cap has collapsed to ~$17M.
lesson: A market cap far below lifetime capital raised is one of the clearest signals of a broken venture bet, even without a formal shutdown.

name: Cyphort
summary: Advanced threat-detection vendor was acquired by Juniper Networks in 2017 for an undisclosed but reportedly modest sum after raising roughly $65M, well below investor expectations.
lesson: A crowded threat-detection market can force even well-funded, technically credible vendors into an underwhelming acqui-hire exit.

name: CyPhy Works (Aria Insights)
summary: Founded by iRobot co-founder Helen Greiner to build tethered and autonomous drones for security and industrial use, the company rebranded as Aria Insights in 2018 and wound down in 2019.
lesson: Repeated repositioning between defense, industrial, and consumer drone markets can exhaust runway before any segment matures.
source: Boston Business Journal / TechCrunch coverage of Aria Insights closure

name: Cyren
funding: publicly traded (Nasdaq: CYRN); valuation fell 89.7% in 2022 to about $5M before collapse
summary: Threat-intelligence and AI/ML-driven email and web security vendor (formerly Commtouch) providing anti-phishing and anti-malware feeds used by other security products. After failing to secure financing or a buyer, Cyren laid off virtually its entire 121-person workforce in February 2023, filed for bankruptcy/liquidation in Israel, the US, UK, Germany and Iceland, and was delisted from Nasdaq; its IP was sold off piecemeal to Data443 and Opin Kerfi.
lesson: A legacy threat-intelligence vendor that never modernized its go-to-market can watch its equity value evaporate almost entirely (to ~$5M) even after decades in business, ending in fire-sale liquidation of the IP.
source: BankInfoSecurity, 'Cyren Ceases Operations After Financing, Sale Efforts Fail' (2023)

name: Cyren (formerly Commtouch)
summary: Long-running cloud security company providing web/URL content categorization, phishing detection and threat intelligence. Its valuation collapsed 89.7% in 2022; after financing and sale efforts failed, Cyren laid off its entire workforce, delisted from Nasdaq, and filed for insolvency in Israel in February 2023. Core assets were later bought out of bankruptcy by Data443 for up to $3.5M.
lesson: Being a public company does not protect against slow decline: Cyren was outcompeted by larger, better-funded cybersecurity/content-filtering platforms for years before a final failed refinancing effort forced total shutdown.

name: Cyveillance
summary: Early cyber threat-intelligence and brand-protection vendor lost momentum against newer entrants and was acquired by LookingGlass Cyber in 2015 for an undisclosed, reportedly modest sum.
lesson: First-mover threat-intelligence vendors can be leapfrogged by better-productized competitors and end up as tuck-in acquisitions.

name: D-ID
summary: Israeli AI avatar/video company signed a strategic partnership with Microsoft in early 2025 but days later laid off about 22 employees, roughly a quarter of its roughly 88-person workforce, as part of an efficiency push amid intensifying competition from HeyGen, Synthesia, and others.
lesson: Even a marquee enterprise partnership does not guarantee financial stability in a crowded avatar-video market; distribution deals need to convert to revenue fast enough to outrun burn.

name: D2iQ (Mesosphere)
summary: Pioneer of the Mesos-based DC/OS platform that rebranded as D2iQ as Kubernetes overtook its core technology bet, eventually being acquired by Nutanix in a modest deal.
lesson: Betting the company on one orchestration technology left no way back once the market coalesced around a rival standard.

name: Daegis (formerly Unify Corporation)
summary: E-discovery and information governance software provider that wound down operations and delisted in 2016 after failing to achieve profitable scale in a crowded market.
lesson: Late entry into a consolidating e-discovery market without differentiated technology made profitability unreachable.
source: SEC filings and PR Newswire wind-down notice (2016)

name: Daily Burn
summary: On-demand workout video service Daily Burn, owned by IAC, was shut down in 2022 as the crowded streaming fitness market made it uncompetitive against newer entrants.
lesson: A first-mover in streaming fitness content can still be squeezed out once bigger platforms and hardware bundles enter the space.

name: Damballa
summary: Network-based malware detection pioneer failed to meet revenue expectations and was sold to Core Security for under $10M in 2016, a fraction of the capital it had raised.
lesson: Being an early technology pioneer offers no protection if the company cannot convert that lead into sustainable revenue before better-funded rivals catch up.

name: Daqri
summary: Daqri built industrial AR smart helmets and headsets for manufacturing but shut down in 2019 after failing to reach commercial scale, selling off patents.
lesson: Heavy hardware R&D costs for niche industrial AR devices can outpace a slow-forming enterprise sales cycle.

name: Data Capital Management
funding: small AUM hedge fund, backers undisclosed
summary: Miami-based hedge fund that used machine learning models to trade futures and equities, founded by a former Bloomberg data scientist. Closed its fund to investors in 2019 after underwhelming, inconsistent returns relative to its AI-driven promise.
lesson: Small AI-driven hedge funds face a brutal reality: without years of proven risk-adjusted outperformance, institutional allocators pull capital quickly, and the fund cannot reach the scale needed to be viable.
source: Bloomberg coverage of AI hedge fund closures (2019)

name: DataHero
summary: Self-service BI and data visualization startup that shut down in 2016 after failing to gain enough traction against bigger BI incumbents.
lesson: Self-serve BI tools need a wedge beyond charts and dashboards to avoid being outgunned by Tableau-class incumbents.

name: Datananas
summary: French startup Datananas built an AI-assisted cold-email and outbound sales-automation platform for SMB sales teams across Europe. The lightly-funded company was unable to scale beyond a regional customer base against much larger international competitors and ceased independent operations around 2020.
lesson: A regionally-focused outbound-automation tool with minimal funding struggled to survive once well-capitalized global platforms (Outreach, Reply.io, Apollo) expanded aggressively into the same SMB segment.

name: DataRobot
funding: $1B+ raised; valued at $6.3B in 2021 peak round
summary: Automated machine learning (AutoML) platform that raised at a $6.3B valuation in 2021 but faced a sharp valuation reset, multiple rounds of layoffs (2022-2024), and executive turnover as enterprise AutoML demand shifted toward generative AI and cloud-native ML platforms.
lesson: A large late-stage valuation built on the pre-LLM AutoML paradigm became a liability once the market's attention and budgets pivoted rapidly to generative AI infrastructure.
source: Reports on DataRobot layoffs and valuation decline, Forbes/TechCrunch 2022-2024

name: Daybreak Health
summary: Daybreak Health provided school-partnered teletherapy for teenagers but ceased clinical operations in 2024 after struggling to build a sustainable reimbursement model around school district partnerships.
lesson: Selling into school districts brings long sales cycles and thin, unpredictable per-student economics that are hard to scale profitably.

name: Deadspin (original)
summary: After G/O Media ordered the sports-and-culture site to 'stick to sports,' the entire editorial staff resigned in protest in 2019, effectively gutting the brand under its private-equity owner.
lesson: Editorial staff who built a brand's voice can walk away en masse when new ownership tries to override that identity.

name: Debuild
summary: Debuild let users describe a web app in plain English and generate a working low-code application. Founder Sharif Shameem quietly wound the company down in 2023 to pursue a new generative AI venture.
lesson: Being an early, flashy AI-first mover is not enough when incumbents and fast-following competitors have years of product maturity and much larger war chests; novelty alone does not create retention.

name: Decentraland
summary: Decentraland, a blockchain-based virtual world with tokenized land parcels, saw its MANA token and land values collapse after the 2021-2022 metaverse hype cycle, with reports showing very low daily active users relative to its market capitalization.
lesson: Speculative land/token value in a metaverse platform can vastly outpace actual user engagement, exposing the gap once hype-driven capital recedes.
source: DappRadar and Bloomberg reporting on Decentraland low daily active users, 2022

name: Decentriq
summary: Swiss-based Decentriq built confidential 'data clean rooms' using confidential computing so multiple parties can jointly analyze sensitive data without exposing it to each other. Following a $15M Series A in March 2022, no further funding round has been publicly disclosed through 2026, and the company's headcount has stayed small (around 27 employees), indicating slower-than-expected growth in a data clean room market increasingly served natively by cloud hyperscalers.
lesson: Data clean rooms became a hot category that cloud incumbents (Snowflake, Google, AWS) rapidly built native offerings for; independent confidential-computing clean-room vendors face a shrinking window before hyperscaler bundling erodes their differentiation.
source: Help Net Security, Decentriq press materials

name: Declara
summary: AI-driven learning and knowledge-data platform that used machine learning to curate personalized content and data pipelines for corporate/educational clients; shut down operations in 2018 following funding difficulties and internal disputes reported in the press.
lesson: AI data platforms serving a narrow vertical (corporate learning) can run out of runway quickly if the core data/ML value proposition doesn't produce clear, measurable customer ROI.
source: Press coverage of Declara's 2018 shutdown (EdSurge, TechCrunch)

name: deCODE genetics
summary: Icelandic population-genomics pioneer that mapped disease genes filed for bankruptcy after its drug discovery efforts failed to generate products.
lesson: A world-class genomics dataset does not automatically convert into a marketable drug pipeline.

name: Deep Space Industries
summary: Asteroid-mining and in-space propulsion startup was quietly acquired by Bradford Space in 2019 after failing to raise the capital needed for its resource-prospecting roadmap; the asteroid-mining business unit was effectively shuttered.
lesson: Asteroid mining requires capital and timelines far beyond what venture funding cycles will tolerate absent near-term revenue.

name: Deep Vision Inc.
summary: Edge AI chipmaker building low-power computer-vision processors for cameras and IoT devices raised significant venture funding but was quietly absorbed by an acquirer for a fraction of its raise around 2023 after failing to win enough design wins against larger edge-AI silicon competitors.
lesson: Custom silicon for edge computer vision requires winning design sockets years in advance of revenue, a race that well-capitalized incumbents are better positioned to win.
source: Semiconductor trade press coverage of Deep Vision's funding and eventual acquisition

name: DeepAI
funding: Minimal disclosed outside funding, largely bootstrapped
summary: One of the earliest consumer-facing AI image generation and API tools plateaued as a low-margin utility product once OpenAI's DALL-E, Midjourney, and Stable Diffusion offered dramatically higher-quality output, leaving DeepAI as a legacy low-cost/low-differentiation option rather than a growth business.
lesson: Being first to market in generative AI provides little durable advantage once well-funded competitors ship materially higher-quality models; a commodity utility position erodes fast in a fast-moving model race.

name: DeepArt.io
funding: Minimal/no disclosed outside funding, research-project origin
summary: One of the very first neural style-transfer web apps (built by the Tuebingen researchers behind the original neural style transfer paper) attracted major early attention for turning photos into paintings, but never built a sustainable commercial model, was overtaken by dramatically higher-quality diffusion-based tools like Prisma and Midjourney, and the service eventually went offline.
lesson: Being the original research demo that sparks a whole product category (neural style transfer) does not guarantee commercial survival once better-funded, better-designed consumer products (Prisma, then diffusion models) commercialize the same underlying idea more effectively.

name: Deliv
summary: Crowdsourced same-day delivery and route-optimization platform serving retailers like Best Buy, Macy's and Walgreens. Shut down in mid-2020 after failing to reach profitability amid COVID-driven demand spikes that strained its finances rather than saving them; Target acquired its routing/batching technology.
lesson: A surge in demand does not automatically fix broken unit economics — Deliv's costs scaled with volume as fast as revenue, and investors declined to fund the gap.
source: Supply Chain Dive, CNBC, Retail Dive (2020)

name: Deliverr
summary: AI-driven fast-fulfillment and warehouse-network logistics startup acquired by Shopify for $2.1B in 2022 as the centerpiece of Shopify's push to compete with Amazon on delivery speed. Shopify abandoned the fulfillment strategy within a year, taking a $1.6B impairment on Deliverr and 6 River Systems before selling the combined logistics business to Flexport for an equity stake in 2023.
lesson: A blockbuster acquisition price is not validation of the underlying business — Deliverr's fulfillment network could not make Shopify's vertical logistics bet profitable fast enough, resulting in a $1.6B write-down within 18 months.
source: Wikipedia (Deliverr), Supply Chain Dive, RMW Commerce Consulting (2022-2023)

name: Demand Media (eHow)
summary: A content farm that used algorithms to mass-produce SEO-optimized articles (eHow, Cracked) went public in 2011, then saw its stock collapse after Google's Panda algorithm update devalued its content, leading to a split into Leaf Group.
lesson: A business model built entirely around gaming a third-party search algorithm is fundamentally at that platform's mercy.

name: Dendreon
summary: Developer of Provenge, the first FDA-approved cancer immunotherapy vaccine, collapsed into Chapter 11 bankruptcy because manufacturing costs and a $93,000 price tag made commercial uptake unsustainable.
lesson: FDA approval is not commercial viability if the cost structure cannot support market adoption.

name: Denial Esports
summary: The North American multi-title esports org folded in 2018 after years of roster instability and an inability to secure a franchise slot in major leagues.
lesson: Mid-market esports orgs without a franchised league seat are highly exposed to sponsorship market downturns.

name: Denizen
summary: Neobank built for globally mobile expats and immigrants shut down in 2022 after failing to find a sustainable path to scale in a narrow niche.
lesson: A well-defined niche audience can still be too small to support the fixed costs of running a licensed banking product.

name: Descartes Labs
summary: A Los Alamos National Lab spinout, Descartes Labs built satellite-imagery and AI models for climate, agriculture, energy and defense analytics. After years of heavy cash burn without a durable path to profitability, majority control passed to private-equity firm Antarctica Capital, and in October 2024 it was folded into EarthDaily Analytics, losing its independent identity.
lesson: Deep-tech geospatial AI requires enormous, sustained capital for satellite and compute infrastructure; without a clear enterprise revenue engine, even technically impressive platforms end up absorbed into better-capitalized rivals at a fraction of their ambition.
source: Via Satellite, Geospatial World, PitchBook (2023-2024 Descartes Labs ownership coverage)

name: Deserve
funding: ~$130M (Sallie Mae, Visa, Mission Holdings, Goldman Sachs)
summary: Fintech offering AI/ML-driven credit card underwriting-as-a-service for co-branded cards (students, immigrants with thin credit files). A planned acquisition by Community Federal Savings Bank collapsed in 2023 after regulatory pushback, leaving Deserve without a path to sustainability and forcing it to shut down most operations.
lesson: Credit-card-as-a-service AI underwriting businesses are capital intensive and depend on partner banks; when a lifeline acquisition falls through due to bank-fintech regulatory scrutiny, there may be no fallback plan.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Deserve/CFSB deal collapse (2023)

name: Desk.com (Assistly)
summary: Assistly launched a lightweight helpdesk product, was acquired by Salesforce in 2011 and rebranded Desk.com, then Salesforce shut the product down in 2018 to push customers to Service Cloud.
lesson: Being acquired is not an exit for a product roadmap — an acquirer can sunset a beloved tool once it conflicts with its flagship platform strategy.

name: Desktop Metal
summary: Industrial metal 3D-printing manufacturer went public via SPAC, made a string of acquisitions, then saw its stock collapse and a planned merger with Stratasys collapse amid mounting losses.
lesson: Rolling up multiple acquisitions to build a manufacturing platform can multiply integration and cash-burn risk instead of synergies.

name: Devron
summary: New Jersey-based Devron built a federated data-science platform aimed at letting enterprises run machine learning across distributed, siloed data without centralizing it, competing directly with better-funded federated-learning and privacy-preserving-analytics vendors. The company has disclosed no material funding rounds, revenue milestones, or customer wins since its 2020 founding, and public information about its traction remains sparse years into operation.
lesson: In a category with well-capitalized competitors (Owkin, Apheris, Duality), an underfunded entrant with no visible fundraising or customer proof points risks permanent subscale status — going quiet in a hot category is itself evidence of struggling traction.

name: Dextrous Robotics
summary: Chopstick-like AI robot for automated trailer unloading dissolved after failing to close a follow-on round amid intense competition from Boston Dynamics, Dexterity, and Pickle Robot.
lesson: Technical capability isn't enough with four or five well-funded rivals chasing the same niche.

name: Diamond Sports Group
summary: Owner of the Bally Sports regional sports networks carrying dozens of MLB, NBA, and NHL teams, filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy after cord-cutting gutted subscriber revenue.
lesson: Leveraging up to buy legacy cable sports-rights assets was catastrophic timing just as cord-cutting accelerated.

name: Diapers.com (Quidsi)
summary: Diapers.com's parent Quidsi was acquired by Amazon in 2011 after Amazon undercut its prices in a price war; Amazon shut the Diapers.com brand down entirely in 2017.
lesson: A well-loved baby-products e-commerce brand can be strategically acquired by a dominant competitor specifically to eliminate it, rather than to grow it.

name: Diem (Facebook Libra)
summary: Facebook-led global stablecoin project, rebranded from Libra to Diem, was shut down in 2022 after sustained opposition from US and international regulators, with its assets sold to Silvergate Bank.
lesson: A stablecoin backed by a Big Tech platform with billions of users triggers systemic-risk scrutiny that can override even massive resources.
source: Diem Association wind-down announcement; Silvergate asset purchase, 2022

name: Digg
summary: The pioneering social news aggregator was overtaken by Reddit and Facebook after a disastrous 2010 redesign, and was sold for parts for about $500,000 in 2012.
lesson: A poorly executed redesign can destroy years of community trust almost overnight.

name: Digimarc
summary: Digimarc is the longest-standing player in digital watermarking, embedding imperceptible signals into images and packaging for authentication and content provenance. Despite being a public, revenue-generating company, fiscal 2025 revenue fell to $33.9M with annual recurring revenue collapsing from $20.0M to $13.7M after two major commercial contracts expired, producing a $32.3M GAAP net loss for the year and only $12.9M of cash and securities on hand.
lesson: Even a category-defining, decades-old watermarking incumbent is vulnerable to customer-concentration risk: losing just two large contracts can collapse annual recurring revenue and expose a thin cash cushion, showing that public-company status and long tenure are not proof against distress.

name: Digit
funding: ~$61M before being acquired by Oportun in 2021 for ~$213M
summary: AI-powered automated savings app that analyzed spending patterns to move small amounts into savings without user intervention. Acquired by Oportun in 2021; Oportun discontinued the standalone Digit app in 2023 to focus resources on its core lending business.
lesson: Even a well-regarded AI savings product with a successful acquisition exit can still be discontinued when the acquirer's own financial pressures force it to prioritize core revenue lines over acquired side products.
source: Fintech press coverage of Oportun discontinuing Digit (2023)

name: Digital Guardian
summary: Data loss prevention (DLP) vendor that raised over $200M and shelved earlier IPO ambitions was ultimately sold to HelpSystems (Fortra) in 2021 for terms reportedly well below its cumulative venture investment.
lesson: Data loss prevention is a capital-intensive, long-sales-cycle category where even well-funded vendors can struggle to generate venture-scale returns.

name: Digital Media Solutions
summary: Performance marketing company Digital Media Solutions went public via SPAC in 2020 but its stock collapsed over 90% amid heavy debt load and slowing ad revenue, leading to major restructuring.
lesson: SPAC-driven public listings in performance marketing exposed heavily-leveraged businesses to severe market corrections.
source: SEC filings, Digiday coverage of Digital Media Solutions stock collapse

name: Digital Reasoning
summary: Digital Reasoning was an early NLP/AI company applying natural-language understanding to intelligence-community and financial-compliance use cases, at one point positioned as a leader in 'cognitive computing' for national security. After years of high-profile funding and a peak brand presence, the company scaled back significantly and largely faded from the defense/intel AI conversation as larger, better-capitalized players absorbed the market.
lesson: Being an early mover in an AI category (NLP for intelligence) is not a durable moat once well-funded, more focused competitors enter with faster iteration cycles.
source: general industry reporting on Digital Reasoning's intelligence-community NLP business

name: DigitalGenius
summary: London-based conversational AI agent platform for e-commerce and retail customer service, acquired by localization-AI company Unbabel in 2023 in a deal reported as a modest, distressed outcome relative to funds raised.
lesson: Mid-tier conversational AI vendors without a clear wedge get squeezed between platform incumbents and cheaper LLM-native entrants.

name: Directly
summary: Directly built a crowdsourced customer-support expert network for enterprise brands, but shut down operations in 2020 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Marketplace models for customer support require enormous supply-side liquidity, and gig-expert quality is hard to guarantee at enterprise scale.
source: Crunchbase News coverage of Directly's 2020 shutdown

name: Discord Store (game storefront)
summary: Discord shut down its game storefront and Nitro Games subscription library in 2019 after just over a year, citing low usage relative to its investment.
lesson: A large user base for one product doesn't automatically transfer demand to an adjacent product bundled alongside it.

name: Dispatch
summary: Early sidewalk delivery-robot startup (Carry) wound down operations in 2018 as larger-funded competitors like Starship and Kiwibot scaled faster in the same niche.
lesson: Being an early mover in a capital-intensive robotics category doesn't help if better-funded rivals scale faster.
source: TechCrunch coverage of last-mile delivery robot startups, 2018

name: Dispatch (delivery robots)
summary: Sidewalk delivery-robot startup acquired by Amazon in 2019, whose technology became the basis for the Amazon Scout delivery robot program. Amazon cancelled Scout in January 2023, effectively ending Dispatch's product line entirely.
lesson: Being acquired by a tech giant is not a guaranteed survival path — internal strategic priorities at the acquirer can shut down even a technically functioning delivery-robot program years later.

name: Disperse
summary: UK-based computer-vision construction progress-monitoring startup that ceased operations around 2023 after struggling to raise further funding.
lesson: Construction progress-monitoring software faces a crowded competitive field, making it hard for smaller players to reach scale before funding dries up.
source: UK tech press coverage of Disperse closure

name: DNA My Dog
summary: Early consumer dog DNA testing company that was outcompeted by better-funded, larger-database rivals like Embark and Wisdom Panel, losing market relevance over time.
lesson: In genomics-based products, database size directly determines test accuracy, so early entrants without continued heavy investment get outcompeted by larger rivals.
source: Industry comparison reviews of dog DNA test providers

name: DNAinfo
summary: Billionaire Joe Ricketts abruptly shut down hyperlocal news sites DNAinfo and Gothamist just a week after their newsrooms voted to unionize, laying off around 115 staffers.
lesson: A media outlet fully dependent on one wealthy owner's goodwill can be shut down for reasons unrelated to its underlying business performance.

name: Docker Inc
summary: Container technology pioneer whose developer tool became an industry standard, but whose enterprise sales business failed to generate proportional revenue; sold its enterprise unit to Mirantis.
lesson: Ubiquitous open-source adoption doesn't guarantee a company can capture commensurate enterprise revenue.

name: Docurated
summary: Docurated built an AI-powered sales content management and analytics platform that helped reps find and personalize the right collateral for prospects. Facing intensifying competition from Highspot and Seismic, the company merged into Widen (a digital asset management vendor) in 2019, effectively ending its run as an independent sales-enablement company.
lesson: Sales-content AI without the broader enablement workflow (coaching, analytics, buyer engagement) that Highspot and Seismic offered left Docurated too narrow to compete on its own.
source: Crunchbase, industry reporting on Widen merger

name: Doddle
summary: London-based reverse-logistics and parcel-collection startup that burned through tens of millions of pounds (losing £24M on just £687K revenue in 2015) operating standalone high-street stores, closing 17 stores and laying off over 100 staff in 2017. Pivoted to a pure software/kiosk model for returns and last-mile logistics, and was ultimately acquired by Blue Yonder in November 2023.
lesson: A capital-intensive physical retail footprint built to solve a software problem (parcel returns) was the wrong architecture from the start — the company only became viable after abandoning stores entirely for an IT-provider model.

name: Doma (States Title)
summary: Used machine learning to automate title insurance underwriting decisions in seconds instead of days; went public via SPAC in 2021 but its stock collapsed over 90%, leading to major layoffs, a strategic pivot away from direct underwriting, and eventual going-private acquisition by North American Title Holding in 2023.
lesson: Automating a regulated insurance underwriting process with AI still leaves the company exposed to full title-insurance loss risk and transaction-volume cyclicality.

name: Domain Money
summary: Fee-only financial planning startup offering fixed-price advisor sessions shut down after concluding the human-advisor-plus-software model could not scale profitably.
lesson: Blending human financial advice with software economics is hard to scale profitably at consumer price points.

name: Domino Data Lab
funding: $233M+ raised; valued at $2.7B in 2021 peak round
summary: Enterprise data science and MLOps platform that reached a $2.7B valuation in 2021 but faced significant growth deceleration, valuation markdowns, and workforce reductions in 2023 as enterprise ML platform spend consolidated around hyperscaler-native and generative AI tooling.
lesson: Enterprise data science platforms that priced growth at 2021-era multiples struggled once budget scrutiny and the generative AI pivot compressed the addressable market for legacy MLOps tooling.
source: Reports on Domino Data Lab valuation and layoffs, 2023 (The Information)

name: Domo
summary: Business intelligence and dashboarding platform that IPO'd in 2018 but has seen its stock price fall over 90% since, with persistent unprofitability despite the massive pre-IPO capital raised.
lesson: A successful IPO does not offset years of extreme cash burn if the underlying unit economics never turn sustainably profitable.
source: Domo SEC filings and public market performance

name: Done Global (Done Health)
summary: An ADHD-focused telehealth startup whose founder and clinical president were criminally charged by the DOJ in 2024-2025 for allegedly running a scheme to distribute Adderall prescriptions without legitimate medical need.
lesson: Optimizing prescription volume through algorithmic shortcuts in controlled-substance care invites criminal liability.
source: Department of Justice press release, Bloomberg

name: DoNotPay
summary: Consumer 'robot lawyer' app that promised AI-generated legal documents and automated dispute resolution, including a widely publicized (and abandoned) plan to have AI argue a case in court via earpiece. The FTC sued DoNotPay for deceptive advertising, finding it never verified its AI performed like a real lawyer; the company settled in January 2025 for $193,000 and was barred from making unsubstantiated 'AI lawyer' claims.
lesson: Overselling AI capability in a regulated professional-services domain invites regulatory action that can permanently damage brand trust, even when the underlying product retains real users.

name: Doorman
summary: Last-mile delivery scheduling startup letting consumers pick evening delivery windows that shut down in 2019 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Consumer-facing delivery scheduling layers struggle without owning enough of the underlying delivery economics.

name: Doostang
summary: Invite-only social recruiting network for elite university/MBA job seekers failed to scale beyond a niche audience and shut down as LinkedIn absorbed professional networking.
lesson: A defensible niche can be erased when a horizontal platform (LinkedIn) expands into it.

name: Doppler Labs
summary: Maker of the 'Here One' smart earbuds featuring active audio filtering and an early voice-assistant integration; shut down in late 2017 after failing to achieve profitable consumer hardware sales.
lesson: Ambitious 'computer for your ears' hardware was too expensive and complex to manufacture and sell profitably at consumer price points.

name: Dorabot
summary: Shenzhen computer-vision and deep-learning robotics firm for automated loading, sorting, and induction ceased operations amid the 2024 robotics funding downturn.
lesson: Early-mover status doesn't protect you once better-funded rivals and a downturn hit together.

name: Dot & Bo
summary: Curated furniture and home goods ecommerce startup shut down in 2017 after failing to overcome the high shipping costs and returns typical of furniture ecommerce.
lesson: Furniture ecommerce margins are easily wiped out by freight and return logistics.

name: Douugh
summary: Australian-founded neobank targeting US customers with automated savings tools wound down operations in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Launching a neobank in a foreign, already-crowded market multiplies customer acquisition costs beyond what small raises can sustain.

name: Doxa
summary: Inclusive-hiring startup that used blind, skills-first screening to reduce bias in early-stage candidate evaluation; after an initial wave of interest in bias-reduction hiring tech around 2017-2018, the company went quiet with no further funding announcements or product updates.
lesson: The late-2010s blind-hiring wave produced many well-intentioned seed-stage tools that couldn't out-compete each other for the same small pool of bias-conscious enterprise buyers.
source: General HR-tech press coverage of blind-hiring startups (2017-2018 cohort)

name: Draganfly
summary: Draganfly builds AI-enabled drones for public-safety, defense, and humanitarian use, including thermal/AI detection payloads pitched to police and military customers. As a small public company it has faced chronic unprofitability, dilutive financings, and a stock price that has fallen sharply from its post-IPO highs despite periodic high-profile government pilot programs.
lesson: Winning headline-grabbing government pilot programs does not guarantee recurring procurement revenue sufficient to sustain a public drone-hardware company's cost structure.
