source: general public market reporting on Draganfly (DPRO) financials

name: Dragon Systems
funding: Acquired by Lernout & Hauspie (2000) for ~$580M in stock
summary: Pioneering speech-recognition company (Dragon NaturallySpeaking) founded by Jim and Janet Baker; sold to Belgian rival Lernout & Hauspie in an all-stock deal in 2000, and the founders lost nearly their entire fortune months later when L&H's stock collapsed amid an accounting fraud scandal.
lesson: An all-stock acquisition is only as safe as the acquirer's books; founders who don't diversify out of acquirer stock can be wiped out by fraud they had no part in.

name: DreamStudio (Stability AI consumer app)
summary: Stability AI's own consumer-facing image-generation web app, DreamStudio, failed to build a durable paying subscriber base as most users instead ran the open-source Stable Diffusion model for free via third-party interfaces, undermining Stability's own first-party consumer monetization even as it gave away the underlying model.
lesson: Open-sourcing a foundation model while also trying to sell a first-party consumer app built on that same model creates a direct internal conflict: free community tools built on the open model will structurally undercut your own paid product.

name: Drift
summary: Drift pioneered conversational marketing and AI chatbots for inbound sales qualification, reaching a peak valuation over $1.1B in 2021. Growth slowed sharply in 2022-2023, the company underwent multiple rounds of layoffs and leadership changes, and it was ultimately acquired by SalesLoft in 2024 at a valuation widely reported to be a significant markdown from its peak.
lesson: A category-defining unicorn can still see its valuation collapse when growth-at-all-costs spending meets a demand slowdown, forcing a distressed sale even after a strong initial run.
source: TechCrunch, industry reporting on SalesLoft acquisition

name: Drive.ai
summary: Self-driving vehicle startup running autonomous shuttle pilots in Texas ran low on funding as investor enthusiasm for robotaxi timelines cooled; sold its team and some assets to Apple in mid-2019 in an acqui-hire after failing to close a new funding round.
lesson: Autonomy startups racing on multi-year timelines are exceptionally exposed to shifts in investor risk appetite.

name: DroneUp
summary: Walmart's primary drone-delivery partner underwent major layoffs and scaled back store-based delivery hubs in 2024 as Walmart pulled back on its drone delivery ambitions amid weak unit economics.
lesson: Depending on a single retail anchor partner for drone-delivery volume leaves a startup vulnerable to that partner's changing priorities.
source: Trade press coverage of DroneUp layoffs and Walmart drone program scale-back, 2024

name: Drs. Foster and Smith
summary: Once one of the largest pet supply catalog and e-commerce retailers, acquired by Petco in 2015 and ultimately discontinued as a standalone brand in 2018 as Petco consolidated its online operations.
lesson: Legacy catalog-era retailers that don't modernize fast enough get acquired for their customer base and then wound down once the acquirer no longer needs the separate brand.
source: Industry trade press coverage of Petco brand consolidation, 2018

name: Dubsmash
summary: Lip-sync and short video creation app acquired by Reddit in 2020 that was ultimately shut down in 2023 as Reddit deprioritized standalone video products.
lesson: An acquisition can still end in shutdown if the acquirer's strategic priorities move away from the acquired product.

name: e-Steel
summary: Online steel trading marketplace that raised over $100M during the dot-com boom but folded within two years as transaction volume never materialized at scale.
lesson: Heavy industry buyers with entrenched relationship-based purchasing are slow to move volume onto a new digital channel.
source: Contemporary trade press on e-Steel's 2001 shutdown

name: e-Therapeutics
funding: Publicly listed on AIM; raised £28.9M in 2024 delisting round
summary: UK computational-biology company using network-pharmacology AI to discover RNAi medicines, publicly listed on London's AIM market for over two decades. In April 2024 the company announced it would quit AIM entirely, citing a 'lack of institutional UK interest' and 'risk appetite' for its story, ceasing trading in May 2024 and exploring a future Nasdaq listing instead.
lesson: More than twenty years as a public AI-driven drug discovery company without a marketed product exhausted the patience of UK public investors specifically, illustrating how geography and market sentiment — not just company execution — can end a public listing.

name: E8 Security
summary: Machine-learning-based network and endpoint behavioral analytics platform for detecting advanced threats. Failed to raise further growth capital and was acquired by fellow struggling UEBA vendor Fortscale in 2018 in a distressed consolidation.
lesson: When too many venture-backed startups chase the same behavioral-analytics thesis simultaneously, most end up merging or shutting down rather than reaching independent scale.
source: Security trade press coverage of Fortscale-E8 Security merger, 2018

name: EarthNow
funding: undisclosed (backed by Bill Gates, Airbus, SoftBank)
summary: Ambitious satellite venture backed by Bill Gates and Airbus promised a constellation delivering real-time video of the entire planet analyzed with computer vision, but the project stalled and quietly wound down around 2021 without ever launching an operational satellite.
lesson: Marquee investor names cannot compress the enormous capital and engineering timelines required to build and launch a real-time global imaging constellation.
source: SpaceNews coverage of EarthNow's stalled satellite constellation plans

name: EasyMile
funding: ~€100M+ raised, incl. €55M round from Searchlight Capital
summary: French autonomous shuttle software maker that filed for judicial restructuring in 2024 after failing to find new investors to finance its public-transit pilots. Withdrew from the public transportation segment in 2025 and discontinued shuttle projects across Europe, including in Germany.
lesson: Even well-capitalized European AV shuttle operators found that publicly subsidized pilot revenue never converted into a sustainable recurring business at scale.

name: eBags
summary: Online luggage and bags retailer was acquired by Samsonite in 2017 and shut down in 2020 amid pandemic-driven travel collapse and parent-company struggles.
lesson: A niche ecommerce category tied closely to travel is acutely exposed to macro demand shocks.

name: Echo Fox
funding: Privately funded by Rick Fox and investors
summary: The multi-title esports org co-founded by Rick Fox forfeited its League of Legends franchise slot and dissolved after a bitter ownership dispute involving co-owner Amit Raizada.
lesson: Multi-owner esports orgs need clean cap-table governance before scaling, or internal disputes can void the franchise itself.

name: Eclipse Senior Living
summary: A senior living operator created by REIT Ventas to manage over 100 communities; the pandemic devastated occupancy and margins so severely that Ventas transitioned all communities to other operators and shut down Eclipse's corporate operations by the end of 2021.
lesson: A new operating company built around distressed senior living assets is extremely exposed to macro shocks like a pandemic that hits occupancy across an entire portfolio at once.

name: Editas Medicine
summary: One of the original CRISPR gene-editing companies discontinued its lead in vivo program after disappointing trial results, forcing a strategic pivot and repeated leadership changes.
lesson: Being a CRISPR pioneer with strong IP does not guarantee the first clinical program succeeds.

name: eGain
summary: Customer engagement software vendor eGain has remained a public micro-cap for over two decades with a persistently depressed stock price and stagnant growth relative to cloud-native CX rivals.
lesson: Surviving as a public company for decades is not the same as thriving — stagnant growth against faster-moving SaaS rivals can trap a vendor in permanent decline.
source: eGain SEC filings and long-term stock performance history

name: EHang
summary: Chinese autonomous aerial vehicle and drone maker's stock collapsed over 60% in a single day in 2021 after short-seller Wolfpack Research alleged its flagship contracts and revenue were largely fabricated.
lesson: Rapid pre-revenue valuation growth in an aerial-robotics story stock warrants heavy scrutiny of underlying contract and revenue claims.
source: Wolfpack Research report on EHang and subsequent Reuters coverage, 2021

name: eHealth Inc
summary: Publicly traded online health insurance marketplace saw its stock crash more than 90% from its 2020 highs as Medicare Advantage policy churn and rising acquisition costs battered profitability.
lesson: Even an established online insurance marketplace can be destabilized by shifts in carrier commission economics.

name: Eldarune
summary: Seedify-incubated blockchain fantasy RPG ecosystem marketed as an 'AI-driven' multi-game universe (World of Elymnias, Heroes of Eldarune, ELDA Rush). Shut down its games in 2025 after failing to secure follow-on funding amid the wider Web3-gaming downturn.
lesson: Peak-era community traction (hundreds of thousands of players) did not translate into sustainable revenue once broader crypto-gaming capital dried up, showing hype-driven growth is fragile without a real monetization engine.

name: Electric Last Mile Solutions
summary: Commercial EV maker Electric Last Mile Solutions filed for bankruptcy in 2022, less than a year after its SPAC merger, following an accounting investigation into its founders' prior venture.
lesson: Founder history and internal accounting controls matter enormously; unresolved governance red flags can sink a company within months of going public.
source: Reuters, June 2022, Electric Last Mile Solutions bankruptcy

name: Element AI
summary: High-profile 'AI-as-a-service' studio co-founded with Yoshua Bengio that struggled to turn research proofs-of-concept into repeatable enterprise products; sold to ServiceNow for roughly $230-500M, well below its prior $600-700M valuation, and ServiceNow wound down the existing business after absorbing the talent.
lesson: A brilliant research bench is not a business model; productizing AI for enterprises requires as much go-to-market discipline as science.
source: Globe and Mail, TechCrunch, ServiceNow newsroom

name: Element Analytics
summary: Oakland industrial IT/OT data-unification platform (Element Unify) raised a 2020 Series B but is no longer active.
lesson: Data-integration plays are hard to monetize standalone when platform incumbents bundle 'good enough' integration for free.

name: Element Insurance
summary: German digital insurance carrier and B2B2C insurance-as-a-service provider filed for insolvency after major partners pulled back and losses mounted.
lesson: Full-stack insurance carriers need enormous reserves of capital to survive underwriting losses while scaling.

name: Element Insurance AG
summary: Berlin-based cloud-native, API-first direct insurer for non-life risks; filed for insolvency in December 2024 after its primary reinsurer cancelled cover, with final insolvency proceedings opened in March 2025, affecting around 400,000 policyholders.
lesson: Being a fully licensed digital carrier, not just a tech vendor, means reinsurance and capital adequacy failures can end the company overnight, regardless of how strong the technology stack is.

name: Element Robotics / Agrist
summary: Early-stage harvesting robotics venture Element Robotics wound down around 2023 after its autonomous picking hardware failed to reach commercial reliability targets.
lesson: Harvesting robots that cannot consistently match human-level speed and accuracy in variable field conditions will struggle to attract paying grower customers.

name: Elio Motors
summary: Elio Motors, developer of a low-cost three-wheeled autocycle, never reached production after over a decade of delays and effectively wound down operations by 2021.
lesson: Repeatedly delaying vehicle production while continuing to raise money from retail investors erodes credibility and eventually funding access.
source: SEC filings and Jalopnik coverage of Elio Motors' prolonged non-production, 2020-2021

name: Ello
summary: AI-powered reading tutor app for young children using speech recognition to listen to kids read aloud and adapt instruction; shut down in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Speech-recognition-based literacy tutoring for young children requires very high accuracy to earn parent trust; technical shortfalls in noisy home environments undermine retention.
source: Company shutdown announcement 2023; EdTech Insiders coverage

name: Elroy Air
summary: Elroy Air develops autonomous, AI-piloted cargo aircraft for military resupply and commercial logistics, backed by USAF's Agility Prime program. Despite strong government interest and demos, the company faced a difficult 2024 fundraising environment for capital-intensive autonomous aircraft hardware and had to cut staff while stretching its runway toward certification and production.
lesson: Government interest and pilot contracts validate a concept but do not fund the capital-intensive path from prototype to certified production hardware; that gap is where many autonomy-hardware startups stall.
source: general defense-tech and aerospace industry reporting on Elroy Air 2024 layoffs

name: Elvie
summary: UK femtech pioneer Elvie, maker of wearable breast pumps and pelvic-floor trainers, entered administration in March 2025 after its funding runway expired; rival Willow acquired its assets.
lesson: Even category-defining hardware startups with nine-figure funding can run out of runway when manufacturing costs, supply-chain disruption, and intensifying competition outpace revenue growth.

name: Elysium Space
summary: Space-burial startup that flew symbolic portions of cremated remains to orbit or the Moon ceased new bookings around 2022 after its main satellite ride-share partnerships wound down and it failed to scale beyond a niche customer base.
lesson: A novelty niche product riding on ride-share launch capacity has limited total addressable market and thin unit economics.

name: eMagin Corporation
funding: Public company (NYSE American: EMAN) prior to acquisition
summary: eMagin, a maker of OLED microdisplays used in military and AR/VR headsets, was acquired by Samsung Display in 2024 in a going-private deal after years of financial losses and delisting risk.
lesson: Component suppliers to the AR/VR hardware industry can face chronic unprofitability even when their technology is used across multiple downstream headset makers.

name: Embark Trucks
summary: Autonomous long-haul trucking company that went public via SPAC in 2021, but as its stock collapsed over 99%, it laid off 70% of staff and shut down in 2023, selling remaining assets to Applied Intuition.
lesson: SPAC-driven public listings gave capital-hungry autonomy startups a shorter runway and far more scrutiny than staying private would have.

name: Emerge
summary: Digital freight marketplace startup that abruptly ceased operations in 2023 amid allegations of financial misrepresentation to investors and lenders.
lesson: Freight marketplaces built on opaque financial reporting collapse quickly once lenders and investors lose trust.
source: FreightWaves, Emerge shutdown and fraud allegations 2023

name: Emitwise
summary: UK-based Emitwise built AI-driven carbon-accounting software to automate Scope 1-3 emissions tracking for supply chains. In mid-2025 it could not sustain independent operations and its technology platform was acquired by Green Project Technologies, which absorbed the product into its own carbon-accounting suite.
lesson: Carbon accounting became a crowded category almost overnight; without a durable differentiation or path to profitable scale, well-funded entrants still end up consolidated into larger competitors.
source: ESG Today, PRNewswire coverage of Green Project Technologies' acquisition of Emitwise (2025)

name: eMoods / Moodfit
summary: Moodfit, a self-guided mood-tracking and mental fitness app, failed to scale beyond a small user base and effectively ceased active development around 2022 amid competition from better-funded meditation and mood apps.
lesson: Bootstrapped consumer mental wellness apps struggle to compete on marketing spend against venture-backed category leaders.

name: Emotech
summary: UK-based voice and emotional-AI startup building conversational assistant technology for consumer devices and robotics; entered administration around 2022 after failing to convert early hardware partnerships into sustained revenue.
lesson: Betting heavily on voice-enabled consumer hardware partnerships left the company exposed when those hardware programs stalled or were cancelled.
source: UK Companies House filings, tech press

name: Empow
summary: Israeli AI-driven security orchestration platform (i-SIEM) that used natural-language-processing and machine-learning correlation of network security data to automate threat detection and response. Ceased operations around 2022 after failing to differentiate sufficiently in the increasingly crowded security orchestration (SOAR/XDR) market.
lesson: The AI-driven SOAR/XDR security orchestration space became saturated with well-funded entrants and incumbent platform expansion, squeezing out smaller independent players lacking a defensible niche.
source: Israeli tech trade press coverage of Empow closure

name: Empower Behavioral Health Systems
summary: A regional virtual behavioral health startup that scaled quickly during the pandemic telehealth boom but shut down in 2021 when demand normalized and it could not compete against nationally scaled platforms.
lesson: Pandemic-era demand spikes attracted regional operators that could not survive once national platforms with more capital moved into the same markets.

name: Empty Set Dollar
summary: Algorithmic stablecoin protocol lost its peg and was effectively abandoned in 2021, one of several seigniorage-model stablecoins that failed to sustain value during volatility.
lesson: Reflexive tokenomics that rely on continuous new demand cannot survive a sustained loss of confidence.
source: DeFi post-mortem coverage of algorithmic stablecoin collapses, 2021

name: Encore Discovery Solutions
summary: E-discovery services provider that shut down abruptly in 2013 after data-handling and financial problems, leaving law firm clients scrambling to recover case data.
lesson: E-discovery vendors handling sensitive litigation data must maintain financial and operational reliability, or client relationships collapse overnight.
source: Law Technology News and Above the Law coverage (2013)

name: Endlesss
summary: Social live music-jamming and collaboration app that shut down in 2023 after failing to raise further funding to sustain operations.
lesson: Novel collaborative music formats need a large committed user base fast enough to justify continued investor funding.

name: Endomondo
summary: Fitness tracking app Endomondo was acquired by Under Armour in 2015 and shut down in 2021 as the company retreated from connected fitness.
lesson: A crowded fitness-tracking app market makes it difficult to justify running multiple overlapping acquired products.

name: Ener1
summary: Ener1 made lithium-ion batteries for EVs through subsidiary EnerDel and filed for Chapter 11 in 2012 after its main customer, Think Global, went bankrupt and demand fell short of projections.
lesson: Concentrating revenue on a single early-stage EV customer creates existential risk if that customer fails.

name: Enigma Technologies
summary: Enigma built an enterprise data-intelligence and machine-learning platform for aggregating and modeling public and private data, but underwent major layoffs and a significant strategic downsizing in the early 2020s after struggling to convert its ambitious data platform into durable recurring revenue.
lesson: A broad 'universal data platform' vision requires an unusually long and expensive path to product-market fit, and repeated pivots can exhaust investor patience.

name: Enjoy Technology
summary: Mobile retail concept founded by former Apple retail chief Ron Johnson that paired data-driven, personalized in-home product demonstrations (originally for Apple, then AT&T, then EVs) with a small tech-enabled sales-associate fleet. Filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2022 less than a year after a SPAC merger, unable to scale the labor-intensive white-glove model profitably.
lesson: A personalization-driven service model that depends on real humans doing home visits doesn't get cheaper with scale the way software-only personalization does.
source: Bankruptcy filings and trade press on Enjoy Technology Chapter 11, June 2022

name: Enlitic
summary: One of the earliest deep-learning radiology startups, Enlitic pioneered AI for reading X-rays and CT scans but struggled for years to gain regulatory clearance and hospital adoption, going through repeated leadership changes, layoffs, and pivots before fading into near-total obscurity.
lesson: Being first to market with a bold AI vision in medicine doesn't guarantee success if regulatory pathways and clinical workflow integration aren't solved early.
source: TechCrunch and Forbes coverage of early medical AI pioneers

name: Enron Corporation
summary: Energy trading giant Enron, once a pioneer of deregulated energy markets, collapsed into bankruptcy in December 2001 after systemic accounting fraud using off-balance-sheet special purpose entities was exposed.
lesson: Innovative energy trading models built on hidden debt and fraudulent accounting eventually collapse regardless of market position.
source: SEC enforcement records, Wall Street Journal

name: Entelo
summary: Early AI-sourcing and diversity-recruiting platform; rather than reaching an independent IPO or major standalone exit, it was folded into SilkRoad Technology in 2020, which was itself acquired by Cornerstone OnDemand in 2022 - a roll-up outcome rather than a standalone win.
lesson: Being an early mover in AI sourcing isn't enough if faster, better-funded rivals catch up - you can end up as a line item in someone else's roll-up.

name: Entelos
summary: One of the pioneering computational/systems biology companies, building 'virtual patient' disease simulation models (PhysioLab) used by pharma to predict drug responses before clinical trials — an early precursor to today's AI-for-biology platforms. Entelos filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2011 and its assets were auctioned off; its consulting business was later acquired by Rosa & Co. in 2014.
lesson: Computational disease-simulation platforms were commercially ahead of both the compute and data availability needed to make them broadly useful — a cautionary precedent for today's AI-biology 'digital twin' startups riding on much better infrastructure.

name: Envel
summary: AI-driven neobank that promised to automatically move money between 'envelopes' (spending, saving, investing) using predictive algorithms. Shut down in 2022 after failing to raise a follow-on round in a tightening fintech funding environment.
lesson: A thin seed round is not enough runway to build banking infrastructure, acquire customers, and prove an AI-personalization thesis before the funding market turns.
source: Fintech press coverage of Envel shutdown (2022)

name: Envia Systems
summary: Envia Systems claimed breakthrough high-energy-density lithium-ion battery chemistry and signed a GM development deal, but the technology failed to meet cycle-life and safety targets, and the company was effectively wound down after litigation with GM.
lesson: Overstating lab-scale battery performance to a major automotive partner can trigger both technical failure and legal collapse.

name: Enview
summary: Enview built AI/LIDAR-based 3D object-detection analytics marketed for military and infrastructure applications, including work with defense-adjacent customers. The company never reached the scale of better-funded geospatial-AI peers and quietly narrowed its focus and public presence in the years after its initial funding rounds.
lesson: In a geospatial/AI-analytics field with several well-capitalized competitors (Orbital Insight, Palantir, BlackSky), an under-funded entrant can stall out well short of exit even with real technology.
source: general industry reporting on Enview's geospatial AI product

name: Ersatz Labs
summary: Ersatz Labs built one of the earliest cloud deep-learning-as-a-service platforms for enterprises to train neural networks without in-house expertise, but shut down around 2016 after failing to raise a follow-on round as larger cloud providers began offering similar capabilities.
lesson: Being an early mover in 'deep learning as a service' meant little once AWS, Google and Microsoft folded equivalent capabilities into their core cloud platforms.

name: Escape Dynamics
summary: Startup developing a beamed-microwave-propulsion reusable launch vehicle shut down in 2016 after concluding the ground-based microwave beaming infrastructure required was not commercially viable at the scale investors would fund.
lesson: A propulsion concept that requires building an entirely new ground infrastructure network faces two capital-intensive technology risks instead of one.

name: eSolar
summary: eSolar developed modular solar thermal tower technology but wound down operations around 2016 as utility-scale photovoltaic panels became far cheaper than solar thermal.
lesson: A technically elegant solar thermal architecture lost out once conventional photovoltaic costs fell faster than anyone predicted.

name: Esperanto Technologies
summary: Built a 1,000-core RISC-V AI accelerator chip but landed too few customers after years of development and wound down its silicon business.
lesson: A technically impressive chip with no anchor customer cannot outrun the cash burn of custom silicon development.

name: Essential Products
summary: Android co-founder Andy Rubin's company shipped the Essential Phone but sold poorly against Apple/Samsung; shut down in 2020.
lesson: Star founders and huge funding rounds can't overcome a saturated market and a single failed hero product.

name: eStara
summary: Click-to-call and web customer contact vendor eStara was acquired by Oracle in 2011, and the standalone product was discontinued within a year as it was merged into Oracle's marketing cloud stack.
lesson: Niche communication-bridge technologies acquired by large enterprise vendors are frequently absorbed into a broader suite and lose their brand identity.

name: Esurance
summary: Early data-driven direct-to-consumer auto insurer; despite over a decade under Allstate's ownership and continued investment in digital and algorithmic underwriting, never achieved sustainable profitability or scale against GEICO, and Allstate retired the brand entirely in March 2023.
lesson: A decade of corporate backing and technology investment cannot overcome a business that never reaches the scale needed for profitable direct-response underwriting economics.

name: eToys.com
summary: Online toy retailer that briefly outvalued Toys R Us during the dot-com boom filed for bankruptcy in 2001 after overspending on marketing and logistics infrastructure.
lesson: A high stock valuation is not a substitute for a profitable operating model.

name: Euclid Analytics
summary: In-store retail analytics company using Wi-Fi signals to track shopper foot traffic was acquired by WeWork in 2016 and subsequently wound down as WeWork abandoned the retail-analytics product line.
lesson: An analytics startup acquired by a company outside its core market can be shut down when the acquirer's strategic priorities shift.

name: Even (earned wage access)
summary: Earned-wage-access and financial wellness startup was acquired by Walmart in 2019 to become Even Responsible Finance, but Walmart shut the standalone brand down in 2023 in favor of other benefits partners.
lesson: Being acquired by a single anchor customer concentrates existential risk in that one relationship.
source: TechCrunch/Fortune coverage of Walmart discontinuing Even 2023

name: Even (Even Responsible Finance)
funding: ~$45M+ (Khosla Ventures and others) before Walmart partnership/acquisition
summary: Fintech offering AI-driven income smoothing and financial wellness tools, best known for its deep integration as Walmart's employee financial benefit (later rebranded One@Work). The standalone Even consumer app was discontinued as Walmart folded the functionality into its own 'Even' financial super-app and later into One.
lesson: Deep dependence on a single enterprise distribution partner (Walmart) for AI financial-wellness tools left the startup's fate entirely tied to that partner's own product strategy shifts.
source: Fintech press coverage of Even/Walmart One app transition (2022-2023)

name: Evergreen Solar
summary: Massachusetts solar wafer manufacturer that filed for bankruptcy after Chinese competitors undercut its production costs despite state subsidies.
lesson: Domestic manufacturing subsidies could not offset a structural cost disadvantage versus lower-cost overseas producers.

name: Evernote
summary: Pioneering notes app once reached a $1B+ valuation but suffered years of stagnation and declining relevance against Notion, culminating in its 2023 sale to Bending Spoons.
lesson: Early category leadership can erode without continued product innovation and focus.

name: Everoad
summary: French digital road-freight platform that struggled to reach profitability and was absorbed/restructured in 2019 after failing to compete against larger, better-capitalized freight-tech rivals.
lesson: A crowded freight-tech field with well-funded incumbents leaves little room for undercapitalized regional platforms.

name: Everpix
summary: Highly praised photo storage and organization service that shut down in 2013 after running out of money and failing to raise a follow-on round or find an acquirer.
lesson: Even a product users love can die if storage costs and funding runway do not line up with revenue growth.

name: EverString
summary: EverString offered AI-based predictive lead scoring and ideal-customer-profile modeling for B2B sales and marketing teams, raising over $100M including a $65M Series C from Sequoia and Lightspeed. After a leadership pivot to an ABM platform failed to gain traction, the company laid off most staff and ceased operations in 2019.
lesson: A late-stage pivot into an already-saturated adjacent category (account-based marketing) rarely rescues a company that hasn't found product-market fit in its original niche.

name: Everyday Robots (Alphabet)
funding: internally funded by Alphabet's X moonshot factory
summary: Alphabet's general-purpose household and office robot project, incubated for years inside X, was shut down in July 2023 after failing to demonstrate a clear near-term commercial path.
lesson: Even a well-funded corporate moonshot will be cut if it cannot show a believable path to commercialization within a reasonable horizon.
source: IEEE Spectrum / Wired coverage of Everyday Robots shutdown, 2023

name: Evil Geniuses (LCS exit)
summary: Facing unsustainable annual franchise fees and player costs relative to League of Legends revenue, Evil Geniuses sold its LCS slot back to Riot Games in 2023 and exited League esports entirely.
lesson: Paying a large upfront franchise fee only pays off if league-wide viewership and sponsorship growth keeps pace with rising team costs.

name: Evisort
summary: AI-powered contract lifecycle management and document intelligence platform spun out of Harvard/MIT research. After a $100M Series C in 2021, the company overhired, cycled through multiple rounds of layoffs across sales, customer success, and marketing in 2023-2024, and struggled to convert pipeline while burning cash toward a next round it needed to survive. Workday acquired the company in September 2024 in what employees described internally as a distressed sale rather than a strong exit.
lesson: Overhiring on the back of a large late-stage round without matching go-to-market execution creates a burn rate the business can't grow into, often forcing a fire-sale acquisition well below the last private valuation.

name: Ex-Human (AI companion/creative avatars)
summary: Small AI companion and generative-persona startup raised only a modest seed round and struggled to differentiate in an increasingly crowded AI-companion/generative-character market against far better funded players (Character.AI, Replika), remaining a marginal, low-visibility player.
lesson: Entering an AI-companion/generative-persona category already dominated by a well-funded leader with strong network effects requires either a distinct wedge or comparable capital; neither being present left the startup stuck in obscurity.

name: Exabeam
funding: over $325M raised, including a 2021 round at a ~$2.4B valuation; merged with LogRhythm in 2024
summary: AI/ML-driven UEBA and next-gen SIEM platform that automated behavioral threat detection. Facing waning demand as Microsoft and platform consolidation squeezed point-solution SIEM vendors, Exabeam was forced into a 2024 merger with Thoma Bravo-owned rival LogRhythm in which Exabeam's own shareholders had their shares cancelled for no consideration -- triggering shareholder litigation -- followed by 30% workforce cuts in the combined company.
lesson: Being acquired by your own competitor's private-equity owner in a 'merger of equals' framing can mask what is functionally a distressed wind-down, especially when existing shareholders receive nothing.
source: The Register, 'Merged Exabeam and LogRhythm cut jobs, face lawsuit' (Jul 2024)

name: Excalibur Almaz
summary: Private space tourism venture that acquired Soviet-era Almaz space station modules and reentry capsules planned crewed lunar and orbital tourism flights but dissolved in 2015 having never flown a paying customer.
lesson: Refurbishing legacy hardware for a new commercial market still requires the full capital and certification cost of a new vehicle program.
source: Isle of Man company filings / SpaceNews

name: Exos Aerospace
summary: Reusable suborbital rocket company ceased operations in 2023 after its SARGE vehicle suffered a launch failure and it could not secure further financing.
lesson: A single high-profile flight failure can be fatal when a company has no cash cushion to fund a return-to-flight investigation.

name: Expect Labs
summary: Expect Labs built the MindMeld voice/AI conversational platform originally for consumer apps, then pivoted toward enterprise voice-AI solutions, but was largely wound down and its core MindMeld assets were separately acquired by Cisco in 2017, leaving little of Expect Labs as an independent company.
lesson: A pivot from consumer to enterprise voice AI, undertaken too late relative to the market's evolution, can result in a fire-sale outcome even for genuinely useful technology.

name: Explorium
summary: External data enrichment and feature discovery platform for ML models, which underwent significant layoffs and a strategic pivot toward generative AI-focused offerings in 2024 after its original automated feature-engineering product failed to sustain its earlier growth trajectory.
lesson: A strong niche in automated feature discovery for classical ML models lost relevance as the market's attention and budget shifted rapidly toward generative AI tooling.
source: Industry reporting on Explorium's 2024 layoffs and repositioning

name: F45 Training
summary: Franchise fitness chain F45 Training went public in 2021 backed by celebrity investors, but its stock collapsed over 95% amid franchisee struggles before being taken private in 2023 at a fraction of its IPO value.
lesson: Rapid franchise unit growth without validating franchisee-level profitability can unravel quickly once growth slows.

name: Fab.com
summary: Design-focused flash-sale commerce site (fashion, home goods) burned through over $300M chasing hypergrowth, pivoted its business model multiple times, and was sold for parts (to PCH International) in 2015 after a failed strategy reset.
lesson: Chasing growth-at-all-costs on a flash-sale model without proven repeat-purchase economics leads to a valuation collapse when funding tightens.
source: TechCrunch, Fast Company coverage of Fab.com collapse 2013-2015

name: Fable Studio
summary: Pioneered 'neural storytelling' and GPT-3-powered interactive VR characters with Wolves in the Walls, then repeatedly pivoted through 'virtual beings' before abandoning interactive gaming entirely to launch Showrunner, an AI-generated animated-TV platform.
lesson: Multiple strategic pivots away from its original AI-game-character thesis signal the initial product never found a scalable audience, even though the underlying AI-character technology eventually found traction in a different medium (TV).

name: Fabric
summary: Israeli/US warehouse micro-fulfillment robotics (ASRS) company that laid off 40% of ~300 staff in 2022 while pivoting from an operated-service model to a software/hardware platform model, then cut another 15% in 2024.
lesson: Repeated years of deep layoffs plus a fundamental business-model pivot signal a company still searching for product-market fit.

name: Fabric (CommonSense Robotics)
summary: Israeli micro-fulfillment robotics company (rebranded from CommonSense Robotics) that automated small urban warehouses for rapid grocery delivery. Cut 40% of staff in 2022 while pivoting from an operated-network model to a software/hardware platform sale, then cut another 15% in 2024 as the rapid-delivery thesis cooled.
lesson: Building and operating your own physical fulfillment network is far more capital-intensive than licensing the software — a mid-flight business-model pivot signals the original model's unit economics never worked.

name: Fabric (formerly CommonSense Robotics)
summary: Micro-fulfillment robotics company that built small automated warehouses using AI-optimized robots to pick online grocery orders in under an hour, rebranding from CommonSense Robotics to Fabric in 2020. Struggled to land enough large grocery partners to justify its capital-intensive infrastructure, laid off staff repeatedly, and sold off assets/wound down its core robotics business by 2023.
lesson: Micro-fulfillment robotics requires deep-pocketed grocery partners willing to commit capital for years before ROI — a startup betting its balance sheet on that adoption curve runs out of runway first.
source: Grocery Dive, trade press coverage of CommonSense Robotics/Fabric rebrand and later struggles, 2019-2023

name: FaceFirst
summary: Retail-security facial-recognition vendor faced a wave of Illinois BIPA class-action lawsuits and reputational damage after a major retail customer's use of its technology drew media scrutiny, contributing to a sharp contraction in its retail customer base through the early 2020s.
lesson: Selling facial recognition into consumer-facing retail environments carries acute biometric-privacy litigation risk that can outweigh the revenue from any single large account.
source: Trade and legal press coverage of BIPA litigation involving FaceFirst retail deployments

name: Factmata
summary: London-based AI startup founded by Dhruv Ghulati aiming to detect fake news and toxic/harmful content for brand safety and misinformation clients. Struggled to convince major platforms to integrate its tech as Google and Facebook built competing in-house tools, and ran out of runway; sold in a distressed deal to Cision in late 2022.
lesson: A thin capital base ($4M total) was not enough to compete once the platforms it depended on for distribution built the same capability internally, showing the danger of relying on Big Tech as your primary customer and biggest potential competitor at once.

name: Fair.com
summary: Flexible car-subscription and leasing startup Fair laid off most of its staff and sold assets in 2021 after its capital-intensive vehicle-holding model proved unsustainable.
lesson: Owning and depreciating a large vehicle fleet to offer flexible subscriptions requires balance-sheet strength that most startups lack.
source: TechCrunch, May 2021, Fair layoffs and restructuring

name: Fakespot
summary: AI startup that used NLP to detect fake product reviews and untrustworthy sellers on Amazon and other marketplaces. Acquired by Mozilla in 2023 and integrated into Firefox as 'Review Checker,' but Mozilla shut the service down in 2025, saying recent acquisitions like Fakespot and Pocket 'didn't fit a model we could sustain.'
lesson: An acquisition is not always a safe landing: when the acquirer's own strategy shifts, an integrated product can be discontinued regardless of how well it served users, underscoring the risk of depending on a single corporate parent for survival.

name: Fancy
summary: Social commerce and product-discovery retail platform that let users curate shoppable collections shut down in 2018 after years of declining engagement and failure to build a sustainable retail-transaction business.
lesson: Social product-discovery platforms need a durable transaction and retention model, not just aesthetic appeal, to justify large funding rounds.

name: Faraday Future
summary: China-backed luxury EV startup that promised to rival Tesla but has produced only a tiny number of its FF 91 vehicles amid years of near-bankruptcy, executive turnover, and fraud allegations from the SEC.
lesson: Grandiose promises without manufacturing discipline erode investor and consumer trust long before a product ships at scale.

name: Farfetch
summary: Luxury fashion marketplace whose platform relied heavily on AI-driven personalization, search and inventory matching across thousands of boutiques and brands. Came within hours of bankruptcy in December 2023 due to unsustainable cash burn and an over-leveraged expansion strategy (including the ill-fated New Guards Group acquisition); rescued via an emergency deal that wiped out equity holders, with South Korea's Coupang acquiring the core business in 2024.
lesson: Aggressive M&A-fueled expansion (New Guards Group, off-price DTC brands) diluted focus and burned cash faster than the core marketplace's AI-personalization advantage could offset.
source: Reuters, Bloomberg coverage of Farfetch near-collapse and Coupang rescue deal, December 2023-2024

name: Farmcrowdy
summary: Nigerian agtech marketplace Farmcrowdy, once Africa's first Y Combinator-backed agtech startup, laid off most staff and scaled back sharply in 2020 after its crowdfunding-for-farms model failed to scale.
lesson: Agricultural crowdfunding models depend on managing thousands of smallholder farmer relationships, which is far more operationally complex than the fintech layer alone.

name: Farmers Edge
summary: Canadian precision-agriculture data company Farmers Edge, which went public in 2021, saw its stock collapse and filed for creditor protection under CCAA in 2024.
lesson: Going public does not resolve a data-services business model that hasn't proven it can convert acreage under contract into profit.
source: BNN Bloomberg, Farmers Edge CCAA filing 2024

name: Farmigo
summary: Online farmers-market and CSA delivery marketplace Farmigo shut down its consumer marketplace business in 2017 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Local food delivery marketplaces face brutal last-mile logistics costs that are hard to offset with typical grocery margins.

name: Farmshare Inc.
summary: Community-supported agriculture management platform Farmshare shut down around 2019 after failing to build a sustainable subscription revenue base among small farms.
lesson: Software serving small, low-budget farm operations often cannot generate enough per-customer revenue to sustain a dedicated venture-backed company.

name: Farmstead
summary: AI-powered online grocery delivery startup Farmstead shut down in 2021 after failing to raise a follow-on funding round.
lesson: Grocery delivery startups without differentiated logistics advantages struggle to raise follow-on capital in a crowded space.

name: FarmWise Labs
summary: Autonomous weeding-robot startup FarmWise laid off a significant portion of its staff in 2023 as it struggled to scale robot deployment profitably across specialty-crop farms.
lesson: Field robotics for niche specialty crops face a smaller addressable market that limits the scale needed to spread hardware R&D costs.

name: Fast
summary: One-click checkout startup for ecommerce sites shut down abruptly in 2022 after burning through funding faster than it could sign up merchants, despite a high-profile launch.
lesson: Infrastructure startups need real merchant adoption metrics, not just hype, before scaling spend.

name: Fast (Fast.co)
summary: One-click checkout startup that shut down abruptly in April 2022 despite recent large fundraising, after reports surfaced of minimal revenue, poor merchant adoption, and unsustainable spending.
lesson: Impressive fundraising totals can mask an absence of real merchant demand for a product.

name: Fast Radius
summary: On-demand digital manufacturing company went public via SPAC then filed for bankruptcy after failing to control costs.
lesson: SPAC-funded manufacturing companies with heavy fixed costs need real revenue traction to survive as public companies.

name: Fathom Digital Manufacturing
summary: On-demand digital manufacturing (3D printing, CNC, injection molding) roll-up platform that went public via SPAC at $1.5B in 2021; revenue kept declining and it was taken private at a steep discount in 2024.
lesson: SPAC roll-ups of several smaller manufacturers can mask weak organic growth.

name: Fauna
summary: Serverless, globally-distributed database built by ex-Twitter engineers that shut down its commercial cloud service in 2025 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: A technically ambitious database can lose to simpler, cheaper alternatives even with strong engineering pedigree.

name: FaZe Clan
summary: The influencer-driven esports and content org went public via SPAC at a billion-dollar valuation, then saw its stock collapse over 90% amid heavy losses before going private.
lesson: A large creator and fan following doesn't automatically translate into a business with sustainable margins.

name: Federated Media Publishing
summary: An early blog-advertising network that represented influential bloggers for brand deals wound down operations around 2015 as native advertising shifted to in-house platform tools.
lesson: Ad-network intermediaries between advertisers and independent creators get squeezed out once platforms build direct monetization tools.

name: Federated Wireless
summary: Spectrum-sharing and private wireless network platform using AI-based Spectrum Access System technology to manage CBRS shared spectrum for enterprises and carriers. Underwent significant layoffs and business retrenchment in 2023 as CBRS private-network adoption grew more slowly than projected.
lesson: Even a first-mover position in a novel regulatory spectrum-sharing framework (CBRS) couldn't offset slower-than-expected enterprise private-network adoption curves.
source: Fierce Network and trade press coverage of Federated Wireless layoffs, 2023

name: FedML / TensorOpera
summary: FedML launched as an open-source and commercial federated-learning platform for training AI models across distributed data silos. After roughly two years, founder Salman Avestimehr rebranded the company to TensorOpera in 2024, broadening it into a general generative-AI training/deployment platform — an implicit acknowledgment that federated learning alone was too narrow a wedge to build a venture-scale business around.
lesson: Federated learning as a standalone product category has repeatedly proven too narrow to anchor a large company; multiple ventures (FedML, Cape Privacy) have had to broaden into general privacy/AI infrastructure plays to keep raising capital and growing revenue.

name: FedNat Holding Company
summary: Publicly traded Florida homeowners insurer ceased writing new policies and had its primary subsidiary placed into liquidation in 2023 after years of hurricane losses and litigation costs eroded its capital base.
lesson: Rising reinsurance costs and litigation exposure can force even long-established regional carriers into insolvency.

name: Fei Protocol / Tribe DAO
summary: Algorithmic stablecoin protocol suffered an $80M hack in 2022 and, after a governance vote, the Tribe DAO chose to shut down and return remaining treasury funds to holders.
lesson: A major smart-contract hack combined with governance paralysis can be a faster death sentence than slow financial decline.
source: Fei/Tribe DAO governance shutdown vote, September 2022

name: Fernish
summary: Furniture rental startup for apartment dwellers shut down in 2022 after failing to raise a new funding round amid tightening venture capital.
lesson: Asset-heavy rental commerce models need continuous capital access that dries up quickly in downturns.

name: Fetchr
summary: Middle East logistics and delivery marketplace that ceased operations around 2020 after running out of cash amid intensifying regional competition.
lesson: Regional logistics marketplaces need durable cost advantages, not just funding, to survive competition from larger regional players.
source: MENA tech press coverage of Fetchr's 2020 shutdown

name: Fidor Bank
summary: Pioneering German digital bank was acquired by France's BPCE Group in 2016, and BPCE announced a full wind-down of Fidor Bank's German banking operations in 2023.
lesson: Being an early digital-banking pioneer does not guarantee long-term survival once an acquirer decides the business no longer fits its strategy.

name: Field Trip Health
summary: A network of ketamine-assisted psychotherapy clinics that went public in 2020, Field Trip Health sold off its clinic assets and effectively wound down after its psychedelic drug-development spinout failed to progress and cash ran out.
lesson: Brick-and-mortar clinical care businesses layered with speculative drug-development bets carry two capital-intensive burn profiles at once.

name: Fifth Domain
summary: Fifth Domain built AI-driven, gamified cyber-skills training platforms for the Australian government and defense workforce, positioned as a national champion in sovereign cyber capability. The company collapsed into administration in 2023 after failing to convert government enthusiasm and grant support into a sustainable commercial base.
lesson: Government grants and political enthusiasm for 'sovereign capability' startups can mask the absence of a real recurring-revenue business underneath.
source: general Australian press reporting on Fifth Domain administration 2023

name: Fifth Season
summary: Pittsburgh-based robotic vertical farming startup Fifth Season abruptly shut down operations in late 2022, laying off its entire staff.
lesson: Automation-heavy vertical farms still face the same energy-cost economics that sink less automated peers.
source: AgFunder News, Fifth Season shutdown coverage 2022

name: FinanceFox
summary: Swiss digital insurance folder app that let users manage all their policies in one place shut down its consumer service in 2021 after pivoting unsuccessfully to an enterprise licensing model.
lesson: A policy-management app without a brokerage or underwriting revenue engine has a structurally weak business model.

name: Finless Foods
summary: Finless Foods was developing cultivated bluefin tuna, but it laid off the vast majority of its staff in 2023 as investment in cultivated seafood collapsed well before regulatory approval was reached.
lesson: Companies pursuing multi-year regulatory pathways need capital reserves that outlast investor sentiment cycles.

name: FireEye Products (Trellix)
summary: FireEye's core network/endpoint security products business, once a market leader after its 2013 IPO, was sold off in 2021 for $1.2B to Symphony Technology Group and merged with McAfee Enterprise into Trellix, while the higher-margin Mandiant incident-response unit was separated and later sold to Google.
lesson: A pioneering security product can still lose its independent standing once growth slows and margins compress against nimbler cloud-native rivals.

name: Firefly Space Systems (original entity)
summary: Small-launch rocket startup lost a key investor after a lawsuit from Virgin Galactic over an alleged stolen engine design and filed for bankruptcy in 2017; assets were bought out of bankruptcy and relaunched as Firefly Aerospace.
lesson: IP disputes with a well-funded competitor can sever a startup's funding lifeline overnight regardless of the underlying merits.

name: First Republic Bank
summary: Private bank serving high-net-worth clients that was seized by regulators and sold to JPMorgan in May 2023 after a wave of uninsured deposit withdrawals exposed large unrealized losses on low-rate mortgages.
lesson: Long-duration, low-yield assets funded by short-term deposits become a solvency risk once interest rates rise sharply.

name: Fishtree
summary: Personalized-learning platform that used AI to recommend curriculum content to K-12 teachers; ceased operations after failing to convert pilots into sustained district contracts.
lesson: K-12 pilots that impress teachers do not automatically convert to district-wide paid deployments; procurement complexity kills many promising adaptive-learning tools.

name: Fisker Automotive
summary: Luxury plug-in hybrid maker, backed by a $529M DOE loan, filed for bankruptcy after battery supplier A123's collapse and recalls.
lesson: Startups building complex hardware are exposed to their suppliers' failures, not just their own execution.

name: Fisker Automotive (1st company)
summary: Henrik Fisker's first EV company built the Karma plug-in hybrid but collapsed into bankruptcy in 2013 after battery supplier A123's bankruptcy, manufacturing delays, and a government loan default.
lesson: Overreliance on a single battery supplier creates existential single-point-of-failure risk for an EV startup.

name: Fisker Inc
summary: EV maker of the Fisker Ocean SUV filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2024 after quality complaints, recalls, and inability to scale production or secure additional financing.
lesson: Automotive manufacturing requires operational discipline and capital reserves far beyond what most software-style fundraising cycles provide.
source: Reuters, June 2024 Fisker Chapter 11 filing coverage

name: Fisker Inc. (2nd company)
summary: Henrik Fisker's second EV venture launched the Ocean SUV but filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2024 after severe production quality issues, plunging sales, and inability to raise further capital.
lesson: Rushing a complex vehicle to market without a mature manufacturing and quality process leads to a death spiral of recalls and returns.

name: Fitness First (UK)
funding: Private equity owned (BC Partners, later Oaktree)
summary: Once Europe's largest gym chain, Fitness First underwent a debt restructuring in 2012 after private-equity leverage left it unable to service its debt amid falling membership.
lesson: Aggressive private-equity leverage on a physical fitness chain can force restructuring even without a single external shock.

name: Fitocracy
summary: Gamified fitness social network Fitocracy shut down its consumer app in 2018 after failing to convert an engaged early community into a sustainable business.
lesson: Strong early community engagement does not guarantee a viable monetization path for a fitness social app.

name: Fitplan
summary: Celebrity-trainer workout app Fitplan wound down operations in 2023 after failing to sustain subscriber growth against larger fitness content platforms.
lesson: A content-licensing model built around individual trainer personalities can struggle to scale against platforms with broader content libraries.

name: Fits.me
summary: Estonian virtual fitting-room startup using AI-driven robotic mannequins and body-shape modeling to predict apparel fit online, with clients like Thomas Pink and QVC. Acquired by Rakuten in 2015 to run as a standalone unit, but Rakuten later folded the technology into its own platform and discontinued Fits.me as an independent product.
lesson: "Standalone business" promises made at acquisition time are frequently reversed once the parent's strategic priorities shift — founders should plan for technology absorption, not independence.
source: TechCrunch, Retail Dive, "Rakuten buys virtual fitting room company Fits.Me", 2015

name: Five AI
summary: UK robotaxi startup that pivoted from operating its own fleet to selling B2B autonomy software and safety-assurance tooling before putting itself up for sale. Acquired by Bosch in April 2022 for undisclosed terms after failing to secure independent funding to scale.
lesson: A late pivot from consumer robotaxi ambitions to enterprise tooling can salvage technology and talent, but rarely returns capital to early investors at the hoped-for multiple.

name: Flickr
summary: Pioneering photo-sharing site that declined for years under Yahoo's neglect before being sold off to SmugMug in 2018 in a much-diminished state.
lesson: A once-dominant product can lose all relevance if an acquirer starves it of investment for years.
source: The Verge, Flickr sale to SmugMug 2018

name: FlightCar
summary: Airport car-sharing startup letting travelers rent out their cars while away that shut down in 2017 amid insurance disputes, lawsuits, and unsustainable operating losses.
lesson: Insurance and liability complexity in peer-to-peer vehicle sharing can create legal exposure that outpaces revenue.

name: Fliptop
summary: Fliptop built an early predictive lead-scoring platform that used machine learning on CRM and marketing-automation data to rank prospects. The company was unable to differentiate against a wave of similarly-positioned predictive-scoring startups (Infer, Lattice Engines, EverString) and sold its technology assets in 2016, effectively shutting down.
lesson: Predictive lead-scoring became commoditized quickly once a dozen well-funded startups launched near-identical products within a few years of each other; differentiation collapsed to price and distribution.

name: Flirtey
summary: Drone-delivery pioneer that conducted some of the first FAA-approved delivery flights in the US ceased most commercial operations by 2022 as larger-funded rivals like Zipline and Wing captured the emerging market.
lesson: Being first to prove regulatory feasibility doesn't guarantee commercial survival against better-capitalized later entrants.
source: Industry press coverage of Flirtey's operational wind-down

name: FloydHub
summary: Early 'Heroku for deep learning' cloud training platform that let ML engineers train models without managing infrastructure. Shut down its cloud service in 2021, citing inability to reach sustainable scale against larger, better-capitalized cloud and MLOps competitors.
lesson: Being early to a good idea (simplified cloud ML training) is not enough if larger clouds and well-funded competitors can replicate the value proposition with far more capital.

name: Fluidic Energy (NantEnergy)
summary: Fluidic Energy (later rebranded NantEnergy) built zinc-air batteries for microgrids in emerging markets but wound down after failing to reach sustainable commercial scale despite heavy investment from Patrick Soon-Shiong.
lesson: Emerging-market microgrid battery deployments face slow sales cycles that can outlast even well-funded patient capital.

name: Flywheel Sports
summary: Boutique indoor cycling chain Flywheel Sports permanently closed all studios and shut its at-home bike business in 2020 amid competition from Peloton and pandemic closures.
lesson: Boutique studio chains with high fixed costs are highly vulnerable to a well-funded at-home competitor and demand shocks.

name: Forethought
summary: Built generative AI customer-support agents (Solve, Assist) and raised a strong Series C in 2021, but cut roughly 30% of staff in 2023 amid the broader SaaS/AI funding contraction and intensifying competition from well-capitalized rivals like Intercom Fin and Sierra.
lesson: Being an early GenAI customer-support agent mover doesn't insulate against a crowded field once every major CX platform ships its own native AI agent.

name: Forever 21
summary: Fast-fashion giant filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in September 2019 after aggressive global overexpansion left it with too many oversized stores as mall traffic and fast-fashion competition (Zara, H&M, Shein) intensified.
lesson: Overexpansion into large-format mall leases becomes a fatal liability once shopping habits and competitors shift online.
source: Wall Street Journal, Reuters coverage of Forever 21 Chapter 11, 2019

name: Forge Global
summary: Private-company secondary share marketplace went public via SPAC in 2022 but its stock fell over 90% as trading volumes and late-stage valuations declined sharply.
lesson: Marketplaces tied to speculative asset trading volumes suffer badly when the broader risk appetite cools.

name: Forto
summary: German digital freight forwarder (formerly FreightHub) that cut roughly half its workforce and restructured operations in 2023 after aggressive expansion outpaced sustainable revenue.
lesson: Rapid geographic expansion in freight forwarding without matching operational efficiency leads to painful restructuring when funding tightens.

name: Forto (FreightHub)
summary: Berlin digital freight forwarder (rebranded from FreightHub) that reached a $2.1B valuation on a $250M Series D in 2022, then endured repeated rounds of layoffs in 2022 and 2023 (roughly 10%, then about one-third of staff — 200 people) as freight rates collapsed, ultimately exploring a sale or merger.
lesson: Peak-cycle unicorn valuations for digital freight forwarders proved unsustainable once shipping rates normalized — Forto's cascading layoffs and exploration of a sale show how quickly a $2B+ valuation can become a survival negotiation.
source: The Loadstar, trans.info, Munich Eye, PitchBook (2022-2024)

name: ForUsAll
summary: 401(k) provider for small businesses drew a Department of Labor lawsuit over a partnership letting participants allocate retirement savings into cryptocurrency, damaging its enterprise reputation.
lesson: Retirement plan providers face outsized regulatory risk when offering speculative investment options to plan participants.

name: Forward (Forward Health)
summary: Backed by Adrian Aoun and large venture rounds, Forward built AI-powered primary care clinics and later self-serve health 'CarePods,' but could never make the capital-intensive physical clinic model profitable and abruptly ceased all operations in 2025.
lesson: Physical, hardware-heavy healthcare delivery models require a much longer and costlier path to unit economics than most software-only AI startups.
source: TechCrunch and Fierce Healthcare shutdown coverage (2025)

name: Forward Health
summary: Concierge primary-care startup founded by ex-Google exec Adrian Aoun built high-tech physical clinics and later automated 'CarePods'; it shut down operations after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: High-touch, capital-intensive physical clinics are extremely expensive to scale nationally.

name: Fove
summary: Fove built the first commercially available eye-tracking VR headset via Kickstarter, but failed to scale beyond a niche developer audience before quietly ceasing consumer hardware sales.
lesson: Introducing an advanced feature (eye tracking) years before mainstream headsets adopted it left too small a market to sustain a standalone hardware company.
source: Road to VR reporting on Fove headset and company trajectory

name: Franchise Group (Pet Supplies Plus parent)
funding: Public company (NASDAQ, later delisted); leveraged buyout structure
summary: Parent holding company of pet retail chain Pet Supplies Plus that filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in November 2024 under heavy debt load, emerging in mid-2025 only after Pet Supplies Plus was sold off to a new owner.
lesson: Roll-up holding companies that load acquired retail chains with leveraged-buyout debt can drag even a healthy underlying brand into bankruptcy when corporate-level obligations come due.
source: TheStreet; Yahoo Finance reporting on Franchise Group Chapter 11, November 2024

name: Frank
summary: Financial-aid application startup sold to JPMorgan Chase for $175M in 2021; JPMorgan later sued founder Charlie Javice alleging she fabricated millions of fake user accounts to inflate the sale price.
lesson: Acquirers must independently verify user metrics rather than relying on target-provided data during diligence.

name: Frank And Oak
summary: Montreal-based menswear subscription and e-commerce brand filed for bankruptcy protection in 2018 after overexpanding into retail stores, and was later acquired out of restructuring by La Maison Simons.
lesson: Subscription-box brands that pivot into costly brick-and-mortar retail can outrun their cash runway.
source: Retail Dive, Fashion Network coverage of Frank And Oak bankruptcy 2018

name: Freespira
summary: Freespira developed an FDA-cleared home-based breathing biofeedback device for panic disorder and PTSD, but struggled with reimbursement and adoption before ceasing meaningful operations by 2023.
lesson: Device-based digital therapeutics face a doubly hard commercialization path, needing both device manufacturing scale and clinical reimbursement.

name: Freetrade
summary: UK commission-free trading app closed its EU operations and took a steep down-round valuation cut after failing to reach profitability amid intense competition.
lesson: Commission-free trading apps need massive scale or ancillary revenue to survive against better-capitalized rivals.

name: FreeWire Technologies
summary: FreeWire Technologies made battery-integrated EV fast chargers designed to avoid costly grid upgrades but underwent major layoffs in 2024 amid a broader EV charging slowdown and funding difficulties.
lesson: Clever engineering to sidestep grid infrastructure costs still needs matching EV adoption timing to sustain the business.

name: Freight Farms
summary: Container-farm maker Freight Farms, a pioneer of shipping-container hydroponic farms, ceased operations in 2024 after failing to raise further financing.
lesson: Small-format containerized farming struggles to reach the scale economics needed to compete with larger indoor operators.

name: FreightHub
summary: Berlin-based digital freight forwarder that merged with competitor Forto in 2019 after struggling to scale independently in the crowded European freight-tech market.
lesson: Undifferentiated freight-forwarding platforms often end up as consolidation targets rather than standalone winners.

name: Freightos
summary: Israeli freight-booking marketplace using AI-driven rate comparison across ocean, air and land freight, which IPO'd on Nasdaq in January 2023. Stock fell over 30% after founder Zvi Schreiber stepped down from the board, the company has never turned a profit, and it announced cutting up to 15% of its workforce in 2025-2026 to reach adjusted profitability.
lesson: Going public does not solve a marketplace's fundamental unprofitability — years after IPO, Freightos was still cutting headcount to reach breakeven, with founder departure adding further uncertainty for public investors.
source: Yahoo Finance, The Loadstar, Journal of Commerce (2025-2026)

name: Freshly
summary: Freshly sold prepared, ready-to-heat healthy meals by mail and was acquired by Nestlé in 2020, but Nestlé shut the business down in 2023 after it failed to become profitable.
lesson: A large strategic acquisition does not guarantee survival if the acquired unit's core economics never improve.

name: Freshly Picked meal delivery (Territory Foods)
summary: Territory Foods delivered chef-prepared, dietitian-approved meals sourced from local chefs across US metros, but scaled back significantly and effectively wound down by 2023 amid weak unit economics in prepared-meal delivery.
lesson: Distributed local-chef sourcing adds supply-chain complexity that undermines the cost advantages meal delivery needs to survive.

name: Freshworks Studio (Farmshelf)
summary: Small-scale in-store and in-restaurant vertical farming unit maker Farmshelf ceased independent operations around 2022 after failing to scale beyond niche hospitality installations.
lesson: Micro-scale vertical farming units serving niche hospitality customers rarely generate enough volume to sustain a standalone hardware company.

name: FREYR Battery (now T1 Energy)
summary: Norwegian-founded FREYR Battery cancelled its planned $2.6B Georgia battery gigafactory and rebranded as T1 Energy in 2025, abandoning its original battery cell manufacturing mission to pivot to solar module production.
lesson: When battery cell economics and demand don't materialize on schedule, even well-capitalized gigafactory plans can be abandoned entirely.

name: Friday Health Plans
summary: Tech-enabled ACA marketplace health insurance startup expanded into multiple states quickly, then became insolvent in 2023, with regulators in several states placing it into liquidation.
lesson: Underpricing insurance risk while expanding into new state markets can trigger simultaneous, catastrophic losses.

name: FRIDAY Insurance
summary: German digital car insurer backed by Baloise ceased writing new retail auto policies in 2023 and sold its existing policy book after concluding the direct-to-consumer underwriting model could not be made profitable.
lesson: A strong balance-sheet backer can fund years of underwriting losses but won't indefinitely subsidize a structurally unprofitable model.

name: Frontback
summary: French dual-camera photo-sharing app that gained early buzz but shut down in 2016 after failing to grow its user base sustainably.
lesson: Photo-sharing apps entering a market already dominated by Instagram face an uphill network-effects battle.

name: FTX
summary: Once the world's second-largest crypto exchange, FTX collapsed in November 2022 after Alameda Research's balance sheet was exposed as built on FTX's own illiquid token.
lesson: Commingling customer funds with a trading affiliate is fraud, not a business model.

name: Full Stack Modular
summary: Modular high-rise construction company, successor to the Forest City modular tower program in Brooklyn, that wound down after cost overruns and quality issues on its flagship project.
lesson: Unproven modular manufacturing techniques applied to complex high-rise projects can produce cost overruns that erase the promised savings.
source: The Real Deal, New York YIMBY coverage of modular tower issues

name: Functionize
summary: Functionize raised significant venture funding to build an AI-powered automated software-testing platform but faced prolonged growth struggles as more narrowly focused, faster-moving AI testing and coding-agent startups captured enterprise budgets after 2023.
lesson: Raising large rounds early does not guarantee durable advantage; a testing-automation incumbent can be squeezed by both legacy QA vendors and newer, more nimble AI-native entrants simultaneously.

name: Fundbox
funding: ~$500M+ (equity and debt, including General Catalyst, Khosla Ventures)
summary: AI-driven small business lender using real-time cash-flow and invoice data to automate credit decisions. Cut roughly 70% of staff in 2023 amid deteriorating small-business credit performance and difficulty raising further debt facilities, leaving the company a shell of its former scale though not formally shut down.
lesson: AI credit models trained through a benign rate environment can be tested severely once rates and default rates rise; heavy reliance on warehouse debt facilities amplifies the damage when underwriting performance slips.

name: Funding Circle (US consumer/SMB business)
funding: IPO'd on LSE in 2018 raising ~£300M; total prior VC funding ~$400M
summary: UK-founded SME lending marketplace using data-driven credit models to price loans; expanded into the US, Germany and the Netherlands. Wound down its US small-business lending operations in 2022 after its stock had fallen over 90% from IPO price, refocusing on its core UK market.
lesson: Expanding an algorithmic lending model into new geographies faster than local credit performance could be validated led to a costly retreat once public-market scrutiny exposed weak unit economics abroad.
source: Financial Times, TechCrunch coverage of Funding Circle US wind-down (2022)

name: FutureAdvisor
funding: ~$21.5M before acquisition by BlackRock in 2015 for ~$150M
summary: Algorithm-driven robo-advisor providing automated portfolio management and tax-loss harvesting. Acquired by BlackRock in 2015 to power white-label robo-advisory for banks and wealth managers; BlackRock discontinued the direct-to-consumer FutureAdvisor product in 2018-2019 to focus on its B2B Aladdin-based offerings.
lesson: A strong strategic acquisition by a major asset manager can still end with the original consumer-facing AI product being sunset once the acquirer repositions the technology purely as B2B infrastructure.
source: Industry press coverage of BlackRock discontinuing FutureAdvisor consumer app (2018-2019)

name: Fuzzy Pet Health
summary: San Francisco pet telehealth startup offering 24/7 vet chat and ship-to-home prescriptions that abruptly shut down in June 2023 amid allegations of financial mismanagement, unpaid vendors and staff, and a settled hostile-workplace lawsuit against its CEO.
lesson: Heavy venture funding cannot compensate for poor governance; mismanagement and leadership dysfunction can sink a well-capitalized telehealth startup even with a real product-market need.

name: Fyde
summary: Zero-trust remote access startup was quietly acquired by Barracuda Networks in 2019 in a small, largely unpublicized deal after a short independent run.
lesson: Being early to the zero-trust access category doesn't guarantee independent survival if funding runs out before reaching scale.

name: Fyodor Biotechnologies
summary: Wellness-diagnostics startup Fyodor Biotechnologies failed to gain sufficient commercial traction for its at-home diagnostic products and wound down operations.
lesson: Diagnostic-adjacent wellness products face slower regulatory and adoption timelines that can exhaust startup runway.

name: Fyre Media (Fyre Festival)
summary: Luxury music festival venture collapsed spectacularly when the promised Bahamas festival turned out to be built on fraudulent marketing, leading to the founder's imprisonment.
lesson: Marketing hype divorced from operational reality in event/travel experiences can constitute outright fraud, not just failure.

name: Gaana
summary: One of India's earliest major music streaming apps that laid off most of its staff and scaled down operations dramatically in 2024 after years of losses.
lesson: Being an early market leader in streaming is not enough if free-tier economics never convert to sustainable paid conversion.

name: GameFly (rental business)
summary: The Netflix-style DVD and game-disc-by-mail rental pioneer wound down its physical rental business in 2019 as digital distribution and downloads made mail-order rental obsolete.
lesson: A rental logistics business built around physical media has no defense once its industry moves natively to digital distribution.

name: GameOn
summary: AI chatbot delivering personalized sports and esports fan engagement (scores, trivia, highlights) for teams like the NBA, Yankees and Arsenal. Functioning product with real customer traction still could not generate sustainable revenue (never exceeding $1M/year), and its founder was later indicted for allegedly fabricating financial statements to investors.
lesson: A working AI product with real enterprise logos is not sufficient if pricing and contract terms never scale into real revenue; when growth numbers don't match reality, some founders resort to fabricating traction rather than confronting the business-model problem.

name: GapJumpers
summary: "Blind auditions" hiring platform that stripped resumes of identifying details and had candidates complete skills challenges instead, with early customers like Dolby and Pivotal Labs; after an Inc.com feature calling it a threat to the "$3 billion resume industry," the company failed to scale beyond a niche following and went largely inactive.
lesson: Being profiled as a category-killer in the press doesn't guarantee employers will actually change deep-rooted resume-screening habits at scale.
source: Inc.com, Forbes HR Council coverage of blind hiring

name: Garden.com
summary: Online gardening supplies retailer shut down in 2001 after the dot-com downturn dried up funding for its niche, low-margin category.
lesson: Niche ecommerce categories with thin margins are especially exposed when capital markets tighten.

name: Gavelytics
summary: Litigation analytics startup providing judge- and court-level insights for California litigators. On June 29, 2022, founder and CEO Rick Merrill notified customers and employees the company would close its doors the next day, ending operations abruptly rather than securing an acquisition.
lesson: Regional litigation-analytics products face a structurally small addressable market (one state's litigators); without an acquisition offer lined up before cash runs out, an abrupt shutdown can happen with almost no notice.

name: Gawker Media
summary: A network of irreverent blogs (Gawker, Deadspin, Jezebel, Gizmodo) built a large audience but was forced into bankruptcy after a $140M jury verdict in Hulk Hogan's Peter Thiel-funded invasion-of-privacy lawsuit.
lesson: A single unresolved legal liability, especially one bankrolled by a hostile billionaire, can bankrupt an otherwise profitable media company overnight.

name: GE Digital / Predix
summary: GE's ambitious industrial IoT software platform, meant to make GE a top-10 software company, was drastically scaled back and largely divested after 2018 amid huge losses.
lesson: An industrial conglomerate cannot force software-company economics onto a platform built primarily to serve its own hardware ecosystem.

name: General Magic
funding: Publicly traded (NASDAQ), raised $100M+ across its life
summary: Legendary Apple spin-out that pioneered mobile computing and later launched Portico, an early voice-activated personal assistant/voice portal service (2001), well ahead of its time; the company folded in 2002-2004 after failing to commercialize its visionary technology.
lesson: Being a decade ahead of the market with voice-assistant concepts meant the underlying wireless and speech infrastructure simply wasn't ready to support the vision commercially.

name: Generation Orbit Launch Services
summary: Air-launched small-satellite rocket developer (GOLauncher) shut down commercial launch operations in 2022 after a failed hypersonic test-vehicle flight and inability to secure further funding for its orbital vehicle.
lesson: Pivoting between hypersonic test-vehicle contracts and an orbital launch product split focus and capital too thin to succeed at either.

name: Genesis Global Capital
summary: Major institutional crypto lender owned by Digital Currency Group suspended withdrawals after the FTX collapse exposed its exposure and filed for Chapter 11.
