lesson: Institutional lenders are only as sound as the riskiest counterparty in their loan book.

name: Genesis Global Trading
summary: One of the largest crypto prime brokers and lenders filed for bankruptcy in January 2023 after exposure to Three Arrows Capital and FTX left it unable to meet withdrawal obligations to Gemini and other partners.
lesson: Wholesale lenders that don't match loan durations to counterparty risk can be brought down by a handful of bad loans.

name: Genies
summary: AI-driven avatar and digital-identity platform for celebrities and brands (backed by Bob Iger among others) reached unicorn valuation but underwent layoffs (reported ~15-20% of staff in 2023) as the broader avatar/metaverse market cooled and the company repeatedly repositioned its product between gaming, social avatars, and later AI companion/persona tools.
lesson: Raising at a peak metaverse-hype valuation created pressure to sustain growth expectations that cooled sharply once the broader avatar/metaverse market lost momentum, forcing costly repositioning.

name: Genius (Rap Genius)
summary: A crowdsourced lyrics-annotation platform raised significant venture funding at a media-company valuation but repeatedly laid off staff from 2023 onward after failing to build a business matching that valuation.
lesson: A community-driven content platform with huge engagement can still fail to convert that engagement into venture-scale revenue.

name: Genius Education Group (Jingrui/Elite Education)
summary: Chinese AI-supported premium tutoring and adaptive learning group that listed on the Hong Kong exchange in 2021; filed for bankruptcy-style liquidation in 2022 after China's for-profit tutoring ban destroyed its core revenue base within months of its IPO.
lesson: Going public right before a regulatory shock is a worst-case timing scenario — public investors and the company itself absorbed the full impact of the policy change simultaneously.
source: Reuters/HKEX filings on Genius Education Group liquidation 2022

name: Genocea Biosciences
summary: T-cell-directed herpes vaccine and later cancer immunotherapy programs failed to show sufficient efficacy, leading to bankruptcy after burning through hundreds of millions.
lesson: A differentiated immunology platform still needs a clinical winner within a finite cash runway.

name: Genome Medical
summary: Combined AI-assisted genetic risk triage with a nationwide telegenetics counseling network to help health systems and payers scale genomic medicine, but could not build a sustainable reimbursement model for genetic counseling services and ceased operations in 2024.
lesson: Services businesses layered on top of AI-driven risk stratification still need payers to reimburse the human service component, which can be the real bottleneck to scale.
source: Fierce Healthcare and GenomeWeb coverage of Genome Medical's 2024 closure

name: GenRocket
summary: Ojai, California-based GenRocket built a synthetic test-data generation platform used by banks, healthcare and insurance firms for software testing. Despite operating since 2012 and counting large enterprise customers, the company has raised only a reported $4.7M in over a decade, a strikingly small capital base for its category that signals persistent difficulty scaling beyond a niche testing-tools customer base.
lesson: Some synthetic-data niches (test data management) support durable but small businesses; staying capital-starved for over a decade while well-funded competitors (Tonic.ai, Delphix) consolidate the same market limits a company's ability to compete for larger enterprise deals.

name: Genvid Technologies
summary: Built technology for AI-augmented 'Massive Interactive Live Events' (MILEs), letting thousands of viewers simultaneously influence a live-streamed game world. Despite a $113M raise, the company underwent repeated rounds of layoffs and internal leadership turnover.
lesson: A technically ambitious, infrastructure-heavy new game format required both platform buy-in and mass audience behavior change simultaneously, a compounding go-to-market risk that large funding alone could not de-risk.
source: Glassdoor / LinkedIn layoff reports; Fast Company profile, 2020

name: Geofeedia
summary: Social media surveillance tool sold to police departments collapsed after an ACLU investigation revealed it was used to monitor Black Lives Matter activists, prompting platforms to cut off its data access.
lesson: Building a business entirely dependent on social platforms' data APIs is fragile if the use case violates those platforms' policies.

name: Geolitica (PredPol)
summary: PredPol (later renamed Geolitica) sold predictive-policing software to hundreds of U.S. police departments, claiming its algorithm could forecast where crime would occur. Investigations by The Markup found accuracy rates under 0.5% and systematic over-targeting of low-income Black and Latino neighborhoods. Facing mounting bans, lawsuits, and reputational damage, the company ceased independent operations in late 2023, with SoundThinking absorbing its engineering team and some IP but not its crime-prediction technology.
lesson: AI government tools that materially affect civil liberties face a much higher evidentiary bar; algorithmic bias findings can end municipal contracts and the business overnight.

name: Geometric Intelligence
summary: NYU-linked machine learning research lab acquired by Uber in 2016 to found Uber AI Labs. Uber shut down its AI research division entirely in 2020 as part of broader cost cuts during the pandemic.
lesson: Corporate AI research labs are often the first casualty of a parent company's belt-tightening, regardless of the underlying research quality.
source: VentureBeat, TechCrunch reporting on Uber AI Labs shutdown, 2020

name: German Bionic
summary: Maker of AI-powered 'Apogee' powered industrial exoskeletons for warehouse/logistics workers; filed for bankruptcy despite reportedly having a full order book.
lesson: A full order book doesn't guarantee survival if working-capital financing for hardware production dries up faster than orders convert to cash.

name: Get Satisfaction
summary: Community-based customer feedback and support platform acquired by Sprinklr in 2015, after which the standalone product was discontinued and customers migrated off the platform.
lesson: A differentiated community-support niche can still lose to broader all-in-one CX suites that bundle the same capability.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Sprinklr's 2015 acquisition of Get Satisfaction

name: Getaround
summary: Peer-to-peer car-sharing marketplace that went public via SPAC in 2022 at a rich valuation but saw its stock collapse over 99%, leading to a Nasdaq delisting in 2024.
lesson: A SPAC listing can mask unresolved unit-economics problems that eventually surface in public markets.

name: Getir
summary: Istanbul-founded rapid grocery delivery pioneer that expanded aggressively into the US, UK and Germany, acquiring Gorillas and FreshDirect, then retreated from all three markets in 2023.
lesson: Aggressive international expansion in quick-commerce outpaced the ability to reach profitability anywhere.

name: Getsurance
summary: German digital insurance broker targeting freelancers and gig workers with gadget and liability policies shut down in 2019 after failing to reach profitability in a crowded niche.
lesson: Niche insurance products for underserved segments still need enough addressable market to justify the acquisition spend.

name: Gfinity plc
summary: The British publicly listed esports company saw its share price collapse more than 95% from its 2014 listing amid repeated losses, forcing multiple restructurings and a much-diminished business.
lesson: Going public before an esports business model is proven exposes a company to punishing, permanent capital-market discipline.

name: Ghost Locomotion (Ghost Autonomy)
funding: ~$220M raised, incl. OpenAI Startup Fund investment
summary: Started as Ghost Locomotion selling a consumer highway-autopilot retrofit kit, then pivoted repeatedly toward crash-prevention and later multimodal LLM-based driving. Despite a late-2023 partnership and $5M investment from the OpenAI Startup Fund, shut down worldwide operations in April 2024, citing an uncertain path to profitability given the long-horizon investment autonomy still required.
lesson: Bolting a hot new technology trend onto a still-unsolved hard problem does not fix an underlying business that has already pivoted multiple times without finding product-market fit.

name: Gigaom
summary: A respected tech-industry news and research site abruptly shut down in 2015 after failing to raise further financing to cover mounting debts.
lesson: A trusted editorial brand can still collapse suddenly if working capital runs out faster than a financing round can close.

name: Gigster
summary: Software-development freelance/agency marketplace that collapsed around 2020 after mass layoffs and failure to deliver consistent project quality at scale.
lesson: Marketplaces for complex, high-touch services like custom software struggle to standardize quality without heavy internal project management.

name: Gild
summary: Data-science hiring platform that scored developers on code quality and online activity to predict on-the-job performance; despite a strong pitch and Series funding, it was acquired in 2016 not by an HR company but by hedge fund Citadel for internal use - ending its run as an independent HR-tech product.
lesson: An acquisition that pulls your technology out of the HR industry entirely is a sign the standalone hiring-software business never found its footing.

name: Gilt Groupe
funding: ~$236M; sold to Hudson's Bay for $250M (2016), down from a $1.1B peak valuation
summary: Pioneer of algorithmically curated flash-sale e-commerce, once valued over $1B, using data science to personalize daily limited-time luxury sales. Sold to Hudson's Bay in 2016 for less than what it had raised, and Hudson's Bay shut the brand down entirely in 2018 after folding it into Saks Off Fifth.
lesson: Algorithmic flash-sale curation was easily commoditized once every major retailer added its own personalized daily-deals feature, eliminating Gilt's original differentiation.
source: Trade press on Hudson's Bay's 2016 Gilt acquisition and 2018 shutdown

name: Ginger.io (early pivot)
summary: Ginger's original consumer mood-tracking and behavioral analytics app was discontinued around 2016 as the company pivoted entirely to enterprise on-demand behavioral health coaching, which later became the successful Ginger Health.
lesson: Sometimes a failed consumer product is the necessary detour to discover a much larger enterprise opportunity.

name: Ginkgo Bioworks
summary: Synthetic biology 'foundry' company combining automated lab robotics with AI/ML to design organisms and molecules for pharma, agriculture and industrial customers, and a serial acquirer of distressed AI-science peers (Zymergen assets, Reverie Labs). After going public via SPAC at roughly $15-17.5B, its stock fell more than 95% from peak, revenue guidance was slashed (a projected $628M cell-engineering business shrank to ~$175M), and the company cut at least 35% of its workforce (400+ jobs) in 2024 amid roughly $383M in first-half losses, later pivoting toward selling AI-enabled lab robots.
lesson: A platform-as-a-service model for synthetic biology (charging customers for foundry access) proved far less scalable and lucrative than SPAC-era projections assumed, and being a buyer of other distressed AI-science startups did not insulate the acquirer from the same underlying market problem.

name: GitDuck
summary: GitDuck built collaborative live-coding and pair-programming tools for developer teams; the product went inactive around 2022 as remote-collaboration needs shifted toward AI-native copilots instead of human-to-human live streaming tools.
lesson: Human-to-human collaboration tooling built just before the generative-AI wave was rapidly overtaken in relevance by AI-to-human pairing tools; timing relative to a major technology shift can invalidate an otherwise reasonable product thesis.

name: Glamsquad
summary: On-demand beauty services app (hair, makeup, manicures at home) shut down in 2020 after the COVID-19 pandemic eliminated demand for in-home beauty appointments and the gig-labor unit economics never reached profitability.
lesson: On-demand service marketplaces with high labor costs per transaction are especially vulnerable to demand shocks that hollow out volume.

name: Global Eagle Entertainment
funding: publicly traded, hundreds of millions in debt
summary: Satellite-based in-flight connectivity and entertainment provider filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in July 2020 after the COVID-19 pandemic collapsed airline traffic, its core customer base, on top of an already heavy debt load.
lesson: A satellite connectivity business tied to a single cyclical customer industry (aviation) has no hedge when that industry stops flying.

name: Globalstar (original entity)
summary: Satellite phone operator built by Loral and Qualcomm filed Chapter 11 in 2002 after mobile phone subscriptions came in far below projections, though the network and brand survived reorganization.
lesson: Satellite phone economics collapse quickly when cheap terrestrial cellular coverage expands faster than the satellite rollout.

name: GlowRoad
summary: Indian social-commerce reseller marketplace connecting small resellers with wholesale suppliers that scaled back operations significantly in 2023 amid funding constraints and heavy competition from larger platforms.
lesson: Reseller marketplaces built on thin per-order margins are vulnerable once larger platforms subsidize the same customer segment.
source: Indian tech press (Entrackr) coverage of GlowRoad's 2023 struggles

name: Glue Networks
summary: Network automation and orchestration software company (Gluware precursor entity) focused on automating configuration and provisioning of SD-WAN and branch networks. Ceased independent operations in 2017 after failing to reach sustainable scale, with remaining technology and talent dispersing to other automation vendors including Cisco.
lesson: Network automation orchestration layers needed either a strong channel partnership or direct incumbent backing to reach enterprise scale before running out of capital.
source: Trade press coverage of Glue Networks wind-down, 2017

name: GoBank
summary: Green Dot's mobile-first checking account brand was discontinued in 2018 as the company consolidated its banking products under other brands.
lesson: Large incumbents can retire a promising consumer sub-brand once portfolio consolidation looks more efficient.

name: GoBone (PulsePet)
summary: App-controlled robotic 'smartbone' dog toy funded via Kickstarter that shipped to backers but the company subsequently went quiet, with no further product updates or retail availability in following years.
lesson: Kickstarter-funded hardware toys often struggle to convert initial campaign hype into a sustainable retail business once fulfillment costs and support demands mount.
source: Kickstarter campaign page and subsequent product review coverage

name: GoHealth
summary: Medicare insurance marketplace went public via a $1.2B-plus IPO in 2020 but its stock collapsed over 95% amid high customer acquisition costs and regulatory scrutiny of Medicare Advantage marketing.
lesson: Lead-generation-heavy insurance brokers are exposed to acquisition cost inflation and increased regulatory oversight.

name: Gold's Gym
summary: Gold's Gym International filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2020, closing dozens of corporate-owned locations after pandemic-driven revenue loss.
lesson: Franchise-heavy gym brands can still face bankruptcy at the corporate level when owned locations can't cover fixed costs during a shock.

name: Golden Spike Company
summary: Startup announced plans to offer commercial crewed missions to the Moon using existing launch vehicles, but never secured a customer or the capital to begin hardware development and quietly dissolved.
lesson: Announcing an ambitious mission architecture without committed customers or funded hardware development is marketing, not a company.

name: Good Catch
summary: Good Catch (Gathered Foods) sold plant-based tuna and seafood alternatives at major retailers but significantly scaled back operations in 2023 as plant-based category sales growth slowed across the board.
lesson: Even retail-shelf presence cannot save a plant-based brand once overall category growth stalls and funding tightens.

name: Good Eggs
summary: Online local-food grocery delivery startup Good Eggs shut down its New York and Los Angeles operations in 2015 and scaled back sharply, with the broader business winding down further by 2019.
lesson: Expanding a low-margin perishable-goods delivery model to multiple cities simultaneously can outrun the operational maturity needed to sustain it.

name: Good Technology
summary: Mobile device and data security vendor withdrew a planned IPO after its valuation cratered, and was ultimately sold to BlackBerry in 2015 for $425M — far below the $1B+ it had once expected.
lesson: Delaying an IPO while market sentiment and competitive position deteriorate can permanently destroy the exit value investors were counting on.

name: goodcarbon
summary: Berlin-based goodcarbon built a B2B data platform and marketplace to help companies source and track high-quality carbon credits. It scaled back sharply as the voluntary carbon market slump reduced corporate offset budgets and buyer appetite for early-stage marketplace intermediaries.
lesson: Early movers in a nascent marketplace category are exposed to broad category-level demand shocks that no amount of individual execution can fully offset.
source: Sifted Scout coverage of European carbon accounting and offsets startups (2024)

name: GoodData
summary: Embedded analytics and BI platform that underwent major layoffs and a distressed sale/restructuring around 2023 after years of struggling to match cloud-native BI competitors.
lesson: Being an early mover in cloud BI is not enough if the product fails to keep pace with newer, more self-service analytics platforms.

name: Google Daydream
summary: Google's Daydream mobile VR platform and headset were discontinued in 2019 after just three years, as smartphone-based VR failed to gain lasting traction against standalone headsets.
lesson: Smartphone-tethered VR was quickly outcompeted by cheaper, more convenient standalone headsets like Oculus Go and Quest.

name: Google Glass (consumer and enterprise editions)
summary: Google Glass launched to consumers in 2013 to privacy backlash and was discontinued for consumers in 2015; the pivoted Enterprise Edition was also discontinued in 2023 after failing to reach broad adoption.
lesson: Wearable camera-equipped AR devices faced social and privacy backlash that even a full B2C-to-enterprise pivot could not fully overcome.
source: Google Glass Enterprise Edition discontinuation reporting, 2023

name: Google Jump
summary: Google's Jump VR camera rig and cloud stitching service for 360-degree video production was shut down in 2019 as demand for cinematic VR content production tools failed to materialize at scale.
lesson: Building production infrastructure for a content format before consumer demand is proven risks the tooling becoming obsolete before adoption occurs.

name: Google Stadia
summary: Google's cloud-gaming platform launched with heavy marketing but failed to build a large enough library or user base, leading Google to shut it down in 2023 and refund purchases.
lesson: Even a company with immense infrastructure and capital can misjudge whether the market is ready for a new distribution model.

name: Google Tilt Brush
summary: Google's Tilt Brush, a pioneering 3D VR painting app, was discontinued as a commercial product in 2021 and open-sourced, as Google deprioritized VR content investment.
lesson: Creative VR tools acclaimed by early adopters can still be shelved when the parent company's broader VR strategy shifts away from content investment.

name: Google+
summary: Google's multibillion-dollar bid to challenge Facebook never achieved meaningful organic engagement and was shut down in 2019 following a data-exposure bug.
lesson: Forcing adoption across a company's other products cannot manufacture genuine social engagement.

name: GoPro Karma
summary: GoPro's first consumer drone, Karma, was recalled shortly after launch in 2016 due to a power-loss defect, relaunched in 2017, and discontinued entirely in 2018 as GoPro exited the drone market amid DJI's dominance.
lesson: Entering an established hardware category late with an unreliable first product compounds competitive disadvantage.
source: TechCrunch coverage of GoPro's exit from the drone market, 2018

name: Gorillas
summary: Berlin rapid grocery delivery pioneer that scaled to dozens of cities in under two years before being acquired by rival Getir in a distressed down-round deal.
lesson: Hypergrowth in 10-minute grocery delivery outran the ability to reach profitable dark-store economics.

name: GoStudent
summary: Austrian tutoring unicorn that reached a $3B valuation but laid off roughly a third of staff across 2023-2024 amid a funding-environment slowdown and cash-burn concerns.
lesson: Rapid multi-country expansion funded by growth-stage capital is fragile when funding markets tighten.

name: Gotcha Mobility
summary: South Carolina-based micromobility and car-share operator serving smaller college towns that ceased operations in 2023 as funding dried up.
lesson: Serving smaller secondary markets doesn't insulate a mobility startup from the same capital constraints as big-city rivals.

name: Gothamist (original, DNAinfo era)
summary: A well-regarded New York City news and culture blog was acquired by Joe Ricketts' DNAinfo in 2017, then shut down alongside DNAinfo just months later after the newsroom unionized, before being revived by New York Public Radio in 2018.
lesson: Acquisition by an owner hostile to unionization can end a media brand's operation almost as quickly as the deal that created it.

name: Grabango
summary: Computer-vision cashierless checkout company competing directly with Amazon's Just Walk Out, aiming to retrofit existing grocery stores without gates or turnstiles. Shut down in October 2024 after failing to secure additional funding, joining a wave of failed checkout-free retail bets.
lesson: Computer-vision cashierless retrofits proved far more capital-intensive and operationally fragile at scale than the demos suggested, and grocery margins couldn't absorb the infrastructure cost.
source: CNBC, "Amazon cashierless tech competitor Grabango shutters", October 2024

name: Grantland
summary: Bill Simmons' ESPN-backed sports and pop-culture site built a devoted following but was shuttered after Simmons' contract with ESPN was not renewed.
lesson: A media brand built tightly around one star founder's voice is existentially exposed to that founder's relationship with the parent company.

name: Graphcore
summary: UK AI chip maker (IPU processors) that struggled to gain design wins against Nvidia, saw revenue far below projections and repeated layoffs, and was ultimately acquired by SoftBank in 2024 after failing to secure new funding or a public listing at its earlier valuation.
lesson: Technically impressive AI accelerator architecture is not sufficient without a software ecosystem and hyperscaler design wins to challenge Nvidia's dominance.
source: SoftBank acquisition of Graphcore announcement, July 2024

name: GreenSky
summary: Point-of-sale home-improvement lender acquired by Goldman Sachs in 2021 for $2.24B as part of its consumer banking push; Goldman sold it to Sixth Street in 2023 for roughly a quarter of that price as it retreated from consumer lending.
lesson: Even a well-established fintech can become a write-down when its acquirer's own strategic priorities change.

name: Grid.ai
summary: Cloud training platform built by the creators of PyTorch Lightning, offering managed large-scale model training. The standalone Grid.ai commercial product was discontinued around 2023 as the company pivoted its business fully toward the Lightning AI platform.
lesson: Popular open-source framework ownership (PyTorch Lightning) does not automatically translate into a successful adjacent commercial cloud product; the company had to pivot its go-to-market entirely.
source: Lightning AI rebrand and Grid.ai product sunset, 2023

name: Griddy
summary: Texas retail electricity startup Griddy sold power at wholesale prices to consumers but collapsed in 2021 when Winter Storm Uri sent prices to the market cap, saddling customers with thousands of dollars in bills and triggering its shutdown by regulators.
lesson: Passing raw wholesale price volatility directly to consumers without a cap or hedge is a business model one extreme weather event away from disaster.

name: Griswold Home Care
summary: One of the largest 1099-contractor home care franchise networks in the US; it shut down all California locations in 2016 after a Department of Labor ruling required home care workers to be classified as W-2 employees.
lesson: An entire home care business model built on contractor classification can become instantly unviable in a single state the moment labor law changes.

name: Gro Intelligence
summary: AI/data platform aggregating agricultural, weather, geological and commodities data to model global food and climate systems, named one of TIME's most influential companies. After a decade and over $125M raised, the company could not make payroll by February 2024, its CEO departed, it laid off 60% of staff, and it fully shut down by June 2024 amid an SEC investigation and employee lawsuits over labor law violations.
lesson: Selling data-derived climate/agriculture insight is a hard, low-margin consulting-like business unless it converts into scalable recurring software revenue; over-reliance on a handful of large clients left the company with no cushion when relationships soured.

name: Grokker
summary: Corporate wellness video platform Grokker ceased operations in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale amid competition from larger wellness benefit platforms.
lesson: A content-only wellness offering can be squeezed out by broader benefits platforms that bundle more services for employers.

name: Grooveshark
summary: Ad-supported streaming site that let users freely upload and share tracks; shut down in 2015 as part of a settlement with major record labels over copyright infringement.
lesson: Growth built on unlicensed content is not sustainable against well-resourced rights holders.

name: Grouparoo
summary: Open-source reverse ETL startup that wound down operations in 2023, with its team and technology joining Airbyte.
lesson: Being early to a hot category (reverse ETL) is not sufficient if better-funded competitors capture the market faster.

name: Groupon
summary: Daily deals pioneer IPO'd in 2011 at a multi-billion-dollar valuation but its stock lost over 99% of its value as the deals model proved unsustainable and merchants churned.
lesson: A viral growth model built on one-time deal customers rarely produces durable repeat revenue.

name: Grow (Grin + Yellow)
summary: Latin American bike and scooter-sharing giant formed by the merger of Brazil's Yellow and Mexico's Grin that shut down operations across the region in 2020 amid the COVID-19 demand collapse and cash shortages.
lesson: Merging two cash-burning competitors doesn't fix the underlying unit economics, especially heading into a demand shock.

name: Grubhub
summary: Early online food ordering and delivery pioneer IPO'd in 2014 but was sold by Just Eat Takeaway to Wonder Group in 2024 for about $650M, a steep loss from the $7.3B acquisition price paid in 2021.
lesson: Market share leadership in food delivery erodes fast without continual reinvestment against better-funded rivals.

name: Guardhat
summary: Connected safety-wearables (smart hardhat) company for construction and industrial sites that faced significant financial difficulty and scaled back operations in the early 2020s.
lesson: Wearable hardware for construction safety faces long enterprise sales cycles that can outlast a startup's runway.
source: Trade press coverage of Guardhat's financial struggles

name: Guevara
summary: UK peer-to-peer car insurance startup that let groups of drivers pool premiums and share surplus shut down in 2016 after struggling to get regulatory approval and reach meaningful scale.
lesson: Peer-to-peer insurance models face steep regulatory and actuarial hurdles that are hard to clear with early-stage capital.

name: Guvera
summary: Australian music streaming startup that collapsed after a scandal-plagued attempted IPO was rejected by regulators over its financial disclosures.
lesson: Aggressive fundraising narratives that outpace real financial fundamentals eventually collide with regulatory scrutiny.
source: Australian Financial Review, Guvera IPO scandal coverage

name: Gymboree
funding: Publicly traded, then private equity owned
summary: Gymboree, which began as a children's play-and-music class franchise before expanding into apparel retail, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy twice (2017 and 2019), with the second filing liquidating all Gymboree and Crazy 8 stores.
lesson: A brand's original childcare/enrichment concept can be diluted and ultimately sunk by an unrelated, debt-laden retail expansion built on top of it.

name: Habit
summary: Habit offered personalized nutrition plans based on DNA and blood-marker testing and was acquired by DSM in 2016, but DSM shut the consumer service down in 2019 after it failed to reach mainstream adoption.
lesson: Personalized-nutrition testing services require repeat consumer engagement that many one-time DNA test buyers never provide.

name: Habito
funding: $76M+ (Series C, investors incl. Mosaic Ventures, Ribbit Capital)
summary: UK-based AI-driven online mortgage broker aiming to automate mortgage advice and matching; shut down its consumer brokerage operations in 2023 after failing to reach profitability amid the UK's sharply rising interest-rate environment squeezing mortgage volumes.
lesson: AI-driven mortgage-matching platforms depend heavily on origination volume, which can collapse quickly in a fast-rising-rate environment regardless of algorithmic efficiency.

name: Halo Neuroscience
summary: Halo Neuroscience developed a transcranial direct current stimulation headset marketed for athletic performance and focus, and was acquired by Flex in 2020 largely for its engineering talent and IP after failing to build a standalone consumer business.
lesson: A neurotech hardware startup that cannot reach consumer scale often ends as a talent and IP acquihire rather than a true product exit.

name: Handy
summary: Home services marketplace that merged into ANGI Homeservices in 2018 at a valuation far below its earlier venture funding rounds, after years of high customer-acquisition costs and worker-classification legal battles.
lesson: Chronic worker-classification lawsuits and high paid-acquisition costs can force even a well-known consumer/business services brand into a distress sale.
source: TechCrunch coverage of the Handy-ANGI merger (2018)

name: Happn
summary: Location-based French dating app that let users see people they'd crossed paths with in real life; underwent mass layoffs in 2022 and shrank dramatically.
lesson: A distinctive location mechanic doesn't guarantee retention once bigger apps copy the core idea with more resources.

name: Harvest CROO Robotics
summary: Strawberry-picking robot developer Harvest CROO Robotics faced major funding shortfalls and slowed development significantly by 2023 after years of delayed commercial deployment.
lesson: Complex multi-armed harvesting robots for delicate fruit require far longer and more expensive development cycles than initial timelines suggest.
source: AgFunder News, Harvest CROO Robotics coverage

name: HauteLook
summary: Flash-sale fashion ecommerce site was acquired by Nordstrom in 2011 for $180M and was folded into Nordstrom Rack before being shut down in 2021.
lesson: Flash-sale ecommerce formats can lose relevance even under a well-capitalized retail parent.

name: Haven
funding: Backed by Maersk and McKinsey Ventures
summary: Ocean freight booking and supply-chain visibility joint venture backed by Maersk and McKinsey that was shut down in 2020 as Maersk consolidated digital efforts internally.
lesson: Corporate-backed joint ventures can be wound down quickly once the parent's strategic priorities shift internally.

name: Haven Life (MassMutual)
summary: MassMutual's AI and algorithmic-underwriting term life insurance subsidiary offering instant, data-driven decisions without medical exams; slashed more than 65% of its workforce in 2022 and was being wound down as MassMutual let the standalone brand fade.
lesson: Even well-capitalized corporate-backed AI underwriting ventures get shut down when the parent's strategic priorities shift and standalone economics do not justify continued investment.

name: Hazy
summary: London-based Hazy built synthetic data generation technology for regulated industries like banking. In November 2024, SAS acquired Hazy's 'principal software assets' — not the whole company — with only about a dozen of Hazy's employees joining SAS, an outcome that effectively wound down the independent business rather than delivering a full-value acquisition for the team and investors.
lesson: An 'acquisition' headline can mask a distressed outcome: when only IP and a fraction of the team are retained, it signals the standalone business could not sustain itself and investors likely saw a below-expectation return.
source: ITWeb, TechTarget, Pebblous synthetic data industry report

name: Health IQ
summary: Life insurance marketer targeting 'health-conscious' consumers with lower premiums shut down after struggling to convert its niche audience into a sustainable underwriting book.
lesson: A clever marketing wedge does not guarantee the underlying insurance economics will work at scale.

name: HealthTap
summary: One of the earliest AI symptom-checker and virtual-doctor platforms, HealthTap raised significant funding and attracted millions of users, but repeated pivots between consumer, enterprise, and telehealth models never produced durable revenue, and the company faded from prominence.
lesson: Repeatedly pivoting a consumer health AI product between business models without settling on one durable value proposition erodes both team focus and investor patience.
source: TechCrunch and CNBC early coverage of HealthTap's pivots

name: HEAVY.AI (formerly MapD/OmniSci)
summary: GPU-accelerated analytics database (originally MapD, rebranded OmniSci, then HEAVY.AI) targeting large-scale data analytics and geospatial visualization for AI-driven workloads, which underwent repeated rebranding, leadership changes, and staff reductions as it failed to reach durable scale against cloud-native analytics competitors.
lesson: Repeated rebranding and strategic pivots can signal — and accelerate — a struggle to find a defensible enterprise niche against faster-moving cloud-native competitors.
source: Industry reporting on OmniSci/HEAVY.AI rebrands and staff changes, 2020-2023

name: Hedgeable
summary: Early robo-advisor offering algorithmic, risk-adjusted portfolio management with alternative-asset exposure, positioned as a more sophisticated alternative to Betterment and Wealthfront. Shut down in 2018 after failing to raise a new funding round, unable to compete with better-capitalized robo-advisor rivals and bank-backed entrants.
lesson: Being an early, technically ambitious robo-advisor was not enough once incumbents (Schwab, Vanguard, big banks) launched free or low-cost automated advisory products, saturating the market against thinly-capitalized independents.
source: TechCrunch, Barron's coverage of Hedgeable shutdown (2018)

name: Helbiz
summary: NYC-based micromobility company that went public via SPAC in 2021, acquired multiple failing scooter rivals, but was delisted from Nasdaq in 2024 amid steep losses.
lesson: Rolling up other struggling scooter companies compounds losses rather than fixing the underlying economics.

name: Hello Bello
summary: Hello Bello, the baby-products brand founded by Kristen Bell and Dax Shepard, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in October 2023 after a single manufacturer's price increases and major retailers' refusal to accept matching price hikes crushed its margins.
lesson: Over-reliance on a single third-party manufacturer for a low-margin consumable (diapers) leaves a DTC baby brand with no pricing flexibility when input costs spike faster than retail partners will tolerate.

name: HelloWallet
funding: ~$21M before acquisition by Morningstar in 2014 for ~$52.5M
summary: Employer-benefit personal finance platform using algorithmic recommendations to guide employees' saving and spending decisions. Acquired by Morningstar in 2014; the standalone HelloWallet product was gradually sunset as Morningstar folded its technology into other workplace retirement offerings.
lesson: B2B2C financial-wellness AI tools distributed through employer benefits can win a strong acquisition outcome, yet still see the original product retired once absorbed into an acquirer's broader retirement-services strategy.
source: Industry coverage of Morningstar retiring HelloWallet branding (2018-2019)

name: Helprace
summary: Small independent helpdesk and community-support software vendor Helprace ceased active development and support around 2019 amid a saturated low-end helpdesk market.
lesson: Bootstrapped helpdesk tools without a distribution advantage struggle to survive once free and freemium alternatives dominate the entry-level market.
source: Product review site archives noting Helprace's discontinued updates

name: High Fidelity
summary: High Fidelity, founded by Second Life creator Philip Rosedale to build a next-generation social VR platform, pivoted away from consumer VR to spatial audio and eventually wound down its VR ambitions entirely.
lesson: Even an experienced virtual-world founder can struggle to repeat past success when the underlying hardware market isn't ready.
source: VentureBeat/UploadVR reporting on High Fidelity pivot and shutdown

name: Higher Ground Education (Guidepost Montessori)
summary: Higher Ground Education, once the world's largest Montessori school operator with 150+ Guidepost Montessori schools, filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2025 after racking up $440M in operating losses over five years and losing nearly 150 schools to foreclosure.
lesson: Rapidly scaling physical school locations on venture capital rather than tuition-covered unit economics leaves a childcare operator unable to service debt the moment growth slows.
source: Forbes, July 2025; Chapter11cases.com bankruptcy filing coverage

name: Highrise
summary: 37signals' simple contact-management CRM Highrise was spun out as an independent company in 2014 but stagnated against modern CRMs and was effectively wound down by 2018.
lesson: A once-innovative simple CRM can be leapfrogged if it stops shipping features while competitors integrate messaging, automation, and analytics.
source: 37signals blog posts on Highrise's spinout and later stagnation

name: Higi
summary: Deployed a national network of AI-enabled health-screening kiosks in retail pharmacies to capture biometric data and risk-screen users, but advertising- and data-driven revenue never covered the capital cost of maintaining thousands of kiosks, leading to a full shutdown.
lesson: Physical AI-screening infrastructure at national retail scale carries maintenance and real-estate costs that a data-monetization business model rarely covers fast enough.
source: MedCity News and Chicago Tribune coverage of Higi's 2023 closure

name: Hippo Insurance
summary: AI-underwritten homeowners insurer using proactive risk data; stock fell over 90% post-SPAC as loss ratios from wildfire and weather exposure blew through models, forcing it to exit direct retail homeowners underwriting.
lesson: AI underwriting models trained on limited historical data struggled to price emerging climate and catastrophe risk correctly, and the company had to retreat from the very retail insurance business it was built to disrupt.

name: hiQ Labs
summary: Data-science company that scraped public LinkedIn profiles to predict employee attrition and skills gaps for corporate HR teams; became famous for the landmark hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn scraping case, but the underlying business collapsed years before the litigation ended - courts found the company "essentially defunct" by 2022, and a final settlement required it to destroy its scraped data and algorithms.
lesson: Winning a legal precedent doesn't save a company if the underlying dispute drags on long enough to drain the business itself.
source: Wikipedia (hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn), Privacy World, Justia

name: Hire an Esquire
summary: Legal recruiting marketplace matching contract attorneys with law firms and corporate legal departments; ceased operations after failing to scale its marketplace model.
lesson: Legal staffing marketplaces face high customer-acquisition costs relative to placement fees, squeezing margins.

name: HireArt
summary: NYC-based recruiting marketplace connecting vetted candidates with employers wound down operations after failing to scale its high-touch vetting model profitably.
lesson: High-touch human vetting in a marketplace is expensive to scale and margins can stay thin indefinitely.

name: Hired (Vettery)
summary: Once a $500M-valued tech-recruiting marketplace using AI-driven candidate-employer matching, Hired began winding down operations in December 2020 and was bought at a discount by rival Vettery (Adecco-owned); the combined brand was ultimately folded into Adecco's LHH Recruitment Solutions in 2024, erasing both original brands.
lesson: A marketplace matching model can burn through a $500M valuation and still be forced into a distressed sale when take-rate economics never work at scale.

name: Hired.com
summary: Tech-talent recruiting marketplace once valued near $400M was acquired by Adecco-owned Vettery in 2020 at a steep markdown after failing to sustain growth and profitability.
lesson: Marketplace startups that scale headcount ahead of durable unit economics often end up sold to a rival at a fraction of peak valuation.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Adecco/Vettery acquiring Hired 2020

name: HiringSolved
summary: One of the earliest AI-powered candidate-sourcing engines, indexing public profiles to find and rank passive candidates; despite being an early mover, it was progressively outpaced by better-funded, faster-iterating rivals like SeekOut and hireEZ and lost relevance in the sourcing-AI category it helped pioneer.
lesson: First-mover advantage in AI sourcing erodes quickly once well-capitalized competitors ship comparable features faster.

name: Hitbox.tv / Smashcast
funding: Privately funded, merged with Azubu in 2017
summary: The livestreaming platform built around fighting-game and esports communities merged with Azubu to form Smashcast in 2017, but the combined entity still shut down in 2020, unable to compete with Twitch and YouTube Gaming.
lesson: Merging two struggling challengers rarely creates enough combined scale to overcome a dominant platform's network effects.

name: Hodlnaut
summary: Singapore crypto lender halted withdrawals in August 2022 and applied for creditor protection after suffering losses from the Terra/LUNA collapse.
lesson: Concentrating lending yield strategies in a single volatile asset class leaves no margin for systemic shocks.
source: Monetary Authority of Singapore statements on Hodlnaut, 2022

name: Hointer
summary: Highly automated jeans store founded by a former Amazon supply-chain executive that used QR codes, robotics and data-driven inventory to let shoppers try on clothes without browsing racks. Closed its Seattle flagship in 2015 after failing to prove the automated-fitting-room concept could scale profitably beyond a single showcase store.
lesson: An impressive automation demo in one flagship store doesn't validate a retail format's unit economics across a chain — proof-of-concept and profitable scale are different problems.
source: GeekWire and retail trade coverage of Hointer's Seattle store closure, 2015

name: Homejoy
summary: On-demand home cleaning marketplace backed by Google Ventures shut down after burning through cash and facing worker-classification lawsuits.
lesson: Subsidized on-demand marketplaces can collapse quickly when customer acquisition costs outpace retention and legal risk mounts.

name: Hometeam
summary: A NY/NJ home care marketplace for hiring vetted caregivers that lost its founding CEO and president in 2018 and was forced to abandon its direct-to-consumer model, pivoting to a B2B Medicaid dual-eligible business.
lesson: A 1099-marketplace pricing advantage evaporates the moment regulators require W-2 employment, and pivoting away from the original vision often signals the first business never found true product-market fit.

name: Homicide Watch
summary: An award-winning hyperlocal crime-reporting site covering every homicide in Washington, D.C. shut down in 2014 after its founders could not build a financially sustainable independent operation.
lesson: Critically acclaimed, civically valuable local journalism can still fail commercially without a scalable revenue model.

name: Homie
funding: $70M+ (Series C, investors incl. Vivint Smart Home founders)
summary: Flat-fee real estate brokerage using AI-driven pricing and matching tools to cut agent commissions; shut down its real estate brokerage and mortgage/title operations in 2023, citing the challenging housing market and litigation over its business model.
lesson: A commission-disruption model financed on venture capital is especially exposed when transaction volumes fall and legal challenges to the underlying fee structure emerge simultaneously.

name: Hopin
summary: Virtual-events SaaS platform rocketed to a $7.75B valuation during the pandemic, then saw demand collapse as in-person events returned; sold its core product to RingCentral for a fraction of capital raised.
lesson: Pandemic-driven demand spikes can reverse just as fast, punishing companies that scaled to match the peak.

name: Hortonworks
summary: Hadoop distribution company that IPO'd in 2014 but saw its stock collapse as cloud data warehouses replaced on-prem Hadoop, ultimately merging into Cloudera in 2019 from a position of weakness.
lesson: Public listing doesn't validate a business model if the underlying technology category is being displaced by cloud-native alternatives.

name: HotJobs
summary: Once a top-three online job board, HotJobs was bought by Yahoo in 2002, then sold to Monster Worldwide in 2010 and folded into Monster's declining job-board business, effectively ending as a brand.
lesson: Legacy job boards were structurally vulnerable once search-driven aggregators like Indeed changed how candidates found jobs.

name: HouseCashin
summary: Small marketplace using AI-driven lead scoring to connect distressed property sellers with cash investors; quietly shut down around 2022 after failing to scale beyond a niche user base against larger, better-capitalized cash-buyer marketplaces.
lesson: A thin AI lead-scoring layer is not enough differentiation in a crowded cash-buyer marketplace segment dominated by larger players with more capital for lead generation.

name: Houseparty
summary: The group video-chat app spiked during COVID lockdowns but usage collapsed once Zoom and FaceTime added similar features; Epic Games shut it down in 2021.
lesson: A pandemic-driven usage spike can mask the absence of durable competitive advantage.

name: HQ Trivia
summary: The live mobile trivia game show was a viral 2017-18 phenomenon but usage crashed after co-founder disputes and it failed to build a sustainable business, shutting down in 2020.
lesson: A live scheduled format that depends on constant novelty struggles to retain users once the fad fades.

name: Huckleberry
summary: AI-driven small-business and renters insurance startup; struggled with profitability and capital-raising, cut nearly half its staff, and was ultimately acquired by Paychex in 2023 in a distressed sale rather than continuing as an independent company.
lesson: A differentiated AI quoting experience was not enough to overcome weak unit economics in commoditized small-business insurance once venture funding dried up.

name: Huddle
summary: Australian peer-to-peer insurance startup offering community-pooled coverage for niche groups like motorcyclists shut down in 2021 after failing to scale beyond small affinity communities.
lesson: Peer-to-peer insurance pools work best at a scale that most affinity-group startups never reach.

name: Hukkster
summary: Browser-based shopping assistant that used automated price-tracking to alert shoppers when watched items dropped in price or went on sale, an early entrant in algorithmic deal-detection for e-commerce. Shut down in 2016 after failing to build a large enough user base or monetization model to justify continued investment.
lesson: A useful price-tracking algorithm is easy for retailers and larger platforms (browser extensions, Amazon itself) to replicate natively, leaving a standalone startup with no lasting wedge.
source: Trade press coverage of Hukkster shutdown, 2016

name: Human Longevity Inc
summary: Founded to build the world's largest genomic and health database paired with AI-driven diagnostics for early disease detection, HLI was plagued by founder conflicts between Craig Venter and investors, mass layoffs, and failed to commercialize its whole-body scanning and genomics products.
lesson: Even world-renowned scientific founders can sink a company when governance conflicts distract from execution on a genuinely hard AI-diagnostics business model.
source: STAT News and San Diego Union-Tribune coverage of HLI leadership turmoil

name: Humane
summary: Launched the $699 AI Pin wearable in 2024 to scathing reviews over overheating, poor battery life and unreliable autonomous features; sold most assets to HP for $116M in February 2025 while permanently shutting down the flagship device.
lesson: Shipping ambitious autonomous-agent hardware before the underlying AI and thermal engineering is ready destroys trust faster than it can be earned back.

name: Humanyze
summary: People-analytics pioneer using wearable "sociometric badges" to capture movement, proximity and voice-tone data to predict workforce outcomes; when remote work emptied offices in 2020 its hardware-dependent model collapsed, and after a late digital-only pivot the company was restructured via a debt-purchase deal and ultimately acquired for roughly $325K in stock by Livechain.
lesson: A product built around physical-office sensor hardware can be wiped out overnight by a work-model shift it never priced in, and a late pivot rarely recovers lost ground.

name: Hungry Planet
summary: Hungry Planet made plant-based meat alternatives targeted at foodservice and institutional buyers but quietly wound down around 2022 amid intensifying competition from Beyond Meat and Impossible Foods.
lesson: Smaller plant-based meat producers without major retail distribution get squeezed out once category leaders scale.

name: Hyzon Motors
summary: Hydrogen fuel-cell truck maker Hyzon Motors wound down US commercial operations in 2025 after an SEC investigation into its revenue recognition practices and years of heavy losses.
lesson: Overstating early revenue in a nascent hydrogen trucking market invites regulatory action that can end operations even with substantial funding.
source: SEC filings and Reuters, 2024-2025, Hyzon Motors investigation

name: IAM Robotics
summary: Pittsburgh-based warehouse item-picking robot maker (Swift) ceased operations in 2023 after failing to secure additional funding in a tightened venture climate for robotics.
lesson: Warehouse robotics requires deep, patient capital; a funding-market downturn can be fatal even with working technology.
source: The Robot Report coverage of IAM Robotics closure, 2023

name: IAM Robotics (Onward Robotics)
summary: Pittsburgh warehouse-picking robotics company that laid off a significant share of staff in January 2023 while launching a new goods-to-person robot, having already pivoted away from its original mobile-manipulation platform in 2021. Rebranded as Onward Robotics later in 2023.
lesson: Two forced product pivots and a rebrand within two years is a pattern consistent with a robotics company that never found a reliably sellable warehouse-automation product.

name: Ibeo Automotive Systems
funding: Backed by Valeo and ZF, undisclosed total
summary: German lidar pioneer and one of the earliest automotive lidar suppliers. Filed for insolvency in September 2023 after a major shareholder withdrew financial support, with assets later acquired by MicroVision for up to €15M.
lesson: Being an early technology pioneer provides no durable moat once well-capitalized entrants scale faster - Ibeo was outlasted by companies it predated by years.
source: The Robot Report, EE News Automotive

name: IBM Watson Health
summary: IBM's flagship healthcare AI division failed to deliver on promises of AI-driven cancer treatment recommendations and was sold off in pieces for about $1B.
lesson: Overpromising AI capability in high-stakes domains like healthcare invites credibility collapse when results disappoint.

name: IBM Watson Health (Watson for Drug Discovery)
funding: ~$4B in IBM acquisitions folded into the unit
summary: IBM's flagship attempt to apply Watson AI to scientific/medical research, including a dedicated 'Watson for Drug Discovery' product meant to mine gene-drug-disease relationships. IBM halted sales of the drug-discovery tool in 2019 citing disappointing results, and in January 2022 sold the core Watson Health data and analytics assets to private equity firm Francisco Partners for roughly $1 billion — a fraction of the capital IBM had invested — with the assets rebranded as Merative.
lesson: Marketing an AI system as capable of transforming scientific discovery before the underlying models could reliably generalize to messy real-world biomedical data led to a costly, public retreat from an entire market category.

name: ICO Global Communications
summary: Mobile satellite communications operator building a medium-Earth-orbit constellation filed Chapter 11 in 1999 after the rise of terrestrial cellular networks undercut its business case before launch.
lesson: A multi-year satellite build cycle can be overtaken by faster-moving terrestrial competitors solving the same problem more cheaply.

name: Identified
summary: Social-data recruiting analytics startup was acquired by Workday in 2014 for roughly $175M but its consumer-facing product was shut down and never integrated as a standalone offering.
lesson: An acquihire outcome can still represent failure of the original product vision even when the exit price looks attractive.

name: Identified.com
summary: Social-data recruiting platform that built a patent-pending "SYMAN" AI to mine Facebook and LinkedIn profiles for hiring insights; despite significant funding and buzz, it was absorbed as an acquihire by Workday in 2014 mainly for its R&D team rather than continuing as an independent product.
lesson: Flashy social-data AI can attract a big-name acquirer, but when the deal is framed around the engineering team rather than the product, that's a sign the original business model didn't work.

name: Idibon
summary: Natural language processing and text analytics startup offering machine-learning-driven language classification tools. Shut down in 2016 after failing to reach sustainable revenue.
lesson: Pre-transformer-era NLP tooling companies often struggled to differentiate technically while also building a repeatable sales motion; both gaps compounded into an early shutdown.

name: Ieso Digital Health
summary: Ieso Digital Health delivered text-based, evidence-generating CBT therapy to NHS patients in the UK but wound down its clinical service in 2024 after failing to secure sustainable long-term NHS contract funding.
lesson: Even deep integration with a single national payer like the NHS creates concentration risk if contract renewal terms shift unfavorably.
source: Digital Health News, UK trade press

name: iflix
summary: A Netflix-style streaming service targeting emerging markets raised over $300M but was sold off for parts to Tencent in 2020 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Emerging-market streaming requires enormous capital to build content and infrastructure against well-funded global rivals.

name: IFTTT
summary: Consumer workflow-automation pioneer ('If This Then That') that underwent severe layoffs and near-collapse of its team in 2023 after failing to convert its huge user base into a sustainable business.
lesson: A large free user base does not guarantee a viable business model without a clear path to monetization.
source: Reporting on IFTTT layoffs and business struggles 2023

name: IGA Worldwide
summary: In-game advertising network IGA Worldwide struggled to generate sufficient advertiser demand and sold its assets in 2009 during the broader collapse of the in-game ad sector.
lesson: New ad inventory formats need proven advertiser demand, not just novel supply, to sustain a standalone business.
source: VentureBeat, GamesIndustry.biz coverage of IGA Worldwide 2009

name: IGN Pro League
summary: IGN's attempt to run a dedicated professional esports league and studio was discontinued after just a year due to high production costs and limited traction.
lesson: Media companies entering esports production need a sustained multi-year commitment, not a one-season experiment.

name: Ike
summary: Autonomous trucking startup founded by former Google and Otto engineers that was acquired by Nuro in 2022 primarily for its engineering talent, ending as an independent company.
lesson: Talent-acquisition exits in capital-intensive AV trucking are common when independent scaling proves too costly.

name: Ike (Ike Robotics)
summary: Autonomous highway trucking startup founded by ex-Google/Uber and Otto engineers. Acquired by Nuro in 2022 primarily for its engineering team and technology, with the independent Ike product and brand discontinued shortly after.
lesson: An acquihire framed as a 'partnership' is frequently the softest available landing for an autonomous-vehicle startup that could not independently fund the years of testing needed to commercialize.

name: Ike Robotics
summary: Safety-focused autonomous trucking startup backed by Bain Capital Ventures and others, acquired by Nuro in 2022 in a deal widely seen as a distressed sale as the AV-trucking sector's funding dried up.
lesson: Even safety-first, well-regarded engineering teams in capital-intensive autonomy get absorbed once the sector-wide funding window closes.

name: imeem
summary: Social music and video sharing site that struck licensing deals with major labels but burned through cash and sold its assets to MySpace Music in 2009.
lesson: Expensive content-licensing deals can outpace ad-supported revenue, making unit economics unsustainable.

name: Imergy Power Systems
summary: Imergy Power made vanadium redox flow batteries for microgrids and grid storage but shut down in 2016 after running out of capital.
lesson: Vanadium flow battery economics remained uncompetitive against falling lithium-ion prices during the mid-2010s.

name: Imperfect Foods
summary: Imperfect Foods sold surplus and "ugly" produce boxes to reduce food waste, but rising costs and slowing subscriber growth forced it to shut down in 2024 despite years of growth.
lesson: Mission-driven subscription grocery models must reach profitability before investor patience for growth-at-a-loss runs out.

name: Improbable
summary: The SpatialOS cloud-simulation platform failed to gain broad developer adoption for large-scale multiplayer games, forcing a pivot to metaverse tooling and deep layoffs in 2023.
lesson: Infrastructure-layer bets on how games 'should' be built can stall if the developer ecosystem doesn't adopt the new paradigm.

name: Improbable (SpatialOS gaming platform)
summary: British deep-tech company whose SpatialOS distributed-simulation platform aimed to power massive AI-driven multiplayer game worlds. After peaking at a $3.4B valuation, it sold its core gaming unit (MSquared/MPG) for $97M in December 2023 and pivoted to venture building, a fraction of its prior valuation.
lesson: A technically impressive simulation platform raised at a valuation far ahead of proven commercial demand in gaming; when the metaverse/Web3 gaming hype cycle cooled, the mismatch between valuation and revenue forced a distressed sale.

name: Improbable (SpatialOS metaverse business)
funding: $600M+ raised, including a SoftBank-led round valuing it at $2B+
summary: UK-based Improbable built the SpatialOS platform for large-scale multiplayer/metaverse worlds and raised over $600M, but wrote down its metaverse (M2) business and cut significant staff in 2023 after failing to find commercial traction.
lesson: Massive infrastructure investment for a speculative future market (the metaverse) can outpace actual paying enterprise or developer demand.
source: Sifted/TechCrunch reporting on Improbable layoffs and writedown, 2023

name: Improbable Defense (Improbable)
summary: Improbable built large-scale simulation technology (SpatialOS) that it repositioned toward synthetic training environments for UK/NATO defense customers under its Improbable Defense unit. The parent company's core gaming/metaverse business collapsed, forcing SoftBank to write down its stake by roughly $1B and Improbable to lay off large portions of staff, even as the defense-focused arm continued operating separately.
lesson: A defense-tech unit can be commercially sound while its parent company's unrelated consumer bet destroys enterprise value; investors underwriting the group as a whole absorb both outcomes.
source: general public reporting on Improbable/SoftBank writedown and restructuring

name: Inbound Health
summary: A hospital-at-home startup spun out of Allina Health that treated complex, often elderly patients in their homes instead of hospital beds; it shut down in December 2025 after a funding round fell through amid uncertainty over renewal of the federal Acute Hospital Care at Home waiver.
lesson: Hospital-at-home economics depend entirely on a temporary federal waiver — building a venture-scale business on a policy that could lapse at any Congressional session is a fundamental risk investors eventually price in.

name: Indigo Ag
summary: Agricultural biology company applying microbiome science and AI/data analytics to develop crop-boosting microbial treatments and a carbon-credit marketplace. After peaking at a $3.5B valuation, the company underwent three rounds of layoffs (2021, 2023, 2024), struggled to commercialize its microbiome products and grain marketplace against incumbents like Cargill and ADM, and its valuation reportedly cratered by roughly 94% in a subsequent funding round before it split into separate business units to survive.
lesson: Applying rigorous science and AI to a real problem (soil/plant microbiome) does not overcome go-to-market disadvantages against entrenched, low-margin agribusiness incumbents with decades of grower relationships.

name: Indix
summary: Product-data intelligence company that built an AI-driven catalog of e-commerce pricing and product attributes for brands and retailers to power pricing and merchandising decisions. Shut down in 2018 after concluding it could not build a large enough standalone commercial business around product data alone, despite strong VC backing.
lesson: Being the data layer beneath retail pricing/merchandising tools is a crowded, thin-margin position unless you can also own the application layer that customers pay for directly.

name: Infarm
summary: Berlin-based in-store vertical farming company Infarm collapsed in 2023, filing for insolvency in Germany and the Netherlands after mass layoffs and unit withdrawals from retailers.
lesson: Distributed micro-farm networks in retail stores add logistics complexity that can outweigh the benefits of proximity to consumers.

name: Infer
summary: Infer built AI-based predictive lead and account scoring for enterprise sales and marketing teams, raising over $30M from investors including August Capital. Facing the same crowded predictive-scoring market as Lattice Engines and EverString, the company sold its technology assets around 2017 in a below-expectations outcome for a once-hyped category leader.
lesson: Predictive lead scoring turned out to be a feature, not a standalone company, for most of the startups that raised large rounds purely around that single capability.

name: Infer Inc.
summary: Infer built a predictive-analytics platform applying machine learning to enterprise sales and marketing decisions (predictive lead scoring), but wound down independent operations around 2017-2018 after failing to scale beyond a narrow niche as larger sales/marketing platforms absorbed similar predictive features.
lesson: A general-purpose predictive-AI platform applied to a narrow business function (lead scoring) is vulnerable once CRM/marketing-automation incumbents build the same capability natively.

name: Inflection AI
summary: Built the empathetic 'Pi' personal AI assistant and raised one of the largest-ever seed-to-Series rounds for an AI startup, but in March 2024 Microsoft hired CEO Mustafa Suleyman and most research staff and licensed its models, leaving Inflection to pivot to enterprise AI with a fraction of its former ambition.
lesson: Even massive war chests can't guarantee product-market fit against Big Tech competitors who can simply hire away the team.

name: InnovAge
summary: The largest for-profit PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly) operator; its 2021 IPO was followed within months by CMS enrollment freezes over care-quality failures, a CEO ouster, shareholder fraud suits and a stock collapse of over 80%.
lesson: Rapid for-profit scaling of a Medicare-regulated care model without matching investment in clinical quality and compliance invites regulatory sanctions that can crater a public listing.

name: Innovbot
summary: UK low-cost wireless vibration-sensor predictive-maintenance startup ceased operating after failing to scale past pilots.
lesson: Cheap hardware sensors aren't a moat without a defensible software/data layer.

name: Inovio Pharmaceuticals
summary: DNA vaccine developer's COVID-19 candidate was repeatedly delayed by FDA holds and its device-based delivery approach, missing the vaccine race entirely.
lesson: A vaccine delivery technology that requires specialized hardware faces a much slower regulatory and manufacturing path.

name: Inpher
funding: $25M+ from JP Morgan, Swisscom and others
summary: Swiss/US-based Inpher built the XOR Secret Computing Engine, a secure multiparty computation (MPC) platform letting data scientists train ML models on encrypted data. After nearly a decade of operation and partnerships (including with CPP Investments), the company's core technology and team were acquired by decentralized confidential-computing network Arcium in November 2024, an outcome that closed out the standalone company rather than scaling it into an independent exit.
lesson: Cryptographic privacy-preserving computation (MPC, homomorphic encryption) has long sales cycles and high R&D costs; a decade of operation without reaching independent scale often ends in a technology/team acquisition rather than a return-generating exit.

name: Insitro
summary: Machine-learning-native drug discovery company founded by former Coursera CEO Daphne Koller, using large-scale cellular data and ML models to identify drug targets and predict clinical outcomes. Despite strategic agreements with Eli Lilly and Gilead, insitro cut 22% of its workforce (about 65 people) in May 2025, with Koller calling it one of the hardest days of her career, citing a 'tumultuous' funding market and the need to extend runway into 2027.
lesson: Even a heavily credentialed founding team and strong pharma partnerships cannot fully insulate a capital-intensive ML-plus-wet-lab model from a prolonged biotech funding downturn.

name: Instafreight
summary: German digital road-freight forwarder that merged into rival Forto in 2022 after failing to independently reach profitability amid intense competition among European freight-tech startups.
lesson: When multiple well-funded competitors chase the same freight-tech niche, consolidation often follows the funding downturn.

name: Insteon
summary: One of the longest-running smart-home platforms, it abruptly shut down all servers and operations in April 2022 with no warning, bricking devices for thousands of customers.
lesson: Even a mature, long-established smart-home brand can vanish overnight if it runs out of cash.

name: Instill Corporation
summary: Foodservice supply-chain B2B marketplace that raised heavily during the dot-com boom and shut down operations after failing to reach profitable transaction volume with restaurant distributors.
lesson: Fragmented, low-margin distribution industries are hard to digitize profitably without owning logistics as well as the marketplace layer.
source: Trade press coverage of dot-com era foodservice marketplaces

name: InsWeb
summary: One of the earliest online insurance comparison sites went public in 1999 but sold its remaining lead-generation assets in 2013 after years of declining revenue and competition from newer aggregators.
lesson: Being first to digitize insurance shopping doesn't protect market share once better-funded aggregators enter the category.

name: Insys Therapeutics
summary: Maker of fentanyl spray Subsys collapsed into bankruptcy after founder John Kapoor and executives were convicted of bribing doctors to overprescribe the powerful opioid.
lesson: Aggressive off-label sales tactics for a controlled substance invite criminal liability that can end a company overnight.

name: Intel Vaunt
funding: Corporate-funded internal project at Intel (New Devices Group)
summary: Intel's Vaunt smart glasses project, designed to be an unobtrusive everyday AR display, was shelved in 2018 before commercial launch as Intel restructured its wearables division.
lesson: Internal corporate incubation projects can be cancelled due to shifting parent-company priorities even when the underlying prototype receives positive press.
source: The Verge reporting on Intel Vaunt shutdown, 2018

name: Interactions Corporation
summary: Intelligent virtual assistant (IVA) company providing voice-based customer service automation for large enterprises; pursued a SPAC merger around 2021-2022 that fell apart, leaving the company financially strained with no clear path to a public listing.
lesson: A collapsed SPAC deal amid the 2022 market downturn left a capital-intensive voice AI company without the growth funding it had been counting on.

name: International Business Times (IBT Media)
summary: A global business-news outlet grew rapidly through an SEO-driven, high-volume publishing strategy but faced repeated fraud and traffic-manipulation allegations tied to its founders' connections to Olivet University, damaging its credibility.
lesson: An aggressive SEO-volume publishing strategy built on ties to controversial funders can generate persistent credibility and legal exposure that outlasts any traffic gains.

name: International Launch Services (ILS)
summary: Joint venture marketing Russian Proton rockets to commercial satellite customers effectively ceased operations around 2022 after sanctions following Russia's invasion of Ukraine cut off its only launch vehicle from the Western commercial market.
lesson: A launch services business built entirely around one nation's rocket is existentially exposed to that nation's geopolitics.

name: Interplay Entertainment
summary: The Fallout/Baldur's Gate publisher spiraled into serious financial distress in the mid-2000s, selling off its most valuable IP (Fallout) to survive.
lesson: Selling your crown-jewel IP to stay solvent is often a company's last move before effective collapse.

name: Interviewed.com
summary: Early AI-assisted mock-interview and hiring-practice platform aimed at helping candidates prepare for and employers assess technical interviews; failed to raise beyond seed funding and shut down as better-funded technical-interview platforms captured the category.
lesson: Being early to "AI for interview prep" doesn't matter if you're under-capitalized relative to the well-funded players who define the category a few years later.

name: Intraspexion
summary: Deep-learning 'litigation prevention' startup that scanned internal corporate communications to flag employment-discrimination risk before lawsuits were filed. Despite patents and pilot programs, the company never announced meaningful commercial scale, and its public and product activity effectively stalled after the late 2010s, leaving it a dormant, sub-scale operation.
lesson: A narrow, single-use-case predictive product (one risk category, one buyer persona) can struggle to reach the scale needed to justify continued VC investment, even with defensible patents.

name: InVision
summary: Pioneering design collaboration and prototyping platform that was overtaken by Figma's real-time collaborative editor, eventually discontinuing its core product in 2024.
lesson: A prototyping tool without native real-time multiplayer editing became obsolete once a competitor built that from the ground up.

name: Invitae
summary: Genetic-testing company scaled rapidly through acquisitions to become a genomics giant but never reached profitability and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2024, selling off its assets.
lesson: Rolling up acquisitions to chase scale doesn't fix a fundamentally unprofitable core business.

name: IPsoft (Amelia)
summary: IPsoft, maker of the Amelia enterprise AI virtual agent, underwent major layoffs and restructuring in 2023 amid mounting losses despite two decades of operation and heavy investment.
lesson: A long operating history and marquee enterprise logos do not protect an AI vendor from collapse if the cost of delivering the AI exceeds what customers will pay.
source: Business Insider reporting on Amelia/IPsoft layoffs, 2023

name: Iridium SSC (original)
summary: Motorola-backed satellite phone constellation launched 66 satellites but filed the largest bankruptcy of its time in 1999 after signing up a fraction of the subscribers needed to cover debt service; assets were later bought for $25M and relaunched as Iridium Communications.
lesson: Building the technically hardest version of a product first can bankrupt a company even when the underlying network eventually finds a market.

name: Iris Automation
summary: Iris Automation built computer-vision 'detect-and-avoid' AI for drones, positioning the technology for both commercial BVLOS operations and defense/public-safety airspace-integration use cases. Facing a slower-than-expected regulatory path to scaled deployment, the company underwent major layoffs and pivoted away from its original hardware/software product line in 2023.
lesson: Building AI safety systems that depend on aviation regulators approving new operating rules ties a startup's runway to a government rulemaking timeline it cannot control.
source: general defense-tech and drone industry reporting on Iris Automation 2023 restructuring

name: Iris Dating
summary: AI-driven dating app that claimed to learn users' 'type' from swipes; shut down after being exposed for using fabricated/bot profile photos to appear more populated than it was.
lesson: Faking network density to mask a cold-start problem destroys trust faster than slow organic growth would have cost you.

name: IRL
summary: The event-planning social app claimed to be the 'anti-Facebook' and reached unicorn status, then shut down in 2023 after investigations found most of its claimed users were bots.
lesson: Inflated user metrics eventually collide with reality, especially once regulators start asking questions.

name: Iron Finance
summary: Partially-collateralized algorithmic stablecoin project (TITAN/IRON) suffered a bank-run style collapse in June 2021, with the TITAN token price falling to near zero within hours.
lesson: Partial-collateral algorithmic stablecoins can enter irreversible death spirals once redemptions outpace the backing.
source: CoinDesk/The Block coverage of Iron Finance collapse, June 2021

name: Iron Ox
summary: Robotic indoor farming startup Iron Ox laid off a large share of its workforce in 2022-2023 and scaled back its greenhouse-robotics ambitions after failing to hit growth targets.
lesson: Fully autonomous greenhouse robotics adds significant capex on top of the already-thin margins of controlled-environment agriculture.
source: AgFunder News, Iron Ox layoffs coverage 2022-2023

name: IronNet
funding: over $400M raised, including SPAC proceeds; public via SPAC in 2021
summary: AI-driven network detection and response (Collective Defense) company founded by former NSA director Gen. Keith Alexander. Despite raising over $400M, IronNet posted losses of $242.6M and $111M in its final two fiscal years, laid off staff repeatedly, and ceased all operations in October 2023, filing for Chapter 7 bankruptcy with only $77,000 in assets against $33.8M in debts. Investors also sued Alexander alleging inflated government-contract projections.
lesson: A prestigious founder pedigree and heavy government-contract narratives cannot substitute for real revenue; overstated pipeline claims plus a premature SPAC listing accelerated a total collapse.
source: TechCrunch, 'IronNet, founded by former NSA director, shuts down and lays off staff' (Oct 2023); Bloomberg Law bankruptcy coverage

name: iSpeech
summary: Early text-to-speech and speech-recognition API and consumer app company that had modest early traction but faded from relevance during the 2010s as Google, Amazon, and later ElevenLabs offered far higher-quality, better-funded alternatives.
lesson: Being first to market with a speech API doesn't guarantee survival once platform giants and better-funded startups leapfrog quality within a few years.

name: iTutorGroup
summary: AI-matched online English-tutoring company (brands including VIPABC and TutorABC) that became a landmark case when the U.S. EEOC sued it in 2022 for using AI hiring software that automatically rejected older female applicants, resulting in a $365,000 settlement — one of the first AI-discrimination enforcement actions in hiring.
lesson: Deploying AI screening tools without bias audits creates direct legal liability; regulators are increasingly willing to bring enforcement actions specifically targeting AI-driven discrimination.
source: EEOC v. iTutorGroup settlement press release, September 2023

name: Iyaz Health
summary: A small mental health scheduling and access startup that failed to gain traction and quietly shut down in 2022 amid intensifying competition from well-funded marketplaces like Headway and Alma.
lesson: Entering the therapist-marketplace category without a differentiated wedge against well-capitalized incumbents is extremely difficult.

name: Jabong
