summary: Indian fashion ecommerce marketplace was sold to rival Myntra (Flipkart Group) in 2016 for roughly $70M, a steep discount to the $225M+ it had raised.
lesson: Fast-scaling fashion marketplaces in emerging markets can burn capital faster than the category can support.

name: JackThreads
summary: Men's flash-sale fashion e-commerce site was acquired by Thrillist Media Group in 2014 but was shut down/restructured in 2016 as flash-sale demand cratered and the combined company pivoted to media.
lesson: The flash-sale e-commerce category as a whole lost its differentiation once full-price sites added regular discounting.
source: Digiday, Business Insider coverage of JackThreads wind-down

name: Jasper AI
summary: AI copywriting and marketing content platform built on top of GPT and other third-party models. Faced a sharp 2023 slowdown as ChatGPT commoditized generative text for consumers, reportedly saw internal valuation markdowns and layoffs, and pivoted toward an enterprise marketing content suite.
lesson: Building a product wrapper on someone else's foundation model can look like a defensible business right up until the model provider ships a free consumer product that replicates the core use case.
source: Forbes, Business Insider reporting on Jasper AI's 2023 struggles

name: Jaunt VR
summary: Jaunt built VR cinematic camera technology and content but pivoted multiple times before winding down operations and selling assets around 2020 amid weak demand for VR video.
lesson: Betting heavily on cinematic VR video content assumed consumer headset adoption that never materialized at the needed scale.
source: Road to VR and TechCrunch reporting on Jaunt wind-down, 2019-2020

name: Jawbone
summary: Wearable maker Jawbone (UP trackers) raised nearly $1B but collapsed under manufacturing defects, patent battles with Fitbit, and cash burn, liquidating in 2017.
lesson: Massive capital raised cannot compensate for recurring hardware quality failures and losing ground to a faster competitor.

name: Jenny Craig
funding: Private equity owned (North Castle Partners, later H.I.G. Capital)
summary: Weight-loss company Jenny Craig abruptly ceased U.S. operations in 2023 after being outcompeted by GLP-1 weight-loss drugs and digital alternatives.
lesson: An in-person, appointment-based weight-loss model became obsolete once a more effective medical alternative reached mass adoption.

name: Jet Airways
summary: Once India's largest private airline grounded its entire fleet under $1.2B of debt; a 2021 revival attempt also failed.
lesson: Heavily leveraged airline expansion without disciplined cost control eventually catches up with even market leaders.

name: Jet.com
summary: E-commerce marketplace best known for its real-time algorithmic "smart cart" pricing engine that dynamically discounted baskets based on shipping and payment-method efficiencies. Walmart acquired it for $3.3B in 2016 to accelerate its digital transformation, but folded Jet's technology into Walmart.com and shut the standalone brand down in 2020.
lesson: A clever AI-pricing mechanic can win a headline acquisition without ever proving it can sustain a standalone consumer brand against Amazon.
source: Walmart corporate announcements on Jet.com brand discontinuation, 2020

name: Jetty
summary: Renters insurance and security-deposit-alternative startup wound down its core consumer insurance business in 2023 and sold off parts of its operations after failing to reach sustainable unit economics.
lesson: Bundling insurance with adjacent fintech products like deposit alternatives adds complexity without automatically adding profitability.

name: Jezebel
summary: A prominent feminist pop-culture site descended from Gawker Media was shut down by G/O Media in 2023 citing declining traffic and revenue, before being revived by Paste Magazine in 2024.
lesson: A recognizable, influential media brand can still be discarded by a cost-cutting owner if its standalone numbers don't clear the bar.

name: Jibo
summary: Pioneering 'social robot' positioned as a warm, autonomous home AI companion, but shipped years late into a market Amazon Echo and Google Home had already captured with cheaper, more useful voice assistants; servers were shut off in 2019, effectively bricking devices.
lesson: Being first to conceive a category doesn't matter if cheaper, faster-shipping competitors define the category before you launch.

name: Job.com (AI relaunch)
summary: Blockchain/AI-branded relaunch of the veteran Job.com domain, promising AI-driven matching and blockchain-verified credentials for job seekers; despite a splashy funding round and rebrand, it failed to establish meaningful market share against incumbent job boards and quietly stalled.
lesson: Bolting trendy technology labels onto a legacy domain doesn't create product-market fit on its own.
source: General HR-tech press coverage of Job.com AI relaunch

name: Jobaline
summary: Voice-AI hiring platform for hourly and bilingual workers that analyzed callers' speech patterns to recommend candidate-job matches; generated strong press around its "voice analyzer" but visibility and funding activity dropped off sharply after 2015, and the company never scaled beyond its early pilots.
lesson: A novel biometric signal can generate media buzz without translating into a durable enterprise sales motion.

name: Jobfox
summary: Matching-based job search startup aimed to replace resume keyword search with compatibility scoring but never gained enough employer adoption and wound down.
lesson: A better matching algorithm doesn't win if it can't get both sides of the marketplace to switch behavior.

name: Jobr
summary: "Tinder for jobs" swipe-based matching app acquired by Monster Worldwide for $12.5M in 2016 with promises of integration; the standalone app and site were quietly shut down and its features absorbed into Monster's core app.
lesson: A viral swipe-based UX can win an acquisition, but if the acquirer only wants the feature (not the brand), the startup effectively disappears.

name: Jobster
summary: Recruitment advertising and social-recruiting startup burned through large VC rounds without finding a sustainable model and folded during the 2008 downturn.
lesson: Heavy ad spend to acquire employer customers without proven retention is unsustainable through a downturn.

name: Jobvite
summary: Early applicant-tracking-system leader was sold to private equity firm K1 Investment Management in 2018 at a valuation well below prior funding rounds, then merged into Employ Inc, losing its standalone identity as newer ATS vendors like Greenhouse and Lever captured market share.
lesson: Being an early ATS category leader doesn't guarantee survival once better-designed, venture-backed challengers emerge.
source: TechCrunch/press coverage of K1 acquiring Jobvite 2018

name: Joyable
summary: Joyable offered a CBT-based coaching app for social anxiety, pivoted its model multiple times, and ultimately shut down in 2020 as larger, better-funded mental health platforms captured the market.
lesson: In a fast-consolidating category, repeated pivots burn runway faster than they find product-market fit.

name: Joyent
summary: Early cloud infrastructure pioneer that was acquired by Samsung in 2016 in a deal seen as a fire sale after failing to compete with AWS.
lesson: Being early to cloud infrastructure wasn't enough once AWS's economies of scale became insurmountable for smaller IaaS providers.

name: Juicero
summary: Made a $400+ wifi-connected juicer that pressed proprietary produce packs; became a punchline when reporters showed the packs could be squeezed by hand just as fast.
lesson: Don't over-engineer a solution consumers can replicate with their bare hands.

name: Julep
summary: Beauty subscription and D2C nail/cosmetics brand ceased operations in 2018 after its assets were sold following a failed pivot to social selling.
lesson: Pivoting a subscription brand's business model late in its life rarely reverses declining fundamentals.

name: Julep Beauty
summary: Seattle beauty subscription brand was acquired by Glansaol in 2016; after Glansaol's Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2018, new owner AS Beauty abruptly discontinued Julep's Maven subscription box in February 2019.
lesson: Being folded into an overleveraged multi-brand roll-up can doom an otherwise viable brand when the parent collapses.
source: GeekWire, BeautyMatter coverage of Julep Maven shutdown 2019

name: Julie Desk
summary: French AI scheduling-assistant agent similar to x.ai's Amy, acquired by workplace-services group Circles/Sodexo in 2021 in a modest deal after struggling to scale beyond a niche premium-concierge customer base.
lesson: Single-purpose scheduling agents proved a feature, not a durable company, across multiple markets.

name: Jumio
summary: AI-powered identity/document verification company used for KYC and fraud/trust-and-safety checks. Founder and CEO Daniel Mattes grossly overstated 2013-2014 revenue (claiming $101M against actual $9.5M) and sold personal shares based on the inflated figures; the company restated financials, filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy in March 2016, and Mattes later paid the SEC $17M to settle fraud charges. Assets were acquired by Centana Growth Partners and relaunched under new management.
lesson: Founder-level financial fraud can destroy a category-leading company almost overnight; the underlying technology and market were sound enough that a successor entity survived, but investors and employees bore the cost of leadership's dishonesty.

name: Jump Bikes
summary: Electric bike-share startup (originally Social Bicycles) acquired by Uber in 2018; Uber shut down and sold off Jump's operations in 2020 amid COVID-driven cost cuts.
lesson: A promising acquisition can still be unwound when the parent company's own priorities shift.

name: Juno Finance
summary: Crypto-friendly neobanking app underwent mass layoffs and scaled back sharply after the 2022 crypto market crash eroded its core user base and revenue.
lesson: Banking products built around a single volatile asset class inherit that asset class's boom-bust cycles.

name: Juno Therapeutics
summary: CAR-T therapy JCAR015 caused five patient deaths from cerebral edema, forcing an FDA clinical hold before the company was acquired by Celgene in 2018.
lesson: Fatal neurotoxicity in a CAR-T trial can end a flagship program even amid an otherwise hot cell-therapy market.

name: Just Energy Group
summary: Retail energy and natural gas marketer Just Energy filed for creditor protection in Canada and Chapter 15 in the US in 2021 after also being hit hard by Winter Storm Uri's extreme price spikes in Texas.
lesson: Retail energy resellers carry hidden tail risk from extreme wholesale price events that can wipe out years of accumulated margin.

name: Juvo
funding: ~$130M (August Capital, Wing Venture Capital, Peter Thiel)
summary: Startup using machine learning to build 'Identity Scores' for unbanked prepaid mobile phone users in emerging markets, enabling mobile carriers to extend micro-credit for airtime and data. Wound down around 2022 after failing to convert its telco-embedded credit-scoring model into a sustainably profitable business at scale.
lesson: Embedding AI credit scoring deep into telco billing systems across many emerging markets required years of integration work per carrier, and revenue per user proved too thin to justify the capital intensity.
source: Fintech/telecom industry press coverage of Juvo wind-down (2022)

name: K Health
summary: K Health built an AI-driven symptom checker and virtual primary care app using anonymized patient data, but after failing to build a profitable direct-to-consumer subscription business, it underwent major layoffs and refocused on white-labeled AI for health systems and payers.
lesson: Consumer AI health apps face brutal customer acquisition costs; pivoting to B2B distribution is often the only viable path, but doing so late can cost most of the original team and momentum.
source: Fierce Healthcare and Forbes coverage of K Health's 2023-24 restructuring

name: Kahuna
summary: Mobile marketing automation startup Kahuna raised tens of millions but was quietly acquired for its technology around 2021 after failing to achieve independent scale in the crowded martech space.
lesson: A crowded marketing-automation category punishes vendors without a clearly differentiated wedge.

name: Kaiber
summary: AI music-video and animation generation platform, popular with musicians for lyric videos, raised a Series A in 2023 but faced intensifying competition from larger, better-funded video-gen entrants (Runway, Pika, Luma, Kling, Sora) through 2024-2025, struggling to maintain differentiation and reportedly scaling back operations.
lesson: A promising niche (AI music videos) can get steamrolled once general-purpose video-generation platforms from far better-capitalized competitors add equivalent capability as just one feature among many.

name: Kairos
summary: Miami-based facial-recognition API startup, whose founder publicly refused to sell the technology to law enforcement over civil-rights concerns, struggled commercially and effectively ceased operations around 2020 after years of executive turnover and thin revenue.
lesson: Taking a principled stand against a lucrative but ethically fraught customer segment can be right for society while still starving a startup of the revenue it needs to survive.
source: TechCrunch and Miami Herald coverage of Kairos's struggles and wind-down

name: Kalera
summary: Publicly traded vertical farming company Kalera filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in April 2023 and later sold its assets to Sunnyside Fresh.
lesson: Rapid multi-facility expansion without operational profitability accelerates cash burn to insolvency.

name: Kaloom
summary: Canadian company building an AI-managed, disaggregated 'network cloud' fabric intended to replace traditional data center and telecom edge networking hardware/software stacks. Ceased operations in 2023 after failing to reach commercial scale.
lesson: A radical rearchitecture of the network stack requires operators to rip out proven infrastructure; without a killer early-adopter use case, sales cycles outlasted the startup's funding.
source: Trade press coverage of Kaloom shutdown, 2023

name: Kapost
summary: Content marketing platform Kapost struggled to differentiate in a crowded market and was effectively wound down, with its technology and customers folded into Uberflip around 2019.
lesson: Content marketing platforms without a defensible niche get absorbed by consolidating competitors.

name: Karakuri
summary: UK AI-driven automated meal-assembly line maker (FRYR, SEMBLR) for food manufacturers closed after funding talks and an acquisition by Henny Penny fell through.
lesson: A strategic investor's minority stake isn't a committed exit.

name: Karhoo
summary: London-based ride-hailing marketplace aggregating taxis and private-hire cars that collapsed into administration in 2017 just months after a high-profile US launch.
lesson: Aggressive multi-country expansion funded by debt-like structures can implode quickly when investor confidence wavers.

name: Karmaloop
summary: Streetwear e-commerce pioneer filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in March 2015 after founder Greg Selkoe's side ventures drained cash and vendor payments fell months behind; sold to Shiekh Shoes in 2016.
lesson: Diverting core-business cash into unrelated side ventures starves the flagship company of the capital it needs to survive.
source: Boston Globe, Boston Magazine coverage of Karmaloop bankruptcy 2015

name: Kasa Living
funding: $150M+ (equity + debt, investors incl. Baldwin New Venture Partners)
summary: Tech-enabled short-term rental and hospitality brand using AI-driven dynamic pricing across leased apartment units; significantly downsized operations and exited numerous markets starting in 2023 amid unsustainable unit economics on its leased portfolio.
lesson: Short-term rental arbitrage models with AI dynamic pricing remain fundamentally exposed to fixed lease costs when occupancy assumptions don't hold.

name: Kaskada
summary: Real-time feature engineering and feature-store platform for ML, which ceased independent operations in early 2024 when its team and technology were acquired by DataStax in a small talent acquisition rather than continuing as a standalone company.
lesson: The feature-store category became crowded and partially commoditized by cloud-native and open-source (Feast) alternatives, squeezing standalone vendors' independent growth prospects.
source: DataStax acquisition of Kaskada team, February 2024

name: Katapult
summary: Rent-to-own e-commerce financing platform that went public via SPAC in 2021 at a ~$1.1B valuation; stock collapsed over 95% amid weak credit quality and was later delisted from Nasdaq.
lesson: SPAC valuations built on growth projections can mask fundamentally weak lending unit economics.

name: Katerra
summary: Construction technology company that used data-driven/AI-assisted design and supply-chain optimization to industrialize homebuilding; filed for bankruptcy in June 2021 after years of operational losses, mismanagement, and an inability to control costs across its vertically integrated model.
lesson: Vertically integrating construction, manufacturing, and AI-driven design simultaneously multiplies execution risk faster than any single AI capability can offset.

name: Kayako
summary: Long-running helpdesk software vendor Kayako suffered years of financial distress and mass layoffs before being acquired in a distressed sale by ReadMe in 2023.
lesson: Early movers in a category can still be starved of resources and out-innovated by better-funded, faster-iterating rivals over a long enough timeline.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Kayako's 2023 acquisition by ReadMe

name: Keecker
summary: French startup building a mobile home-projector robot ceased operations in 2020 after failing to reach profitable scale in the crowded smart-home category.
lesson: Novel multi-function home robots face a steep uphill climb proving a must-have use case over cheaper single-purpose devices.
source: French tech press (Les Echos/Maddyness) coverage of Keecker closure

name: Keen IO
summary: Event analytics API startup that shut down its service in 2021 after failing to reach sustainable scale against larger analytics platforms.
lesson: Developer-analytics infrastructure is a crowded market where undifferentiated APIs struggle to retain customers long-term.

name: Kespry
summary: Drone data analytics company serving construction, mining, and insurance industries that shut down in 2022, selling off its technology assets.
lesson: Hardware-plus-software drone startups face brutal margin compression as commodity drone hardware becomes cheap and analytics alone struggle to fund the business.
source: TechCrunch, Commercial UAV News coverage of Kespry shutdown

name: Kheiron Medical Technologies
summary: UK-based breast-cancer screening AI company (Mia) achieved strong clinical validation and NHS pilot deployments, but converting pilots into large-scale, reimbursed national screening contracts proved much slower than projected, straining its funding runway and forcing restructuring.
lesson: Strong clinical validation data does not guarantee fast procurement — national health system sales cycles can outlast a startup's funding runway even for genuinely effective AI.
source: Sifted and MedTech Dive coverage of Kheiron Medical's funding and NHS deployments

name: Kid City USA Enterprises
summary: Kid City USA, a Daytona Beach-based operator of 140+ early-childhood and daycare centers across 11 states, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in January 2026 after a string of abrupt facility closures left employees unpaid.
lesson: Multi-state childcare chains are exposed to the same working-capital fragility as smaller operators once occupancy and subsidy revenue can't cover a large fixed real-estate and payroll footprint.

name: Kidaptive
summary: AI-driven adaptive learning platform for young children (Leo's Pad) that pivoted repeatedly from consumer app to a learner-analytics licensing business before quietly winding down.
lesson: Repeated business-model pivots (consumer app to B2B analytics) burn both cash and team focus; each pivot needs to fully validate demand before the next reinvention.
source: Crunchbase; EdSurge historical coverage of Kidaptive pivots

name: Kidbox
summary: Kidbox, a kids'-clothing subscription box that partnered with Walmart in 2019, quietly ceased fulfilling orders and shut down customer operations around 2023 following years of customer-service and fulfillment complaints.
lesson: Subscription apparel for fast-growing children faces high return/exchange rates and sizing churn that erode margins even with a major retail partnership.
source: Consumer review aggregators; DTC subscription-box industry coverage

name: Kidpik
summary: Kidpik, a children's clothing subscription box that went public via IPO, was delisted from Nasdaq in December 2024 for failing minimum shareholders'-equity requirements and moved to OTC pink-sheet trading.
lesson: Going public does not solve a subscription retailer's underlying unit-economics problem; a failed merger and persistent losses can push a small-cap kids' brand to delisting within a few years of its IPO.

name: Kidville
summary: Kidville, operator of children's play, music, and enrichment centers in Manhattan and franchised nationally, saw its franchise company file for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in August 2020 amid pandemic-driven closures, though some individually owned locations continued.
lesson: Enrichment-class businesses built around in-person attendance had almost no ability to pivot when pandemic lockdowns eliminated foot traffic overnight.

name: Kindbody
summary: Fertility clinic chain Kindbody closed multiple clinics and saw its valuation cut from $1.8B to as low as $400-600M by 2024 amid larger-than-projected losses, weaker-than-expected employer utilization, and a revolving-door C-suite.
lesson: Capital-intensive brick-and-mortar fertility clinics scaled on employer-benefit growth projections are exposed when actual utilization and enterprise deal sizes fall short of the underwriting model.

name: Kindred AI
summary: Built AI-driven robotic piece-picking for e-commerce warehouses (SORT technology) but couldn't reach broad commercial deployment fast enough; acquired by UK grocery-tech firm Ocado in 2020 at a price reported well below its prior valuation, with much of the original team let go.
lesson: Robotic picking accuracy that works in demos often falls short of the reliability threshold retailers need for 24/7 fulfillment.

name: Kinvey
summary: Mobile backend-as-a-service platform acquired by Progress Software in 2017; Progress discontinued Kinvey in 2022 as part of a product portfolio rationalization.
lesson: Even a successful acquisition exit can end in product discontinuation years later if the category consolidates around the acquirer's core business.

name: KiOR
summary: Biofuel company that promised to turn wood chips into crude oil substitute but filed for bankruptcy after its Mississippi plant never hit production targets.
lesson: Overpromising production yields to investors and regulators eventually collides with physical and chemical reality.

name: Kissmetrics
summary: Customer analytics platform that struggled to differentiate against Mixpanel and Amplitude and shut down its product in 2018.
lesson: Analytics tools need a durable technical or distribution edge to survive well-funded direct competitors.

name: Kistler Aerospace
summary: Reusable launch vehicle developer (K-1 rocket) filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2003 after failing to complete its vehicle despite over a decade of development and a later NASA COTS award it could not fulfill.
lesson: Reusable launch hardware is a capital sink that punishes companies for slipping schedules by a decade rather than a year.

name: Kite
summary: Kite built an AI-powered autocomplete engine for Python and other languages, reaching 500,000 developers before shutting down in November 2022 and open-sourcing its remaining code.
lesson: Founder Adam Smith said Kite 'failed to build a business because our product did not monetize' — individual developers would not pay, and building production-quality AI code synthesis was underestimated by roughly a decade of technology maturity.

name: Kittyhawk.io
summary: Drone fleet-management and airspace-compliance software company, used across construction and other industrial sectors, that shut down in 2021 after running out of funding.
lesson: Enterprise drone-operations software needs enough differentiated value to avoid being commoditized by larger platform players.
source: Commercial UAV News coverage of Kittyhawk.io shutdown

name: Kivnon Logistica
summary: Spanish AI-guided AGV maker for factory material handling, heavily exposed to automotive manufacturing, shut down after a loss on revenue as automotive demand weakened.
lesson: Concentration in one cyclical end-market can sink a revenue-generating industrial vendor.

name: Kiwibot
summary: Campus sidewalk-delivery robot operator that expanded across dozens of US college campuses, but struggled with the unit economics of delivery robotics and scaled back operations sharply amid funding difficulty in the post-2022 downturn.
lesson: Cute, camera-ready delivery robots generate press easily but the underlying per-delivery unit economics remain punishing at small fleet scale.

name: Klout
summary: Klout, which scored social media influence, was acquired by Lithium Technologies in 2014 and ultimately shut down in 2018 after failing to build a sustainable business model around its scores.
lesson: A viral engagement metric doesn't automatically translate into a durable monetizable product.

name: Knewton
summary: Adaptive-learning pioneer raised huge venture rounds on the promise of AI-personalized courseware but never found a durable business model, and sold its assets to Wiley.
lesson: Being early with impressive AI technology doesn't guarantee a viable go-to-market or revenue model.

name: Knightscope
summary: Public security-robot maker (K5) has never achieved profitability, relies on recurring equity raises to fund operations, and has faced persistent going-concern warnings despite years of deployments.
lesson: Going public does not solve a fundamentally unprofitable rental-hardware business model.
source: Knightscope SEC filings and going-concern disclosures

name: Knip
summary: Europe's first fully digital insurance broker, using algorithmic policy management to advise customers; ceased independent Swiss operations by late 2019/2020 after a 2017 merger with Komparu failed to create a sustainable combined business.
lesson: Being a first mover in digital insurance brokerage did not create a durable moat once the underlying economics of broker commissions could not cover customer acquisition and platform costs.

name: Knock (home-swap iBuyer)
funding: $600M+ (equity + debt, investors incl. RRE Ventures, Foundry Group)
summary: Offered an AI-assisted 'Home Swap' bridge-loan product to let sellers buy before selling, using algorithmic pricing for backstop guarantees; laid off large portions of staff in 2022-2023 amid a cooling housing market and was later acquired by a SPAC/restructured at a steep down-valuation.
lesson: Guarantee-backed AI pricing products are extremely capital intensive and vulnerable when rising rates suddenly compress the market they were priced for.

name: Knotel
summary: Flexible office space provider positioned as an enterprise-focused WeWork alternative filed for Chapter 11 after the pandemic gutted office demand.
lesson: A lease-arbitrage business model is acutely vulnerable to sudden demand shocks like a pandemic.

name: Kobo360
summary: Nigerian trucking and logistics marketplace connecting shippers with truckers that underwent major restructuring and staff cuts around 2022-2023 amid funding shortages across African logistics-tech.
lesson: Emerging-market logistics marketplaces are especially exposed when global venture funding for the sector tightens.

name: Kodezi
summary: Kodezi built an 'AI CTO' — an autonomous system meant to maintain, heal, and evolve codebases — raising a series of small pre-seed and seed checks between 2022 and 2025 totaling roughly $2.6M, remaining a micro-scale operation years after founding while competitors raised hundreds of millions for similar autonomous-coding visions.
lesson: In a category (autonomous coding agents) where credible competitors were raising nine-figure rounds, a startup stuck raising six-figure to low-seven-figure checks year after year is structurally unable to compete on model quality or compute, regardless of the vision's merit.

name: Koding
summary: Cloud-based development environment and collaborative IDE that raised significant funding but never found a scalable business model before shutting down.
lesson: A slick collaborative dev-environment product needs enterprise workflow fit, not just individual developer delight, to sustain funding rounds.

name: Konarka Technologies
summary: Konarka developed flexible organic photovoltaic solar film but shut down and liquidated in 2012 after failing to achieve commercially viable efficiency and cost at scale.
lesson: Breakthrough materials science demos don't guarantee a manufacturable, cost-competitive product.

name: Konnektive
summary: Konnektive built CRM and AI-assisted lead routing/scoring tools initially targeted at direct-response and telesales operations. The company's association with high-risk direct-response and subscription-billing merchants drew regulatory and payment-processor scrutiny, and it never expanded beyond a niche, reputation-constrained customer base.
lesson: Building sales-AI tooling for a customer base concentrated in a regulator-scrutinized vertical (direct-response marketing) caps growth and long-term enterprise credibility.
source: Industry reporting, FTC actions against direct-response merchants using similar platforms

name: Koru
summary: Seattle predictive-hiring startup using machine learning to score candidate "fit" for Fortune 500 clients like Citi and Zillow; ended in an "unusual exit," splitting its assets and selling the predictive-hiring product to UK assessment firm Capp & Co. while its original bootcamp platform went to Entangled Group.
lesson: A mid-pivot product with real enterprise logos can still fail to reach exit-worthy scale, forcing a sell-for-parts wind-down instead of an acquisition premium.

name: Kozmo.com
summary: Promised one-hour delivery of small goods in major cities and shut down in 2001 after the free delivery model proved impossibly expensive.
lesson: Free ultra-fast delivery for low-value orders destroys margins regardless of funding size.

name: Kreditech
funding: $300M+ (equity and debt, including Peter Thiel's Naspers-backed round)
summary: Hamburg-based fintech using machine learning to underwrite consumer loans in emerging and underbanked markets using thousands of alternative data points. After years of high loan losses and inability to reach profitability across its many country operations, the company scaled back drastically and effectively became insolvent by 2019.
lesson: AI credit scoring on thin/alternative data across many emerging markets simultaneously produced unmanageable default rates; expanding underwriting AI faster than the model could be validated led to unsustainable losses.
source: TechCrunch, Finextra coverage of Kreditech financial troubles (2018-2019)

name: Kuna Systems
summary: Smart home security-light and camera startup using onboard computer vision for motion and person detection ceased operations in mid-2019, leaving customers' cloud-connected devices with degraded functionality.
lesson: Consumer smart-home hardware with cloud-dependent computer vision features leaves customers stranded the moment the company runs out of runway.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Kuna Systems' shutdown, 2019

name: Kurzweil Applied Intelligence
summary: Founded by Ray Kurzweil, one of the earliest commercial speech-recognition companies, producing pioneering medical and business dictation software; collapsed in the mid-1990s after a major accounting fraud scandal in which executives were later criminally convicted of inflating revenue.
lesson: Even category-pioneering speech recognition technology couldn't survive leadership fabricating financial results to hit growth targets investors expected.
source: US DOJ press releases, contemporary business press

name: Kymeta Corporation
summary: Flat-panel satellite antenna maker, once valued highly on Bill Gates-backed funding, underwent major layoffs and restructuring in 2023-2024 after failing to reach profitable volume manufacturing amid slower-than-expected market adoption.
lesson: Hardware companies that raise at high valuations on a novel antenna technology still need a manufacturing cost curve that reaches profitable unit economics.

name: Lacework
funding: $1.9B+ raised, including a $1.3B round in Nov 2021 at an $8.3B valuation
summary: Cloud security platform whose Polygraph engine used unsupervised machine learning/behavioral anomaly detection to baseline cloud workloads without manual rules. After raising at an $8.3B valuation in the 2021 boom, Lacework cut 20% of staff in 2022 and headcount fell 35% by late 2023; by April 2024 it was in talks to sell to rival Wiz for just $150M-$200M -- a roughly 98% valuation collapse.
lesson: Raising at a peak-bubble valuation on the promise of ML-driven anomaly detection created expectations the business could never grow into once markets reset, producing one of cybersecurity's most extreme down-round collapses.
source: TechCrunch, 'Lacework, last valued at $8.3B, is in talks to sell for just $150-200M' (Apr 2024)

name: Landis
funding: $185M+ (equity + debt, investors incl. Sequoia, Second Century Ventures)
summary: Rent-to-own platform using AI-driven underwriting to assess prospective homeowners and purchase homes on their behalf; laid off most of its staff and wound down its core rent-to-own product in 2023 as rising rates made its financing model unworkable.
lesson: Rent-to-own AI underwriting models that rely on cheap debt to finance home purchases become unviable almost overnight when interest rates spike.

name: Lantern
summary: Lantern offered CBT-based digital coaching for anxiety and body image issues but shut down in 2018 after failing to scale beyond early adopters into a sustainable B2B or consumer business.
lesson: Early clinical promise in a niche digital-CBT product does not guarantee a large enough addressable market to justify venture funding.

name: LastPass
summary: Popular password manager that suffered a series of breaches in 2022 exposing encrypted vault data, causing a mass exodus of users and lasting reputational damage.
lesson: For a security product, a single serious breach can permanently erode the trust the entire business model depends on.

name: Latitude (AI Dungeon)
summary: Creator of AI Dungeon, one of the first mainstream AI-generated text-adventure games built on GPT-3. A 2021 content-moderation crisis, triggered after OpenAI flagged the generation of CSAM-adjacent content, led to an over-aggressive filter, a collapse in App Store ratings from 4.8 to 2.6, and lasting reputational damage that the product never fully recovered from at its early scale.
lesson: Being first to prove a category (AI-generated interactive fiction) does not guarantee lasting advantage if content-safety and moderation are treated as an afterthought rather than a launch-day requirement.

name: Lattice Engines
summary: Lattice Engines pioneered predictive lead scoring and AI-driven sales/marketing analytics. After more than a decade and $100M+ raised, the company was acquired for parts by Dun & Bradstreet in 2019 in what was widely seen as a distressed sale rather than a growth exit.
lesson: Being a category creator doesn't guarantee a winning outcome; a decade of fundraising without reaching scale can end in an asset sale rather than an exit for shareholders.
source: Crunchbase, press coverage of D&B acquisition

name: Lawdingo
summary: On-demand legal consultation marketplace connecting consumers with lawyers by phone; failed to reach scale and shut down within a few years.
lesson: On-demand marketplaces for legal advice struggle with thin margins and inconsistent lawyer supply/demand matching.

name: LawGeex
summary: One of the earliest AI contract review companies, known for a 2018 study claiming its AI outperformed human lawyers on NDA review accuracy, and used by Global 2000 legal departments. In 2023 the company was effectively broken up: Robin AI acquired its enterprise contract-review assets in February, and LegalSifter acquired its remaining enterprise client base (17 Global 2000 companies) that September. The founders pivoted entirely to a new SMB venture, Superlegal, leaving the LawGeex brand as an inactive legacy platform.
lesson: Being a first-mover pioneer doesn't protect a company once a wave of better-funded, LLM-native competitors arrives; founders who see the writing on the wall may extract more value by selling off assets and restarting than by fighting for share in a category they helped create.

name: LawPivot
summary: Free legal Q&A platform where businesses could get preliminary legal answers from vetted attorneys; shut down after failing to convert free Q&A traffic into a sustainable revenue model, with remnants absorbed by Rocket Lawyer.
lesson: Free legal-advice platforms attract high user volume but struggle to monetize without a clear paid conversion path.
source: TechCrunch and VentureBeat coverage of LawPivot

name: LawTrades
summary: Freelance legal marketplace connecting startups with on-demand lawyers; shut down after failing to build a durable two-sided marketplace.
lesson: Legal marketplaces need enough transaction volume and trust signals to overcome the high-stakes nature of legal work.

name: Laybuy
summary: New Zealand buy-now-pay-later provider was delisted from the London Stock Exchange and wound down its consumer operations in 2023 after losing market share to larger BNPL rivals.
lesson: Smaller BNPL entrants struggle to compete once dominant players like Klarna and Afterpay lock in merchant partnerships.

name: Layer (game-asset AI)
summary: AI-powered 2D game-asset generation tool aimed at indie game developers raised a modest seed round but faced intense competition from general-purpose image generators and newer, better-funded specialized entrants (Scenario, Meshy) offering similar or broader capability, constraining its growth to a small niche.
lesson: A thin funding cushion in a fast-consolidating generative-asset niche leaves little room to out-execute better-capitalized specialized competitors targeting the same indie-developer audience.

name: LDK Solar
funding: Publicly traded (NYSE: LDK), billions in debt
summary: Chinese silicon wafer maker LDK Solar filed for bankruptcy protection in 2014 after accumulating massive debt during the solar oversupply crisis of the early 2010s.
lesson: Debt-financed capacity expansion timed at the top of a commodity cycle leaves no room to survive the downturn.

name: Le Tote
summary: Clothing rental subscription startup acquired the bankrupt Lord & Taylor in 2019, then itself filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in August 2020 and shut down Lord & Taylor entirely, unable to sustain either business through the pandemic.
lesson: Acquiring a struggling legacy retailer to gain scale can drag down an otherwise-promising subscription model rather than strengthen it.
source: Retail Dive, CNBC coverage of Le Tote bankruptcy 2020

name: Le Tote / Lord & Taylor
summary: Data-driven clothing rental subscription service that used algorithmic personalization to curate boxes, then acquired 193-year-old department store Lord & Taylor from Hudson's Bay in 2019 to fuse e-commerce data with physical retail. Both entities filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy in August 2020 after the pandemic wiped out already-thin cash reserves.
lesson: Layering a legacy department-store turnaround on top of an already cash-constrained subscription startup multiplies operational risk instead of diversifying it.
source: PR Newswire, Forbes, "Le Tote and Lord & Taylor... Chapter 11", August 2020

name: LeadCrunch
summary: LeadCrunch used AI lookalike-modeling to identify and generate B2B leads that matched a company's best existing customers, raising over $20M from investors. Growth slowed considerably after 2020 amid intensifying competition in predictive lead generation, and the company scaled back sharply, effectively fading from the category.
lesson: AI lookalike-modeling for lead generation proved easy for larger, data-rich competitors (6sense, ZoomInfo) to replicate, eroding the differentiation a smaller specialist needed to survive.

name: Leap Motion
funding: $94M raised across venture rounds; sold for a fraction of $200M+ prior valuation
summary: Leap Motion, a pioneer of hand-tracking sensors for VR/AR, was sold to UltraHaptics (later renamed Ultraleap) in 2019 in a fire-sale deal after burning through significant funding without reaching profitability.
lesson: Pioneering an input technology ahead of hardware demand can leave a company financially exhausted before the market matures enough to monetize it.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Leap Motion/UltraHaptics acquisition, 2019

name: Leap Transit
summary: San Francisco luxury commuter bus startup shut down by regulators in 2015 for operating without proper permits, less than a year after launch.
lesson: Skipping local transit permitting requirements to move fast can end a company almost overnight.

name: LearnVest
summary: Financial planning startup acquired by Northwestern Mutual for roughly $250M in 2015 was shut down three years later when the insurer discontinued the standalone product.
lesson: Strategic acquirers can sunset even well-funded fintech products when they don't fit the parent company's core distribution.

name: LeddarTech
summary: Canadian lidar and sensor-fusion software company. Despite going public via SPAC in 2024, disclosed going-concern doubt, received a Nasdaq delisting notice, and filed for bankruptcy under Canada's Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act in June 2025, with its entire board resigning immediately after.
lesson: A SPAC listing timed at the trough of investor enthusiasm for lidar can accelerate rather than prevent a company's collapse.

name: Legal OnRamp
funding: Backed by General Electric legal department and VC funding
summary: Legal process outsourcing and collaboration platform originally spun out of GE's legal department; wound down after failing to scale beyond its founding client relationships.
lesson: Legal innovation platforms built around a single anchor client often struggle to generalize to a broader market.
source: Above the Law and Law.com coverage of Legal OnRamp's decline

name: Legal Robot
summary: San Francisco Bay Area seed-stage startup that used machine learning to score and flag risky contract language for consumers and small businesses. Raised only seed funding, never announced a follow-on round after the late 2010s, and visible product and company activity effectively ceased, a common pattern of quiet failure rather than a public shutdown announcement.
lesson: Consumer-facing legal AI without a clear paid enterprise wedge struggled to raise follow-on capital in the pre-LLM era, when the underlying NLP could not yet deliver lawyer-grade accuracy.

name: Legal Utopia
summary: UK-based AI legal chatbot aimed at helping consumers understand legal rights and connect with solicitors. After early seed funding and media attention as an access-to-justice AI play, the company's public and commercial activity wound down by 2024 without a disclosed acquisition, consistent with running out of runway in a low-margin consumer legal-advice niche.
lesson: Consumer legal-advice AI faces a brutal combination of low willingness-to-pay and high regulatory sensitivity around giving legal guidance, making venture-scale economics very hard to reach.

name: LendUp
funding: ~$360M (Andreessen Horowitz, Kleiner Perkins, GV, QED Investors)
summary: Fintech lender that used algorithmic underwriting to offer an alternative to payday loans with a 'ladder' to build credit. Repeated CFPB and state regulatory enforcement actions over deceptive practices and fees led it to sell its loan business (LendUp Loans) and wind down in 2022.
lesson: Even well-marketed 'responsible' algorithmic lending in a heavily regulated, subprime-adjacent category can collapse under compounding consumer-protection enforcement actions that erode both trust and unit economics.
source: CFPB enforcement actions and TechCrunch coverage of LendUp wind-down (2021-2022)

name: Leocare
summary: French insurtech offering app-based home and auto insurance was placed into receivership in 2023 after running out of cash amid a broader European insurtech funding pullback.
lesson: Multi-line insurance startups need enough runway to absorb underwriting volatility while still building brand trust.

name: LeoSat Enterprises
summary: Planned high-throughput laser-linked satellite constellation for enterprise data backhaul shut down in late 2019 after failing to secure the roughly $100M needed to move past the planning phase.
lesson: A constellation business plan requiring billions in eventual capital must first prove it can raise the much smaller amount needed to de-risk the design.

name: Lernin Games
summary: Lernin Games, a Spanish edtech startup building learning games for toddlers, ran out of cash and shut down after failing to convert its funded product into a sustainable business.
lesson: A seed-stage raise gives a toddler-focused edtech startup only a narrow runway to prove engagement and monetization before capital runs out.

name: Lernout & Hauspie
funding: Publicly traded (NASDAQ), market cap once exceeded $9B
summary: Belgian speech-recognition giant that aggressively acquired rivals (including Dragon Systems) to build a global speech technology empire; collapsed into bankruptcy in 2001 after an accounting fraud scandal revealed fabricated Asian revenues, with executives later criminally convicted.
lesson: One of the largest speech-tech companies ever built was destroyed not by technology or market failure but by systematic accounting fraud used to fund an acquisition spree.
source: SEC litigation releases, contemporary financial press

name: Letgo
summary: Secondhand goods marketplace app raised nearly $1B before merging with rival OfferUp in 2020, and the letgo brand was ultimately discontinued in 2022.
lesson: Two well-funded rivals fighting for the same local marketplace niche often end in a costly consolidation rather than a standalone win.

name: Level Money
summary: AI-driven personal budgeting app that predicted safe-to-spend amounts based on income and bill patterns. Acquired by Capital One in 2015 for its technology and team, but Capital One shut the standalone app down in 2018 to fold the technology into its own products.
lesson: An acqui-hire exit for AI personal-finance tools doesn't guarantee product survival; once absorbed into a bank's roadmap, the original consumer product can be discontinued despite a loyal user base.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Level Money shutdown by Capital One (2018)

name: LevelEleven
summary: LevelEleven built AI-informed sales gamification and performance-management dashboards for enterprise sales teams. After a decade of moderate growth, the company merged into Bloomfire in 2021 in a deal widely characterized as a distressed consolidation rather than a growth exit for investors.
lesson: Sales gamification proved to be a feature that larger revenue-intelligence and enablement suites could bundle in, squeezing out standalone point solutions that never reached independent scale.

name: LifePod Solutions
summary: A Boston voice-first proactive care assistant for aging-in-place seniors that used smart speakers for check-ins, reminders and emergency alerts; the company went dark after a single funding round and is no longer active.
lesson: Voice hardware for seniors requires continuous investment in caregiver trust and household adoption that a single funding round rarely covers.

name: Lightning eMotors
summary: Commercial EV and charging infrastructure company Lightning eMotors went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for bankruptcy in 2024 after failing to scale production profitably in a slower-than-expected commercial EV market.
lesson: Small-scale commercial EV converters face brutal capital requirements that a SPAC raise alone can't guarantee will be sufficient.

name: LightSquared
summary: Attempted to build a hybrid satellite-terrestrial LTE network but the FCC blocked deployment after tests showed severe interference with GPS receivers, forcing Chapter 11.
lesson: Spectrum plans that interfere with critical safety infrastructure like GPS will be blocked no matter how much capital has already been spent.

name: Lily Robotics
summary: Promised a throw-and-shoot autonomous camera drone using promo footage shot with a different prototype; shut down amid an SEC/DOJ fraud investigation before ever shipping.
lesson: Faking demo footage to sell preorders is fraud, and it eventually catches up with founders.

name: Limbix
summary: Limbix built a digital therapeutic for adolescent depression and pursued FDA clearance, but wound down its clinical product efforts in 2022 as reimbursement and adoption hurdles proved too steep.
lesson: Pediatric digital therapeutics face an especially narrow reimbursement path that can strand even well-funded, clinically-validated products.

name: Limeade
summary: Corporate wellness platform Limeade went public on the Australian exchange in 2019 but its valuation collapsed, leading to its acquisition by WebMD Health Services in 2023 at a steep discount.
lesson: An enterprise wellness SaaS company can still fail to reach profitability even with recurring corporate contracts if growth costs outpace revenue.

name: LimeGlass
summary: London-based startup applying natural language processing to central bank communications and financial news to generate real-time trading signals for institutional traders. Folded around 2016 after struggling to land paying institutional clients at scale.
lesson: Selling NLP-derived trading signals to buy-side institutions requires proving statistically significant alpha over long track records; a small startup with limited runway rarely gets the time needed to build that trust.
source: UK fintech press coverage of LimeGlass closure (2016)

name: Limewire
summary: Popular peer-to-peer file-sharing client for music shut down by a federal court injunction after the RIAA won a copyright infringement lawsuit.
lesson: Facilitating widespread copyright infringement invites legal action that can end a business overnight.
source: Arista Records LLC v. Lime Group court records

name: Link AKC (American Kennel Club smart collar)
funding: Backed by American Kennel Club and Vetnique; undisclosed amount
summary: AKC-branded integrated smart GPS collar that was discontinued as an integrated product after a change in company structure, with the brand later relaunched as a smaller add-on tracker module.
lesson: A trusted brand partnership (AKC) doesn't guarantee product survival if the underlying hardware business can't sustain its cloud and cellular service costs against newer competitors.
source: AT&T product discontinuation listing; product review sites

name: Lion Electric
summary: Canadian electric school bus and truck maker that filed for bankruptcy protection after demand fell short of production capacity and cash reserves ran low.
lesson: Building manufacturing capacity ahead of firm, funded customer orders is a dangerous bet in commercial EVs.

name: LittleThings
summary: A lifestyle and feel-good content site that built enormous traffic through Facebook shut down abruptly in 2018 after a Facebook News Feed algorithm change collapsed its referral traffic overnight.
lesson: Building a media business almost entirely on a single platform's referral traffic leaves no cushion when that platform deprioritizes publishers.

name: Livemocha
summary: Early social-network-style language-learning pioneer that was acquired for parts by Rosetta Stone in 2013 and fully discontinued a few years later.
lesson: Being first to a category doesn't protect against faster-moving mobile-native competitors like Duolingo.

name: LivePerson
summary: Pioneer live-chat and conversational AI company LivePerson saw its stock collapse over 95% from its peak amid a founder-conduct scandal, executive departures, and going-concern doubts starting in 2023.
lesson: Even a category pioneer with decades of revenue can implode when governance failures and leadership scandal destroy market trust.
source: Wall Street Journal investigative reporting on LivePerson's 2023 governance crisis

name: Living Social
summary: Daily deals marketplace once valued near $6B raised nearly $1B before being sold to rival Groupon for a nominal $1 in 2016.
lesson: Deals marketplaces without repeat-purchase loyalty burn through capital chasing an ever-churning customer base.

name: Living.com
summary: Online furniture retailer shut down in 2000 after failing to solve the logistics and returns costs of shipping bulky furniture.
lesson: Category economics for bulky, high-return goods make naive ecommerce models unprofitable.

name: Livingly Media
summary: A women's lifestyle content network (LoveToKnow, Lonny) built through SEO-optimized articles across a portfolio of niche sites was gradually wound down and sold off in pieces as programmatic-ad rates declined.
lesson: A content-farm-style portfolio strategy dependent on programmatic display advertising is highly exposed to declining CPM rates over time.

name: Loadsmart
summary: AI-powered digital freight brokerage and transportation management platform that underwent significant restructuring and layoffs starting Q4 2022 as freight demand cooled sharply, part of an industry-wide FreightTech contraction where VC investment in supply chain tech dropped over 77% year-over-year.
lesson: AI-driven freight-matching algorithms cannot outrun a macro freight-rate collapse — Loadsmart's repeated restructuring mirrored an entire cohort of digital freight brokerages forced to cut deeply in 2022-2023.

name: Loc8tor
funding: Publicly listed on AIM (London); raised tens of millions
summary: UK electronics company whose pet-tracking device line (an early RF-based pet locator) was discontinued around 2013 after failing to achieve profitable scale against GPS-based rivals.
lesson: Non-GPS radio-frequency locator technology was quickly outpaced by cheaper, more accurate GPS and Bluetooth-based competitors entering the pet tracker market.
source: UK tech press coverage of Loc8tor's decline

name: Local Bounti
summary: Publicly traded controlled-environment agriculture company Local Bounti, which went public via SPAC in 2022, underwent a severe debt restructuring in 2024 to avoid bankruptcy after mounting losses.
lesson: SPAC-funded agtech companies still face the same fundamental margin pressures as their privately held peers.

name: Local Motors
summary: 3D-printed and co-created vehicle manufacturer Local Motors, known for the Olli autonomous shuttle, abruptly ceased operations in 2022 after running out of cash.
lesson: Novel manufacturing approaches don't reduce the fundamental capital intensity of building and certifying vehicles.

name: Locomation
funding: ~$50M+ raised, incl. Wilbur-Ellis and Volvo investment
summary: Pittsburgh-based developer of truck 'convoy' platooning autonomy, where a lead truck with a human driver is followed autonomously by a second truck. Laid off most of its non-engineering staff in February 2023 after failing to raise a new round, effectively winding down commercial operations even as management publicly denied a full shutdown.
lesson: Platooning as an interim step to full autonomy proved to be its own hard, capital-intensive problem rather than a shortcut to trucking-industry revenue.

name: Loft (Brazil AI-driven iBuying)
funding: $800M+ (equity + debt, investors incl. SoftBank, Andreessen Horowitz)
summary: Latin American proptech that combined an AI-driven home valuation and mortgage platform with an iBuying model; underwent massive layoffs (over half its staff cut across 2022-2023) and multiple down-round valuation cuts as its Brazil-focused iBuying arm proved unsustainable amid inflation and rate hikes.
lesson: Exporting the US AI-iBuying playbook into an emerging market with higher inflation and rate volatility amplified the same fundamental pricing-lag risk that hit Zillow Offers.

name: Loggly
summary: Early cloud log management pioneer acquired by SolarWinds in 2016 at a valuation well below its funding, as Splunk and Datadog captured the larger enterprise observability market.
lesson: Being first to a category doesn't matter if better-funded, broader-platform competitors out-execute on enterprise sales.

name: Logically
summary: London-based AI fact-checking and misinformation-detection company founded after Brexit and the 2016 US election, working with governments (UK Cabinet Office paid it £1.2M+ in 2023) and platforms including Meta and TikTok. It filed for administration after losing key contracts with TikTok and Meta amid a broader political backlash against anti-misinformation programs; assets were transferred to a new entity, Kreatur, via pre-pack administration.
lesson: Misinformation-detection startups that depend heavily on a small number of Big Tech and government contracts face concentrated political risk — when platforms rolled back anti-misinformation initiatives under free-speech pressure, Logically lost its core revenue almost overnight.

name: LookingGlass Cyber Solutions
summary: Spun out of the NSA, LookingGlass built AI-assisted threat-intelligence platforms (scoutPRIME) used by secure government agencies and critical infrastructure operators. After more than a decade of fundraising without an independent path to scale, it was absorbed into ZeroFox in 2023 in a sub-scale acquisition rather than continuing as a standalone company.
lesson: A strong government pedigree and real technology are not enough in a crowded threat-intel market; without differentiated growth, an early mover ends up as an acquisition of convenience for a larger, also-struggling player.

name: Loop AI Labs
summary: Cognitive computing and voice/language AI startup that claimed enterprise NLP and speech capabilities; ceased normal operations around 2017 amid investor lawsuits alleging misrepresentation of its technology and customer traction.
lesson: Overstating AI capabilities and customer traction to investors eventually surfaces in litigation and destroys the company's ability to raise further capital.
source: Court filings, Silicon Valley Business Journal

name: Loot
summary: UK neobank targeting students and young people collapsed into administration in 2019 after running out of funding and failing to secure a rescue deal.
lesson: Targeting a low-revenue demographic like students makes it especially hard to reach profitability before funding runs out.

name: Loot Crate
summary: The gaming and geek-culture subscription box pioneer expanded too fast, mismanaged inventory and vendor payments, and filed for bankruptcy in 2019.
lesson: Subscription-box logistics and cash-flow management can sink a fast-growing consumer brand as easily as a lack of demand.

name: Lordstown Motors
summary: Electric pickup truck maker Lordstown Motors filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after a fraud investigation into overstated pre-orders and a collapsed partnership with Foxconn.
lesson: Overstating demand signals like pre-orders to public markets invites regulatory scrutiny that can destroy a company's credibility and financing.
source: Reuters, June 2023, Lordstown Motors Chapter 11 filing

name: Lori Systems
summary: Kenyan digital freight and trucking marketplace for East African supply chains that scaled back drastically and effectively ceased core operations in 2023 amid a funding crunch.
lesson: Digitizing fragmented trucking markets is capital-intensive and highly sensitive to macro funding cycles.
source: African tech press, Lori Systems retrenchment 2023

name: Loyal3
summary: Commission-free stock purchase platform that partnered directly with public companies shut down as brokerages moved to zero-commission trading industry-wide.
lesson: A commission-free niche disappears fast once the entire industry adopts the same pricing.

name: LucasArts
summary: Disney shut down the storied studio after acquiring Lucasfilm, cancelling Star Wars 1313 and shifting to a licensing-only model.
lesson: An acquisition can end a beloved studio overnight when the buyer's strategy doesn't include internal development.

name: Luko
summary: French AI-driven home insurance startup; filed for bankruptcy in 2023 after accounting irregularities scuttled a rescue acquisition by Admiral, and its home insurance book was ultimately sold to Allianz Direct for roughly 5M euros in 2024.
lesson: Accounting discrepancies discovered during acquisition due diligence can collapse an otherwise viable rescue deal, turning a distressed sale into a near-total wipeout for investors.

name: Lumen5
summary: Early AI-assisted text-to-video tool for turning blog posts and articles into social videos plateaued as a mid-market SaaS product, unable to keep pace with the far more capable generative-video wave (Runway, Pika, Sora-class models) that emerged from 2023 onward, leaving it increasingly positioned as a legacy template-based tool rather than a cutting-edge generative product.
lesson: Being an early mover in 'AI video' with template-and-stock-footage automation did not translate into staying power once true generative video models made from-scratch synthesis possible; the underlying technology approach became obsolete.

name: Lumina Networks
summary: Spun out of Brocade to commercialize open-source ONOS/CORD-based SDN orchestration software for telecom operators. Abruptly ceased operations in mid-2020, laying off its entire staff without prior notice after failing to secure follow-on funding.
lesson: Betting a company on open-source telecom SDN standards moving faster than carrier procurement cycles actually move is a timing trap that can exhaust funding before revenue materializes.
source: SDxCentral and Light Reading coverage of Lumina Networks shutdown, 2020

name: Luminar Technologies
summary: High-profile lidar and perception-software supplier with marquee Volvo and Nissan design wins. Cut roughly 30% of its workforce across 2024 restructurings, its CEO stepped down following an ethics inquiry, and its stock lost over 95% of its SPAC-era value amid continued heavy losses.
lesson: A marquee automotive design win is not the same as a profitable production ramp - Luminar's Volvo deal generated headlines years before it generated cash flow.

name: Luminary
summary: Podcast subscription platform that spent heavily on exclusive celebrity content but failed to build a large paying subscriber base, eventually laying off most staff and selling its show catalog.
lesson: Paying premium prices for exclusive content does not guarantee subscriber conversion in a market full of free alternatives.

name: Luminosity Gaming
funding: Privately funded, backed by OverActive Media
summary: Facing financial strain, the Canadian esports org sold its prized CS:GO roster to 100 Thieves in 2020 to raise cash and significantly downsized its remaining operations.
lesson: Selling off a marquee roster for liquidity is often a sign an esports org's broader business model isn't self-sustaining.

name: Luxe
summary: On-demand valet parking startup operating in several major US cities that shut down its consumer parking service in 2017, with Volvo licensing its technology afterward.
lesson: On-demand valet labor costs proved too high relative to what urban parkers were willing to pay.

name: Lykos Therapeutics (MAPS PBC)
summary: The spinout from MAPS developing MDMA-assisted therapy for PTSD saw the FDA reject its new drug application in 2024 over data integrity and trial design concerns, followed by layoffs and leadership departures.
lesson: Even a first-in-class therapy with strong efficacy signals can be halted by regulators over trial conduct and unblinding concerns.
source: FDA complete response letter coverage, STAT News

name: Lytro
summary: Pioneered light-field cameras that let users refocus photos after the shot, but consumers didn't value the feature enough to justify the price; assets acquired by Google in 2018.
lesson: A technically brilliant camera innovation can still fail if it doesn't solve a problem consumers actually have.

name: Machine Zone
summary: The Game of War/Mobile Strike maker built a massive ad-spend machine but couldn't sustain user acquisition costs as growth slowed, shedding staff and selling off assets.
lesson: Growth fueled primarily by expensive paid user acquisition is fragile once acquisition costs rise or engagement dips.

name: Machinet
summary: Machinet built an AI coding assistant focused specifically on Java and enterprise JVM ecosystems, but struggled to gain traction or raise a meaningful follow-on round as broader, multi-language tools like Cursor and Copilot expanded strong Java support of their own.
lesson: A single-language niche strategy can work when a category is immature, but once general-purpose leaders add strong support for that same language, the niche advantage evaporates quickly.

name: Machinima
summary: The pioneering gaming and esports content network never found a sustainable ad-revenue model as YouTube's own creator ecosystem matured, and WarnerMedia shut it down in 2019.
lesson: A content network built on aggregating creators can be disintermediated once the underlying platform empowers those creators directly.

name: Machinima (media/blogging network)
summary: Originally a gaming-content blog and video network majority-owned by Warner Bros., it was shut down abruptly in 2019 with mass layoffs after failing to build a sustainable advertising model as YouTube's direct creator tools matured.
lesson: Intermediary content networks lose their reason to exist once the underlying platform gives creators direct monetization tools.

name: Magic Leap
summary: Raised the largest AR war chest ever for its Magic Leap One headset, then missed consumer sales targets by orders of magnitude and pivoted entirely to enterprise/defense.
lesson: Enormous funding and secrecy can't substitute for validating real consumer demand before shipping.

name: Magisto
funding: $46M+ raised prior to acquisition; acquired by Vimeo 2019 (~$200M reported)
summary: Early AI-powered automatic video-editing app for consumers and small businesses was acquired by Vimeo in 2019 for a reported ~$200M, but Vimeo discontinued the standalone Magisto product and brand in 2022, folding its remaining functionality into Vimeo's broader Create tools as newer generative-video technology made Magisto's template-based automated editing approach obsolete.
lesson: Even a well-priced acquisition exit is not a permanent success if the underlying product's technology approach (template-driven automated editing) is later made obsolete by newer generative techniques, leading the acquirer to shut the brand down.

name: Mahalo.com
summary: Jason Calacanis' human-edited search and content site pivoted repeatedly (search, video courses, HowTo.com) after Google's Panda update devalued its SEO-driven content pages, eventually shutting down.
lesson: A content strategy optimized primarily for search ranking rather than reader value is fragile to search-algorithm changes.

name: Major League Gaming
summary: One of the earliest professional esports leagues, MLG burned through significant VC funding on live events and its GameBattles platform before selling its core assets to Activision Blizzard in 2016 for a fraction of the capital raised.
lesson: Being an early esports-league pioneer doesn't guarantee survival if broadcast and sponsorship revenue arrive years after your cash runs out.

name: MakeTime
summary: On-demand CNC manufacturing capacity marketplace that wound down independent operations after failing to reach the transaction density needed to match idle machine-shop capacity with buyer demand profitably.
lesson: Matching fragmented, illiquid industrial capacity with demand requires far higher transaction density than most manufacturing marketplaces achieve early on.
source: Manufacturing trade press coverage of MakeTime's 2019 wind-down

name: Maluuba
summary: Canadian deep-learning and NLP research lab acquired by Microsoft in 2017 to bolster its AI research. It became part of Microsoft Research Montreal, which Microsoft ultimately shut down in 2021.
lesson: Being acquired by a well-resourced tech giant is not a guarantee of longevity; internal reprioritization at the parent company can still shutter a once-promising research lab years later.
source: MobileSyrup, VentureBeat coverage of Microsoft Research Montreal closure, 2021

name: Managed by Q
summary: Office management and facilities-services marketplace acquired by WeWork; operations were wound down as WeWork retrenched during its own crisis.
lesson: An acquisition by a troubled parent company can doom an otherwise-functioning business.

name: MannKind Corporation
summary: Inhaled insulin product Afrezza faced multiple near-bankruptcies before winning approval, but adoption remained tiny for years against injectable insulin's entrenched market position.
lesson: A better delivery mechanism doesn't overcome physician and patient inertia around an established standard of care.

name: Manthan Systems
summary: India-founded retail analytics and merchandising-intelligence software company was acquired for a fraction of its funding by Aurea/ESW Capital in 2021 after struggling to convert its analytics platform into sustained profitable growth.
lesson: Analytics platforms that require lengthy, high-touch retailer implementations can burn through venture funding faster than they can prove renewal value.
source: Industry retail-tech and M&A press coverage, 2021

name: ManyPets (Bought By Many)
summary: UK-founded pet insurance insurtech that expanded into the U.S. in 2021 but announced in late 2024 it would exit the American market entirely after failing to reach profitability there.
lesson: Insurance is capital-intensive and heavily regulated state-by-state; even a well-funded unicorn insurtech can be forced to retreat from a new geography once loss ratios and regulatory costs outpace premium growth.
source: Coverager; Sifted reporting on ManyPets US exit, November 2024

name: ManyPets (US)
summary: UK-founded pet insurer exited the US market and cut staff after underwriting losses from medical cost inflation made American expansion unsustainable.
lesson: International insurance expansion can outrun a carrier's ability to price risk accurately in a new market.

name: Maple
summary: NYC-based prepared meal delivery startup co-founded by a celebrity chef shut down in 2017, with its team acquired by Deliveroo.
lesson: A crowded meal-delivery market punishes startups that can't reach delivery density fast enough.

name: MapR
summary: Hadoop distribution vendor that raised hundreds of millions but lost ground to Cloudera/Hortonworks and cloud-native data platforms, eventually selling its assets to HPE in a fire sale.
lesson: Being one of three well-funded competitors chasing the same enterprise market left little room for anyone but the leader to survive.

name: Marathon Patent Group
summary: Patent licensing and monetization firm that failed to generate sustainable returns from its patent portfolio and ultimately abandoned IP licensing altogether, rebranding as Marathon Digital Holdings, a bitcoin miner.
lesson: When a core IP-licensing business model stops generating returns, a full pivot away from the original industry signals the original strategy failed.
source: SEC filings and CoinDesk coverage of Marathon Patent Group's pivot

name: Marble
summary: San Francisco sidewalk delivery-robot startup was acquired by Ford in 2018 primarily for its engineering talent, and its delivery-robot product was discontinued.
lesson: An acqui-hire outcome for engineering talent often means the original product vision is shelved entirely.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Ford's acquisition of Marble team, 2018

name: Marcus by Goldman Sachs
summary: Goldman Sachs' consumer banking venture racked up billions in losses on credit cards and personal loans, prompting the bank to retreat from mass-market consumer banking starting in 2023.
lesson: An institution without consumer banking DNA can underestimate the cost and risk of acquiring and servicing retail borrowers.

name: Marin Software
summary: Search and social ad management platform Marin Software went public in 2013 but saw its stock collapse over the following decade as it struggled to retain customers against Google and Facebook's own tools, repeatedly restructuring to avoid delisting.
lesson: Third-party ad-management layers are structurally at risk once the underlying ad platforms build competing native tools.
source: SEC filings, AdExchanger coverage of Marin Software decline

name: MaritzCX
funding: Formed from Maritz Research and Allegiance merger
summary: Customer experience management platform MaritzCX, itself a merger of legacy feedback vendors, was acquired by InMoment in 2018 after struggling to compete against Qualtrics and Medallia at scale.
lesson: Rolling up legacy market-research firms into a CX platform does not automatically produce the product velocity needed to compete with venture-scaled leaders.

name: MarketForce
summary: East African B2B retail distribution marketplace that collapsed in 2024, laying off nearly all staff after running out of funding amid a broader African tech funding downturn.
lesson: Retail distribution marketplaces in low-margin emerging markets are especially exposed when funding markets tighten.
source: African tech press (TechCrunch/Disrupt Africa) coverage of MarketForce's 2024 collapse

name: Markforged
summary: Industrial 3D-printing/composite-manufacturing company SPAC'd at ~$2.1B in 2021; revenue declined and cash nearly halved through 2024, compounded by a $17.3M patent-infringement judgment, before being acquired for just $116M in 2025.
lesson: SPAC-era hardware-manufacturer valuations were often disconnected from unit economics.

name: Massive Incorporated
summary: Massive Incorporated built an in-game advertising network and was acquired by Microsoft in 2006 for around $200M, but Microsoft shut the unit down in 2010 after the in-game ad market failed to scale as projected.
lesson: Emerging ad formats can be acquired at a premium before the underlying market has proven it can scale.
source: TechCrunch, Reuters coverage of Massive Inc shutdown 2010

name: Masten Space Systems
summary: Vertical-takeoff/vertical-landing rocket and lunar lander developer, a longtime NASA contractor, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in mid-2022 after cost overruns on its NASA CLPS lunar lander contract; assets were later acquired by Astrobotic.
lesson: Fixed-price government contracts can bankrupt a small contractor if technical costs run ahead of milestone payments.

name: MasterClass
summary: Celebrity-taught online course platform saw sharp subscriber growth during the pandemic reverse afterward, leading to layoffs and a roughly 75% internal valuation markdown.
lesson: Pandemic-boosted consumer subscription demand for enrichment content proved largely temporary.

name: Matches Fashion
funding: Acquired by Frasers Group for £52M in Dec 2023
summary: UK luxury fashion e-commerce retailer entered administration in March 2024, just three months after Frasers Group bought it, cutting over half its staff and fully ceasing operations by July 2024 after years of mounting losses.
lesson: A distressed acquisition target needs a credible turnaround plan and committed follow-on funding, not just a lower price, to survive.
source: Business of Fashion, Fashionista coverage of Matches Fashion administration 2024

name: Matroid
summary: Stanford spinout led by Reza Zadeh built a no-code platform for training custom object-detection models but ceased independent operations around 2023 as larger cloud-vendor AI platforms commoditized the no-code computer-vision tooling category.
lesson: No-code AI tooling layered on top of commodity object-detection models is vulnerable once cloud giants bundle the same capability for free.
source: Industry trade press and Crunchbase status update on Matroid, 2023

name: Mattel Aristotle
summary: Mattel canceled its Aristotle smart nursery hub — an Alexa-like AI device marketed to monitor and interact with babies — in October 2017 after child-privacy advocates and members of Congress raised alarms before launch.
lesson: Always-listening AI devices aimed at infants trigger acute privacy and child-safety scrutiny; the backlash can kill a product before it ever ships.

name: Mattermark
summary: Mattermark used data science to score private company growth signals for sales, investing and research teams. Founder transition, rising data-acquisition costs and inability to reach profitability led the company to sell its assets to FullContact in 2018 after effectively ceasing independent operations.
lesson: Data-aggregation businesses have brutal unit economics when the cost of acquiring/maintaining data grows faster than willingness to pay for it.

name: Maven
summary: General Motors' car-sharing brand operating in over a dozen US cities was shut down in 2019 as GM redirected investment toward Cruise's autonomous vehicle efforts.
lesson: Corporate mobility experiments get cut quickly when a flashier bet (like autonomy) captures leadership's attention.

name: Maxwell Health
summary: Benefits administration and HR platform for small businesses was acquired by Sun Life Financial in 2018 for a fraction of its raised capital and was later folded into Sun Life's own tools.
lesson: Selling to a strategic acquirer at a steep discount is often the last resort before an outright shutdown.
source: Boston Business Journal/TechCrunch on Sun Life-Maxwell Health deal 2018

name: Mayfield Robotics
summary: Bosch-incubated startup behind the Kuri home robot discontinued the product in mid-2018 before wide shipment despite a successful CES 2017 launch and thousands of pre-orders.
lesson: Strong trade-show buzz does not always translate into a business case corporate backers will keep funding.

name: MediaMath
summary: MediaMath, an early independent demand-side platform, shut down operations in 2023 after running out of cash and failing to secure rescue financing or a sale.
lesson: Independent ad-tech infrastructure players face existential risk when scale economics favor a few dominant platforms.

name: Medium
summary: Evan Williams' blogging and publishing platform repeatedly pivoted its business model, from ad-supported to a metered paywall to a flat subscription, laying off staff multiple times while never matching its early venture-scale ambitions.
lesson: Repeatedly changing a publishing platform's core monetization model erodes trust with both the writers and readers it depends on.

name: MedMen
