summary: Cannabis retail chain that branded itself as the 'Apple Store of weed' expanded aggressively across multiple states, then saw its stock collapse over 99% and default on debt.
lesson: Aggressive multi-state retail expansion can collapse fast when unit economics never catch up to store count.

name: MedyMatch Technology
summary: Israeli startup developed AI to detect brain hemorrhages and strokes from CT scans in the ER, partnering closely with Samsung, but never scaled beyond a handful of deployments and gradually receded from the market as better-funded rivals like Viz.ai and Aidoc captured the stroke-AI category.
lesson: In a fast-moving diagnostic AI niche, being early with a single strategic partner is not enough if faster-scaling, more broadly funded competitors define the category.
source: Calcalist and MobiHealthNews coverage of the MedyMatch-Samsung partnership

name: Meerkat
summary: The live video-streaming app rode SXSW buzz but was crushed within weeks after Twitter cut off its social-graph access and launched rival Periscope.
lesson: Building a growth engine on top of a rival platform's API leaves you exposed to a single policy change.

name: Megvii (Face++)
summary: One of China's 'AI Dragon' facial-recognition giants was added to the US Entity List in 2019 over alleged human-rights abuses in Xinjiang, which crippled its international expansion and forced it to repeatedly withdraw and refile IPO applications in Hong Kong over several years amid mounting losses.
lesson: Facial-recognition technology sold into state-surveillance programs can permanently close off international capital markets and customers even for a technically dominant player.
source: Reuters coverage of Megvii's US blacklisting and stalled Hong Kong IPO

name: Melinta Therapeutics
summary: Antibiotic maker with four approved drugs filed Chapter 11 in 2020 after revenue failed to cover debt and operating costs, the second antibiotic bankruptcy in a year.
lesson: Multiple approved antibiotics still couldn't overcome the structurally poor economics of the antibacterial market.

name: Mensia Technologies
summary: A French medtech company developing FDA/CE-marked neurofeedback devices for ADHD and other conditions, Mensia Technologies filed for insolvency in 2023 after struggling to achieve reimbursement and commercial scale.
lesson: Neurofeedback and neurotech devices face slow clinical adoption curves that can outlast the runway of even well-regarded medtech ventures.

name: Merlyn Mind
summary: AI voice-assistant and orchestration platform purpose-built for K-12 classrooms, spun out of IBM Watson veterans; faced significant funding and growth struggles by 2024 amid a broader collapse in venture appetite for hardware-adjacent AI-education products.
lesson: Hardware-adjacent AI products for schools carry higher capital intensity (devices, integrations, IT support) than pure software, making them more exposed when follow-on funding tightens.
source: EdWeek/EdSurge coverage of Merlyn Mind funding difficulties 2024

name: Meroxa
summary: Real-time data integration and change-data-capture platform that shut down in 2024 after failing to reach sustainable growth.
lesson: Real-time data infrastructure requires deep enterprise trust and long sales cycles that can outlast available runway.

name: Merrill Corporation
summary: Long-standing legal and financial transaction services company (M&A dataroom, litigation support) that was restructured and sold off in pieces amid a heavy debt load, with its core dataroom business sold to Datasite investors in 2018.
lesson: Legacy document-services companies carrying heavy debt struggle to compete against cloud-native rivals with lower cost structures.
source: Mergermarket and PE Hub reporting on Merrill Corp restructuring (2015-2018)

name: Meru Networks
summary: Enterprise Wi-Fi networking company with virtualized, software-intelligent wireless architecture. After going public in 2010 and struggling to compete with Cisco, Aruba and Meraki, was acquired by Fortinet in 2015 for roughly $44M — a fire-sale price far below its cumulative funding and peak market value.
lesson: Going public did not solve Meru's fundamental competitive-differentiation problem in enterprise Wi-Fi; the eventual acquisition price reflected years of losing share to better-capitalized incumbents.
source: Fortinet acquisition announcement 2015; trade press on Meru's decline

name: Meta Company
summary: Meta Company (unrelated to Facebook's later rebrand), founded by Meron Gribetz, built AR glasses for spatial computing but filed for bankruptcy in 2019 after failing to achieve commercial scale.
lesson: Ambitious spatial-computing AR hardware startups faced the same capital-intensity and long development timelines that sank several peers in the same period.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Meta Company bankruptcy, 2019

name: Metacog
summary: Metacog built a machine-learning analytics platform for capturing and analyzing behavioral data to power adaptive enterprise and education products, but shut down operations around 2018 after failing to scale commercial revenue beyond pilot deployments.
lesson: Behavioral/process analytics platforms that never move past pilot-stage deployments with a handful of customers struggle to reach sustainable scale.

name: MetalSite
summary: Steel-industry B2B trading exchange backed by large steel producers that shut down after failing to attract enough independent buyer volume beyond its founding sponsors.
lesson: A marketplace funded and dominated by incumbent suppliers rarely earns the independent buyer trust needed for real liquidity.
source: Industry trade press coverage of MetalSite closure (2001)

name: Metamoto
summary: Simulation-as-a-service startup for validating autonomous-vehicle software. Acquired by Israeli safety-verification firm Foretellix in October 2020, after which it immediately stopped selling or renewing its simulation subscriptions, effectively ending as an independent product and company.
lesson: In AV tooling, a small, underfunded simulation vendor is often acquired mainly for its team and IP rather than its ongoing business - a soft landing, not a real exit, for investors.

name: Metanautix
summary: Big-data query and integration startup acquired by Microsoft in 2016; its technology was folded into Microsoft's data platform and not sold as a standalone product.
lesson: An acqui-hire can be a positive outcome for founders while still ending the product as an independent entity.

name: Metromile
summary: Pay-per-mile auto insurer with AI claims-automation assistant AVA; after IPO struggles and cash burn, was acquired by Lemonade in 2022 in an all-stock deal valuing it at a steep discount to its SPAC valuation.
lesson: A genuinely innovative AI claims product could not offset weak underwriting economics in a niche low-mileage-driver segment, leading to a distressed sale.

name: MFG.com
summary: Early online manufacturing sourcing marketplace connecting buyers with contract manufacturers that faded from prominence over the 2010s as newer, better-funded manufacturing marketplaces like Xometry overtook it.
lesson: Being first to a marketplace category provides no lasting advantage if the product experience stalls while competitors modernize.
source: Manufacturing sourcing industry trade press coverage of MFG.com's decline

name: Miasolé
summary: Thin-film solar panel maker that was sold at a steep discount to a Chinese conglomerate after burning through hundreds of millions without reaching profitability.
lesson: Being among the most heavily funded startups in a sector is no guarantee against a distressed fire-sale exit.

name: Mic
summary: A millennial-focused news site chased Facebook video pivots, laid off most editorial staff in 2017 after a traffic collapse, and was sold for parts in 2019.
lesson: Rebuilding an entire editorial strategy around a single platform's algorithm leaves no fallback when that algorithm changes.

name: Microsoft HoloLens
funding: Corporate-funded by Microsoft, including a $22B U.S. Army IVAS contract
summary: Microsoft's HoloLens mixed-reality headset line was effectively discontinued in 2024 after Microsoft scaled back HoloLens 2 production, cut the team, and the U.S. Army IVAS contract faced repeated soldier complaints and delays.
lesson: Even a well-capitalized incumbent can abandon an AR hardware line after years of investment if it fails to find a durable enterprise or consumer market beyond a single large government contract.
source: Business Insider/The Verge reporting on HoloLens discontinuation, 2024

name: Midway Games
summary: The Mortal Kombat publisher accumulated heavy debt and underperforming titles, filing Chapter 11 and selling assets piecemeal.
lesson: Carrying too much corporate debt while release quality declines is a slow-motion bankruptcy.

name: Mighty Buildings
summary: 3D-printed prefab home manufacturer that raised substantial venture funding but underwent repeated mass layoffs and scaled back operations sharply in 2023-2024.
lesson: Novel construction manufacturing technology (3D printing) still must clear the same unit-economics bar as traditional homebuilding to reach scale.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Mighty Buildings layoffs

name: Millennial Media
summary: Millennial Media, a mobile ad network that IPO'd in 2012, saw its valuation crater amid competition from Facebook and Google and was acquired by AOL in 2015 for about $238M, far below its peak market cap.
lesson: Ad networks without proprietary inventory or data are vulnerable when platform giants enter the same market.
source: TechCrunch, Fortune coverage of AOL-Millennial Media acquisition 2015

name: Mimo (Rest Devices)
summary: Rest Devices' Mimo, a sensor-embedded baby onesie that tracked breathing and sleep, was discontinued around 2018 as the company pivoted away from consumer baby wearables toward industrial wearables.
lesson: A crowded baby-monitor hardware category with thin margins pushed even an early, well-reviewed entrant to abandon the consumer baby market entirely.
source: Rest Devices company history; baby-tech press coverage 2014-2018

name: Mindbloom (original life-gamification app)
summary: An earlier, unrelated startup also named Mindbloom built a gamified personal-growth and goal-setting web app aimed at improving wellbeing, but shut down around 2012 after failing to retain users beyond an initial novelty period; the name was later reused by an unrelated ketamine therapy company founded in 2019.
lesson: Gamification alone rarely sustains long-term engagement in behavior-change products without deeper clinical or social hooks.

name: Mindshare Technologies
summary: Customer experience feedback and analytics vendor Mindshare Technologies was acquired by Service Management Group (SMG) in 2018, folding its product line into SMG's broader CX platform.
lesson: Mid-market CX feedback vendors are frequent consolidation targets as larger CX measurement platforms roll up market share.
source: SMG 2018 acquisition announcement for Mindshare Technologies

name: Mindstrong Health
summary: Founded by former NIMH director Tom Insel, Mindstrong used smartphone-interaction AI ('digital phenotyping') to detect signs of mental health relapse, but after a strategic pivot away from its original consumer product and mounting cash burn, the company shut down entirely in 2023.
lesson: A scientifically prestigious founding team and a genuinely novel biomarker approach can still fail if the company pivots away from its core product before proving commercial viability.
source: STAT News coverage of Mindstrong Health's 2023 shutdown

name: Mint
summary: Pioneering personal finance and budgeting app, acquired by Intuit for $170M in 2009, was shut down in 2024 with users pushed to migrate to Credit Karma.
lesson: Even category-defining products get sunset when a parent company decides consolidation serves its strategy better.

name: Mirror (fitness)
summary: Home-fitness mirror startup Mirror was acquired by Lululemon for $500M in 2020, but Lululemon discontinued the hardware and took a writedown in 2023 as demand collapsed.
lesson: An acquirer overpaying for a connected-fitness hardware trend can be forced into a costly reversal once category demand fades.

name: Misfit
summary: Made stylish fitness trackers and was acquired by Fossil Group in 2015, but Fossil shut down the Misfit wearables brand in 2018.
lesson: A successful design-led exit doesn't guarantee the product survives inside a larger portfolio company.

name: Mission Motors
summary: Electric motorcycle and powertrain maker Mission Motors shut down in 2015 after pivoting from consumer motorcycles to enterprise EV powertrains failed to generate sufficient revenue.
lesson: A late pivot from a struggling consumer product to enterprise sales rarely succeeds without a proven enterprise go-to-market motion.

name: Mixer
summary: Microsoft's live-streaming platform never closed the gap with Twitch despite exclusive deals with stars like Ninja, and was shut down in 2020.
lesson: Buying exclusive star creators can't overcome an entrenched platform's network effects and habit formation.

name: Mixin Network
summary: Cross-chain messaging and asset network suffered a $200M hack in September 2023 attributed to a cloud database compromise, severely damaging user trust and effectively ending the project's active usage.
lesson: Relying on centralized cloud infrastructure for custody of decentralized assets reintroduces the single point of failure blockchain aims to remove.

name: mOasis
summary: Soil-moisture-retaining polymer startup mOasis, which raised funding from prominent agtech investors, shut down around 2019 after its product failed to gain broad grower adoption.
lesson: Agronomic input products need extensive multi-season field validation before growers will pay a premium over established practices.

name: ModCloth
funding: ~$78M pre-acquisition; acquired by Walmart (2017), resold to Go Global Retail (2019)
summary: Indie fashion e-commerce retailer known for its data-driven 'Be The Buyer' program that used crowdsourced and algorithmic signals to guide merchandising decisions. Walmart acquired it in 2017 to build out its digitally-native-brand portfolio, then sold it off in 2019 for a fraction of the price after it failed to hit growth targets; the brand effectively wound down as an independent operation shortly after.
lesson: Data-driven niche merchandising algorithms don't automatically scale once a mass-market acquirer tries to fold the brand into a broader portfolio strategy.
source: Trade press on Walmart's ModCloth acquisition and 2019 divestiture

name: Mode Media
summary: Formerly Glam Media, this lifestyle content and ad-tech network raised over $300M but shut down abruptly in 2016 after failing to find a buyer amid an unsustainable cash burn rate.
lesson: Raising hundreds of millions to build a content network doesn't guarantee an exit if the underlying ad-tech economics never worked.

name: Modern Age
summary: A longevity and healthy-aging clinic chain offering hormone therapy and wellness treatments for aging adults; it shut down in 2023 and was sold in a distressed deal after failing to reach sustainable unit economics.
lesson: Premium in-person longevity clinics face the same brutal real-estate and staffing cost structure as any medical practice, and novelty branding doesn't fix that math.

name: Modern Animal
summary: Venture-backed veterinary clinic chain that raised over $75M at a high valuation but was forced to close several clinic locations and cut staff in 2023 amid funding pressure.
lesson: Brick-and-mortar veterinary rollups require enormous per-location capital, and rapid multi-city expansion on venture funding can outpace real unit economics.
source: Industry reporting on Modern Animal clinic closures, 2023

name: Modern Meadow
summary: Modern Meadow began as one of the first cultivated-meat startups before abandoning food entirely in 2019 to focus solely on lab-grown leather materials, effectively failing in the nutrition space it started in.
lesson: Cultivated-protein science that proves easier to commercialize in materials than in food signals a fundamental product-market mismatch for the original vision.

name: Modsy
summary: AI-powered interior design startup that used 3D room modeling to let customers visualize and shop furniture in real time. Shut down its core design business abruptly in June 2022 after a planned acquisition fell through at the last minute, cutting ~250 contract designers with days' notice and leaving customers awaiting refunds.
lesson: Betting the company's survival on a single acquisition falling through with no funding backstop leaves zero runway to manage an orderly wind-down.
source: Fortune, TechCrunch, "Modsy shuts down design services", June-July 2022

name: Modulous
summary: UK-based modular building design software and platform company that entered financial administration in 2024 after failing to secure further funding.
lesson: Construction-tech platforms tied to a slowing housing development pipeline are highly vulnerable to funding-market downturns.
source: Construction News (UK) coverage of Modulous administration

name: Modzy
summary: A CarSaver/Booz Allen spinout, Modzy built an enterprise MLOps model-deployment platform aimed at government and commercial customers, but was reabsorbed into Booz Allen Hamilton in 2023 in a distressed sale after failing to raise further independent funding.
lesson: The MLOps model-deployment category became crowded with better-capitalized rivals and hyperscaler-native tooling, leaving thinly differentiated platforms with no path to independent scale.

name: MOG
summary: On-demand music streaming and internet radio service acquired by Beats Electronics in 2012 primarily for its technology, with its consumer service quickly discontinued.
lesson: Being acquired does not guarantee a product's survival if the acquirer values only the technology or team.

name: Mojio
summary: Connected-car platform Mojio, which powered carrier telematics programs, sold off assets and wound down in 2023 after carrier partners scaled back or ended their programs.
lesson: Over-reliance on a small number of large carrier partners for distribution creates existential risk if those contracts end.

name: Momentus
summary: Momentus develops autonomous, AI-guided in-space transportation ('space tugs') with interest from U.S. national-security customers for orbital logistics. Its 2021 SPAC merger closed under an SEC settlement over misrepresented technology-readiness claims by the founder, and the stock subsequently lost over 95% of its value amid execution delays and repeated capital raises.
lesson: Overstating technology readiness to close a SPAC deal invites SEC enforcement that permanently damages investor trust, regardless of whether the underlying government use case is real.
source: general public reporting on Momentus SEC settlement and SPAC performance

name: Momentus Inc.
summary: In-space transportation (space tug) company went public via SPAC in 2021 but its founder was forced out over national-security concerns, and by 2024 it faced a Nasdaq delisting notice and severe cash shortfalls.
lesson: Founder background and regulatory scrutiny can unravel a SPAC-funded space company even after the deal closes and cash is in hand.

name: Monese
summary: UK-based neobank for underbanked and migrant customers underwent a severe down-round and distressed majority-stake sale around 2025 after years of losses and failed acquisition talks.
lesson: Serving an underbanked niche with low-margin customers requires reaching scale faster than most seed-stage neobanks manage.

name: Monster Worldwide
summary: The pioneering online job board Monster.com was sold to Dutch staffing firm Randstad in 2016 for about $429M, a steep decline from its dot-com-era peak valuation above $8B.
lesson: Even category-defining pioneers can be dismantled by more efficient search-based successors like Indeed and LinkedIn.
source: Reuters coverage of Randstad-Monster Worldwide acquisition 2016

name: Moov Inc.
summary: Wearable fitness coaching device maker Moov shut down in 2018 after failing to find a sustainable business model against larger wearable competitors.
lesson: A niche wearable feature (motion coaching) is hard to defend once major players add similar functionality.

name: Morphe (Forma Brands)
funding: Backed by Sagon Phior, funding undisclosed
summary: Influencer-driven cosmetics brand's parent Forma Brands filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in January 2023 after closing all US stores; sales had fallen from $500M (2020) to $295M (2021), and creditors took over via a $690M debt-for-equity deal.
lesson: Over-reliance on a small set of influencer partnerships (James Charles, Jeffree Star) for brand equity creates fragile demand when influencer reputations sour.
source: NBC News, Retail Dive, Cosmetics Business coverage of Forma Brands Chapter 11, 2023

name: Motif FoodWorks
summary: Motif FoodWorks developed plant-based protein and heme ingredients for food manufacturers as a spinout of Ginkgo Bioworks, but weakening demand for alt-protein ingredients led it to wind down and sell assets in 2024.
lesson: B2B ingredient suppliers to alt-protein are only as healthy as the consumer demand of the brands they supply.

name: Motif Investing
funding: ~$126M (Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Renren, Norwest Venture Partners)
summary: Thematic/algorithmic investing platform letting users trade baskets of stocks ('motifs') built around themes and quantitative screens. Ceased brokerage operations in May 2020, selling its technology and patents to Charles Schwab, after failing to reach profitable scale against the shift to zero-commission trading.
lesson: A differentiated thematic/algorithmic investing product couldn't survive the industry-wide move to zero-commission trading, which eliminated the fee revenue the business model depended on.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Motif Investing shutdown and Schwab IP sale (2020)

name: Motional
summary: $4B robotaxi joint venture between Aptiv and Hyundai built on nuTonomy's technology. After repeated delays, paused commercial robotaxi launch plans in 2024, cut roughly 550 jobs (about 40% of staff), and saw Aptiv slash its ownership stake from 50% to about 15% and stop funding further development, pushing commercial launch to 2026.
lesson: Even a well-funded automaker-Tier1 joint venture can stall out when both parents lose patience with the multi-year, multi-billion-dollar burn rate required to reach true driverless commercial scale.

name: Movandi
summary: Developed 5G mmWave repeater and AI-driven beamforming technology to extend mmWave coverage for mobile operators and fixed wireless access. Shut down operations in 2023 after mmWave 5G deployments failed to scale as broadly as anticipated.
lesson: Betting heavily on mmWave 5G becoming a mainstream deployment mode was a market-timing risk; when carriers deprioritized mmWave in favor of mid-band spectrum, the addressable market shrank under the company.
source: Light Reading coverage of Movandi shutdown, 2023

name: Moven
summary: One of the earliest AI-driven neobanks, offering real-time spending analytics and behavioral coaching. Failed to scale its direct-to-consumer banking business against Chime, Simple and other rivals, and pivoted to licensing its technology (Moven Enterprise) to banks before effectively winding down its core business around 2019-2020.
lesson: Being an early mover in AI-driven consumer banking wasn't enough; without a durable unit-economics advantage, better-funded neobanks caught up and out-executed the pioneer.
source: American Banker, Finextra coverage of Moven's B2B pivot and decline (2019-2020)

name: MoviePass
summary: A subscription service offering nearly unlimited movie theater tickets for a flat monthly fee; the company paid full price for every ticket redeemed, resulting in catastrophic losses.
lesson: A subscription price below your per-unit cost of goods sold is not a growth strategy, it's a countdown to insolvency.

name: Moxie Software
summary: Enterprise CRM and knowledge-management vendor Moxie Software (formerly Consona/Onyx CRM lineage) wound down after failing to compete with cloud-native CRM suites, with assets later absorbed by other vendors.
lesson: Legacy on-premise CRM vendors carrying private-equity debt struggled to fund the cloud transition fast enough to survive against SaaS-native competitors.
source: Industry trade press coverage of Moxie Software's decline

name: Msg.ai
summary: Enterprise chatbot and conversational AI platform Msg.ai (formerly Snaps' rival) wound down operations around 2019 after failing to secure follow-on funding.
lesson: First-generation enterprise chatbot vendors often underestimated how much NLP infrastructure and integration work was needed to satisfy brand customers.
source: Industry reporting and Crunchbase records on Msg.ai's wind-down

name: Mt. Gox
summary: Once handling over 70% of all bitcoin transactions, the Tokyo exchange collapsed after admitting roughly 850,000 BTC had been lost or stolen through years of undetected hacking.
lesson: Exchanges holding customer assets need auditable, professional-grade security from day one.

name: Multichain
summary: Cross-chain bridge protocol handling billions in TVL collapsed in mid-2023 after its CEO was arrested by Chinese authorities and hundreds of millions in user funds mysteriously moved out of its contracts.
lesson: Centralized control over supposedly decentralized bridge infrastructure creates a single point of catastrophic failure.
source: Multichain official statements and on-chain analysis, July 2023

name: MultiOn (AGI Inc.)
summary: Early browser-automation 'personal AI agent' API that struggled to differentiate once OpenAI, Anthropic and Google shipped native computer-use/agent features, forcing a rebrand toward a narrower AGI research bet under the name AGI Inc.
lesson: Point-solution browser agents are easily commoditized once model labs ship the same capability natively.
source: AI Market Watch, Crunchbase, company site

name: Munchery
summary: On-demand prepared meal delivery startup shut down abruptly in 2019, leaving customers and vendors unpaid.
lesson: Food delivery logistics have thin margins that heavy funding cannot mask indefinitely.

name: Mutable AI
summary: Mutable AI launched an AI-powered code-refactoring and documentation tool (AI Genie) but saw little public product activity or funding news after 2023, effectively fading from the competitive AI coding assistant landscape as larger players dominated the category.
lesson: In a fast-consolidating category, small teams without a follow-on funding event or a clear enterprise wedge can simply go quiet rather than formally shut down — a slow fade that is just as much a failure as a public closure.

name: Muve Music
summary: Mobile carrier-bundled unlimited music streaming service offered by Cricket Wireless that was discontinued in 2014 after Cricket's acquisition by AT&T.
lesson: Carrier-bundled media products are highly exposed to shifts in the parent telecom's ownership and strategic direction.

name: Muxtape
summary: Simple mixtape-sharing website that went viral quickly but was forced by the RIAA to shut down its music-hosting feature within a year of launch.
lesson: Viral growth without licensing agreements in place is a temporary state, not a strategy.

name: Mycroft AI
summary: Open-source voice assistant project aiming to offer a privacy-respecting alternative to Alexa and Google Assistant; struggled to reach sustainable revenue and the founder announced the company was ceasing operations in 2023.
lesson: Open-source idealism around a privacy-first voice assistant could not generate enough revenue to compete with free, subsidized offerings from Amazon and Google.
source: Mycroft AI founder statement, Hackaday coverage

name: MYnd Analytics (CNS Response)
summary: MYnd Analytics (originally CNS Response) developed the PEER EEG-based platform to guide psychiatric medication selection but never achieved commercial scale, its stock collapsing and the company effectively winding down its core business over the 2010s-2020s.
lesson: A scientifically interesting biomarker platform without a clear reimbursement pathway can languish as a micro-cap for years without reaching viability.

name: Myspace
summary: The dominant social network of the mid-2000s lost its lead to Facebook after clumsy redesigns, ad clutter, and management turmoil under News Corp ownership.
lesson: Acquisition by a media conglomerate can smother the product agility that made a startup win.

name: Mythic
summary: Mythic developed analog in-memory-compute AI chips for edge inference along with a software platform, but ran critically low on cash in 2022, laid off most staff and nearly shut down before restructuring with new leadership and reduced operations.
lesson: Novel chip architectures (analog compute) carry execution risk on top of ordinary AI-hardware capital intensity, and near-death restructuring often permanently shrinks ambition.

name: Naberly (proptech AI leasing assistant)
summary: Small AI chatbot startup providing automated leasing and resident-communication assistants for property managers; shut down in 2022 after failing to raise a Series A amid a tightening venture funding environment for early-stage proptech.
lesson: Early-stage AI leasing assistants face intense competition from well-funded incumbents (like RealPage/Chatbot providers) and need a fast path to differentiated traction before seed capital runs out.

name: Nada (fractional real estate AI investing)
summary: Platform enabling fractional home-equity investing paired with AI-driven property valuation and selection; wound down consumer operations in 2023 after failing to scale its fractional-ownership model amid a slowing housing market and investor pullback from novel real estate finance products.
lesson: Novel AI-driven fractional ownership structures face a double burden of proving both the technology and an unfamiliar financial product to a skeptical market.

name: Namely
summary: HR and payroll platform for mid-sized companies raised over $260M at a high valuation but conducted repeated rounds of layoffs starting in 2019 and shrank significantly as it failed to reach profitable scale against Rippling, Gusto, and TriNet.
lesson: Raising at aggressive valuations without a path to profitability leaves a company exposed once investor sentiment shifts.

name: Nanigans
summary: Facebook ad-buying platform Nanigans, once a fast-growing adtech firm, shut down in 2019 after losing customers to in-house tools and larger competitors.
lesson: Point-solution ad-buying tools are exposed when platform owners and holding companies build competing in-house capabilities.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday coverage of Nanigans shutdown 2019

name: Nanosolar
summary: Thin-film CIGS solar panel maker, once one of the most hyped cleantech startups, that quietly shut down after years of production delays and missed targets.
lesson: Heavy pre-revenue hype and funding cannot substitute for solving hard manufacturing yield problems.

name: Napster
summary: The pioneering peer-to-peer music-sharing service was shut down by court injunction in 2001-2002 after a landmark RIAA copyright infringement lawsuit.
lesson: A product built on unresolved legal liability can be shut down overnight regardless of user love.

name: Nara Logics
summary: Nara Logics built an AI recommendation and personalization engine marketed for marketing and retail use cases, but wound down operations in the early 2020s after failing to achieve the scale needed to compete with larger personalization and recommendation platforms.
lesson: Horizontal AI personalization infrastructure without owning the underlying customer data or channel is vulnerable to being out-executed by platforms that already sit inside the marketing stack.
source: Crunchbase, industry reporting on Nara Logics wind-down

name: Nasty Gal
summary: Fast-growing online fashion retailer that used data-driven trend forecasting and personalized email merchandising to build a cult following, reaching a ~$350M valuation. Filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in November 2016 after overexpansion into physical stores and inventory mismanagement outpaced its data-driven merchandising discipline; assets sold to Boohoo Group in 2017.
lesson: Strong algorithmic merchandising signals can be overridden by poor inventory and expansion discipline at the executive level — data tools don't compensate for operational mismanagement.
source: Trade press coverage of Nasty Gal Chapter 11 bankruptcy, November 2016

name: Natero
summary: Customer success software startup Natero was acquired by Freshworks in 2019 and folded into Freshsuccess, discontinuing Natero as a standalone product.
lesson: Undersized customer-success vendors often end up as feature acquisitions rather than standalone winners once larger suites decide to compete directly.

name: National CineMedia
summary: The largest cinema advertising network in the US filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 due to a heavy debt burden and slower-than-expected post-COVID theater attendance recovery.
lesson: Even a market-leading position can't offset a debt structure built for pre-pandemic attendance levels.

name: NationSwell
summary: A media platform covering social innovation and civic solutions journalism wound down its consumer media operations around 2020, shifting fully to a private membership and consulting model.
lesson: Solutions-oriented journalism can struggle to build a large enough ad-supported audience, forcing a pivot away from public media entirely.

name: Navya
summary: French publicly listed maker of driverless electric shuttles. After firing its CEO over revenue shortfalls and failing to find new investors, filed for receivership in January 2023 and was converted to judicial liquidation in April 2023, with its business sold for a token €1.4M to Gaussin/Macnica.
lesson: A public listing does not solve a hardware-plus-software AV business's fundamental unit-economics problem if shuttle deployments never scale beyond subsidized pilots.

name: Naya Health
summary: Naya Health, maker of a $1,000 'smart' wearable breast pump, went dark on customer support in late 2018 and officially shut down in January 2019 after failing to raise more capital or find a buyer.
lesson: Premium hardware for new parents must ship a reliable product before scaling marketing; unresolved device defects combined with thin cash reserves killed trust and the company simultaneously.

name: Neato Robotics
summary: Robot-vacuum maker was acquired by German appliance company Vorwerk in 2017, which discontinued the Neato brand entirely in 2023 after failing to keep pace with Roomba and Chinese rivals.
lesson: Being acquired by a strategic parent does not guarantee long-term survival if the parent later exits the category.
source: The Verge coverage of Neato Robotics brand discontinuation, 2023

name: Neeva
summary: Ad-free AI search engine built on proprietary ranking and language models by former Google advertising executives. Shut down its consumer product in 2023, with the team and technology acquired by Snowflake.
lesson: A subscription-funded alternative to ad-supported search faces a brutal uphill battle for consumer habit change; pivoting the underlying AI/search technology to enterprise use was ultimately more viable than the original consumer bet.
source: TechCrunch, The Verge coverage of Neeva shutdown and Snowflake acquisition, 2023

name: Neighborhood Goods
summary: A 'department store of the future' startup that opened rotating-brand locations in Texas, New York, Austin and Newport Beach shut down all four stores in January 2024 after staff cuts and lease terminations, without filing for bankruptcy.
lesson: Novel retail-store concepts must prove unit economics at one location before signing additional high-rent leases.

name: Neoforma
summary: Healthcare-supply B2B marketplace that IPO'd at the peak of the dot-com bubble, saw its stock collapse over 99%, and was eventually acquired by GHX at a small fraction of its former value.
lesson: A marketplace built to IPO on hype rather than proven unit economics leaves shareholders exposed when sentiment turns.
source: SEC filings and GHX acquisition announcement (2006)

name: Neon (AI video app)
summary: Consumer AI app experimenting with generated video/content features had a brief viral spike on app store charts before rapidly fading from relevance as the market flooded with similar novelty AI camera/video apps, illustrating the extremely short half-life of undifferentiated consumer generative-AI apps.
lesson: Undifferentiated novelty consumer AI apps riding app-store trends face brutal retention curves; without a defensible product wedge, virality alone cannot sustain a company.

name: Neos
summary: UK smart-home insurer that used IoT sensors to prevent water and fire damage claims ceased underwriting new business in 2023 after its backers concluded the model could not scale profitably.
lesson: IoT-driven loss prevention can improve claims ratios but still needs enough scale to cover customer acquisition and hardware costs.

name: Neos Insurance
summary: UK connected-home insurer combining IoT sensors with AI-driven risk monitoring; stopped writing new policies under its own brand in March 2020 after majority owner Aviva absorbed its technology and book into other brands.
lesson: Smart-home and IoT-driven underwriting proved better suited as a feature bolted onto an established carrier's brand than as a standalone consumer insurance company.

name: Nerdy Inc.
summary: Parent company of Varsity Tutors, which built an AI-powered instructor-matching and adaptive tutoring platform; went public via SPAC at a ~$1.6B valuation in 2021 but its stock subsequently fell more than 90% amid weak profitability and slowing enrollment growth.
lesson: SPAC-era edtech valuations often front-loaded pandemic tutoring demand; public markets punished the mismatch between growth-stage cost structures and normalized post-pandemic revenue.
source: SEC filings (NRDY); public stock price history since 2021 SPAC listing

name: Nervana Systems
summary: AI chip and deep-learning research startup acquired by Intel in 2016 to anchor its AI hardware strategy. Intel discontinued the Nervana NNP chip line in 2019 after acquiring Habana Labs, effectively abandoning the Nervana bet.
lesson: Even a strategic acquisition by a chip giant doesn't guarantee product survival if the parent company later decides a competing internal or acquired technology is a better bet.
source: CRN, AnandTech coverage of Intel's Nervana discontinuation, 2019

name: Netography
summary: AI/ML-powered network detection and response (NDR) startup providing cloud-scale network traffic analytics without deployed sensors. Netography cut staff, including its marketing department, in February 2024 as part of a broader wave of cybersecurity venture belt-tightening, reflecting the difficulty smaller NDR entrants faced competing against larger, better-capitalized platforms.
lesson: In a capital-intensive category like network detection and response, a smaller AI-native entrant can be squeezed by funding downturns well before its product differentiation has time to compound.
source: SecurityWeek, 'Layoffs Hit Security Vendors Okta, Proofpoint, Netography' (Feb 2024)

name: Netsocket
summary: Software-defined network virtualization company offering a 'Virtual Network Operating System' aimed at service providers building NFV/SDN infrastructure. Ceased operations in 2016 after failing to convert early operator trials into large, recurring commercial contracts.
lesson: Early NFV/SDN virtualization startups often outran actual carrier operational readiness; the sales cycle for transformational network software proved much longer than typical venture timelines allow.

name: Neural.love
summary: AI image generation, upscaling, and restoration service built a modest paying user base but remained a small, largely undifferentiated player as larger, better-marketed, better-funded competitors (Midjourney, Leonardo, Ideogram) dominated the same core text-to-image use case, limiting its growth to a long-tail niche.
lesson: Competing directly on general text-to-image generation without a distinct wedge or well-funded marketing engine relegates a tool to long-tail status once category leaders establish dominant mindshare.

name: Neurala
summary: Neurala developed AI vision systems originally for consumer robotics before pivoting toward industrial inspection and government/defense applications including border-security and Army-funded pilots. Repeated pivots across markets (toys, robotics, manufacturing QA, defense) left the company without a single scaled commercial anchor.
lesson: Serial market pivots (consumer robotics to industrial QA to defense) each reset the sales cycle and customer-learning curve, burning capital without compounding traction in any one vertical.
source: general industry reporting on Neurala's product pivots and government pilots

name: Neuromation
summary: Neuromation raised roughly $60M through a 2018 initial coin offering to build a blockchain-incentivized marketplace for synthetic data generation, distributed model training and data labeling. The company later admitted that widely publicized engagement figures (claims of ~40 million AI-generated training images) were misleading and removed them from its site; the venture subsequently faded from the market as the broader 2018-2019 ICO/crypto downturn made its token-based business model unsustainable.
lesson: Tying a legitimate technical product (synthetic data generation) to a speculative token economy invites both regulatory and credibility risk; inflated usage claims that later require public retraction erode trust irreparably and accelerate decline once hype-driven capital dries up.
source: The Business of Business, ICO analysis coverage

name: New Age Meats
summary: New Age Meats built cultivated pork sausage prototypes and ran early tastings but shut down in 2024 as investor appetite for pre-revenue cultivated-meat startups collapsed.
lesson: Cultivated meat startups without a clear regulatory approval and commercialization timeline struggle to raise follow-on capital.

name: New Wave Foods
summary: New Wave Foods developed plant-based shrimp alternatives made from algae and plant proteins but quietly ceased operations around 2022 amid weak retail traction and funding constraints.
lesson: Niche plant-based seafood alternatives face a much smaller addressable market than plant-based beef or chicken.

name: NewsCred
summary: A content-marketing platform that licensed news content and built enterprise content workflow tools sold its licensing business and shut down after burning through significant venture funding.
lesson: Pivoting from content licensing to enterprise SaaS marketing tools didn't resolve an underlying inability to reach profitability.

name: Newsvine
summary: An early citizen-journalism and social-news site was acquired by MSNBC, gradually deprioritized, and formally shut down in 2017.
lesson: Citizen-journalism platforms without a growth strategy were largely displaced once social networks absorbed that function.

name: Newsweek (IBT Media era)
funding: Sold for $1 in 2010, later relaunched under IBT Media
summary: After decades as a leading American newsweekly, Newsweek was sold by the Washington Post Company for $1 in 2010, ceased its print edition in 2012-2013, and its later owner IBT Media faced fraud investigations tied to Olivet University.
lesson: A storied legacy media brand can be run into serious financial and ethical trouble by owners chasing traffic and dubious ties over sustainable journalism.

name: Newsy
summary: A video-first news outlet aimed at cord-cutters was rebranded as Scripps News and effectively wound down as a distinct national news brand in 2023 amid Scripps' broader restructuring and cost-cutting.
lesson: An innovative video-news format still needs a sustainable distribution and revenue plan independent of a parent company's shifting strategic priorities.

name: Nexage
summary: Mobile ad exchange Nexage was acquired by AOL's Millennial Media in 2015 for around $107.6M, and its technology was subsequently absorbed and its independent brand discontinued as Millennial Media itself was later acquired.
lesson: Mobile ad exchanges without unique inventory access got consolidated into larger holding companies rather than scaling independently.
source: AdExchanger, Mobile Marketer coverage of AOL-Nexage acquisition 2015

name: Nexiwave
summary: Canadian speech-to-text and voicemail/call-recording search startup that offered enterprise voice search technology; wound down as larger cloud-based transcription and speech-analytics vendors captured the enterprise market.
lesson: Niche enterprise voice-search tools without a strong distribution channel were squeezed out once cloud providers bundled comparable transcription features.

name: Nexosis
summary: Nexosis built an API-based machine-learning-as-a-service platform allowing enterprises to add predictive analytics without hiring data scientists, but shut down in 2019 after failing to scale against larger cloud ML API offerings from AWS, Google and Microsoft.
lesson: Machine-learning-as-a-service APIs are difficult to defend once hyperscalers bundle equivalent capabilities into their core cloud platforms at scale.

name: NextVR
summary: NextVR, a VR live sports and events broadcasting company, was acquired by Apple in 2020 for a reported fraction of its prior valuation in what was widely viewed as an acquihire rather than a strategic win for investors.
lesson: Live VR broadcasting required continuous heavy production investment while paying audiences for VR sports content remained small.

name: Nikola Corporation
summary: Hydrogen and electric truck maker Nikola filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2025 after founder Trevor Milton was convicted of fraud for exaggerating the company's technology.
lesson: Fabricating product demonstrations to inflate investor confidence eventually collapses the entire enterprise once exposed.
source: Reuters, February 2025, Nikola Corporation Chapter 11 filing

name: Node (Node.io)
summary: Node built an AI 'relationship intelligence' engine that scored and mapped business relationships to surface warm-path sales introductions. Despite significant Series B funding, the company was unable to convert its data platform into a sustainable enterprise sales motion and quietly shut down around 2019.
lesson: A powerful data/AI engine without a clear, sticky end-user workflow struggles to justify enterprise contract renewals.

name: Nokia OZO
summary: Nokia's OZO, a professional 360-degree VR camera for cinematic content production, was discontinued in 2017 as part of Nokia's broader exit from the VR business after weak sales.
lesson: Even an established hardware manufacturer can misjudge demand for professional VR production tools when the downstream consumer VR content market fails to grow as projected.

name: Noodle.ai
summary: Supply-chain/manufacturing 'FlowOps' AI platform backed by ServiceNow, Honeywell and TPG; despite raising over $100M, it rebranded to 'Daybreak' and raised a fresh round framed as an early Series A.
lesson: A rebrand paired with a reset round labeled as early-stage despite prior nine-figure raises usually signals a down round or restructuring.

name: Noodle.ai (Daybreak)
summary: San Francisco enterprise AI company selling 'FlowOps' supply-chain optimization software to manufacturers. Underwent four rounds of layoffs in three years amid chronic unprofitability and organizational churn, and ultimately rebranded as Daybreak.
lesson: Repeated 'unplanned' layoff cycles and a rebrand under distress are hallmark signs that enterprise AI sales cycles were too long and expensive relative to contract value.
source: Glassdoor, diginomica, Built In San Francisco (2021-2024)

name: Nori
summary: Nori built a blockchain-and-data-driven marketplace for verifying and trading soil-carbon removal credits, aiming to be the transparent tracking layer for farmer-generated carbon. It shut down its exchange in 2024 as the voluntary carbon market's oversupply and credibility crisis collapsed demand for early-stage MRV marketplaces.
lesson: Building the tracking/verification infrastructure for a market ahead of durable buyer demand leaves a startup exposed when that underlying market corrects; infrastructure plays still need a market that shows up.
source: Carbon Pulse coverage of carbon-credit exchange shutdowns (2024)

name: Norse Corporation
funding: reported $30M+ raised, including a $12M investment from KPMG Capital months before collapse
summary: Big-data threat-intelligence company that claimed to use a global sensor network and analytics to visualize live cyberattacks. After failing to ship promised product capabilities that sales teams had already sold, missing revenue targets, and burning through fresh KPMG funding, Norse laid off ~30% of staff in January 2016, fired its CEO, and effectively disbanded within weeks.
lesson: Flashy live-attack-map marketing does not equal a sellable product; a sales organization promising features engineering hasn't built is a leading indicator of collapse.
source: Krebs on Security, 'Sources: Security Firm Norse Corp. Imploding' (Jan 2016); Forbes CEO interview

name: North (Focals)
summary: North, maker of the Focals smart glasses, was acquired by Google in 2020 in an acquihire deal that discontinued the Focals product line and shut down customer support shortly after.
lesson: Consumer smart glasses required both a mature display supply chain and mass consumer demand that had not yet arrived, despite heavy funding.
source: The Verge reporting on Google-North acquisition, 2020

name: North American Star League (NASL)
funding: Backed by parent company e-Sports Entertainment Association
summary: The StarCraft II and multi-title tournament league ceased operations in 2015 after failing to secure sustainable sponsorship and broadcast revenue.
lesson: Tournament leagues tied to a single dominant title are vulnerable when that game's competitive audience declines.

name: Northvolt
summary: Europe's flagship battery gigafactory startup filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection after production delays, quality issues, and a cash crunch despite billions raised.
lesson: Even massively funded gigafactory bets can fail if production ramp-up problems burn cash faster than customers can be delivered to.

name: NS8
summary: Fraud-prevention startup collapsed in September 2020 after founder/CEO Adam Rogas was charged by the SEC with fabricating customer revenue and bank statements to raise over $100M; he later pleaded guilty and was sentenced to five years in prison.
lesson: Fabricated metrics can inflate a funding round for a while, but fraud eventually collapses the company entirely once discovered.

name: NSO Group
summary: NSO Group built Pegasus, an AI-assisted spyware platform sold to governments for intelligence and law-enforcement surveillance. After Pegasus was found deployed against journalists, dissidents, and diplomats, the U.S. Commerce Department blacklisted NSO in 2021, WhatsApp/Meta won a major U.S. lawsuit against it, and the company has struggled with debt, management turnover, and a collapsed valuation ever since.
lesson: Selling dual-use AI surveillance tools to any government customer without end-use controls creates existential legal and reputational exposure once abuse cases surface.
source: general public reporting on NSO Group U.S. Commerce Department blacklisting and WhatsApp v. NSO litigation

name: Ntropy
summary: Ntropy built privacy-conscious financial-data enrichment and labeling infrastructure adjacent to synthetic/privacy-preserving data tooling for fintech ML. After an initial $11M raised across rounds through October 2022, no subsequent funding round has been publicly reported, suggesting the company either plateaued at a small scale or struggled to raise a follow-on round in the tighter 2023-2025 venture market for infrastructure-layer AI tooling.
lesson: A quiet, multi-year absence of follow-on funding announcements after an early raise is itself a warning sign in infrastructure-tooling categories; investors should treat funding silence as seriously as an explicit down-round when diligencing a company's trajectory.

name: Nucleus
summary: Maker of a voice- and video-intercom smart home device with built-in Amazon Alexa integration, aimed at family communication across rooms and homes; shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach profitable scale in the crowded smart-home hardware category.
lesson: Smart-home voice hardware needed both large device volumes and recurring revenue to be sustainable, and Nucleus achieved neither before capital ran out.
source: Company shutdown notice, smart-home trade press

name: Nuix
summary: Australian e-discovery and investigative analytics software company whose 2020 IPO was followed by an accounting and disclosure scandal; shares fell over 90% and it faced an ASIC regulatory investigation.
lesson: Pre-IPO revenue forecasts and related-party dealings need independent scrutiny to avoid post-listing collapse.
source: Australian Financial Review and ASIC investigation reports on Nuix (2021-2022)

name: Numerade
summary: AI and video-based STEM homework help platform that raised a large Series B at a unicorn-adjacent valuation during the pandemic edtech boom, then conducted major layoffs and pivots as post-pandemic demand and free ChatGPT-based alternatives eroded its market.
lesson: Pandemic-era edtech valuations priced in growth rates that reverted sharply once schools reopened and generative AI chatbots became free substitutes for paid homework-help products.

name: Numerate
summary: One of the earliest AI-driven small-molecule drug discovery companies, Numerate partnered with pharma on several disease programs but never achieved a major independent drug approval or acquisition, gradually scaling down and fading from the industry around 2019-2020.
lesson: Being early to an AI drug discovery thesis is not enough — without a breakthrough clinical result or major partnership renewal, quiet attrition is the default outcome.
source: Xconomy and industry archives on early AI drug discovery startups

name: Numinus Wellness
summary: Numinus Wellness built a network of psychedelic-assisted therapy clinics and a research/production arm in Canada, but its stock collapsed and the company underwent major restructuring and leadership changes as the psychedelic clinic model failed to reach profitability.
lesson: Vertically integrating clinics, research, and drug production simultaneously multiplies capital intensity faster than a single early-stage company can fund.
source: Canadian business press, company investor filings

name: Nuritas
summary: Irish AI company used machine learning to discover bioactive peptides from natural sources for pharma, nutrition, and skincare applications, but after ambitious hiring and a slower-than-expected path to commercial peptide royalties, was forced into deep layoffs during the 2023 biotech funding downturn.
lesson: AI-discovery companies that sell into multiple end-markets (pharma, consumer, agri) risk spreading thin the capital and focus needed to prove out any single one commercially.
source: Irish Times and Endpoints News coverage of Nuritas layoffs (2023)

name: Nuro
summary: Autonomous delivery-robot company (R2 vehicle) that raised over $2B including a landmark SoftBank round, then cut roughly 30% of staff in 2022 and again scaled back operations in 2024, pivoting from operating its own delivery fleet to licensing its self-driving stack to automakers and mobility companies.
lesson: Even the best-funded autonomy startups can be forced into a licensing pivot when the capital and time required to operate a proprietary vehicle fleet at scale outlast investor patience.

name: Nurx
summary: Direct-to-consumer telehealth startup for birth control and sexual health prescriptions was merged into Thirty Madison in 2022, with the standalone Nurx brand discontinued the following year after regulatory scrutiny over prescribing practices.
lesson: Consolidation is often the endpoint for DTC telehealth brands that can't sustain independent scale.

name: Nutonian
summary: Creator of the Eureqa automated model-discovery platform, acquired by DataRobot in 2017. The standalone Eureqa product line was subsequently discontinued and folded into DataRobot's broader AutoML platform rather than continuing independently.
lesson: An acquisition can still represent a functional product failure when the acquiring company discontinues the acquired technology as a standalone offering.

name: Nuzzle
funding: Raised via Indiegogo crowdfunding plus undisclosed seed funding
summary: No-monthly-fee cellular GPS pet collar maker that quietly stopped processing orders and ceased operations without formally disclosing a shutdown or return plan.
lesson: A 'no fees' business model for a cellular-connected hardware product is hard to sustain long-term, since the company still bears ongoing network and cloud costs without recurring revenue to cover them.
source: Company Facebook page and customer reports of ceased operations

name: Oasis Labs
summary: Oasis Labs set out to build a privacy-first, confidential-computing cloud platform combined with blockchain technology (later spinning out the Oasis Network) for privacy-preserving data marketplaces and ML. After raising $48M across early rounds, the company's visibility and independent product news largely faded as its blockchain focus diverged from enterprise privacy-computing demand, leaving the original privacy-preserving AI vision without a clear standalone commercial outcome.
lesson: Bundling a genuinely useful enterprise technology (confidential computing) with blockchain-native go-to-market and tokenomics can dilute focus; when crypto-market enthusiasm cools, the underlying enterprise privacy product can get stranded without dedicated commercial momentum.

name: Oath Inc (Verizon Media)
funding: Verizon-owned, acquired AOL for $4.4B and Yahoo for $4.5B
summary: Verizon combined AOL and Yahoo into Oath/Verizon Media hoping to build an ad-tech and media rival to Google and Facebook, but wrote down billions in value and sold the unit to Apollo Global Management in 2021 for just $5B, far below the roughly $9B it had paid.
lesson: Bundling legacy media and ad-tech assets doesn't create a duopoly competitor without genuine platform advantages.
source: Wall Street Journal, CNBC coverage of Verizon Media sale to Apollo 2021

name: Oberlo
summary: Dropshipping tool that let Shopify merchants import products from AliExpress was acquired by Shopify in 2017 and discontinued in 2022 as Shopify shifted strategy toward other fulfillment partners.
lesson: Even category-defining tools can be discontinued once the parent platform's strategic priorities shift.

name: ObjectVideo
summary: Early pioneer of video-analytics computer vision for security and surveillance built a large patent portfolio and pivoted increasingly toward patent licensing and litigation before winding down as an operating product company by around 2017 amid legal disputes and restructuring.
lesson: A strong early patent position in computer vision is not a substitute for a durable operating product business as the underlying technology commoditizes.
source: Trade press coverage of ObjectVideo's patent-licensing pivot and eventual wind-down

name: Ocean Hugger Foods
summary: Ocean Hugger Foods made tomato-based "Ahimi" tuna and eggplant-based "Unami" eel alternatives for sushi restaurants, but wound down operations in the early 2020s after failing to scale beyond niche foodservice accounts.
lesson: Plant-based seafood analogues aimed narrowly at foodservice lack the retail volume needed to reach sustainable scale.

name: OctoML
summary: Model optimization and deployment platform built around the open-source Apache TVM compiler, which ceased independent operations in September 2024, with much of its technology and team moving to Nvidia.
lesson: Model-compiler optimization infrastructure got squeezed as chip vendors (Nvidia) and cloud providers increasingly built equivalent optimization directly into their own stacks.
source: Reports of OctoML shutdown and Nvidia talent move, September 2024

name: Oculus Story Studio
summary: Facebook's in-house Oculus Story Studio, creators of award-winning VR shorts like Henry and Dear Angelica, was shut down in 2017 as Facebook redirected content investment toward interactive games over passive VR films.
lesson: Even a well-funded internal studio can be shut down when the parent company concludes the content format isn't driving hardware sales.
source: Facebook Oculus Story Studio closure announcement, 2017

name: Ofo
summary: Chinese dockless bike-share giant that expanded to 20+ countries but collapsed under debt, deposit-refund scandals, and cutthroat competition with Mobike, retreating from most markets by 2019.
lesson: Blitzscaling into dozens of countries simultaneously without a path to profitability invites collapse.

name: Ohi
summary: Distributed micro-fulfillment startup for D2C brands that shut down in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable unit economics in a soft e-commerce funding environment.
lesson: Asset-light distributed fulfillment networks still carry heavy fixed costs that punish thin margins.

name: OJO Labs
funding: $217M+ (Series D, investors incl. Northwestern Mutual, Breyer Capital)
summary: AI-powered conversational real estate assistant (Ai, the AI concierge) that matched homebuyers with agents; underwent major layoffs in 2022-2023 and significantly scaled back operations amid falling transaction volumes and profitability pressure, though it continued in reduced form.
lesson: Consumer-facing conversational AI in real estate still depends on transaction-linked revenue, so a housing slowdown directly starves the AI product of monetizable volume.

name: Olive AI
summary: Healthcare AI automation startup once valued at $4B shut down and sold off business units after burning through nearly a billion dollars without delivering on its broad AI-automation promises.
lesson: Trying to automate too many disparate workflows at once can prevent an AI startup from proving value in any single one.

name: Ollie
summary: Fresh dog food subscription startup that competed with The Farmer's Dog and Nom Nom but underwent significant layoffs and scaled back operations in 2023 amid a difficult fundraising environment.
lesson: Being an early mover in a category isn't enough once better-capitalized competitors dominate marketing spend and reduce a smaller player's growth runway.

name: OlympusDAO
summary: DeFi 'reserve currency' protocol (OHM) known for its '(3,3)' staking meme saw its token collapse over 99% from late-2021 highs once the unsustainable staking APY model failed as new capital inflows slowed.
lesson: Tokenomics that depend on ever-increasing new buyer inflows to fund existing holder yields are structurally a Ponzi dynamic.
source: OlympusDAO token price history and DeFi post-mortem coverage, 2022

name: Onboard AI
summary: Onboard AI (formerly getonboard.dev) built an AI chatbot for quickly understanding unfamiliar codebases to speed up developer onboarding, but the feature was rapidly commoditized as every major AI coding assistant added codebase-wide chat and context retrieval natively.
lesson: A single-purpose 'codebase Q&A chatbot' proved to be a feature, not a company, once general-purpose coding assistants absorbed the same capability as a standard part of their offering.

name: OnDeck Capital
funding: ~$2.1B in debt and equity; IPO raised $200M in 2014
summary: Early online small-business lender using proprietary algorithmic underwriting (the 'OnDeck Score'). Its stock collapsed more than 90% from its 2015 highs as loan-loss provisions rose and growth slowed; the company was ultimately acquired by Enova International in 2020 at a fraction of its IPO valuation.
lesson: Being an algorithmic-lending pioneer and going public early doesn't protect against a collapse in investor confidence once credit losses rise faster than the model can adapt; the company avoided outright shutdown only via a distressed-price acquisition.
source: Reuters, American Banker coverage of Enova's acquisition of OnDeck (2020)

name: Onduo (Verily)
summary: Alphabet's Verily launched Onduo as an AI-powered virtual diabetes clinic combining connected devices and coaching, but after failing to scale profitably even with Alphabet's backing, Verily discontinued the service in 2022 and folded remaining capabilities into other product lines.
lesson: Even a well-capitalized corporate parent will shut down an AI health service that can't demonstrate a credible path to standalone unit economics.
source: CNBC and Fierce Healthcare coverage of Verily's Onduo discontinuation (2022)

name: One Concern
summary: Stanford-born One Concern promised AI-driven, block-by-block disaster and climate-resilience predictions for cities, insurers and utilities. It raised large rounds and struck high-profile partnerships (including with SoftBank and SOMPO), but reporting and employee reviews describe a persistent gap between marketed prediction accuracy and delivered product value, alongside recurring layoffs.
lesson: Catastrophe-prediction AI is judged on real-world accuracy under real disasters; overselling model certainty to land marquee partnerships erodes trust faster than it builds a defensible business.
source: Glassdoor employee reviews, TechCrunch/Crunchbase funding coverage of One Concern

name: One Kings Lane
funding: ~$237M; sold to Bed Bath & Beyond for ~$30M (2016)
summary: Home-decor flash-sale e-commerce site that used data-driven, personalized daily sales curation, once valued near $900M. Sold to Bed Bath & Beyond in 2016 for a small fraction of capital raised after burning through funding without reaching profitability; the site was fully shut down in 2019.
lesson: Flash-sale personalization drove strong early growth metrics that masked an unsustainable cost structure, leading to a steep valuation collapse once growth slowed.
source: Trade press on Bed Bath & Beyond's 2016 One Kings Lane acquisition and 2019 shutdown

name: Onerep
summary: Data-removal service that suffered a major reputational scandal in 2024 when it was revealed its founder had also created several of the people-search sites the service was meant to scrub users from.
lesson: A privacy service's credibility depends entirely on the integrity of its operators.

name: OneWeb
summary: LEO satellite broadband constellation builder filed Chapter 11 in March 2020 when a SoftBank-led funding round collapsed amid COVID-19 market turmoil; later rescued and merged into Eutelsat.
lesson: Capital-intensive satellite constellations need financing committed well before a downturn, not mid-buildout when investors can walk away.

name: OnLive
summary: A cloud-gaming pioneer years ahead of broadband infrastructure, OnLive burned through massive funding before selling its patents to Sony and shutting down.
lesson: Being technologically first doesn't matter if the underlying infrastructure isn't ready for mass adoption.

name: Onto
summary: UK all-inclusive EV subscription service Onto (formerly E-Car Club) collapsed into administration in 2023 after failing to raise a new funding round amid rising interest rates.
lesson: Subscription models built on fleet ownership are highly sensitive to financing costs and can fail fast when capital markets tighten.
source: Sky News, October 2023, Onto administration filing

name: Ontrak (formerly Catasys)
summary: Ontrak provided AI-driven behavioral health coaching for health plan members, but its stock collapsed over 90% in 2021 after its largest health-plan client terminated its contract, triggering years of restructuring and delisting risk.
lesson: Extreme customer concentration in enterprise health-plan contracts creates existential single-point-of-failure risk.

name: Onvia
summary: B2G/B2B procurement marketplace whose stock cratered after the dot-com bust; survived for two decades as a smaller public company before being acquired by DemandStar at a fraction of its original valuation.
lesson: A marketplace can survive a bust and still never recover its original growth trajectory or valuation.

name: Onyx Software
summary: CRM vendor Onyx Software was acquired by Consona Corporation in 2010 after years of shrinking market share against Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics, and the product was eventually discontinued.
lesson: On-premise CRM vendors that were slow to move to cloud delivery lost ground rapidly once SaaS CRM alternatives matured.

name: OpenAI Codex (standalone API, 2021)
summary: OpenAI's original standalone Codex API model (powering the first GitHub Copilot) was deprecated as a directly accessible product in March 2023 as OpenAI folded code-generation capability into its general-purpose GPT model line instead of maintaining a separate specialized coding API.
lesson: Even a foundational, category-defining model can be discontinued as a standalone product when a parent company decides a unified general-purpose model line serves the business better than maintaining a specialized fork — a reminder that being 'first' doesn't guarantee the original product form survives.

name: Openbay
summary: Online auto repair marketplace Openbay, which connected drivers to repair shops for quotes, ceased consumer operations around 2019 after failing to reach scale against local competition.
lesson: Marketplace models in hyper-local, fragmented service categories need dense geographic liquidity that is expensive to build.
source: Auto repair industry trade press coverage of Openbay's 2019 wind-down

name: OpenDoor's early competitor Knock (unrelated to Home Swap entry, historical B2C listing app)
summary: Early-stage home-swap matching app using algorithmic matching to pair buyers and sellers of similar homes; shut down in 2016 after failing to gain traction before pivoting the team and brand into the later, better-funded Knock Home Swap venture.
lesson: An early algorithmic matching concept without enough capital or data density often needs a full relaunch under new funding to have a real shot at product-market fit.

name: Openfit
summary: Digital fitness app Openfit, spun up by Beachbody to diversify beyond its MLM-style sales model, was shut down in 2023 as its parent company retrenched to its core BODi brand.
lesson: A parent company under financial pressure will cut newer diversification bets before addressing its core business problems.

name: Openpay
summary: Australian buy-now-pay-later company entered voluntary administration and was delisted in 2023 after failing to achieve profitability across its consumer and business lending books.
lesson: BNPL unit economics that depend on low default rates collapse quickly once credit conditions tighten.

name: Openwave Mobility
summary: Mobile network traffic optimization and video/data compression software provider spun out to help operators manage mobile data traffic growth through analytics-driven optimization. Its business was wound down and sold off in pieces around 2018 as the underlying traffic-management market matured and consolidated around larger network vendors.
lesson: Traffic-optimization middleware businesses were vulnerable to being absorbed into network equipment vendors' broader software suites once the point-solution market matured.
source: Telecom trade press coverage of Openwave Mobility's wind-down, 2018

name: Operator
summary: Conversational commerce startup Operator, founded by Robin Chan, shut down in 2018 after its human-plus-bot shopping concierge model failed to reach product-market fit.
lesson: Blending human agents with automation to reduce cost is easy to pitch but hard to make unit-economically sustainable at consumer scale.

name: OpTic Gaming (original entity)
summary: Financial disputes between founder Hector 'H3CZ' Rodriguez and investors led the original OpTic Gaming entity to cease operations in 2020, before the brand was relaunched under Immortals Gaming Club in 2021.
lesson: A beloved brand can survive its founding company's collapse only if a new owner is willing to relaunch it.

name: Optimus Ride
summary: Boston-based low-speed autonomous shuttle startup valued near $165M that ran driverless routes in closed campuses and retirement communities but could not reach commercial scale. Quietly acqui-hired by Magna International in January 2022, with about 120 engineers joining Magna while the Optimus Ride brand and independent operations were dissolved.
lesson: Geofenced low-speed AV pilots can prove technology but rarely generate revenue fast enough to justify continued independent funding.
source: TechCrunch, Boston Globe, The Robot Report

name: Orbital Insight
summary: Orbital Insight was an early leader in AI-driven geospatial intelligence analytics for defense and intelligence customers, backed by In-Q-Tel and Google. Its cellphone location-data business collapsed after Apple's App Tracking Transparency and Life360 cutting off data access; by late 2023 it was down to a $50M valuation seeking emergency loans to avoid bankruptcy, and in 2024 it was acquired as a rescue deal by Steve Wozniak's Privateer Space rather than liquidating.
lesson: Geospatial-intelligence startups that lean on third-party consumer location data are exposed to platform policy shifts (like Apple ATT) that can gut the business model overnight.

name: Osaro
summary: Applied reinforcement learning to warehouse robotic-picking automation, but never reached the reliability or scale needed to compete with Covariant and Berkshire Grey, and quietly wound down operations.
lesson: Reinforcement-learning-for-robotics startups face brutal reliability bars in real warehouses that lab demos routinely understate.

name: Osmo
summary: AI-and-camera-based interactive learning system for young children (physical game pieces recognized via computer vision) acquired by Byju's in 2019; suffered inventory write-downs and business decline as Byju's overall financial distress deepened from 2022 onward.
lesson: Hardware-software AI-education products require sustained retail and manufacturing investment; when an acquirer's cash position deteriorates, capital-intensive hardware lines are often cut first.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Byju's-Osmo acquisition 2019 and later writedowns

name: Osterhout Design Group (ODG)
summary: AR smart glasses pioneer ODG filed for bankruptcy in 2019 after failing to convert years of prototype development into sustainable enterprise or consumer sales; its patents were sold off.
lesson: Decades of hardware R&D without a clear path to commercial shipment can exhaust investor patience even with strong patent portfolios.

name: Otherside / Cortex (AI film pre-viz startup)
summary: Small AI-assisted film pre-visualization and storyboard generation startup targeting independent filmmakers raised a modest seed round but could not sustain a large enough paying customer base in the notoriously budget-constrained independent-film market, and was largely absorbed into freelance/consulting work by its founders rather than continuing as a funded company.
lesson: Targeting independent filmmakers as a primary paying customer base is extremely difficult given the segment's low and inconsistent budgets; enterprise studio or platform distribution deals are usually required to make a creative-AI tool durable.

name: Otonomo
summary: Connected-car data marketplace Otonomo dissolved in 2024 after its SPAC-era valuation collapsed and it failed to find sustainable demand for aggregated vehicle data.
lesson: A data marketplace needs proven buyer demand at scale before building expensive data-aggregation infrastructure.

name: Otonomos
summary: Blockchain-based corporate legal structuring startup aiming to put company incorporation and shares on-chain; wound down as the 2018-2019 crypto downturn dried up demand and funding for blockchain-legal ventures.
lesson: Legal-tech products tightly coupled to a speculative technology cycle are vulnerable when that cycle collapses.
source: Crunchbase and Deal Street Asia coverage of Otonomos

name: Otta
summary: London-based tech-job-matching platform for startups was acquired by Kandidate/folded into a competitor in 2024 after struggling to scale its curated-matching model amid a slower tech hiring market.
lesson: Curated tech-hiring marketplaces are highly cyclical and vulnerable when the broader tech job market contracts.
source: Sifted/UK tech press coverage of Otta 2024

name: Otto
summary: Self-driving truck startup founded by ex-Google engineers, acquired by Uber in 2016; dissolved as a distinct unit by 2018 amid the Waymo trade-secrets lawsuit against Uber and its self-driving trucking pullback.
lesson: Acquiring technical talent under legal clouds can create massive downstream liability that ends the program entirely.

name: Otto (Uber self-driving trucks)
summary: Self-driving truck startup founded by ex-Google/Waymo engineer Anthony Levandowski, acquired by Uber for roughly $680M in stock just months after founding in 2016. Became the center of the landmark Waymo v. Uber trade-secret theft lawsuit, which Uber settled for about $245M in 2018, the same year Uber shut down the Otto trucking program entirely.
lesson: Diligence on IP provenance and founder conduct matters as much as the technology itself - Uber's rushed acquisition of Otto imported catastrophic legal liability that outweighed the technology's value.
source: TechCrunch, Forbes, Harvard JOLT coverage of Waymo v. Uber

name: Ours (formerly Feels)
summary: Ours offered guided relationship and couples therapy sessions but shut down in 2023 as the niche couples-therapy telehealth category failed to reach sufficient scale to sustain the venture-backed business.
lesson: A narrow sub-vertical of an already crowded telehealth category can be too small to support independent venture-scale growth.

name: Outdoor Voices
summary: Athleisure D2C brand's founder Ty Haney was ousted as CEO in 2020 amid mounting losses (~$2M/month); the company later closed its stores and was acquired by Consortium Brand Partners in 2024 to avoid bankruptcy.
lesson: Public founder-board conflict combined with unchecked cash burn can force a distressed sale even for a beloved consumer brand.
source: Fortune, Inc., Retail Dive coverage of Outdoor Voices founder ouster and 2024 sale

name: Ouya
summary: The Android microconsole raised a then-record Kickstarter but shipped an underpowered device with a weak game library and was sold off to Razer in 2015.
lesson: Crowdfunding hype doesn't substitute for a compelling, differentiated hardware and software ecosystem.

name: Overwatch League
funding: Activision Blizzard-funded, ~$20M+ franchise fees per team
summary: Activision Blizzard's city-based franchised league charged teams tens of millions in entry fees but folded in 2023 after several team owners departed amid disputes over revenue sharing and unmet viewership projections.
lesson: Charging franchise owners large upfront fees based on projected media rights value is risky if that value never materializes.

name: Owlet Baby Care
