summary: Owlet went public via SPAC in 2021 but received an FDA warning letter that October for marketing its Smart Sock baby pulse-oximeter monitor without clearance, forcing it to halt sales and crashing its stock over 20% in a single day.
lesson: Consumer health-adjacent baby hardware that makes medical-sounding claims must secure regulatory clearance before scaling; going public without resolving that risk exposed investors to catastrophic downside.
source: FDA Warning Letter, October 2021; InvestorPlace, October 2021

name: Owlstone Medical
summary: UK breath-biopsy company combined chemical sensing with AI/machine-learning analysis to detect diseases like cancer non-invasively from exhaled breath, but slower-than-expected clinical validation and reimbursement pathways forced substantial layoffs during a tight funding market in 2023.
lesson: Novel biomarker-plus-AI diagnostics platforms often need far more clinical validation capital and time than initially modeled, especially for a genuinely new sample type like breath.
source: MedTech Dive and Cambridge Independent coverage of Owlstone Medical's 2023 layoffs

name: own3d.tv
summary: The German esports livestreaming platform shut down in 2014, unable to compete with Twitch's scale and funding despite an early focus on the European esports scene.
lesson: A regional streaming platform needs either a defensible local moat or global scale to survive against a well-funded incumbent.

name: Ozmo
summary: AI-powered product support and troubleshooting platform Ozmo shut down in 2023 after failing to convert telecom and retail pilots into durable, profitable enterprise contracts.
lesson: AI support tools sold into telecom and retail enterprises face long sales cycles that can outlast a startup's runway before contracts scale.
source: Industry and Crunchbase reporting on Ozmo's 2023 shutdown

name: Ozy Media
summary: The digital media startup collapsed within weeks in 2021 after a New York Times investigation and a fraudulent investor conference call exposed inflated traffic and revenue claims.
lesson: Fabricating metrics to investors is an existential risk, not a growth hack.

name: Pachama
funding: ~$80M (incl. backers Serena Williams, Ellen DeGeneres, Amazon Climate Pledge Fund)
summary: Pachama used satellite imagery, LIDAR and machine learning to monitor and verify forest-carbon offset projects. Its own chief scientist quit over data showing the company continued selling credits from projects with rising deforestation, and after carbon-market prices collapsed, Pachama cut roughly 20 employees and was ultimately acquired by Carbon Direct in a distressed sale.
lesson: When your AI is the credibility layer for a market, internal doubts about your own model's integrity are an existential risk, not a PR problem — ignoring them undermines the product's entire reason for existing.
source: Investigative reporting on Pachama credit quality; Trellis coverage of Pachama layoffs; Carbon Direct acquisition announcement

name: Pachyderm
summary: Data versioning and pipeline orchestration platform for ML (data lineage/'Git for data'), which laid off the majority of its staff and effectively wound down its commercial operations in mid-2023 after failing to convert its technology into sufficient enterprise revenue.
lesson: Data versioning infrastructure proved to be a feature enterprises wanted bundled into broader platforms rather than a standalone product they would pay a premium for.
source: Reports of Pachyderm layoffs and wind-down, 2023 (VentureBeat, industry newsletters)

name: PancakeBunny
summary: BSC-based yield-farming protocol was exploited via a flash loan attack in May 2021, crashing its BUNNY token by over 95% in a single day and effectively ending the project's relevance.
lesson: Yield protocols using token minting mechanisms tied to pool prices are vulnerable to flash-loan price manipulation.

name: Pantellos Group
summary: Utility-industry procurement marketplace consortium formed by major US utilities that was eventually shut down and its functions absorbed by other e-procurement vendors.
lesson: A marketplace owned collectively by its largest buyers has weak incentive to open the network to broader competition.
source: Utility industry trade press on Pantellos wind-down

name: Panther Labs
funding: over $220M raised; valued at roughly $1.1B in 2022
summary: Cloud-native, detection-as-code SIEM platform incorporating machine learning-driven analytics for large-scale security data. After reaching unicorn status in 2022, former employees describe a pattern of recurring layoffs euphemistically framed as 'restructures,' alongside frequent executive departures and strategic pivots, even as the company continued operating.
lesson: A unicorn valuation earned during a funding peak can mask an unsustainable burn rate; when growth slows, repeated 'restructures' rather than one transparent resizing erode both morale and investor confidence.

name: Papa
summary: An on-demand companionship and errands marketplace pairing college students ('Papa Pals') with seniors, sold heavily through Medicare Advantage plans; a 2023 Bloomberg investigation into abuse allegations triggered mass contract cancellations from Humana, Aetna and other major payers.
lesson: When your entire revenue model depends on a handful of insurer contracts, safety and vetting failures can unwind years of growth almost overnight.

name: PaperExchange.com
summary: Paper-industry B2B trading exchange that wound down operations after failing to convert its user base of buyers and mills into a sustainable transaction fee business.
lesson: Listing and directory traffic is not the same as committed transaction volume in a marketplace business model.
source: Industry trade coverage of dot-com era paper marketplaces

name: Parallel Wireless
summary: Open RAN software and virtualized radio access network vendor using AI-driven network orchestration to let operators mix multi-vendor radio equipment. Underwent severe financial distress, layoffs and leadership departures starting in 2023 after major operator contracts (including in emerging markets) failed to generate expected returns.
lesson: Chasing large emerging-market Open RAN contracts to drive growth can backfire when those deployments carry thin margins and payment risk, straining cash reserves even for a heavily funded company.
source: Light Reading and Fierce Network coverage of Parallel Wireless financial distress, 2023

name: Parature
summary: Customer service software provider Parature was acquired by Microsoft in 2014 for roughly $100M and folded into Dynamics 365, with the standalone product retired by 2018.
lesson: Point-solution helpdesk vendors acquired by platform giants are frequently absorbed and discontinued rather than scaled.

name: Parcelly
summary: UK click-and-collect parcel locker network startup that shut down in 2018 after struggling to build a large enough retail pickup-point network to be viable.
lesson: Network-effect logistics businesses need critical mass of physical pickup points before they become useful to consumers.

name: Pareteum Corporation
summary: Telecom connectivity software firm inflated revenue by over $40M from non-binding purchase orders; executives were later criminally charged and the company collapsed into Chapter 11.
lesson: Recognizing revenue on commitments customers never actually made is fraud, and it eventually destroys the company it was meant to prop up.

name: Parler
summary: The 'free speech' social network lost hosting and app-store access after the January 6, 2021 Capitol riot, and never recovered its user base.
lesson: Dependence on a handful of infrastructure and distribution gatekeepers is an existential single point of failure.

name: Parrot (consumer drones)
summary: French electronics company Parrot exited the consumer drone market around 2019-2020 after years of shrinking market share against DJI, laying off much of its drone division and pivoting to enterprise and defense drones.
lesson: A dominant incumbent can rapidly capture a hardware category, leaving even well-capitalized challengers no path to profitable share.
source: Reuters coverage of Parrot's consumer drone exit, 2019

name: Parse
summary: Popular mobile backend-as-a-service acquired by Facebook for roughly $85M in 2013, then shut down in 2017 despite serving hundreds of thousands of apps, as it no longer fit Facebook's strategic priorities.
lesson: Even a widely-used developer platform can be shut down by an acquirer if it doesn't align with the parent company's core business strategy.

name: Participant Media
summary: A social-impact film and TV studio (Spotlight, An Inconvenient Truth) ceased operations in 2023 after nearly two decades and dozens of Oscar-nominated films.
lesson: A prestigious, mission-driven content strategy can still be financially unsustainable as distribution economics shift under streaming.

name: Patch
summary: Patch built infrastructure and an API-driven marketplace for carbon credit sourcing and retirement, aiming to be the tracking layer connecting buyers to verified removal projects. As the carbon-removal industry contracted amid the loss of federal support and weak growth capital, Patch's own CEO described the company being forced into a defensive pivot toward strategic partnerships rather than independent scale.
lesson: When policy tailwinds that underpinned a market's growth thesis reverse, infrastructure providers positioned for hypergrowth must be able to survive on much thinner volumes than their model assumed.
source: Trellis, Latitude Media coverage of carbon removal industry contraction (2025)

name: Patch (AOL)
summary: AOL's ambitious network of hundreds of hyperlocal news sites was gutted in 2013-2014 with mass layoffs and site closures after failing to prove a sustainable local-ad revenue model at scale.
lesson: Scaling a local-news format to hundreds of towns simultaneously outpaces the ability to prove unit economics work in even one.

name: Path
summary: Intimate 'close friends' mobile social network never reached scale against Facebook; sold to Daum Kakao in 2015 and shut down in 2018.
lesson: A beautifully designed niche product can still lose to the incumbent's network effects.

name: Paw Protect (Embrace Pet Insurance)
funding: Launched and funded internally by parent company Embrace Pet Insurance
summary: Digital-first pet insurance brand launched by Embrace that was discontinued in September 2025, with the parent company stopping new policy sales and winding the brand down as existing policies expire.
lesson: Even an established insurer with underwriting expertise can conclude a secondary digital-first brand isn't worth continuing if it fails to differentiate enough from the parent's core product in a crowded market.
source: NerdWallet; U.S. News Paw Protect brand discontinuation coverage, 2025

name: Pawp
summary: Pet telehealth and emergency-fund membership startup marketed as a pet insurance alternative that underwent significant layoffs and scaled back growth ambitions after early rapid expansion.
lesson: Marketing a subscription product as an 'insurance alternative' invites regulatory and consumer-trust scrutiny that can slow growth once the initial marketing-driven surge fades.
source: Industry reporting on Pawp staffing changes and business model scrutiny

name: Paxata
summary: Self-service data preparation platform acquired by DataRobot in 2019; the standalone product was discontinued shortly after as DataRobot integrated its technology.
lesson: Data-prep tools raised huge sums competing for a market that hyperscalers and BI vendors later absorbed natively.

name: PeachDish
summary: PeachDish was an Atlanta-based Southern-cuisine meal-kit company that partnered with regional retailers but shut down in 2019 as larger national players like HelloFresh and Blue Apron dominated the category.
lesson: Regional meal-kit players struggle to match the marketing and logistics scale of national competitors.

name: Peapod
summary: One of the earliest online grocery delivery services, owned by Ahold Delhaize, was shut down in most U.S. markets in 2021 amid competition from Instacart and retailer-owned delivery.
lesson: First-mover advantage in ecommerce grocery erodes if the technology platform isn't continually modernized.

name: Pear Therapeutics
summary: Pioneer of FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutics, including AI-driven behavioral algorithms for substance use and insomnia, Pear could not get insurers to reimburse its products at scale, burning through its funding before filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy and selling off assets.
lesson: FDA clearance for an AI-enabled therapeutic is meaningless without a reimbursement pathway that pays for the ongoing cost of delivering it.
source: STAT News and Fierce Healthcare bankruptcy coverage (2023)

name: Pebble
summary: Pioneering smartwatch that ran two record-breaking Kickstarter campaigns but couldn't compete with Apple Watch and Fitbit; assets acquired by Fitbit in 2016.
lesson: Crowdfunding hype doesn't guarantee a sustainable business against deep-pocketed competitors.

name: Pebble Technology
funding: $35M+ VC raised plus record Kickstarter campaigns
summary: Smartwatch pioneer Pebble, popular for fitness and notification tracking, ran out of cash and sold its software assets to Fitbit in 2016, ceasing hardware production.
lesson: Crowdfunding success does not guarantee sustainable capital for ongoing hardware competition against deep-pocketed rivals.

name: Peeple
summary: 'Yelp for people' app letting users publicly rate and review other individuals; faced immediate, overwhelming public backlash over privacy and harassment concerns and never gained real traction.
lesson: Applying a review-platform model directly to human beings crosses a trust threshold that most of the public will reject outright.

name: PeerStreet
summary: Real estate debt investing marketplace that let retail investors buy fractions of mortgage loans wound down operations after loan defaults spiked and liquidity dried up.
lesson: Marketplaces built on credit assets are extremely exposed to interest-rate and default cycles.

name: Peloton
summary: Connected fitness hardware maker whose pandemic-era boom collapsed post-2021, wiping out over 90% of its stock value and forcing out its founder amid mass layoffs.
lesson: Scaling manufacturing and headcount for pandemic-level demand becomes a crippling liability the moment demand normalizes.

name: Peloton Interactive
summary: Peloton's valuation collapsed from roughly $50B to under $2B after pandemic-driven overexpansion in manufacturing and headcount met a sharp post-pandemic demand drop, triggering mass layoffs and a CEO change.
lesson: Scaling production and hiring to match a temporary demand spike can destroy value once the pull-forward reverses.

name: Peloton Technology
summary: Developer of truck 'platooning' technology that let pairs of trucks drive at close following distances using vehicle-to-vehicle communication and automated braking to improve fuel efficiency and safety. Repeatedly missed its own commercialization deadlines and ceased operations, entering liquidation in summer 2021.
lesson: Platooning promised clear fuel-savings ROI but repeatedly slipped commercialization timelines against regulatory and fleet-adoption friction until capital ran out.

name: Penrose Studios
summary: Penrose Studios, an award-winning VR animation studio founded by Eugene Chung, shut down in 2019 after failing to build a sustainable business model around VR narrative films.
lesson: Critical acclaim for VR storytelling did not translate into a commercially sustainable studio absent a large paying VR audience.
source: Variety reporting on Penrose Studios closure, 2019

name: Peppertype.ai
funding: Backed by Bombay Play/gaming-studio founders, undisclosed amount
summary: Peppertype.ai launched as a self-serve AI content-generation tool with a popular AppSumo lifetime-deal offering, but around 2023-2024 discontinued its standalone self-serve product entirely, folding into an enterprise-only platform (pepper.inc) with no public pricing, leaving existing lifetime-deal customers with a degraded or non-functional product.
lesson: Selling lifetime deals to fund a self-serve AI product creates a customer base with permanent low-margin obligations that can force an abrupt, reputation-damaging pivot away from that market segment.
source: AppSumo customer reviews (2024-2025), AI content tool review coverage of Peppertype.ai's shift to pepper.inc

name: Perceptive Automata
summary: Boston startup building 'theory of mind' AI (SOMAI) to help autonomous vehicles predict pedestrian and cyclist intent. Ceased operations in August 2022 after failing to close a Series B round, with co-founders citing the broader AV industry's pullback in R&D spending.
lesson: Picks-and-shovels AI vendors selling into the AV stack live or die by their customers' capital cycles, not their own technical merit.

name: Perch (Texas iBuyer)
funding: $1.1B+ (equity + debt, investors incl. Village Global, Khosla Ventures)
summary: Texas-based iBuyer using AI-driven valuation to make instant offers and provide a 'trade-in' service for homebuyers; wound down its buy-and-sell operations in 2023 as rising rates and falling home values made its balance-sheet model untenable.
lesson: Regional iBuyers face the same fundamental AI-pricing-lag risk as national ones, just at a smaller and often less diversified scale, making them equally vulnerable to rate shocks.

name: Percolata
summary: Retail workforce-scheduling and foot-traffic analytics startup backed by prominent Silicon Valley investors wound down operations around 2020 after a co-founder lawsuit and inability to scale beyond early retail pilot customers.
lesson: Workforce analytics tools face a difficult sale when they ask retail managers to justify staffing cuts, slowing enterprise adoption regardless of the data's accuracy.

name: Percolate
summary: Content marketing platform Percolate raised over $130M at a high valuation but was sold to Seismic in 2019 in what was widely reported as a distressed, fire-sale acquisition.
lesson: Heavily-funded enterprise martech startups can still fail if sales cycles and retention don't justify the burn rate.
source: TechCrunch, Business Insider coverage of Percolate/Seismic deal 2019

name: Persado
summary: AI marketing-language platform Persado, once valued near unicorn status, was acquired by WPP in 2024 in a deal reported to be well below its peak valuation after growth stalled.
lesson: AI-driven martech valuations built during a hype cycle can compress sharply once growth normalizes.
source: Wall Street Journal, AdExchanger coverage of WPP-Persado acquisition 2024

name: Persistent Surveillance Systems
summary: PSS ran wide-area 'spy plane' surveillance over Baltimore, using AI-assisted tracking to follow the movements of an entire city from the air. After ACLU litigation, the Fourth Circuit ruled the program unconstitutional, and Baltimore's city council formally terminated the contract, ending PSS's flagship U.S. deployment and stalling similar pitches to other cities.
lesson: Persistent AI surveillance of civilians triggers constitutional review; a single adverse appellate ruling can eliminate a government sales pipeline built around one flagship city.

name: Petal
funding: ~$400M+ (equity and debt, including Valar Ventures, Jefferies, Route 66 Ventures)
summary: Credit card company using machine learning ('Cash Score') to underwrite consumers with thin or no credit history using cash-flow data instead of traditional FICO scores. Cut roughly 30% of staff in 2022-2023 after losing a warehouse funding facility and facing a tougher consumer-credit environment, significantly scaling back new-account growth.
lesson: Cash-flow-based AI underwriting for thin-file consumers is a compelling alternative to FICO, but the business remains fundamentally dependent on warehouse debt markets, which can be pulled abruptly when macro credit conditions worsen.
source: TechCrunch, Bloomberg coverage of Petal layoffs and funding facility loss (2022-2023)

name: Petnet
summary: Smart automatic pet feeder company that suffered repeated cloud-server outages and, citing COVID-19 impact, furloughed staff and ceased operations in 2020, bricking customer devices.
lesson: Connected hardware companies carry an ongoing infrastructure cost burden that can wipe them out in a downturn, leaving customers with non-functional devices.

name: Petopia.com
summary: Online pet retailer backed heavily by Petco that collapsed after the dot-com crash, selling its assets to Petco and cancelling a planned $100M IPO.
lesson: Strategic corporate backing does not protect an e-commerce startup from a broken unit-economics model when capital markets seize up.

name: Pets.com
summary: Online pet supplies retailer famous for its sock-puppet mascot burned through IPO proceeds on marketing and cheap shipping of heavy, low-margin goods before closing in 2000.
lesson: Marketing spend cannot compensate for a fundamentally unprofitable shipping and margin structure.

name: Petuum
summary: Enterprise 'AI OS' platform aiming to automate ML model building and deployment as autonomous agents for large enterprises, backed heavily by SoftBank's Vision Fund, but shut down in 2023 after failing to convert research strength into a durable enterprise product business.
lesson: SoftBank-scale funding can accelerate research staffing but cannot substitute for a repeatable enterprise sales motion.

name: Petuum Inc
summary: CMU professor Eric Xing's SoftBank-backed AI platform, including industrial and manufacturing automation offerings, shut down after burning through funding amid reports of founders struggling to run the business.
lesson: Research pedigree doesn't substitute for operating discipline.

name: Petzila
funding: Publicly traded on OTC markets prior to acquisition
summary: Maker of the Petzi Treat Cam smart pet camera that was acquired by smart-collar maker Wagz in 2018 to consolidate the connected-pet hardware category; its product line was ultimately discontinued when parent Wagz abruptly ceased all operations in 2023.
lesson: Being acquired by another cash-constrained hardware startup does not eliminate shutdown risk; it just delays and consolidates it, as both companies' products died together when the acquirer ran out of money.
source: PR Newswire Wagz-Petzila acquisition announcement, 2018; The Smart Snout on Wagz shutdown, 2023

name: Phantom Auto
summary: Remote-driving (teleoperation) software provider for robotaxis, forklifts, yard trucks and sidewalk robots, with customers including Maersk and Serve Robotics. Ceased operations in March 2024 after a follow-on funding round fell through, laying off roughly 120 employees.
lesson: Picks-and-shovels providers to the AV industry are just as exposed to the sector's funding winter as the robotaxi operators themselves.

name: Phase Four
summary: In-space electric propulsion (radio-frequency thruster) manufacturer shut down in 2023 after failing to close a new funding round amid a broader downturn in space-tech venture investment.
lesson: Component suppliers to the satellite industry are exposed to the same funding cycles as their customers, compounding downturn risk.

name: Phazr
summary: 5G fixed wireless access and smart antenna startup aiming to deliver low-cost, software-driven 5G network infrastructure to operators. Quietly ceased operations after failing to secure sustained commercial deployments.
lesson: Hardware-heavy 5G infrastructure startups needed either a major carrier anchor customer or deep capital reserves; Phazr had neither in sufficient measure to outlast the pre-commercial 5G period.
source: Industry trade press coverage of Phazr's wind-down

name: Photobucket
summary: Once one of the largest photo-hosting sites on the web, it alienated millions of users in 2017 by abruptly demanding payment to keep third-party image links working, triggering a mass exodus.
lesson: Monetization changes that ignore how customers actually use a free product can destroy years of accumulated user trust overnight.

name: Phrasee
summary: AI-powered marketing copywriting platform Phrasee shut down in 2023, citing an inability to raise further funding amid a tightening venture market and rising competition from general-purpose generative AI tools.
lesson: Narrow AI-copywriting tools can be rapidly commoditized once general-purpose large language models arrive.
source: TechCrunch, Marketing Week coverage of Phrasee shutdown 2023

name: Picnic Works
summary: Seattle robotics company whose AI-guided Picnic Pizza Station let one worker make ~100 customized pizzas/hour for stadiums and high-volume venues; ceased operations after struggling to close a follow-on round.
lesson: A decade of iteration and rebrands without durable unit economics is a warning sign, not resilience.

name: Pie Insurance
summary: AI-driven workers' compensation underwriter for small businesses; suffered material adverse reserve development, notably in New York, from mis-calibrated risk models, leading to a credit-rating downgrade review and a 14% workforce layoff in 2023.
lesson: Workers' comp is a long-tail line where AI pricing models need years of claims development data to validate; Pie scaled faster than its loss models could be proven accurate.

name: Pika Labs
summary: AI video generation foundation model startup, an early leader in text-to-video. Faced intensifying competition from OpenAI's Sora, Runway, Luma, and Google's Veo, and reportedly struggled to retain users and differentiate its product through 2024-2025.
lesson: Being an early mover in a hot foundation-model category is not durable if the largest labs can quickly ship a competitive or superior version bundled into a broader platform.
source: The Information, tech press coverage of AI video-gen competitive landscape, 2024-2025

name: Pillo Health
summary: A Boston home health robot that dispensed medication, tracked adherence and connected patients (many elderly) with caregivers via facial recognition; its technology was absorbed by Stanley Black & Decker in 2020 and the product line was discontinued.
lesson: Consumer health robotics is one of the most capital-intensive categories in aging tech, and even novel, well-reviewed products often end as an acqui-hire rather than a standalone business.

name: Ping (Apple)
summary: Apple's music-focused social network embedded in iTunes failed to gain traction against Facebook and Twitter and was quietly shut down after two years.
lesson: Bolting a social network onto an existing product does not create genuine network effects.

name: Pivotal Commware
summary: Developed holographic beamforming smart antenna technology (backed in part by Bill Gates) intended to extend 5G mmWave and CBRS coverage more cheaply than traditional infrastructure. Shut down in 2023 despite substantial funding, citing inability to reach sustainable commercial scale.
lesson: Even well-capitalized, technically innovative network hardware startups can fail if the underlying deployment model (mmWave/CBRS densification) takes years longer to materialize at scale than projected.
source: Light Reading and GeekWire coverage of Pivotal Commware shutdown, 2023

name: Pivotal CRM
summary: Pivotal CRM, an enterprise CRM suite with roots in several acquisitions, was sunset by owner Aptean around 2020 as customers were migrated to newer platforms.
lesson: CRM platforms passed between multiple private-equity owners over decades rarely receive the reinvestment needed to remain competitive.

name: Pivotal Software
summary: Enterprise PaaS spin-off from EMC/VMware that IPO'd in 2018 but saw its stock collapse within a year, leading VMware to reacquire it in 2019 at a steep loss to public investors.
lesson: An enterprise PaaS built for a pre-Kubernetes world struggled once the market standardized on container orchestration.

name: Pixelbin / PixelBin.io
summary: Small AI-powered image optimization and generative editing API for e-commerce struggled to differentiate against larger, better-marketed image-AI platforms (Photoroom, Cloudinary's AI features) and remained a minor player with limited market traction and funding through 2024-2025.
lesson: A generic 'AI image API for e-commerce' pitch is not differentiated enough on its own once category leaders with much larger marketing budgets (Photoroom) and infrastructure incumbents (Cloudinary) cover the same use case.

name: Pixvana
summary: Pixvana built cloud-based VR video production and streaming tools (SPIN Studio) but shut down in 2020 after failing to reach sustainable revenue as VR video demand stayed niche.
lesson: Tooling businesses built around a content format (VR video) fail if the underlying consumer audience for that format never scales.

name: Placester
summary: Real estate website and marketing platform for agents that incorporated data-driven lead-scoring tools; shut down its consumer-facing platform in 2019 after failing to build a sustainably profitable SaaS business, though some assets/technology were absorbed elsewhere.
lesson: A crowded real estate agent-tools market punishes companies that can't differentiate their AI/data features enough to justify recurring subscription pricing.

name: Planet Mojo / WWA
summary: Web3 game studio whose WWA ('World Wide Agents') platform combined blockchain and AI gaming agents alongside titles like Mojo Melee and Mojo Maker. Shut down all games on July 1, 2025, citing unsustainable market conditions, pivoting the team to an unrelated AI video platform, MovieFlo.AI.
lesson: Bundling an experimental AI-agent layer onto an already-struggling crypto-gaming business compounded rather than solved the underlying unit-economics problem.

name: Planetary Resources
summary: High-profile asteroid-mining company backed by Google and Virgin founders ran out of funding in 2018 and sold its assets to blockchain company ConsenSys in a fire sale.
lesson: A speculative decades-out mission narrative can attract celebrity investors but cannot substitute for a near-term revenue plan.

name: Plant Prefab
summary: California-based prefab home manufacturer that laid off most staff and halted production in 2023 amid financial struggles despite high-profile backers.
lesson: Marquee investors and press attention do not substitute for proven manufacturing unit economics in capital-intensive housing.
source: The Real Deal, Dwell coverage of Plant Prefab struggles

name: Plastiq
summary: Bill-payment platform that let businesses pay non-card-accepting vendors with a credit card; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in May 2023 after burning through venture funding without a durable margin.
lesson: Interchange-fee arbitrage business models are vulnerable when partner banks or card networks tighten terms.

name: Plated
summary: Plated was one of the original meal-kit startups and was acquired by grocery chain Albertsons in 2017, but Albertsons shut the brand down in 2019 after failing to integrate it profitably.
lesson: Meal-kit acquisitions by grocers often fail when the acquirer cannot integrate a fundamentally different operating model.

name: Platfora
summary: Big-data visualization startup that raised over $100M but was acquired by Workday for a fraction of that in 2016; Workday discontinued the product in 2017.
lesson: Heavy capital consumption without proportional revenue growth in enterprise BI often ends in a distressed sale rather than an exit.

name: Playground AI
summary: Text-to-image generation and editing platform founded by former Google/Instagram engineer Suhail Doshi raised a large Series A in 2023 at a reported valuation near $300M, but by 2024-2025 struggled to keep pace on model quality and consumer mindshare against Midjourney, Ideogram, and the rapid proliferation of free/cheap alternatives, prompting a pivot of company focus.
lesson: A high-profile founder and a large early round buy time, not a guaranteed durable moat; in image generation, model-quality leadership shifts fast and every few months a new entrant can leapfrog on output fidelity.

name: PledgeMusic
summary: Direct-to-fan music crowdfunding platform that collapsed in 2019 after failing to pay artists funds raised on its site amid severe financial mismanagement.
lesson: Platforms holding customer or artist funds must maintain rigorous financial controls or risk total collapse.

name: Plenty
summary: SoftBank-backed vertical farming company Plenty, once valued near $1 billion with backing from Jeff Bezos, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in March 2025.
lesson: Even massive venture funding cannot substitute for a path to positive unit economics in indoor farming.

name: Plexxi
summary: Software-defined data center networking company combining optical interconnects with software-controlled network fabric optimization. After burning through substantial venture funding without reaching profitability, sold its assets to HPE in 2017 in a distressed transaction.
lesson: Combining novel optical hardware with software-defined networking created a compelling technical story but also very high capital requirements that outpaced the company's ability to reach scale before funding ran dry.
source: Trade press coverage of Plexxi's asset sale to HPE, 2017

name: Pluralsight
summary: Tech-skills learning platform that IPO'd in 2018 but saw its stock collapse amid slowing enterprise growth, leading Vista Equity Partners to take it private in 2021 at a steep valuation discount.
lesson: Enterprise subscription growth must keep pace with public-market expectations or valuations reset sharply.

name: Plus (PlusAI)
summary: Autonomous-trucking software developer that saw two separate SPAC mergers collapse - Hennessy Capital in 2021 and Churchill Capital IX in 2026 - amid weak public-market appetite for AV listings, repeatedly delaying its path to public capital while commercial trucking revenue remained limited.
lesson: Repeated failed SPAC attempts are a strong signal that public markets do not believe a capital-intensive autonomy business can hit its revenue targets - management should not keep retrying the same exit path.

name: Poachable
summary: Anonymous candidate-matching recruiting startup out of a Silicon Valley accelerator shut down after failing to reach scale in a crowded recruiting-marketplace field.
lesson: Anonymized matching removes signal recruiters rely on, making it a hard sell against incumbents.

name: Poached
summary: Restaurant and hospitality hourly-hiring marketplace shut down in 2023 after failing to scale profitably against larger hourly-work platforms like Instawork and Snagajob.
lesson: Vertical-specific hourly-hiring marketplaces face intense pressure once horizontal platforms enter their niche with more capital.
source: Restaurant/hospitality trade press on Poached shutdown

name: Podio
summary: Danish no-code work-management platform acquired by Citrix in 2012; Citrix formally sunset Podio in 2023 after years of limited investment.
lesson: Products acquired as adjacent bets inside a larger portfolio can be starved of investment and eventually retired.

name: Point (Point Technologies)
summary: US debit card and cash-back banking startup shut down in 2023, returning remaining customer funds after failing to secure additional funding.
lesson: Consumer neobanks funding rewards programs from interchange revenue alone often cannot sustain the model at scale.

name: PointGrab
summary: Israeli computer-vision company built low-resolution optical sensors for smart-building occupancy detection and gesture control, but after pivoting away from consumer gesture control to building analytics, it wound down operations around 2019 having failed to reach scale in either market.
lesson: Pivoting a vision sensor platform between consumer gesture control and enterprise building analytics can leave a company without deep expertise or a defensible position in either market.
source: Israeli tech press (Calcalist/Globes) coverage of PointGrab's wind-down

name: Poloniex
summary: Once a top-five global crypto exchange by volume, Poloniex's relevance declined sharply after 2019 delistings and a 2023 hack that drained over $100M, though it continues to operate at diminished scale.
lesson: Losing product and compliance leadership to newer exchanges can turn a former top-five platform into an afterthought even without a single fatal event.
source: Poloniex hack disclosure and exchange ranking data, 2023

name: Poppy (Meet Poppy)
summary: Poppy, a Seattle 'managed marketplace' for on-demand babysitters backed by Y Combinator and Madrona, shut down in December 2018 after failing to find a business model that paid sitters well while keeping prices affordable.
lesson: On-demand labor marketplaces in childcare face a structural margin squeeze: quality caregiving requires above-market wages that on-demand pricing often can't support.

name: Powa Technologies
summary: Mobile commerce and marketing platform Powa Technologies collapsed into administration in 2016 after its billionaire founder's claims of a multi-billion-dollar valuation proved unsupported by actual revenue.
lesson: Valuations built on founder hype rather than verifiable revenue eventually collide with reality.
source: Financial Times, BBC coverage of Powa Technologies collapse 2016

name: Power2SME
summary: Indian B2B procurement marketplace for small and medium manufacturers that was folded into and rebranded under Moglix in 2022 after failing to scale independently.
lesson: A niche procurement marketplace can be absorbed by a better-capitalized rival once its independent growth stalls.
source: Indian tech press coverage of the Power2SME-Moglix merger (2022)

name: Preact
summary: Customer success software startup Preact was acquired by Aptean in 2018, after which the product was discontinued as a standalone offering.
lesson: Small, thinly-funded SaaS tools in a crowded segment often end their run as a quiet technology acquisition rather than an independent success.

name: PrecisionHawk
summary: Agricultural drone-data pioneer PrecisionHawk wound down its commercial operations and sold off assets in 2023 after years of pivoting business models.
lesson: Repeated business-model pivots in a capital-intensive category can exhaust runway before any version reaches product-market fit.

name: Precog
summary: Precog (later rebranded Reify) built a big-data analytics and predictive-modeling platform for enterprises, but shut down its original product around 2014 after failing to achieve market traction during the crowded early big-data-platform era.
lesson: Entering the big-data-analytics platform race without a clearly differentiated wedge left the company exposed to faster-moving, better-funded competitors.

name: Precor
summary: Commercial fitness equipment maker Precor was acquired by Peloton for $420M in 2020 to build manufacturing capacity, then sold off in 2022 as Peloton retrenched from its overexpansion.
lesson: An acquisition made to solve a short-term capacity problem can be reversed at a loss once the acquirer's strategy changes.

name: Predictim
summary: AI startup that scored babysitters' risk level by scanning their public Facebook, Twitter and Instagram activity with sentiment/behavior analysis, marketed to parents as a hiring-screening tool.
lesson: Building hiring/screening AI on scraped social data invites platform bans and algorithmic-bias backlash before a business model can prove itself.

name: PredPol / Geolitica
summary: Predictive policing software rebranded as Geolitica ceased operations at the end of 2023 after studies found it barely more accurate than random guessing and reinforced racial bias.
lesson: Predictive policing algorithms that reinforce existing patrol bias without proven crime-reduction results eventually lose government customers.

name: Prescient (AASI)
summary: Structural framing systems company for modular and multifamily construction that underwent significant financial restructuring around 2023-2024 amid a slowdown in multifamily construction starts.
lesson: Construction-technology suppliers tied to a single end-market (multifamily) are highly exposed to that market's cyclical downturns.
source: Trade press coverage of Prescient/AASI restructuring

name: Prime Trust
summary: Crypto custodian and infrastructure provider serving fintech and crypto firms filed for bankruptcy in 2023 after a shortfall in customer crypto holdings was discovered.
lesson: Custodians must reconcile customer asset balances continuously; even a plumbing company can implode from unmonitored shortfalls.
source: Prime Trust Chapter 11 filings; Nevada Financial Institutions Division order, 2023

name: Primus Power
summary: Primus Power developed zinc-bromide flow batteries for grid-scale storage but wound down operations around 2019 after failing to reach commercial scale profitably.
lesson: Flow battery chemistries promise long-duration storage but require enormous capital to reach cost parity with lithium-ion at scale.

name: Principle
summary: Popular Mac app for designing animated and interactive UI prototypes whose development was discontinued in 2022 after its solo creator moved on.
lesson: Tools reliant on a single maintainer face existential risk if that person's priorities or capacity change.

name: Prior Knowledge
summary: Predictive modeling API startup (creator of the Veritable platform for probabilistic machine learning as a service), acqui-hired by Salesforce in 2012, with its standalone product discontinued shortly after the talent acquisition.
lesson: Very early 'ML-as-a-service' products often ended in talent acquisitions rather than product continuity because enterprise demand for self-serve predictive APIs hadn't yet matured.
source: Salesforce acquisition of Prior Knowledge coverage, 2012 (TechCrunch)

name: Prism Skylabs
summary: Video-analytics startup that helped retailers analyze in-store camera footage for customer behavior insights shut down in 2018 after failing to find a sustainable enterprise sales model.
lesson: Retail video analytics needs a clear, immediate ROI metric for store operators, or enterprise sales cycles will outlast the runway.

name: Prisma
summary: Berlin-based startup that shut down its hosted Prisma Cloud data-layer product in 2022 after a strategic pivot, laying off staff to refocus entirely on its open-source ORM.
lesson: When a startup's original hosted-platform business model fails to gain traction, refocusing on a narrower open-source niche can be the only path to survival.

name: Privacy Dynamics
summary: Seattle-based Privacy Dynamics built a data anonymization tool to help companies meet GDPR/CPRA compliance. Despite approaching $1M in revenue and processing billions of records for customers, the company shut down in 2025 after the founders concluded that product-market fit remained elusive — deals required outsized effort relative to payoff, and data privacy compliance never became a top-tier, urgent priority for buyers the way the generative-AI boom later did for other categories.
lesson: Compliance-driven privacy tooling can close real deals yet still fail if the underlying regulatory urgency is ambiguous and inconsistently enforced; without a forcing function that makes the problem unavoidable for buyers, sales efficiency never improves enough to reach profitability.

name: Project Ara (Google)
summary: Google's modular smartphone project let users swap out camera, battery, and other modules, but was cancelled in 2016 after failing to solve reliability and cost engineering challenges.
lesson: Modular hardware sounds compelling but real-world engineering tradeoffs can defeat the concept.

name: Promptu
summary: Early mobile voice-search and voice-control company that partnered with carriers like Sprint for voice-activated content search; faded and effectively ceased independent operations in the early 2010s as smartphone voice assistants (Siri, Google Now) took over the category.
lesson: Carrier-dependent voice search was overtaken almost overnight once smartphone platforms built voice assistants directly into the OS.

name: Prophesee
summary: French neuromorphic event-based vision-sensor company used in industrial machine vision and inspection filed insolvency in mid-2024; its technology was partly absorbed via an AMD licensing deal.
lesson: Novel sensor hardware needs one paying beachhead market, not three at once.

name: Prospect.io
summary: Belgium-founded Prospect.io built an AI-assisted email-finding and outbound sales-prospecting tool aimed at SMB sales teams. Facing intense competition from better-funded outbound platforms, the company scaled back and effectively ceased independent operations around 2020.
lesson: The SMB outbound-prospecting category became so crowded with similar tools that undifferentiated entrants struggled to retain customers against larger, better-marketed competitors.

name: Prosper Marketplace
summary: One of the original peer-to-peer lenders, later using machine-learning credit models to price and approve consumer loans. Valuation was marked down over 70% by investors, and the company underwent repeated rounds of layoffs (including cutting 28% of staff in 2016) and leadership turnover as loan volumes and marketplace investor confidence declined.
lesson: Marketplace lenders depending on institutional investor appetite to fund AI-underwritten loans are acutely vulnerable to swings in investor confidence; a credit-quality scare can starve the lending pipeline even if the underlying model is sound.
source: TechCrunch, Fortune coverage of Prosper layoffs and valuation markdowns (2016-2017)

name: Proterra
summary: Electric bus and battery systems maker Proterra filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after supply chain costs and production delays outpaced its ability to raise further capital, later selling its bus unit.
lesson: Diversifying into multiple capital-intensive product lines simultaneously (buses, batteries, charging) can spread resources too thin.

name: Proteus Digital Health
summary: Pioneer of ingestible 'digital pill' sensors that tracked medication adherence failed to find commercial traction with payers and providers and filed for bankruptcy in 2020.
lesson: A technically impressive product still needs a clear reimbursement path to become a business.

name: Provident Financial
summary: UK sub-prime doorstep lender shut down its consumer credit division in 2021 after regulatory pressure and a surge in customer complaints made the business model unviable.
lesson: Legacy consumer lending models can become unprofitable once compliance costs from a wave of mis-selling claims rise sharply.

name: Prowler.io / Secondmind
summary: Cambridge-based reinforcement-learning and decision-AI research lab. Rebranded to Secondmind in 2020 amid a pivot toward automotive/engineering AI applications, then quietly wound down operations.
lesson: A rebrand and vertical pivot can buy time but doesn't fix an underlying lack of a scalable, revenue-generating product-market fit.
source: UK tech press (Sifted, UKTN) coverage of Prowler.io/Secondmind, 2020-2022

name: Pulse Secure
summary: Enterprise VPN vendor's flagship product became one of the most widely exploited attack vectors of 2019-2021 after critical vulnerabilities were used in major ransomware campaigns, badly damaging its reputation before it was folded into Ivanti in 2020.
lesson: Chronic, widely-exploited vulnerabilities in a security product can destroy customer trust faster than any competitive pressure.

name: PurchasePro.com
summary: B2B procurement marketplace that partnered with AOL; collapsed after an accounting fraud scandal involving inflated revenue from the AOL deal, leading to SEC charges and criminal convictions of executives.
lesson: Revenue recognition manipulated through side deals eventually surfaces and destroys marketplace credibility overnight.
source: SEC litigation release on PurchasePro.com fraud case

name: Purple Carrot
summary: Purple Carrot was a pioneering plant-based meal-kit company that was acquired in 2022, but its new parent ceased operations for the brand in 2024 amid the broader meal-kit industry contraction.
lesson: Being first to a plant-based niche within meal kits does not protect against category-wide demand decline.

name: Pypestream
summary: Enterprise conversational AI/agent platform for customer engagement that repeatedly rebuilt its core technology stack and repositioned its pitch (chatbots, then conversational AI, then agentic AI) without matching the scale of rivals like Ada or Intercom, undergoing significant layoffs along the way.
lesson: Constant re-architecture and repositioning can be a sign a company hasn't found the wedge that makes customers stay.

name: Q-Bot
summary: UK robotics company building robots for under-floor insulation retrofits in construction that entered financial administration amid funding difficulties in the mid-2020s.
lesson: Construction robotics serving a niche retrofit market can struggle to reach volume before capital runs out, especially when government retrofit incentives are uncertain.
source: UK construction trade press coverage of Q-Bot administration

name: Q-Cells SE
summary: Germany's Q-Cells was once the world's largest solar cell manufacturer by output but filed for insolvency in 2012 after Chinese competitors drove panel prices down faster than it could cut costs.
lesson: Manufacturing scale in solar cells didn't protect a European incumbent from lower-cost Asian competition.

name: QuadrigaCX
summary: Canada's largest crypto exchange collapsed after founder Gerald Cotten died suddenly in 2018 allegedly holding the only keys to $190M in customer crypto, later found to have been running a Ponzi-like scheme.
lesson: Single-key custody with no institutional controls turns founder mortality into existential company risk.

name: Quanergy Systems
funding: SPAC merger implied $1.4B value; $200M+ raised pre-SPAC
summary: Solid-state lidar and perception-software maker for autonomous vehicles and security applications. Filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in December 2022, just ten months after a SPAC merger valued it at $1.4B, amid volatile capital markets and an inability to reach profitable scale.
lesson: A SPAC listing can mask, but not fix, negative unit economics - Quanergy went from public debut to bankruptcy in under a year once the market repriced pre-revenue hardware/AI names.

name: Quartet Health
summary: Used AI and analytics to match primary care patients with appropriate behavioral health providers and coordinate collaborative care, but after years of subscale growth and rising cash burn, ceased operations in 2023.
lesson: Care-coordination AI platforms depend on deep two-sided integration with both primary care and behavioral health providers — partial adoption on either side undermines the whole value proposition.
source: Behavioral Health Business and Fierce Healthcare coverage of Quartet Health's 2023 shutdown

name: Quartz
funding: Sold multiple times; last sold for reported ~$1
summary: A business-news outlet acclaimed for its mobile-first design was sold by Atlantic Media to Uzabase, then to G/O Media for a nominal price in 2023 after years of losses, before being resold again in 2024.
lesson: Editorial innovation and design acclaim don't insulate a digital publisher from being sold for parts once losses persist for years.

name: Querium
summary: AI-powered STEM tutoring platform (StepWise) that used machine learning to provide step-by-step math coaching for underserved students; funding and public activity effectively ceased in the early 2020s after failing to secure large-scale district or higher-ed adoption.
lesson: Grant- and small-round-funded AI tutoring companies targeting underserved populations often lack the capital runway to reach the scale needed for network effects in adaptive-learning data.
source: Crunchbase company status; SBIR/NSF grant records for Querium

name: Quibi
summary: Jeffrey Katzenberg and Meg Whitman's short-form premium streaming service raised $1.75B for mobile-only shows but shut down just six months after launch amid the pandemic and weak demand.
lesson: Massive funding and star power cannot substitute for validating that people actually want the format.

name: Quidsi (Diapers.com)
summary: E-commerce retailer of baby products (Diapers.com, Soap.com) known for algorithmic real-time price-matching against Amazon during a well-documented 'price war,' which triggered Amazon's acquisition offer. Amazon shut Quidsi down entirely in 2017, folding its customer base into Amazon.com and Amazon Family.
lesson: Winning an acquisition by out-executing a giant on algorithmic pricing doesn't guarantee survival — the acquirer may buy you specifically to eliminate the competitive pressure, then absorb your customers.
source: Trade press coverage of Amazon's 2011 Quidsi acquisition and 2017 shutdown

name: Quil Health
summary: A Philadelphia joint venture combining Comcast's home tech with Independence Blue Cross's healthcare expertise to build aging-in-place monitoring (Quil Assure) and caregiver coordination tools; it shut down in early 2023 after failing to find commercial traction.
lesson: Even deep-pocketed corporate joint ventures with strong distribution can fail to crack aging-in-place monitoring if the product doesn't clearly outperform simpler existing options.

name: Quiqup
summary: UK-based on-demand delivery and logistics startup serving retailers that collapsed into administration in 2021 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Subscale last-mile delivery networks struggle to compete once larger platforms achieve density-driven cost advantages.

name: Quirky
summary: Crowdsourced invention platform that launched Wink smart-home devices; overexpanded into too many simultaneous product lines and filed for bankruptcy in 2015.
lesson: Spreading capital across dozens of unproven products at once starves any one of them of the resources to succeed.

name: Quixey
summary: Mobile app search engine raised over $200M including from SoftBank, but shut down amid allegations that executives had misrepresented revenue figures to investors.
lesson: Inflating metrics to secure large late-stage rounds eventually catches up with a company.

name: Rabbit
summary: The r1 'AI action agent' device sold ~100,000 units on hype but its promised autonomous large-action-model capability never matched the demo, triggering mass returns and security-flaw disclosures; by mid-2025 employees reportedly went unpaid for months.
lesson: Demoing capabilities you haven't shipped ('vaporware agents') creates a reckoning once real users test the product.

name: Rabbit Inc.
summary: Maker of the r1, a dedicated voice-controlled AI device meant to replace app-based smartphone interactions; the launch drew widespread criticism over unreliable functionality, an underwhelming 'large action model,' and reports the device largely ran on Android under the hood, badly denting consumer trust.
lesson: Marketing a voice AI device as a revolutionary new interaction paradigm invites intense scrutiny that a still-immature product could not withstand.
source: The Verge, Marques Brownlee review coverage, Android Authority investigation

name: Radial
summary: US e-commerce fulfillment and omnichannel logistics provider (formerly eBay Enterprise) acquired by Belgian postal group bpost in 2017; bpost wrote down billions in value and scaled back most US operations by 2023.
lesson: Even a large, established fulfillment operator can become a value-destroying acquisition when integration and demand assumptions miss.

name: RadiumOne
summary: Ad-tech firm RadiumOne's IPO plans collapsed in 2014 after CEO Gurbaksh Chahal was fired for a domestic violence conviction, and the company never recovered its earlier growth trajectory or went public.
lesson: A founder's personal conduct scandal can derail an IPO and permanently damage an ad-tech company's momentum.
source: TechCrunch, Recode coverage of RadiumOne CEO scandal 2014

name: Radius Intelligence
summary: B2B predictive marketing data company Radius shut down operations in 2020 after raising over $100M, unable to find a buyer or sustainable path to profitability.
lesson: Data-driven martech platforms need a durable data moat, not just funding, to survive category consolidation.
source: TechCrunch, GeekWire coverage of Radius shutdown 2020

name: Rainmaker Systems
summary: Rainmaker Systems provided outsourced inside-sales and, later, AI-assisted lead-qualification services for technology companies. The publicly-traded micro-cap company struggled with declining revenue and profitability for years and was ultimately delisted and wound down around 2016.
lesson: A public micro-cap sales-services company without a scalable software moat is highly exposed to margin pressure and can quietly wind down even after two decades of operation.

name: Rally Rd
summary: Fractional collectibles investing platform shut down its secondary trading marketplace after regulatory and liquidity challenges made the model unworkable.
lesson: Fractionalized alternative-asset marketplaces need real secondary liquidity, which is hard to bootstrap from scratch.

name: Range Fuels
summary: Cellulosic ethanol producer that shut its Georgia plant after failing to convert wood waste into fuel at commercial scale.
lesson: Lab-scale conversion chemistry often does not translate cleanly to industrial-scale biofuel production.

name: Rara.com
summary: On-demand music streaming service that partnered with device manufacturers but failed to gain meaningful market share against Spotify and shut down in 2016.
lesson: Distribution partnerships alone cannot overcome a crowded market dominated by a category leader.

name: Rasgo
summary: Feature engineering and self-service analytics platform for data teams, which shut down operations in 2023, with founders publicly discussing the difficulty of building a durable business in the crowded, fast-shifting modern data stack tooling category.
lesson: Rapidly shifting developer tooling trends (SQL-based transformation tools, the rise of LLM-driven analytics) can erode a young infra product's relevance before it establishes a durable customer base.

name: RateSetter
funding: ~£45M raised before acquisition by Metro Bank in 2020
summary: UK peer-to-peer lender using algorithmic risk models and a provision fund to match retail investors with borrowers. Acquired by Metro Bank in 2020 for ~£12M (far below prior valuations); Metro Bank closed the retail P2P investment platform to new investment in 2021, effectively ending the P2P model.
lesson: Tightening FCA regulation of P2P investment platforms combined with thin margins on the provision-fund model made a once-prominent AI-assisted P2P lender unviable as a standalone retail investment product.
source: Financial Times, AltFi coverage of Metro Bank closing RateSetter's P2P platform (2021)

name: Rdio
summary: Well-reviewed music streaming service co-founded by Janus Friis that lost the subscriber race to Spotify and Apple Music, filed for bankruptcy, and sold its assets to Pandora.
lesson: A superior product experience alone cannot overcome a market consolidating around better-funded, faster-scaling competitors.

name: Reach Robotics
summary: UK maker of MekaMon augmented-reality gaming robots shut down in 2019 after struggling to build a large enough consumer base for AR-connected robotic toys.
lesson: Novel consumer robotics tied to a nascent technology trend (AR gaming) can struggle if the underlying trend adopts more slowly than expected.
source: UK tech press coverage of Reach Robotics closure, 2019

name: ReachNow
summary: BMW's US car-sharing and ride-hailing service launched in Seattle and other cities but was shut down in 2019 as BMW consolidated mobility efforts into Share Now.
lesson: Automaker mobility spinoffs often get folded or killed when parent-company strategy consolidates.

name: READY Robotics
summary: Maker of ForgeOS, a no-code operating system for programming factory robots, shut down abruptly when a follow-on round fell through; its IP was later acquired by Standard Bots.
lesson: Never let survival hinge on a single funding round closing.

name: RealAtom
summary: Marketplace connecting commercial real estate borrowers with lenders using data-driven matching; quietly ceased operations around 2022 after failing to gain sufficient traction against larger, better-funded CRE fintech competitors.
lesson: A thinly-funded CRE lending marketplace can be squeezed out once larger players with more data and capital enter the same niche.

name: Reali
summary: Real estate platform combining AI-driven home valuation, iBuying-style cash offers, and integrated mortgage/title services; shut down operations abruptly in August 2022, laying off most staff, citing volatile market conditions.
lesson: Bundling iBuying with mortgage and title services multiplies capital intensity, so a housing slowdown can hit multiple revenue lines simultaneously.

name: RealMax
summary: RealMax, a mixed-reality headset startup targeting industrial and consumer markets, failed to achieve meaningful commercial traction against better-funded competitors like Magic Leap and Microsoft HoloLens.
lesson: Smaller mixed-reality hardware entrants without major partnerships struggled to differentiate once well-funded incumbents dominated enterprise MR budgets.
source: Industry reporting on niche mixed-reality hardware startups

name: Rebellion Defense
summary: Rebellion Defense built AI software for battle management, cyber defense, and mission planning and quickly reached unicorn-adjacent valuations backed by top-tier VCs. By April 2023 it had laid off roughly 40% of staff, its co-founder and public face Chris Lynch departed, and reports described the company as having little revenue and burning cash with no clear path to another raise.
lesson: Defense software sales cycles are far longer than VC-style growth expectations; hype and elite pedigree can inflate a valuation well ahead of durable government revenue.

name: Recon Instruments
summary: Recon Instruments, maker of heads-up-display smart glasses for sports (skiing, cycling), was acquired by Intel in 2015 and had its consumer product line shut down by 2017 as Intel deprioritized wearables.
lesson: Being acquired by a large tech company can still end in shutdown if the parent's strategic priorities shift away from the acquired product category.
source: Road to VR reporting on Intel/Recon Instruments wind-down, 2017

name: Recruiter.com Group
summary: Publicly traded AI-enabled recruiting marketplace and RPO platform layering machine-learning matching onto a freelance-recruiter network; by 2023 the company showed a spiking debt-to-asset ratio, negative earnings and heavy share dilution, and was widely flagged by analysts as a financial "value trap."
lesson: Being publicly listed doesn't fix a weak underlying business model - recurring losses and dilution can turn an AI-hiring story into a stock-market warning sign.
source: Yahoo Finance, StockScan, ValueResearchOnline (RCRT filings analysis)

name: Redbox
summary: The DVD-kiosk giant tried to pivot into streaming as physical rental declined, was acquired by Chicken Soup for the Soul Entertainment in 2022, and filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in 2024.
lesson: A legacy hardware business rarely out-runs the decline of its core product just by bolting on a streaming app.

name: REEF Technology
summary: Parking-lot-turned-logistics-hub company (originally ParkJockey) that raised a huge SoftBank-led round at a $12.6B valuation but sold off major parking and delivery-kitchen units in 2023 amid deep losses.
lesson: Pivoting a parking-lot network into multiple unrelated logistics businesses simultaneously diluted focus and burned cash.

name: Reflektive
summary: People-management and marketing-adjacent analytics platform Reflektive shut down in 2021 after failing to reach sustainable unit economics despite heavy funding.
lesson: Even well-funded SaaS platforms can collapse if customer acquisition costs never converge with lifetime value.
source: TechCrunch, Business Insider coverage of Reflektive shutdown 2021

name: Refract.ai
summary: UK-based Refract.ai built AI-powered conversation intelligence for sales call coaching, positioning itself as a European alternative to Gong and Chorus.ai. The company entered administration in 2020 after being unable to raise a competitive follow-on round against much larger US-funded rivals.
lesson: Regional conversation-intelligence challengers found it extremely difficult to raise growth capital once US incumbents like Gong had already captured investor mindshare and set the funding bar sky-high.
source: UK Companies House filings, industry press

name: Reka AI
summary: Built multimodal frontier models (Reka Core, Flash, Edge) with a small team of ex-DeepMind/Google researchers. Reported 2024 acquisition talks with Snowflake for roughly $1B fell through, leaving the company to continue independently under intense competitive and capital pressure from larger labs.
lesson: A technically credible small team can train competitive models, but without a clear enterprise distribution channel or an eventual exit, staying independent against labs with 100x the capital is a fragile position.
source: The Information reporting on Reka-Snowflake talks, 2024

name: Rekor Systems
summary: Rekor Systems sells AI computer-vision license-plate-recognition and roadway-intelligence software to public-safety and transportation agencies. Despite modest revenue growth, the company has posted persistent net losses (over $31M in 2025 alone) and its stock has fallen to near-penny-stock levels, forcing repeated pivots including a recent move into deepfake detection.
lesson: Government contracts can provide steady but thin revenue; without a path to profitability, a public AI-government vendor can grind toward delisting even while its top line grows.

name: Relativity Media
summary: Ryan Kavanaugh's film and TV studio used complex financing structures to fund a rapid slate of movies, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2015, and collapsed again in 2018 under unsustainable debt.
lesson: Financial engineering can fund rapid growth but doesn't fix an underlying business that loses money on its core product.

name: Renegades (esports org)
summary: Riot Games sanctioned Renegades for undisclosed cross-ownership and roster violations, forcing a distress sale of its NA LCS slot in 2018, whose roster became Golden Guardians.
lesson: Franchise leagues enforce ownership and conduct rules strictly, and violating them can force a sale of a team's core asset.

name: Renovo Motors
summary: Vehicle software platform Renovo Motors, which pivoted from electric supercars to autonomous vehicle operating systems, shut down in 2023 after failing to win enough OEM contracts.
lesson: Pivoting to sell software infrastructure to automakers requires long enterprise sales cycles that can outlast startup runway.

name: Rentberry
summary: Rental marketplace that used an AI/blockchain-based bidding system to let tenants bid on rental prices; drew regulatory scrutiny (rent-bidding banned in some jurisdictions) and an SEC settlement over its unregistered token offering, and largely faded from the market.
lesson: Combining a legally contentious pricing mechanism (rent bidding) with an unregistered token sale invites regulatory action that can cripple a young AI real estate startup.

name: Replica Studios
summary: Australian AI voice-generation startup best known for NPC voiceover tools and a landmark 2023 SAG-AFTRA agreement to ethically license and pay actors for AI voice synthesis. Despite that goodwill and adoption by indie studios, it could not secure further funding and shut down completely by June 30, 2025.
lesson: Being first to solve the ethics/licensing problem in AI voice (a genuine differentiator) was not enough to survive an increasingly crowded voice-AI market once well-funded generalist competitors entered gaming.

name: Replicant
summary: Built AI voice agents that autonomously handle full customer service phone calls for contact centers; despite large funding rounds, the company underwent significant layoffs in 2024 as growth slowed and the market grew crowded with LLM-based voice-agent entrants.
lesson: Raising a large round at a peak valuation just before the market got flooded with cheaper LLM-based competitors left less room to adjust when growth slowed.
source: The Information, trade press layoff coverage

name: Replika
summary: AI companion app with generative avatar, voice, and roleplay features was ordered by Italy's data protection authority to stop processing user data in February 2023 over child-safety and emotional-manipulation concerns, then abruptly removed erotic roleplay features for existing paid subscribers, triggering a severe user backlash, documented mental-health harm reports, and a wave of subscription cancellations.
lesson: Monetizing emotionally intimate generative content creates acute regulatory and ethical exposure; abrupt product changes to address that exposure can alienate the exact paying users the business depends on.

name: Replika (Luka Inc)
summary: AI companion app built on proprietary conversational models, one of the earliest large-scale consumer LLM-adjacent products. Italy's data protection regulator banned the app in 2023 over child-safety and data concerns, forcing a controversial rollback of romantic/erotic roleplay features that triggered severe user backlash.
lesson: Consumer AI companion products sit in genuinely novel regulatory and ethical territory; a sudden compliance-driven feature change can alienate the core user base that defined the product's value.
source: Reuters, Motherboard/Vice coverage of Italy's Replika ban and 2023 feature rollback

name: Resso
summary: ByteDance's social-first music streaming app launched to compete in emerging markets but was discontinued in most countries in 2023 as ByteDance consolidated under TikTok Music.
lesson: Even well-funded corporate-backed products get discontinued when the parent company's broader strategy shifts to a different branded offering.

name: Restless Bandit
summary: "Talent rediscovery" AI that mined a company's existing applicant database with machine learning to resurface qualified past candidates; raised a notable $10M round in 2016 but lost visibility as the market consolidated around larger ATS-native AI features.
lesson: A genuinely useful niche AI feature can get swallowed once ATS incumbents build the same capability natively.

name: Retailo
summary: Middle East/Pakistan B2B retail marketplace connecting FMCG suppliers to small shops that scaled back operations and exited multiple markets in 2023 amid funding pressure and unprofitable unit economics.
lesson: Rapid multi-country expansion of a low-margin distribution marketplace without proven per-market profitability invites retrenchment.
source: MENA tech press coverage of Retailo's 2023 market exits

name: Rethink Robotics
summary: Pioneer of collaborative robots (Baxter and Sawyer) for manufacturing and light industrial tasks, founded by Rodney Brooks. Closed after a decade when sales fell far short of targets and mainstream industrial-automation incumbents (ABB, FANUC, Universal Robots) out-executed it commercially; assets were sold to Germany's HAHN Group.
lesson: Being first to market with a genuinely novel robot is not sufficient if incumbent industrial-automation players can match the capability and out-sell you through existing enterprise channels.
source: IEEE Spectrum, ASME, The Robot Report (2018)

name: RethinkDB
summary: Open-source distributed document database that built a beloved developer community but could not convert usage into a sustainable business as MongoDB dominated the space.
lesson: Developer love and technical elegance don't guarantee a viable commercial model against an entrenched market leader.

name: Revolv
summary: Made a popular smart-home hub, was acquired by Google/Nest in 2014, then Nest bricked every customer's device in 2016 by shutting down the servers with no refunds.
lesson: Acquisition can be a death sentence for a hardware product if the acquirer has no roadmap for it.

name: Rex Homes UK (Purplebricks AI valuation push)
summary: UK online estate agency that invested in AI-driven property valuation and hybrid agent tools; failed to translate its model into the US market (exiting in 2021) and was ultimately sold for a nominal sum and delisted from the London Stock Exchange in 2023 after years of losses.
lesson: Expanding an AI-enabled disruptive brokerage model into a new international market without proving unit economics at home first can accelerate an existing company's decline.

name: Rex Real Estate (rexhomes.com)
funding: $60M+ (Series C, investors incl. Scale Venture Partners)
summary: AI/data-driven discount real estate brokerage that bypassed traditional MLS listings to cut commissions; lost a series of antitrust lawsuits against the National Association of Realtors and MLS operators, and shut down its brokerage operations around 2023 after failing to gain sufficient market share.
lesson: Disrupting entrenched real estate distribution channels (MLS) with an AI-driven low-commission model invites sustained legal and structural resistance from incumbents.

name: Rezatec
summary: UK-based Rezatec used satellite data and machine learning to assess climate and environmental risk for forestry, utilities and agriculture clients. It ceased trading amid funding difficulties after a decade of steady but insufficient commercial traction to justify continued investor support.
lesson: Long, capital-intensive incubation periods for geospatial AI need a credible path to a scaled enterprise sales motion well before investor patience for a 'promising but pre-scale' business runs out.
source: UK company filings and industry coverage of Rezatec's 2023 closure

name: RFRNZ
summary: German AI-powered legal research and reference startup that closed down in 2021 after failing to generate sufficient demand from German lawyers, an early casualty of legal AI's slow adoption curve in continental European legal markets.
lesson: Legal AI adoption timelines vary sharply by jurisdiction; a product built for a conservative, precedent-light civil-law market can face a much longer and less certain path to product-market fit than the same idea in a common-law market.

name: Ribbon
funding: $450M+ (equity + debt, investors incl. Greylock, Bain Capital Ventures)
summary: Offered an AI/data-driven cash-backed offer product for homebuyers to compete with all-cash iBuyers; wound down its core consumer cash-offer business in 2023 as rising rates and falling home sales volumes destroyed the unit economics, pivoting to a smaller B2B software model.
lesson: Guarantee-based homebuying products depend on continuous transaction volume; a rate-driven slowdown in home sales can strand the balance-sheet exposure.

name: RightScale
summary: Early cloud management platform leader that lost its market position to native multi-cloud tooling and was ultimately acquired by Flexera at a valuation far below its peak.
lesson: First-mover cloud management platforms lost relevance as AWS, Azure, and GCP built increasingly capable native management tools.

name: Ripe.io
summary: Blockchain-based produce traceability startup Ripe.io scaled back operations significantly around 2022 after failing to convert pilot programs with retailers into recurring commercial contracts.
lesson: Traceability startups need a clear paying customer for the data, not just a compelling blockchain narrative, to sustain commercial contracts.

name: Ripple Foods
summary: Ripple Foods pioneered pea-protein milk as an alternative to soy and dairy, but slowing plant-based milk demand and rising competition led it to wind down consumer operations and sell off assets in 2023.
lesson: Being an early mover in plant-based dairy is not enough once larger incumbents crowd the same shelf space.

name: Riskified
summary: Israeli AI fraud-prevention platform for e-commerce (Shein, Wayfair, Prada, Revolve) that went public on NYSE in July 2021 at a $3.3B valuation. The stock lost more than 80% of its value by 2025, and the company began exploring a sale in March 2025 after receiving takeover interest, despite reaching its first GAAP profit in Q4 2025.
lesson: A 2021-era IPO priced for hyper-growth can permanently destroy shareholder value even if the underlying business eventually becomes profitable — public fraud-detection companies were punished hard once growth-at-all-costs valuations reverted to earnings multiples.

name: RiskSense
summary: Vulnerability risk-management platform was acquired by Ivanti in 2021 for undisclosed, reportedly modest terms as part of Ivanti's broader roll-up strategy rather than as a standalone growth story.
lesson: Being absorbed into a private-equity-backed roll-up is often a sign a company could not sustain independent growth economics.

name: Rivigo
summary: Indian trucking and logistics-tech unicorn known for its relay-driver model that saw its founder-CEO ousted by the board in 2022 amid financial distress and mass layoffs, effectively ending its unicorn trajectory.
lesson: Operationally innovative logistics models can still collapse under founder-board conflict and unsustainable cash burn.
source: Economic Times, Rivigo founder ouster and layoffs 2022

name: RJMetrics
summary: Early e-commerce analytics and data pipeline company acquired by Magento in 2016; Adobe discontinued the standalone product after the Magento acquisition.
lesson: Being acquired for a parent's product roadmap can mean the original product is deprioritized and killed.

name: Roadstar.ai
summary: One of China's best-funded early robotaxi startups, founded by former Baidu, Tesla and Google engineers. Imploded within months of a $128M raise after co-founders publicly accused each other of corruption and faking test data, leading investors to sue for liquidation and freeze the company's bank accounts.
lesson: Founder trust and clean cap-table governance are existential risks in capital-intensive deep tech - no amount of funding survives a public boardroom war.

name: Roambi
summary: Mobile BI visualization app acquired by Actuate (later OpenText) that was formally discontinued in 2020 as OpenText consolidated its analytics portfolio.
lesson: Point-solution BI tools risk being folded into and eventually retired from a larger vendor's product suite.

name: Robin AI
summary: London-based AI contract review platform, embedded in Microsoft Word, that paired LLMs with a human-in-the-loop lawyer review layer for redlining and negotiation, serving Fortune 500 clients like PepsiCo, PwC, and GE. Despite $10M ARR and a $16M pipeline, Robin AI could not close a needed $50M funding round in 2025 to cover multi-year operating losses driven by expensive enterprise sales cycles and costly human-review services. It collapsed in late 2025: HMRC filed a winding-up petition for unpaid tax, Scissero acquired the ~70-person managed-services team in December 2025, and Microsoft acqui-hired roughly 18 engineers for its Word team in January 2026, while the underlying software product itself found no buyer.
