lesson: A costly human-in-the-loop layer bolted onto an LLM product can produce impressive logos and revenue growth while masking unit economics that never approach SaaS margins; when growth stalls, there is no way to raise the next round to cover the burn.

name: Robin Labs
summary: Built a voice-activated AI assistant for drivers (navigation, hands-free tasks) aimed at ride-share and delivery drivers; ceased independent operations around 2018 after failing to reach sustainable scale against built-in smartphone assistants.
lesson: A driver-focused voice assistant became redundant once Google Assistant and Siri offered comparable hands-free functionality natively on every phone for free.

name: Rocket Crafters
summary: Hybrid-rocket-engine launch vehicle startup filed for Chapter 7 liquidation in 2022 after years of delayed engine tests and failing to secure a follow-on funding round.
lesson: Novel propulsion technology that never clears ground-test milestones will exhaust investor patience before it reaches flight.

name: Rocket Fuel
summary: Rocket Fuel was a programmatic ad-buying DSP that went public in 2013 but its stock collapsed amid slowing growth, and it was acquired by Sizmek for $145M in 2017, a fraction of its IPO valuation.
lesson: A successful IPO doesn't guarantee durability if the core ad-tech margins erode under competition.
source: TechCrunch, AdExchanger coverage of Rocket Fuel/Sizmek acquisition 2017

name: Rockets of Awesome
summary: Rockets of Awesome, a kids' clothing subscription brand backed by Foot Locker's investment arm, laid off half its staff and closed its only store in 2020, then stopped paying vendors and sought a buyer in 2022 before winding down.
lesson: DTC apparel subscriptions for fast-growing children face high churn as kids outgrow sizes, and a single strategic retail investor isn't enough to offset structurally weak subscription retention.

name: RolePoint
summary: Employee-referral platform that used algorithmic matching to surface which employees were most likely to know a strong referral for open roles; after raising a modest Series A, it was acquired by SmartRecruiters in 2017 in a deal widely seen in the industry as a distressed, below-expectations outcome for its investors.
lesson: Referral-matching algorithms are a useful feature, not a standalone company - single-feature AI startups often end up folded into a platform rather than becoming one.

name: ROLI
summary: British music technology hardware maker known for the Seaboard expressive keyboard that ran out of cash and entered insolvency administration in 2022 after years of heavy hardware R&D spending.
lesson: Hardware-heavy music technology businesses require far more capital and longer runways than software equivalents.
source: Music Business Worldwide, ROLI administration coverage 2022

name: Romeo Power
summary: EV battery pack maker Romeo Power went public via SPAC in 2020 but, after mounting losses and inability to scale production, was acquired by Nikola in 2022 in what was effectively a distressed sale.
lesson: A SPAC's capital infusion cannot fix a battery manufacturer's inability to hit production and cost targets.

name: Roofstock One
summary: Fractional single-family rental investment product from Roofstock using AI-driven property scoring to let investors buy shares of rental homes; discontinued in 2023 as part of Roofstock's broader restructuring during the housing downturn, though the parent company continued operating.
lesson: Even within a surviving parent company, an experimental AI-driven fractional-ownership product line can be cut first when a downturn forces prioritization.

name: Rooster Teeth
summary: A pioneering internet content company with a fiercely loyal fan community was shut down in 2024 by Warner Bros. Discovery after 21 years amid broader corporate cost-cutting.
lesson: A profitable niche community-driven brand can still be cut when a parent conglomerate prioritizes company-wide cost reduction.

name: Rooster Teeth (media/publishing arm)
summary: A pioneering internet content and podcast network with a devoted fan community was shut down in 2024 by Warner Bros. Discovery after 21 years amid broader corporate cost-cutting, despite reported profitability.
lesson: A profitable niche digital-media brand can still be cut when it doesn't fit a parent conglomerate's narrowing strategic focus.

name: Root Insurance
summary: Telematics and AI-driven auto insurer that priced risk from smartphone driving-behavior data; lost over 95% of its IPO market value by 2022 due to unsustainable loss ratios before a partial 2024 turnaround.
lesson: Novel AI risk-scoring data such as telematics still has to translate into disciplined pricing and reserving; growth-at-all-costs underwriting nearly destroyed the company despite genuinely differentiated data.

name: Rosetta Stone
summary: Once-dominant CD-ROM/software language brand whose stock fell as free mobile apps like Duolingo disrupted its category; acquired by IXL Learning in 2021 at a steep discount.
lesson: A premium paid-software model is highly vulnerable to free, mobile-native disruptors targeting the same need.

name: ROSS Intelligence
summary: AI-native legal research startup built on IBM Watson that let lawyers query case law in natural language, backed by Dentons NextLaw Labs and a Y Combinator cohort. Sued by Thomson Reuters in May 2020 for allegedly using Westlaw headnotes to train its models, ROSS could not raise further funding while ensnared in litigation and ceased operations January 31, 2021. In February 2025 a federal judge ruled ROSS had infringed Westlaw's copyrights, a landmark AI-training-data decision that came after the company was already gone.
lesson: Training data provenance is an existential legal risk for AI-native products, not just a compliance footnote; incumbents with proprietary corpora can use litigation itself as a competitive weapon that starves a startup of follow-on capital long before any verdict is reached.

name: Rotary Rocket Company
summary: Developed the Roton, a single-stage-to-orbit rocket with a helicopter-style rotor for landing; built and flight-tested an atmospheric prototype but shut down in 2001 without reaching orbit.
lesson: Combining several unproven technologies into one vehicle multiplies technical risk faster than funding can keep pace.

name: Running Tide
summary: Running Tide combined ocean-based carbon sequestration (kelp, biomass sinking) with sensor buoys and AI-driven monitoring/verification to prove out and track carbon removal at sea, and was the first to sell ocean carbon credits. It folded in mid-2024 after failing to secure enough durable corporate buyers for its still-unproven MRV and removal approach.
lesson: Novel MRV science paired with a nascent buyer market is a double bet: even strong measurement technology cannot compensate for a market of buyers unwilling to pay for unproven removal claims at scale.
source: Canary Media coverage of climate tech company closures in 2024

name: SambaNova
summary: AI chip and full-stack training/inference platform company that raised at a $5.1B valuation in 2021 but underwent significant layoffs (around 15% of staff in 2024) and struggled to translate its hardware differentiation into enterprise revenue at scale amid intense competition from Nvidia.
lesson: Custom AI silicon requires enormous, sustained capital and ecosystem investment to compete with Nvidia's software moat, and a strong valuation does not guarantee commercial traction.
source: Reports on SambaNova layoffs, 2024 (Reuters, The Information)

name: SambaNova Systems
summary: AI chip and foundation-model company (Samba-1) aiming to offer full-stack alternatives to Nvidia and the API labs. Faced 2024 layoffs, stalled follow-on fundraising, and reported valuation markdowns as Nvidia's dominance and API-model competition squeezed its niche.
lesson: Competing simultaneously on chips and models against both Nvidia and the frontier labs spreads capital and focus thin; full-stack ambition needs full-stack capital.
source: The Information, CRN reporting on SambaNova layoffs and funding struggles, 2024

name: Sambreel
summary: Sambreel operated browser toolbar and ad-injection software that was banned by Facebook, Google, and browser vendors around 2013-2014 for injecting ads without proper disclosure, crippling its business.
lesson: Ad-injection business models built on deceptive practices are eventually shut out by platform policy enforcement.
source: Wired, Ars Technica coverage of Sambreel adware bans 2013-2014

name: Samsung Gear VR
funding: Corporate-funded by Samsung, developed with Oculus
summary: Samsung's Gear VR, a smartphone-powered VR headset built with Oculus software, was discontinued in 2020 as the entire smartphone-VR category was displaced by standalone headsets.
lesson: Smartphone-tethered VR headsets became an evolutionary dead end once standalone headsets offered a simpler, higher-quality experience.

name: Sangamo Therapeutics
summary: Pioneer of zinc-finger gene editing has seen a string of discontinued programs and multiple partner exits despite three decades of platform development.
lesson: An early technological lead in gene editing erodes quickly once CRISPR-based competitors demonstrate cleaner clinical data.

name: Sarcos Robotics
funding: Public via SPAC (~$278M raised); later taken private
summary: Sarcos built AI-controlled robotic exoskeletons and teleoperated systems for defense (including U.S. Army-funded Guardian XO work) and industrial customers. It went public via SPAC in 2021, but persistent losses and a strategic pivot away from hardware manufacturing led to a collapsed stock price and an eventual 2024 sale of assets to Kres Corp/Vecna at a steep discount to its SPAC-era valuation.
lesson: Capital-intensive AI-robotics hardware for defense needs either a captive prime-contractor relationship or years of runway; a SPAC listing accelerates cash burn scrutiny long before the hardware business matures.
source: general public reporting on Sarcos Technology and Robotics SPAC decline and 2024 asset sale

name: Sarcos Technology and Robotics
summary: Public industrial robotics/exoskeleton company (Nasdaq: STRC) that burned ~$83M in the year to Sept 2023 against minimal revenue; cut ~25% of staff and relaunched as software-only 'Palladyne AI' in 2024.
lesson: A SPAC-funded hardware robotics story with minimal revenue can burn nine figures fast; a late pivot to software is usually a survival move.

name: Sary
summary: Saudi Arabian B2B retail marketplace connecting suppliers to small shops that carried out significant layoffs and restructuring in 2023 after slower-than-expected profitability progress.
lesson: Regional B2B distribution marketplaces need a credible path to unit profitability before scaling headcount ahead of revenue.
source: MENA tech press coverage of Sary's 2023 restructuring

name: Satmetrix
funding: Venture-backed, co-creator of Net Promoter Score
summary: Satmetrix, co-creator of the Net Promoter Score methodology and NPS software, was acquired by NICE in 2018 and discontinued as a standalone customer experience platform.
lesson: Owning the intellectual origin of an industry-standard metric does not guarantee long-term independent survival against larger CX platform consolidators.
source: NICE 2018 acquisition press release for Satmetrix

name: Save This Life
summary: One of the largest U.S. pet microchip registry companies that abruptly ceased operations in early 2024/2025, leaving pet owners' microchip registrations inaccessible and forcing shelters nationwide to advise re-registration with other providers.
lesson: A registry business model depends entirely on the operator staying solvent indefinitely; an abrupt shutdown of an identity-critical service like microchip registration can strand life-safety data for millions of pets with no warning.
source: AVMA; AAHA; CBS Pittsburgh reporting on Save This Life closure

name: Savioke
summary: Maker of the Relay hotel/hospital delivery robot, spun out of Willow Garage, ceased independent operations in 2022, with assets later acquired by Richtech Robotics.
lesson: Service-robot rental models in hospitality can struggle to reach the volume needed to cover R&D and support costs.
source: The Robot Report coverage of Savioke wind-down, 2022

name: SAVO Group
summary: SAVO Group was one of the original sales enablement platforms, raising over $80M over nearly two decades to build content management and AI-assisted guided-selling tools. After failing to keep pace with newer entrants like Highspot and Seismic, it was acquired by Seismic in 2016 in a deal widely seen as a distressed consolidation rather than a growth exit.
lesson: Being the category's original pioneer offered no lasting protection once faster-moving, better-designed challengers (Highspot, Seismic) overtook it; SAVO ultimately sold to one of them at a discount.
source: TechCrunch, industry reporting on Seismic acquisition

name: Say Media
summary: Formerly VideoEgg, this media and technology company pivoted repeatedly through video advertising, a blog network, and a content-management platform before winding down operations in 2017.
lesson: Repeated business-model pivots without landing on a durable one eventually exhausts investor patience and cash.

name: ScaleFactor
summary: 'AI-powered' small-business bookkeeping SaaS shut down after a Forbes investigation revealed its automation was largely manual human bookkeeping that frequently produced errors.
lesson: Marketing a product as AI-automated when it isn't reliably working can destroy trust once customers are exposed to the failures.

name: SciFi Foods
summary: SciFi Foods (formerly Artemys Foods) developed hybrid cultivated-beef products using bioreactor technology, but shut down in 2024 after failing to raise a new round amid a broader pullback in cultivated-meat investment.
lesson: Cultivated meat requires years of capital-intensive scale-up, making funding-market timing a make-or-break factor.

name: Scripted
summary: Freelance content-writing marketplace Scripted for marketers shut down in 2019 after failing to scale profitably against lower-cost freelance platforms and in-house content teams.
lesson: Content marketplaces face commoditization pressure that erodes margins faster than growth can compensate.

name: Sea Launch (original venture)
summary: Ocean-platform launch venture combining Boeing, RSC Energia and international partners filed for Chapter 11 in 2009 after a launch failure and heavy debt load; the platform was later sold to a Russian-controlled entity.
lesson: A multinational joint venture with misaligned partner incentives struggles to absorb the cost of a single major launch failure.

name: Secret
summary: Anonymous confession-sharing app raised heavily on a viral launch but was shut down by founders after becoming a magnet for bullying and harassment.
lesson: Founders should be willing to shut down a growing product if it can't be made safe.

name: SecureAuth
summary: Adaptive authentication/identity vendor merged with Core Security in 2019 under private equity ownership, then underwent a distressed recapitalization in 2023 that reportedly wiped out most equity value for earlier investors.
lesson: Private-equity roll-ups in identity security can still fail if the combined business can't generate enough cash flow to service its structure.

name: SecureWorks
funding: publicly traded (IPO 2016, majority owned by Dell)
summary: Managed detection and response provider IPO'd in 2016 at $14/share but its stock fell more than 90% over the following years as growth slowed, leading majority owner Dell/Sophos to take it private again at a steep discount to its IPO valuation.
lesson: A public listing doesn't lock in value; sustained underperformance can erase most of a company's IPO-era valuation over time.

name: Seebo
summary: Israeli industrial AI company pivoted from consumer IoT to manufacturing quality and downtime prediction; workforce shrank from 50 to 35 during the pivot with little visible momentum since its 2021 raise.
lesson: A pivot needs accelerating traction within a couple years or assume slow bleed.

name: Seldon
summary: UK-based open-source model deployment and monitoring platform (Seldon Core) for Kubernetes-based ML serving. Growth stalled as larger cloud-native MLOps platforms and hyperscaler-native tooling (SageMaker, Vertex AI) captured enterprise budgets, and the company scaled back commercial ambitions.
lesson: Open-source infrastructure companies competing against hyperscalers' native tooling need a clear enterprise monetization wedge beyond the open-source project itself.
source: Industry reporting on MLOps market consolidation, 2023-2024

name: SelectQuote
summary: Publicly traded insurance distribution platform lost over 90% of its market value after Medicare Advantage commission changes and customer acquisition cost inflation crushed its margins.
lesson: Insurance distribution businesses reliant on paid customer acquisition are highly exposed to commission rule changes.

name: Selfless Gaming
summary: The esports organization folded in 2016 after mass roster departures and financial strain left it unable to field competitive teams.
lesson: An esports org's core asset is its roster; losing it wholesale can end the business almost overnight.

name: Selina
summary: Latin America-born hostel/co-living hospitality brand expanded to 150+ locations, went public via SPAC, and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy amid unsustainable lease obligations.
lesson: Rapid global real-estate expansion without proving profitability at existing locations first is a recurring hospitality failure pattern.

name: Selventa (formerly Genstruct)
summary: Cambridge, MA computational systems biology company (founded as Genstruct, rebranded Selventa in 2010) that built a causal-reasoning platform and the widely-used Biological Expression Language (BEL) to model disease mechanisms for pharma partners including Pfizer. Despite technical influence on the field, Selventa ceased operations in 2016 after 15 years without achieving a self-sustaining commercial model.
lesson: A company can leave a lasting scientific/technical legacy (BEL is still used in computational biology today) while still failing commercially — technical influence and business viability are not the same thing.

name: Sema4 (GeneDx Holdings)
summary: Sema4 went public via SPAC promising an AI-driven 'health intelligence' platform combining genomic and clinical data, but burned cash rapidly on unprofitable testing operations, cut most of its workforce, and sold off its core diagnostics business in 2023 to survive as a much smaller renamed entity.
lesson: SPAC-fueled growth targets for data-driven diagnostics businesses can outrun the actual reimbursement and margin economics of clinical testing.
source: Fierce Healthcare and MedCity News coverage of Sema4's 2022-23 restructuring

name: Sencha
summary: JavaScript/Ext JS app-development framework company sold to Idera in 2019 in what was widely viewed as a distressed sale after years of declining growth.
lesson: Framework and tooling vendors that fail to keep pace with faster-moving open-source ecosystems eventually lose commercial relevance.

name: Sendy
summary: Kenyan logistics and delivery-tech startup that connected businesses with transport providers, shutting down operations in 2023 after failing to secure follow-on funding.
lesson: Regional logistics platforms without deep-pocketed backers are vulnerable when venture capital retreats from the sector.

name: Sennder
summary: Berlin-based digital freight forwarder using AI-driven pricing and matching algorithms to run a 'digital fleet' of trucking capacity across Europe, once a European trucking unicorn. Conducted significant layoffs in January 2023 amid a broad European freight-forwarding downturn and unprecedented rate corrections.
lesson: A pan-European 'digital fleet' model built on thin trucking margins was highly exposed to the 2022-2023 freight rate collapse, forcing deep workforce cuts even at a well-funded unicorn.

name: senseFly
summary: Swiss agricultural mapping drone maker senseFly, owned by Parrot, was wound down by its parent company in 2023 amid a broader retreat from the commercial drone market.
lesson: A crowded drone-hardware market with thin margins can make even technically strong products unsustainable for their parent company.

name: SenseTime
summary: Once the world's most valuable AI startup and a leading computer-vision unicorn, SenseTime's Hong Kong-listed stock lost roughly 90% of its value in the years after its 2021 IPO amid US sanctions, Xinjiang-related human-rights allegations, and years of mounting net losses.
lesson: Enormous pre-IPO valuations built on government-surveillance contracts can evaporate once sanctions and human-rights scrutiny cut off both customers and capital.
source: Reuters and Bloomberg coverage of SenseTime's post-IPO stock collapse and US sanctions

name: Sensics
summary: Sensics, an early maker of high-end professional VR headsets for simulation and training, wound down its VR hardware business around 2017 as lower-cost consumer VR headsets undercut its enterprise pricing model.
lesson: Premium-priced professional VR hardware businesses were undercut once consumer-grade headsets reached comparable quality at a fraction of the cost.
source: Road to VR retrospective on professional VR headset makers

name: Sensyne Health
summary: UK AI clinical data analytics company, backed by NHS data-sharing partnerships and co-founded by a former UK government minister, went public on the London Stock Exchange but never achieved profitability and collapsed into insolvency after burning through its cash reserves.
lesson: Access to exclusive public health data partnerships is a moat only if it can be converted into a commercially viable, repeatable product — access alone doesn't pay the bills.
source: Financial Times and Sky News coverage of Sensyne Health's 2023 collapse

name: Sentient Technologies
summary: AI research company whose Sentient Investment Management unit used evolutionary computation and distributed AI to trade equities; also built AI products for e-commerce. Despite large funding and years of R&D, the company shut down entirely in November 2018, laying off remaining staff.
lesson: Deep-tech AI trading research burned enormous capital over a decade without ever proving a scalable, defensible business model, showing that novel algorithms alone don't guarantee commercially viable alpha.
source: TechCrunch, VentureBeat coverage of Sentient shutdown (Nov 2018)

name: SetSail
summary: SetSail used AI to automatically capture sales activity data and gamify seller behavior to drive revenue-generating actions. The company raised from Emergence Capital and others but shut down operations in 2023 after failing to scale beyond early adopters in a crowded revenue-intelligence market.
lesson: Sales gamification/activity-capture tools face a low switching cost and get squeezed once larger revenue-intelligence platforms (Gong, Clari) add similar features.

name: Setter (Setter.ai / homebuying AI concierge)
summary: AI-powered home-search and moving-concierge platform intended to simplify the homebuying journey; ceased operations in 2023 after failing to reach scale in a rapidly cooling housing market.
lesson: Consumer AI concierge products layered on top of the home-buying funnel need enough transaction volume to monetize; a sharp housing slowdown can cut off funding before product-market fit is proven.

name: Shadow (Blade Group)
summary: The French cloud-gaming PC-streaming startup raised significant venture funding but entered French receivership in 2023 amid unsustainable infrastructure costs and a prior major data breach.
lesson: Streaming full virtual PCs to consumers carries hosting costs that are brutally hard to make profitable at consumer price points.

name: Shapeways
funding: ~$140M raised, went public via SPAC in 2021
summary: 3D-printing manufacturing marketplace connecting designers and businesses to on-demand production that went public via SPAC, later delisted, and had its assets sold off in 2024 after years of unprofitable operations.
lesson: Capital-intensive on-demand manufacturing marketplaces need scale-driven unit cost improvements that can outpace persistent cash burn.
source: 3D printing industry trade press coverage of Shapeways' 2024 wind-down

name: Sharecare
summary: Digital health and wellness platform Sharecare went public via SPAC in 2022 but its stock collapsed and it was taken private in 2024 at a small fraction of its listing valuation.
lesson: A SPAC valuation built on broad platform ambitions can unravel quickly if no single business line reaches profitable scale.

name: Shift Technologies
summary: Online used-car marketplace Shift ceased operations and liquidated in early 2024 after merging assets with CarLotz failed to fix persistent cash burn.
lesson: Reconditioning and shipping used cars at scale is far more capital- and logistics-intensive than typical e-commerce, punishing thin margins.

name: Shiftgig
summary: On-demand staffing marketplace connecting hourly workers with businesses that wound down around 2020 after failing to reach sustainable margins in a highly competitive gig-staffing market.
lesson: Commoditized hourly-staffing marketplaces face thin take rates that rarely cover the customer-acquisition cost on both sides.
source: Staffing industry trade press coverage of Shiftgig's 2020 wind-down

name: Shipwire
summary: Early cloud-based order fulfillment and warehousing network provider acquired by Propel in 2016, then effectively wound down by 2018 as it lost customers to newer fulfillment platforms.
lesson: Being an early mover in fulfillment tech does not protect against faster, better-funded later entrants improving on the model.

name: ShopClues
summary: Indian online marketplace for unbranded and discounted goods, once valued over $1B, was sold in a distressed 2019 deal for a small fraction of capital raised.
lesson: Marketplaces built around counterfeit-prone, low-trust categories struggle to sustain premium valuations.

name: ShotSpotter (SoundThinking)
summary: Gunshot-detection technology used by police departments has faced a wave of city contract cancellations over accuracy and bias concerns.
lesson: Public-safety AI tools face an existential risk when independent audits and community backlash undermine trust faster than sales growth.

name: Shoutem
summary: Croatian no-code mobile app builder that ceased operations in 2021 after a decade of competing in the crowded app-builder space.
lesson: Small, thinly-capitalized players in a commoditized no-code category struggle to outlast well-funded competitors.

name: Showfields
summary: Experiential 'store-as-media' retail concept dubbed 'the most interesting store in the world' filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in October 2023 and shuttered its remaining Brooklyn, DC and LA locations by January 2024 after failed restructuring.
lesson: Experiential retail concepts still carry the fixed costs of physical real estate, and novelty alone cannot offset negative unit economics.

name: Shuddle
summary: Shuddle, an Uber-like ride-hailing service for unaccompanied children with vetted female drivers, shut down in April 2016 after failing to raise a follow-on funding round.
lesson: Regulated, safety-critical marketplaces for children require deep capital reserves; a single failed raise can be fatal when burn is tied to driver vetting and insurance costs.

name: Shyp
summary: On-demand shipping and pickup marketplace for individuals and small businesses that shut down in 2018 after burning through funding without reaching sustainable unit economics.
lesson: Subsidizing pickup logistics for low-value shipments rarely produces margins that can support venture-scale growth.

name: Sidecar
summary: Early ride-hailing and courier pioneer that introduced features later copied by Uber and Lyft but shut down its ride service in 2015, selling assets to General Motors.
lesson: Being first with an innovative feature doesn't matter if better-funded competitors can copy it and outspend you.

name: Sidewalk Labs
summary: Alphabet's urban-innovation subsidiary that proposed a high-tech 'smart city' district in Toronto (Quayside); the project was cancelled in 2020 and the unit was folded into Google in 2021.
lesson: Ambitious smart-city construction projects require sustained public trust and regulatory alignment that can collapse under privacy and governance concerns.
source: New York Times, The Verge coverage of Sidewalk Labs Quayside cancellation

name: Signals (activity capture startup)
summary: Signals attempted to compete with People.ai and Node in automatically logging sales activity from email/calendar data and scoring accounts for pipeline risk, but could not raise a competitive Series B and shut down operations around 2020.
lesson: Entering the activity-capture/lead-scoring niche after better-funded incumbents (People.ai, Node) had already locked up the largest enterprise design partners left little room to raise a competitive follow-on round.

name: Signature Bank
summary: New York bank with a large crypto and digital-asset deposit base that was closed by regulators in March 2023 amid contagion from Silicon Valley Bank's collapse and a rapid depositor run.
lesson: Uninsured deposit concentration can turn a solvent-on-paper bank into a forced closure within days once confidence breaks.

name: Silicon Valley Bank
summary: Bank central to the startup ecosystem that failed in March 2023 after rising rates devalued its bond portfolio and a concentrated, tech-sector depositor base fled within 48 hours.
lesson: A depositor base concentrated in one interconnected industry can coordinate a bank run faster than traditional liquidity risk models assume.

name: Silvercar
summary: Premium airport rental-car startup (all-silver Audi A4 fleet) that Audi acquired in 2017 for an estimated $300M+, then shut down entirely in 2019 as Audi refocused its mobility strategy.
lesson: A lucrative acquisition exit doesn't guarantee long-term survival once the acquirer's strategic priorities change.

name: Silvergate Bank
summary: California bank that pivoted to become the primary banking rail for crypto exchanges; collapsed in March 2023 after FTX's failure triggered a deposit run it could not cover without fire-selling securities.
lesson: Concentrating deposits in one volatile client sector creates correlated withdrawal risk that ordinary liquidity buffers cannot absorb.

name: Simple
funding: ~$21M before being acquired by BBVA for ~$117M in 2014
summary: Pioneering online bank with automated budgeting/goal-setting features (Safe-to-Spend). Acquired by BBVA in 2014; after BBVA itself was acquired by PNC, PNC shut down the Simple app and migrated customers in 2021.
lesson: Even a successful acquisition doesn't guarantee product longevity when the acquiring parent is later itself acquired and consolidates its digital banking strategy around a different platform.
source: TechCrunch, American Banker coverage of Simple shutdown by PNC (2021)

name: Simple Finance Technology Corp
summary: Early US online banking startup acquired by BBVA in 2014; BBVA itself was sold to PNC in 2021, and PNC shut Simple down.
lesson: Being acquired is not an exit from execution risk if the acquirer is later acquired again.

name: Simple Habit
summary: A meditation app competing directly with Calm and Headspace, Simple Habit struggled to differentiate and effectively ceased independent operations amid intense market saturation in consumer meditation apps.
lesson: Consumer wellness apps entering a category already dominated by two well-capitalized leaders face brutal customer-acquisition-cost economics.

name: Simply Measured
summary: Social media analytics platform Simply Measured shut down in 2019 after being unable to keep pace with larger social-analytics competitors and platform API restrictions.
lesson: Analytics tools reliant on third-party platform APIs are vulnerable when those platforms tighten data access.
source: GeekWire, TechCrunch coverage of Simply Measured shutdown 2019

name: Sisense
summary: Embedded BI and analytics platform once valued over $1B that suffered a major security breach in 2024 and was subsequently sold at a distressed valuation.
lesson: A major security incident can rapidly destroy enterprise trust and valuation even for an established analytics vendor.
source: Sisense breach disclosure and acquisition coverage 2024

name: Sisu Data
summary: AI-powered data analysis platform for automated root-cause and driver analysis, which ceased operations in 2023 despite substantial funding, as enterprise buyers consolidated analytics spend around fewer, broader platforms.
lesson: A large war chest does not overcome a narrow product wedge if enterprise budgets consolidate toward broader analytics platforms rather than point solutions.
source: Industry reporting on Sisu Data's 2023 shutdown

name: Sixense Enterprises
summary: Sixense, an early motion-tracking controller maker (STEM System) for VR, failed to convert its Kickstarter-funded product into a lasting consumer business as bundled headset controllers from Oculus and HTC made standalone tracking peripherals redundant.
lesson: Third-party VR input peripherals became commercially unviable once major headset makers began bundling their own integrated motion controllers.
source: Road to VR reporting on Sixense STEM System and pivot to enterprise, 2016-2018

name: Sizmek
funding: publicly traded, spun off from Digital River 2016
summary: Sizmek, an ad-serving and DSP platform spun off from Digital River, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2019 and sold its assets to Amazon and Vector Capital.
lesson: Legacy ad-serving businesses can collapse quickly once buyers consolidate around fewer, more integrated platforms.

name: Skillz
summary: The competitive mobile skill-gaming platform went public via SPAC in 2020 at a multi-billion-dollar valuation, then saw its stock collapse over 95% amid high user-acquisition costs, a lawsuit against a rival over cheating, and mounting losses.
lesson: A SPAC valuation built on rapid user-acquisition-fueled growth unravels quickly once acquisition costs rise and retention lags.

name: Skip Scooters
summary: San Francisco-based e-scooter operator that lost several city permits and was later acquired by Helbiz, which itself unwound the business.
lesson: Permit-dependent micromobility businesses live and die by city regulatory relationships.

name: Skulpt
summary: Body-composition scanning device maker Skulpt ceased operations in 2018 after failing to achieve enough scale in a niche hardware category.
lesson: A highly specific fitness measurement niche may be too small to sustain an independent hardware company.

name: Sky and Space Global
summary: Nanosatellite IoT and communications constellation operator listed on the Australian Securities Exchange entered administration in 2020 after burning through capital on a small demonstrator constellation without securing enough commercial contracts.
lesson: A public listing provides capital access but does not substitute for proven commercial demand before scaling a constellation.

name: Sky Mavis (Axie Infinity)
summary: The play-to-earn game's Ronin sidechain bridge was hacked for $625M in 2022, and player and economic activity collapsed as crypto gaming interest cooled.
lesson: A play-to-earn economy is only as strong as the security of its underlying blockchain infrastructure.

name: Skybox Security
funding: over $330M raised; reportedly generated $500M+ in cumulative revenue
summary: Israeli attack-simulation and exposure/vulnerability-management analytics pioneer. Despite raising over $330M and building substantial revenue, Skybox abruptly shut down on February 24, 2025, laying off all ~300 employees in Israel and the US with no warning, after selling its technology and business assets to rival Tufin in a selective deal that did not assume Skybox's customer contracts.
lesson: A once-pioneering attack-simulation vendor can stagnate as newer AI-native exposure-management platforms overtake it; even substantial historical revenue does not prevent an overnight, no-warning shutdown when the cash runs out.
source: SecurityWeek, 'Skybox Security Shuts Down, Lays Off Entire Workforce' (Feb 2025); Calcalist, 'The fall of Skybox Security'

name: Skycatch
summary: Drone data-processing company for construction and mining sites that underwent repeated large layoffs and scaled down to near-inactivity by the early 2020s.
lesson: Drone-data startups need a durable, high-margin software layer since drone hardware and basic mapping quickly become commoditized.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Skycatch layoffs and struggles

name: Skyline (UK-based CRE AI, unrelated to Skyline AI Israel)
summary: Small UK proptech startup building AI-driven commercial property valuation tools; ceased operations in 2020 after failing to raise a follow-on round during the COVID-19 downturn in commercial real estate activity.
lesson: Thinly capitalized AI proptech startups are especially vulnerable to abrupt market shocks (like a pandemic-driven CRE freeze) that halt fundraising entirely.

name: Skymind
summary: Commercial company behind the open-source Deeplearning4j (DL4J) Java deep learning framework, which became embroiled in financial and legal disputes with backers around 2019-2020 and effectively dissolved as an independent commercial entity.
lesson: Governance and investor disputes can unwind a company even when its open-source technology has genuine adoption and technical merit.
source: TechCrunch and industry reporting on Skymind's 2019-2020 legal disputes

name: Skysense
summary: Startup building automated wireless drone-charging pads for enterprise fleets quietly ceased operations around 2019 as the drone-in-a-box category consolidated.
lesson: Picks-and-shovels infrastructure for an emerging market can disappear if the market consolidates around vertically integrated competitors.
source: Industry trade press coverage of drone-charging startups, 2019

name: Skytree
summary: Big-data machine learning platform (spun out of academic research at CMU/Georgia Tech) offering scalable ML infrastructure, which wound down operations around 2017 after failing to gain traction against cloud provider ML services and open-source alternatives.
lesson: Early enterprise ML infrastructure vendors were commonly outflanked once cloud providers (AWS, Google) began offering comparable managed ML services natively.
source: Industry and Crunchbase reporting on Skytree's 2017 wind-down

name: Slice Labs
summary: On-demand, AI-priced insurance-as-a-service platform; cut a third of its staff in 2020 and was forced into a narrower B2B insurance-cloud positioning after its ambitious on-demand consumer vision failed to gain traction.
lesson: Insurance-as-a-service infrastructure plays need a committed anchor carrier customer early; without one, on-demand AI pricing technology alone does not generate enough revenue to sustain headcount.

name: Slingshot Esports
summary: The esports media brand launched by Group Nine Media was shut down in 2020 as part of a broader corporate restructuring that cut underperforming digital-media verticals.
lesson: Esports media verticals launched inside larger digital-media companies are often the first cut when parent-company economics tighten.

name: Slyce
summary: Publicly traded visual-search and image-recognition company that partnered with retailers for 'snap-to-shop' commerce features saw its stock collapse and effectively wound down commercial operations around 2019 after failing to convert retail pilots into durable recurring revenue.
lesson: Retail visual-search pilots can generate press coverage without ever becoming a line item retailers are willing to pay for at scale.
source: Canadian small-cap market coverage of Slyce Inc.'s decline, 2018-2019

name: Slync
summary: Freight and supply-chain orchestration software startup that shut down in 2023 after its founder and CEO were criminally charged with defrauding investors.
lesson: Founder fraud can vaporize a well-funded logistics-tech company overnight, regardless of the underlying product.

name: Slync.io
summary: Goldman Sachs-backed logistics orchestration software startup automating freight workflows with AI. Collapsed after its ousted founder and CEO, Chris Kirchner, was indicted and later convicted of wire fraud and money laundering for diverting $25M of investor funds for personal use; the company went into liquidation in October 2023 despite a $24M rescue infusion.
lesson: Founder financial fraud can destroy a well-funded, technically credible logistics-AI company even after new management and emergency capital try to save it.
source: FreightWaves, Journal of Commerce, Wikipedia (Chris Kirchner) (2023-2024)

name: Smallhold
summary: Mushroom vertical-farming company Smallhold laid off much of its staff and closed several of its mini-farm facilities in 2023-2024 amid financial distress.
lesson: Distributed in-store micro-farms multiply maintenance and logistics costs relative to the revenue each unit generates.

name: Smart Sparrow
summary: Australian adaptive courseware platform used by universities to build personalized, data-driven lessons; shut down operations in 2020 after failing to scale beyond a niche higher-ed customer base.
lesson: Adaptive learning tools sold into university IT/LMS procurement cycles face brutally long sales cycles that can starve a startup of growth before product value compounds.
source: EdSurge shutdown coverage 2020; Smart Sparrow customer notices

name: SmartFocus
summary: Customer engagement and marketing platform SmartFocus (formerly Emailvision) went into administration in 2016 after years of declining revenue and failed restructuring.
lesson: A public listing does not guarantee survival if a CX vendor cannot adapt its product fast enough to keep pace with cloud-native challengers.
source: UK press coverage of SmartFocus administration, 2016

name: SmashFly
summary: Recruitment marketing and CRM platform was acquired by Symphony Talent in 2020 after years of competing unsuccessfully against Beamery, Phenom People, and SmartRecruiters, and its brand was retired.
lesson: Recruitment-marketing software became commoditized quickly, squeezing standalone vendors that lacked a broader platform.
source: HR Technologist coverage of Symphony Talent-SmashFly merger 2020

name: SmileDirectClub
summary: Direct-to-consumer clear-aligner company that bypassed in-person orthodontists faced lawsuits over safety and regulatory pushback from dentists, and shut down entirely in December 2023 after bankruptcy.
lesson: Disintermediating licensed clinicians invites regulatory and legal resistance that can outlast the growth story.

name: Snapdeal
summary: Indian ecommerce marketplace once valued near $6.5B saw a collapsed merger attempt with Flipkart in 2017, followed by mass layoffs and a drastic valuation markdown, and now operates as a much smaller value-fashion platform.
lesson: Overexpanding a marketplace ahead of proven unit economics leaves no cushion when a funding-driven price war arrives.

name: SnapRoute
summary: Built a cloud-native, disaggregated network operating system (Boulder OS) aimed at hyperscale and telecom data centers. Failed to gain sufficient commercial traction and pivoted away from networking into cybersecurity, effectively ending its original business.
lesson: Disaggregated network OS software faced brutal competition from both hyperscalers building in-house and established vendors, leaving little room for an independent NOS startup.

name: Snaps
summary: Conversational commerce and business-messaging platform Snaps shut down in 2019 after brand adoption of chat-based commerce lagged well behind investor expectations.
lesson: Betting an entire company on brands shifting customer engagement to messaging apps proved premature without sufficient consumer pull.

name: So-Sure (Sure)
summary: UK startup offering contents and gadget insurance priced through social friend groups wound down its direct-to-consumer product in 2022 amid funding difficulties and pivoted its remaining team toward other ventures.
lesson: Social-pricing gimmicks can generate press coverage without producing a loss ratio insurers can actually underwrite profitably.

name: SoapBox Labs
summary: Dublin-based startup building children's speech-recognition technology for education and toy applications, emphasizing safety and accuracy for young voices; faced significant financial difficulty and scaled back operations in the 2023-2024 downturn in venture funding for voice AI.
lesson: A well-differentiated technical niche (children's speech) still needs a large enough addressable market to justify the R&D cost of building specialized models from scratch.
source: Irish tech press (Business Post, Silicon Republic)

name: Soft Robotics Inc
summary: Robot-gripper and vision AI maker sold its gripper product line to Schmalz in 2024 and spun its AI/vision technology into a new company, Oxipital AI, after its CEO said grippers were too niche a business.
lesson: A narrow hardware niche may not support a standalone venture-scale company.

name: Solar Trust of America
funding: Backed by Solar Millennium AG, conditional $2.1B DOE loan
summary: Solar Trust of America was developing the large Blythe solar thermal project in California but filed for bankruptcy in 2012 after its German parent Solar Millennium went insolvent.
lesson: A project developer's fate can be tied to a parent company's solvency even when the project itself remains technically sound.

name: SolarReserve
funding: $700M+ project financing incl. $737M DOE loan guarantee (Crescent Dunes)
summary: SolarReserve built the Crescent Dunes molten-salt solar thermal plant in Nevada, but chronic technical failures and underperformance led to default on its power contract and bankruptcy of the project entity around 2019-2020.
lesson: Cutting-edge molten-salt storage at utility scale carried operational risks that undermined the entire project's bankability.

name: Solid
summary: Banking-as-a-service infrastructure startup became embroiled in legal disputes with its partner bank Evolve and faced funding difficulties in 2023-2024 amid the broader BaaS industry meltdown.
lesson: BaaS providers are only as stable as their sponsor bank relationships, which can turn adversarial under regulatory pressure.

name: Solon SE
summary: German solar module maker Solon SE filed for insolvency in 2012, one of several German solar manufacturers to collapse amid the industry-wide price war with Chinese producers.
lesson: National subsidy-driven demand doesn't guarantee domestic manufacturers survive global price competition.

name: Solyndra
summary: Solar panel maker that received a $535M DOE loan guarantee but collapsed when Chinese manufacturers crashed silicon and panel prices.
lesson: A capital-intensive hardware bet on a specific manufacturing process can be wiped out overnight by commodity price collapse.

name: Sonder Holdings
summary: Tech-enabled short-term rental company using AI-driven dynamic pricing across a large leased apartment/hotel portfolio; went public via SPAC in 2022, but persistent losses led to a Marriott partnership deal in 2024 and eventual bankruptcy filing in 2025 after failing to achieve sustainable unit economics.
lesson: AI-optimized dynamic pricing cannot rescue a business model built on high fixed lease costs if occupancy and rate assumptions don't hold across a large portfolio.

name: Songkick
summary: Concert discovery and ticketing platform that won a major patent lawsuit against Ticketmaster but shut down its consumer-facing app in 2017 after being acquired by Warner Music Group.
lesson: Winning a legal battle does not guarantee a sustainable standalone consumer business after acquisition by a strategic buyer.

name: SoulCycle
summary: Boutique cycling chain SoulCycle saw major studio closures and declining attendance after the pandemic, losing its premium growth story despite Equinox ownership.
lesson: A premium boutique fitness brand can lose momentum quickly once at-home alternatives and copycats erode its exclusivity.

name: Sound.xyz
summary: Music NFT platform that let artists sell limited-edition song drops directly to fans, but shut down in 2023 as the broader NFT market collapsed.
lesson: Business models built entirely on top of a speculative asset class are extremely exposed when that market cycle turns.

name: SourceAI
summary: French startup SourceAI was one of the earliest GPT-3-based code generators, letting users describe a function in plain English and get working code in roughly 40 programming languages with a usage-based pricing model. It saw early press attention in 2021 but produced no further funding, product, or press activity as the market moved decisively to VC-scale, IDE-integrated competitors.
lesson: Being among the very first to demo a compelling GPT-3 coding proof-of-concept generated press but not durable advantage once well-funded IDE-native competitors (GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, later Cursor) offered a far more integrated developer experience.

name: SourceForge
summary: Once the dominant open-source code hosting site, SourceForge's reputation collapsed after it began bundling adware with installers in 2015, driving nearly all projects to migrate to GitHub.
lesson: Monetization decisions that betray developer trust can destroy a platform's community faster than any competitor.

name: Sourcegraph Cody
summary: Sourcegraph raised $223M and was valued at $2.625B in 2021 on the strength of its code-search platform, later launching the Cody AI coding assistant. By July 2025 it discontinued free, Pro, and Enterprise Starter Cody plans, downsized headcount, and spun out its newer Amp coding-agent product as a separate company.
lesson: Being an early, well-funded incumbent in developer tooling is no defense against a wave of faster-moving, model-native coding agents (Cursor, Copilot, Claude Code); legacy products can be forced to retreat or be spun off even at unicorn valuations.

name: South Pole
funding: Privately held; large multinational carbon project developer
summary: South Pole was the world's largest carbon-offset seller, using remote sensing and modeling to estimate and verify avoided-deforestation credits from projects like Kariba (Zimbabwe). Investigations found it had overstated the climate benefit of Kariba credits by a factor of five or more; the fallout cost it major clients (Volkswagen, Booking.com, Adyen), its CEO, and roughly 20% of its workforce in late 2023.
lesson: When a company's core product is a quantitative climate claim, the accuracy of that measurement is the entire business; papering over shortfalls in the underlying science destroys trust with an intensity proportional to how central that claim was to the brand.
source: Bloomberg, Follow the Money, MIT Technology Review coverage of the South Pole/Kariba scandal (2023)

name: Spaceflight Industries (parent of Spaceflight Inc.)
summary: Rideshare launch broker and satellite-imagery parent company restructured in 2020, spinning off its BlackSky imagery subsidiary as an independent company while the core Spaceflight Inc. rideshare business was later sold to Firefly Aerospace in 2023 after struggling to reach standalone profitability.
lesson: Running an internal satellite-operator business alongside a third-party rideshare-broker business created conflicting incentives that neither unit could resolve profitably.

name: SparkCognition
summary: Austin-based AI platform applying machine learning to industrial and cybersecurity use cases (including AI-driven endpoint/anomaly protection). After over-hiring during the AI boom, SparkCognition conducted mass layoffs and restructured its business, eventually rebranding its core operations as Avathon and narrowing its focus away from broad cybersecurity AI applications.
lesson: Positioning as a broad 'AI for everything' platform -- spanning security, industrial IoT, and finance -- diluted product focus, and the eventual rebrand away from the original brand signaled the original strategy did not scale profitably.
source: Glassdoor employee reviews (Avathon/SparkCognition mass layoff); company rebrand press coverage

name: SparkCognition (Avathon)
summary: SparkCognition pitched itself as a broad AI platform spanning industrial analytics, cybersecurity, and government/defense use cases including U.S. Army and DIU pilots. After years of pivoting across verticals without a durable core product, the company underwent major layoffs and rebranded as Avathon in an attempt to refocus on industrial AI.
lesson: Chasing every AI-adjacent government and enterprise vertical at once (industrial, cyber, defense) dilutes focus; a rebrand after mass layoffs signals a business searching for product-market fit years after raising growth capital.

name: Spatial
summary: Spatial, a virtual workspace and metaverse events platform, laid off roughly two-thirds of staff in 2023 and pivoted away from its original 3D metaverse product toward AI-generated avatars and content, after failing to sustain enterprise VR meeting adoption.
lesson: Virtual-world workplace platforms built during the pandemic-era remote-work boom struggled once companies reverted to simpler video-conferencing tools.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Spatial layoffs and pivot, 2023

name: Spell.ml
summary: Cloud-based ML training and workflow management platform aimed at simplifying distributed training and experiment tracking, which wound down its independent operations around 2022 amid intensifying competition from better-funded MLOps and cloud GPU platforms.
lesson: Early ML training-workflow platforms without a durable technical moat were repeatedly outpaced by larger, better-capitalized entrants in the same space.
source: Industry and Crunchbase status reporting on Spell.ml, 2022

name: Spin
summary: E-scooter startup acquired by Ford in 2018, then sold to Tier Mobility in 2022 after Ford failed to make micromobility work within its business.
lesson: Corporate-owned mobility units often get deprioritized and divested when parent strategy shifts.

name: SpinVox
summary: UK voicemail-to-text company that claimed proprietary AI transcription; investigative reporting alleged much of the 'AI' transcription was actually performed by low-cost human workers overseas, triggering a collapse in investor confidence and a distressed fire-sale to Nuance in 2009.
lesson: Misrepresenting the degree of automation behind an 'AI' product is a governance time bomb that destroys investor and customer trust once exposed.

name: Spire Global
summary: Spire Global operates a satellite constellation delivering weather, maritime and aviation data with AI-driven forecasting products. Despite going public, the company disclosed substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern in early 2025, restated prior financial statements, breached loan covenants, and had to sell its maritime business for cash just to shore up liquidity.
lesson: Satellite constellations are extraordinarily capital-intensive; going public doesn't fix a structurally negative cash-flow business, and financial-reporting failures compound investor distrust exactly when a company most needs market confidence.
source: Bloomberg Law, SEC filings, Motley Fool coverage of Spire Global's 2025 going-concern warning

name: Spirit AI
summary: Built conversational AI and character/story-crafting tools for games and VR, plus toxicity-detection technology. Never disclosed major growth metrics or follow-on funding, and was quietly acquired by Twitch in August 2022 in what industry trackers characterize as a talent/technology acquisition rather than a growth exit.
lesson: A quiet acqui-hire after years of limited disclosed traction is a common, under-reported failure mode in AI-gaming: the company avoids the 'shutdown' headline but never achieved independent scale.

name: Spirit Airlines
summary: US ultra-low-cost carrier filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after a proposed JetBlue merger was blocked by regulators and it couldn't return to profitability post-COVID.
lesson: A blocked exit can leave a structurally unprofitable business with no path forward but bankruptcy.

name: Splash Music
summary: AI text-to-song generator with an app for AI-assisted beatmaking hired former SoundCloud exec Tracy Chan as CEO but shut down its consumer Splash Music & Beatmaker app in January 2024 and its Splash Pro web app in April 2024 after eight months, citing lack of commercial sustainability, and pivoted the team back into Roblox game development.
lesson: Viral generative-music demos do not automatically convert into paying users; without a durable monetization path even well-funded, well-staffed teams will abandon the product category entirely.

name: Splinter
funding: Part of Gizmodo Media Group/G-O Media
summary: A politics and culture site spun out of Fusion was shut down by G/O Media in 2019 as the private-equity-backed owner consolidated and cut underperforming Gawker-descended brands.
lesson: Private-equity ownership of media brands tends to prioritize consolidation and cost-cutting over incubating smaller editorial voices.

name: Splyce
summary: The Netherlands-based multi-title esports organization wound down its competitive rosters after failing to secure a sustainable path in top-tier franchised leagues.
lesson: Without a franchise slot in a marquee league, mid-tier esports orgs struggle to cover rising player salaries from sponsorship alone.

name: Spoken Communications
summary: Cloud contact-center and voice-AI platform provider that struggled financially and was ultimately acquired by Alvaria (formerly Aspect) at a distressed valuation rather than achieving an independent scale outcome.
lesson: Contact-center voice AI is a capital- and sales-intensive category where undercapitalized players get absorbed by larger consolidators rather than reaching independent scale.
source: Trade press, No Jitter contact-center coverage

name: SpoonRocket
summary: Instant meal delivery startup promising 10-minute delivery shut down in 2016 after running out of funding.
lesson: Extreme delivery-speed promises multiply operating costs beyond what thin food margins can support.

name: Spotcap
funding: ~$100M (equity and debt from Access Industries, Finstar, and others)
summary: Berlin-based online lender using data-driven, semi-automated underwriting to provide working capital loans to SMEs across Europe and Australia/NZ. Ceased operations in 2020 as it could not raise further capital during the COVID-19 credit crunch.
lesson: AI-driven SME lenders are highly exposed to macro credit cycles; a sudden shock (COVID-19) that spikes default risk and freezes lending capital can end the business almost overnight even with a working data model.
source: Reuters, TechCrunch coverage of Spotcap wind-down (2020)

name: Sprig
summary: On-demand healthy meal delivery service shut down in 2017 after failing to make its kitchen-to-door model profitable.
lesson: Vertically integrated food delivery requires enormous capital to reach the density needed for profitability.

name: Spring (Shop Spring)
summary: Mobile shopping marketplace app aggregating brands into one checkout experience shut down in 2017 after failing to gain enough traction against larger platforms.
lesson: Aggregator marketplaces need a real distribution edge, not just a better checkout UX.

name: Spring (ShopSpring)
summary: Mobile commerce app that let consumers buy directly from brands through a single checkout shut down in 2017 after failing to achieve the transaction volume needed to justify its funding and staff.
lesson: Aggregating many brands into a single mobile checkout app requires solving both brand-side integration costs and consumer discovery, and failing either dooms the model.

name: Sproutling
summary: Sproutling, a wearable smart baby monitor, was acquired by Mattel in 2016 but the product line was discontinued in October 2018 after failing to hit sales projections.
lesson: An acquisition by a strategic buyer is not an exit guarantee; hardware products absorbed into a large toy company can be shelved if they don't fit core revenue lines.

name: Square Roots
summary: Container-farming and vertical-farm software company Square Roots laid off most staff and shut down its own farming operations in 2023, pivoting to a software-only model.
lesson: Operating physical farms while trying to sell farm-management software to competitors creates conflicting incentives and cost structures.

name: Stability AI
summary: Open-source generative AI company (Stable Diffusion) faced severe cash-flow problems, unpaid compute bills, and a wave of researcher departures, culminating in CEO Emad Mostaque's resignation in 2024.
lesson: Open-source AI model releases build brand but not necessarily a monetization engine to fund the compute costs behind them.

name: StackMob
summary: Mobile backend-as-a-service platform acquired by PayPal in 2013 and shut down the following year as PayPal absorbed the engineering team.
lesson: Acquihire-style acquisitions often mean the acquired product itself has no long-term future.

name: StackPath
summary: Edge computing and CDN platform incorporating AI-based traffic routing and network security, aiming to be an edge infrastructure layer for telecom and enterprise customers. Laid off the large majority of its staff and wound down most core operations in 2023 amid the broader edge-computing capital pullback.
lesson: Building global edge infrastructure to compete with hyperscalers and CDN incumbents required far more sustained capital than the edge-computing hype cycle ultimately supported.
source: Trade press coverage of StackPath layoffs and wind-down, 2023

name: Standard AI (Standard Cognition)
summary: Computer-vision checkout-free technology company that competed directly with Amazon's Just Walk Out abandoned its core autonomous-checkout business in March 2024 amid layoffs, pivoting entirely to in-store camera analytics.
lesson: Checkout-free computer vision proved too costly to retrofit into most stores profitably, pushing well-funded players to pivot to lower-cost analytics products instead.

name: Standard Cognition (Standard AI)
summary: Autonomous, gate-free cashierless checkout technology positioned as an Amazon Just Walk Out competitor for existing grocery stores. After years of pilots failed to convert into broad commercial deployment, the company laid off staff and pivoted away from full autonomous checkout in 2023-2024 toward a computer-vision "Vision Analytics" loss-prevention product, effectively abandoning its founding thesis.
lesson: Retrofitting camera-based autonomous checkout onto legacy store formats remained too expensive and error-prone to reach retailer-wide rollout, forcing a retreat to a narrower analytics product.
source: CSP Daily News, "Standard AI Moves Away From Autonomous Shopping", 2024

name: Starbreeze Studios (VR arcade business)
funding: Public company; entered Swedish corporate reconstruction in 2018
summary: Starbreeze, the Swedish game publisher behind The Void VR arcade chain and StarVR headset venture, entered corporate reconstruction (a form of bankruptcy protection) in 2018 after over-leveraging its VR ambitions, and later divested its VR arcade assets.
lesson: Diversifying into capital-intensive VR hardware and location-based entertainment ventures can destabilize a company's core business if not carefully financed.
source: Reuters reporting on Starbreeze corporate reconstruction, 2018

name: Starry
summary: Fixed wireless broadband provider using proprietary AI-optimized point-to-multipoint radio network technology to deliver last-mile internet access, aiming to disrupt cable/fiber incumbents. Filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after years of heavy cash burn and slower-than-expected subscriber growth.
lesson: Building and operating last-mile wireless network infrastructure is extremely capital-intensive; even with novel spectrum-efficient AI-managed radio technology, subscriber acquisition costs and buildout costs outpaced revenue growth.
source: Reuters coverage of Starry Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing, February 2023

name: Starsky Robotics
summary: Pursued fully driverless (no safety driver) autonomous trucking and ran real driverless truck tests in Florida, but couldn't raise a new round as investors soured on near-term full autonomy; the founder's public shutdown post-mortem became one of the most-cited 'why we failed' essays in the AV industry.
lesson: Proving the core technology works in a pilot doesn't guarantee the capital markets will fund the multi-year path to commercial scale.

name: StarVR
funding: Joint venture funding from Starbreeze and Acer
summary: StarVR, a joint venture between Starbreeze and Acer building ultra-wide-FOV VR headsets for location-based entertainment, ceased operations around 2023 after its parent Starbreeze underwent years of financial restructuring.
lesson: A niche premium VR hardware joint venture is highly exposed to financial distress at either parent company, even if its own technology is well-regarded.
source: Road to VR reporting on StarVR wind-down

name: Step
summary: Teen-focused banking and credit-building app underwent major layoffs and scaled back operations in 2023-2024 after failing to convert its large free user base into sustainable revenue.
lesson: A large free user base in teen banking does not guarantee a viable path to monetization.

name: Stitch Fix
summary: Pioneer of AI-plus-human-stylist personalized apparel subscription boxes, once a Wall Street darling praised for its data-science-driven merchandising algorithms. Revenue and active client counts fell for years after peaking in 2021, the company closed UK operations in 2023, and its stock lost over 95% of value from IPO highs amid repeated leadership changes and failed pivots to direct-buy shopping.
lesson: A sophisticated recommendation algorithm cannot by itself overcome high styling/logistics costs and customer-acquisition fatigue once the initial novelty of algorithmic curation wears off.
source: SEC filings (10-K), retail trade press on Stitch Fix revenue decline, 2022-2024

name: Stitch Labs
summary: Inventory and order management software for ecommerce sellers was acquired by Square in 2019 and discontinued in 2020 as Square consolidated its commerce tools.
lesson: Being acquired doesn't guarantee product continuity if the acquirer's roadmap diverges.

name: Stockpile
summary: Gift-card-based stock investing platform aimed at giving fractional shares as presents shut down after failing to convert gift redemptions into a durable trading business.
lesson: A clever acquisition gimmick doesn't necessarily build a habitual investing product.

name: Storify
summary: A social-media curation tool that let journalists compile tweets into stories was acquired by Livefyre, then Adobe, and shut down in 2018 as usage declined.
lesson: A tool built around one platform's embed API is vulnerable when that platform changes access or the workflow it served falls out of fashion.

name: STR Holdings
summary: STR Holdings made solar panel encapsulant materials and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2016 after demand collapsed amid oversupply and price crashes across the solar supply chain.
lesson: Suppliers to a commoditizing industry get squeezed even harder than the manufacturers they serve during a price war.

name: Stratolaunch Systems (original venture)
summary: Built the world's largest airplane to air-launch rockets, but after founder Paul Allen's death in 2018 the company laid off most staff and canceled its rocket programs in 2019, selling the aircraft and remaining assets to a private equity firm.
lesson: A company single-source-funded by one billionaire's personal wealth can be terminated by that founder's death regardless of the hardware's technical progress.

name: Struckd
summary: Zurich-based no-code 3D mobile game-creation platform acquired by Unity Technologies in 2021 as part of Unity's push into AI- and template-assisted game creation tools. Unity is shutting the platform down on August 1, 2026 as part of its ongoing consolidation of acquired products.
lesson: Acquisitions made to fill a strategic roadmap gap can quietly get cut years later once the acquirer reprioritizes, leaving the acquired product's users and creators with no continuity plan.

name: STX Entertainment
summary: A film and TV studio that raised hundreds of millions, went public via SPAC in 2020, saw its stock collapse, and was ultimately absorbed into Lionsgate in a distressed 2023 deal.
lesson: A SPAC listing can provide capital and a public valuation but doesn't fix a studio's underlying content economics.

name: Subtl.ai
summary: Subtl.ai landed pilot programs with defense organizations for its AI product but never converted soft investor interest into signed term sheets. Each new defense customer required a custom build rather than a repeatable product, and when the 2025 funding environment tightened, the company ran out of runway and shut down.
lesson: Landing defense pilots is not the same as having a repeatable product; if every government customer requires a bespoke build, the business never achieves the margins or scale VCs are underwriting.

name: Suitable Technologies
funding: Willow Garage spin-off, backed by Scott Hassan
summary: Maker of the Beam telepresence robot, spun out of Willow Garage, shut down operations in 2018 after failing to reach profitable scale in the enterprise telepresence market.
lesson: A well-engineered telepresence product still needs a mainstream buying trigger, which remote-work adoption hadn't yet created.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Suitable Technologies shutdown, 2018

name: Sulon Technologies
summary: Canadian AR/VR headset maker Sulon Technologies, known for the Sulon Cortex, quietly shut down around 2017 after its self-contained mixed-reality headset failed to reach market against better-funded competitors.
lesson: Smaller hardware startups without major partners struggled to compete once large tech companies entered the standalone VR/MR headset category.
source: Road to VR reporting on Sulon Technologies wind-down

name: Summon
summary: Los Angeles-based ride-hailing startup that attempted to compete directly with Uber and Lyft in a single metro market and shut down within two years.
lesson: Regional ride-hailing challengers rarely survive head-on competition against nationally-scaled incumbents.

name: Sun Basket
summary: Organic and specialty-diet meal kit subscription service underwent massive layoffs and downsizing starting in 2022 as investor funding for meal kits dried up.
lesson: Premium positioning doesn't exempt meal-kit subscriptions from the category's structural retention and logistics costs.

name: Sundrop Farms
summary: Solar-powered desalination greenhouse company Sundrop Farms, after building large facilities in Australia and the US, faced severe financial distress and restructuring around 2020.
lesson: Combining novel renewable-energy infrastructure with greenhouse agriculture multiplies capital risk across two unproven cost curves at once.
source: AgFunder News, Sundrop Farms financial distress coverage

name: SunEdison
summary: Once the world's largest renewable energy developer, SunEdison filed for bankruptcy after an aggressive debt-fueled acquisition spree collapsed under its own weight.
lesson: Scaling a renewables developer through aggressive leverage and acquisitions can outrun the cash flow needed to service that debt.

name: Sungevity
summary: Residential solar installer Sungevity, known for satellite-based remote quoting, filed for bankruptcy in 2017 after overexpansion and executive turmoil drained its cash.
lesson: A clever customer-acquisition technology cannot offset unsustainable installation and financing cost structures.

name: Sunnova Energy
summary: Residential solar and storage financing company that filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2025 after high interest rates and reduced federal incentives crushed its leveraged leasing model.
lesson: A debt-heavy leasing model for residential solar is highly exposed to rising interest rates and policy shifts.

name: Sunnova Energy International
funding: Publicly traded (NYSE: NOVA), $8.9B in long-term debt at filing
summary: Residential solar financier Sunnova filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2025 after rising interest rates, California policy changes, and a terminated DOE loan guarantee pushed it into insolvency; its assets were sold for about $118 million.
lesson: A solar-lease financing model built on cheap debt becomes fatally exposed when interest rates rise and government support is withdrawn.
source: Houston Public Media, CNBC, Kroll restructuring filings

name: SunPower
summary: Longtime residential solar installer and panel maker that filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2024 after liquidity problems and a collapsing dealer network.
lesson: Decades of market presence don't protect against a liquidity crisis when financing costs rise and demand softens.

name: SunPower Corporation
summary: Once a leading US solar panel and residential installer, SunPower filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in August 2024 after halting lease/PPA support and cutting a quarter of its workforce, selling its installation assets to Complete Solaria for $45 million.
lesson: Even a decades-old market leader can collapse when financing costs rise and dealer-channel unit economics turn negative.

name: Suntech Power
funding: Publicly traded (NYSE: STP), billions in debt
summary: Once the world's largest solar panel manufacturer, China's Suntech Power defaulted on $541M in convertible bonds and its main subsidiary entered bankruptcy in 2013 amid a global panel price collapse.
lesson: Being the largest manufacturer offers no protection when an entire industry oversupplies itself into a price collapse.

name: Sunverge Energy
summary: Sunverge made residential battery storage systems paired with solar and virtual power plant software but ceased operations in 2018 after running out of funding.
lesson: Being early to residential battery storage and virtual power plants wasn't enough before the market and utility incentives matured.

name: Super League Gaming
summary: The youth and amateur esports platform went public via reverse merger in 2018, but its stock collapsed and the company underwent repeated restructurings and pivots away from its original model.
lesson: A public listing accelerates the need for a proven, repeatable revenue model that many amateur esports platforms never achieved.

name: Superpedestrian
summary: MIT-spinout maker of the LINK scooter with proprietary safety tech that shut down its micromobility operations in late 2023 after failing to raise further funding.
lesson: Even differentiated hardware-safety technology couldn't overcome the capital intensity of scooter-fleet operations.

name: Sure (Sure App)
summary: Embedded-insurance infrastructure provider marketed as the AI layer for intelligent insurance; cut roughly 70 staff in a 2025 restructuring amid a difficult fundraising environment for infrastructure-layer insurtechs.
lesson: Infrastructure and API insurtechs depend on carrier and brand partners scaling volume; when partner programs stall, thin per-policy margins leave little buffer for a prolonged funding downturn.

name: Svpply
summary: Social product-curation and shopping-bookmark startup was acquired by eBay in 2012 but was shut down by eBay in 2014 after failing to integrate meaningfully into eBay's retail strategy.
lesson: An acqui-hire into a large e-commerce company can still end in shutdown if the acquired product doesn't fit the parent's core retail roadmap.

name: Sweep AI
summary: Sweep started as a GitHub-based autonomous 'AI junior developer' agent that earned 7,600+ GitHub stars, then abandoned that vision in 2024 to pivot to a much narrower JetBrains IDE autocomplete plugin, shrinking to a 4-person team, after realizing full autonomous coding was 'many years out.'
lesson: Open-source buzz and stars are not proof of a viable business; teams that build ahead of what the underlying model capability can reliably deliver often have to retreat to a much smaller, less ambitious product.

name: Swirl Networks
summary: Bluetooth-beacon based in-store proximity marketing platform for retailers shut down its consumer operations in 2017 after beacon marketing failed to achieve broad retailer or consumer adoption.
lesson: Hardware-dependent proximity marketing requires both retailer installation and consumer opt-in at scale, and missing either kills adoption.

name: Switch
summary: Anonymous, swipe-based "Tinder for jobs" mobile matching app that let candidates connect resumes and social profiles to get AI-curated daily job recommendations; the company quietly shut down around 2018 after failing to out-scale rival swipe-hiring apps.
lesson: Consumer-style swipe mechanics attracted press and seed funding, but the "Tinder for jobs" category as a whole failed to convert novelty into durable hiring outcomes.

name: Sword Ciboodle
summary: Customer experience software vendor Sword Ciboodle was acquired by Genesys in 2013 and its product line was progressively merged into and discontinued in favor of the Genesys platform.
lesson: Mid-market CX platforms acquired by larger contact-center vendors typically get consolidated away rather than run as a separate brand long-term.
source: Genesys 2013 acquisition announcement for Sword Ciboodle

name: Sycamore Networks
summary: Optical networking equipment maker was one of the dot-com era's most extreme boom-bust stories, falling from a peak market cap near $45B to near zero before winding down.
lesson: Valuations built on the assumption that internet bandwidth demand would grow indefinitely can evaporate the moment the buildout cycle pauses.

name: Symbio Robotics
summary: AI software for real-time control of industrial robots used by Ford and Toyota; closed its Emeryville facility and liquidated assets in 2022.
lesson: Selling into legacy automotive robot cells means enterprise sales cycles that can outlast your runway.

name: Symbo Insurance
summary: Insurance infrastructure and micro-insurance API startup operating in India and Singapore ceased its India operations in 2023 after failing to secure continued funding.
lesson: B2B insurance infrastructure providers still depend on their carrier and distribution partners actually scaling volume.

name: Symphony Talent
summary: Talent-marketing and CRM platform formed from merging SmashFly and other assets struggled to gain traction against Phenom and Beamery and effectively wound down its independent operations by 2023.
lesson: Rolling up struggling assets under private equity doesn't fix an underlying competitive-positioning problem.
source: Industry trade press on Symphony Talent decline

name: Synapse Financial Technologies
summary: Banking-as-a-service middleware provider that collapsed into bankruptcy in 2024, leaving reconciliation discrepancies between partner banks and fintech apps that froze an estimated $85M+ in end-customer deposits.
lesson: In layered banking-as-a-service stacks, poor ledger reconciliation between middleware and partner banks can strand real customer money.

name: Synthesis AI
